title: Parametric Shortest Paths in a Linearly Interpolated Graph
summary:
We consider the parametric shortest paths problem in a linearly interpolated graph. Given two positively-weighted directed graphs $G_0=(V,E,ω_0)$ and $G_1=(V,E,ω_1),$ the linearly interpolated graph
  is the family of graphs
  $(1-λ)G_0+λG_1$, parameterized by $λ\in [0,1]$. The problem is
  to compute all distinct parametric shortest paths. We compute a data
  structure in $Θ(k|E|\log |V|)$ time, where~$k$ is the number of
  distinct parametric shortest paths over all~$λ\in [0,1]$ that exist for a
  nontrivial interval of parameters, each corresponding to a linear function
  in a maximal sub-interval of $[0,1]$. Using this data structure, a shortest
  path query takes~$Θ(\log k)$ time.

AI.tldr: Precomputes a piecewise-linear representation of the λ-dependent shortest-path function for linearly interpolated graphs, enabling O(log k) shortest-path queries after O(k|E| log|V|) preprocessing, where k is the number of λ-intervals on which a single (s,t) path is optimal.
AI.motivation: Understand how shortest paths evolve under a linear interpolation of edge weights, and support fast retrieval of the optimal path for any λ in [0,1].
AI.method: Compute and store all distinct parametric shortest paths that are optimal on nontrivial λ-intervals; partition [0,1] into maximal sub-intervals where a fixed path is optimal; represent each path by its λ-linear weight function; build a data structure that supports querying the optimal path for any λ in O(log k) time; preprocessing runs in Θ(k|E| log|V|).
AI.result: A data structure is obtained that encodes the lower envelope of path-weight functions; can answer a shortest-path query for a given λ in Θ(log k) time; the total number of distinct optimal paths over the parameter range is k.
AI.conclusion: The approach yields efficient queries for linearly interpolated graphs when k is small; performance deteriorates with large k (potentially exponential in graph size in worst case); useful as a compact representation of the parametric shortest-path function and invites extensions to multiple parameters or dynamic updates.

title: Strips as Tokens: Artist Mesh Generation with Native UV Segmentation
summary: Recent advancements in autoregressive transformers have demonstrated remarkable potential for generating artist-quality meshes. However, the token ordering strategies employed by existing methods typically fail to meet professional artist standards, where coordinate-based sorting yields inefficiently long sequences, and patch-based heuristics disrupt the continuous edge flow and structural regularity essential for high-quality modeling. To address these limitations, we propose Strips as Tokens (SATO), a novel framework with a token ordering strategy inspired by triangle strips. By constructing the sequence as a connected chain of faces that explicitly encodes UV boundaries, our method naturally preserves the organized edge flow and semantic layout characteristic of artist-created meshes. A key advantage of this formulation is its unified representation, enabling the same token sequence to be decoded into either a triangle or quadrilateral mesh. This flexibility facilitates joint training on both data types: large-scale triangle data provides fundamental structural priors, while high-quality quad data enhances the geometric regularity of the outputs. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SATO consistently outperforms prior methods in terms of geometric quality, structural coherence, and UV segmentation.

AI.tldr: SATO introduces a triangle-strip-inspired token ordering for autoregressive mesh generation with a unified token sequence that can decode to triangles or quads, preserving edge flow and UV layout, enabling joint training on triangle and quad data to improve geometric quality and segmentation.
AI.motivation: Existing token orderings (coordinate-based, patch-based) disrupt edge flow and semantic layout, leading to long sequences and poor geometric regularity; a representation that preserves edge flow and UV boundaries is needed for professional-grade artist-quality meshes.
AI.method: Define a token sequence as a connected chain of faces analogous to triangle strips, explicitly encoding UV boundaries to preserve edge flow and semantic layout; use a unified representation that decodes to either triangles or quadrilaterals and supports joint training on both data types to inject structural priors and enhance regularity.
AI.result: Experimental results show SATO consistently outperforms prior methods in geometric quality, structural coherence, and UV segmentation, with benefits from the unified representation and the triangle-strip-inspired ordering.
AI.conclusion: SATO provides a unified, edge-flow-preserving tokenization framework for autoregressive mesh generation that supports both triangle and quad outputs and joint training, yielding higher-quality meshes.

title: LEGO: Latent-space Exploration for Geometry-aware Optimization of Humanoid Kinematic Design
summary: Designing robot morphologies and kinematics has traditionally relied on human intuition, with little systematic foundation. Motion-design co-optimization offers a promising path toward automation, but two major challenges remain: (i) the vast, unstructured design space and (ii) the difficulty of constructing task-specific loss functions. We propose a new paradigm that minimizes human involvement by (i) learning the design search space from existing mechanical designs, rather than hand-crafting it, and (ii) defining the loss directly from human motion data via motion retargeting and Procrustes analysis. Using screw-theory-based joint axis representation and isometric manifold learning, we construct a compact, geometry-preserving latent space of humanoid upper body designs in which optimization is tractable. We then solve design optimization in this latent space using gradient-free optimization. Our approach establishes a principled framework for data-driven robot design and demonstrates that leveraging existing designs and human motion can effectively guide the automated discovery of novel robot design.

AI.tldr: A data-driven workflow for robot design optimization that learns a geometry-preserving latent design space from existing mechanical designs and human motion data, enabling gradient-free optimization to discover novel humanoid upper-body configurations.
AI.motivation: To reduce reliance on human intuition in morpho-kinematic design, address the large, unstructured design space, and avoid hand-crafting task-specific losses by leveraging motion data and data-driven space learning.
AI.method: Represent joint axes with screw theory; learn an isometric manifold to embed designs of humanoid upper-body into a compact, geometry-preserving latent space; construct a design optimization problem in this latent space; define task loss from human motion using motion retargeting and Procrustes analysis; solve with gradient-free optimization.
AI.result: Establishes a principled data-driven design framework; demonstrates that leveraging existing designs and human motion can guide automated discovery of novel robot designs; provides a tractable optimization setting via the latent, geometry-preserving space.
AI.conclusion: Data-driven construction of design spaces guided by human motion offers a principled path toward automated robot design and can be extended to broader morphologies and tasks.

title: Detection of Hate and Threat in Digital Forensics: A Case-Driven Multimodal Approach
summary: Digital forensic investigations increasingly rely on heterogeneous evidence such as images, scanned documents, and contextual reports. These artifacts may contain explicit or implicit expressions of harm, hate, threat, violence, or intimidation, yet existing automated approaches often assume clean text input or apply vision models without forensic justification. This paper presents a case-driven multimodal approach for hate and threat detection in forensic analysis. The proposed framework explicitly determines the presence and source of textual evidence, distinguishing between embedded text, associated contextual text, and image-only evidence. Based on the identified evidence configuration, the framework selectively applies text analysis, multimodal fusion, or image-only semantic reasoning using vision language models with vision transformer backbones (ViT). By conditioning inference on evidence availability, the approach mirrors forensic decision-making, improves evidentiary traceability, and avoids unjustified modality assumptions. Experimental evaluation on forensic-style image evidence demonstrates consistent and interpretable behavior across heterogeneous evidence scenarios.

AI.tldr: A case-driven multimodal framework for hate and threat detection in forensic analysis that identifies the source and presence of textual evidence and adapts analysis accordingly.
AI.motivation: To address heterogeneous forensic artifacts and ensure evidentiary traceability while avoiding unjustified modality assumptions.
AI.method: Detects evidence configuration (embedded text, contextual text, image-only); conditioning inference on evidence availability; uses text analysis, multimodal fusion, or image-only semantic reasoning via vision-language models with ViT backbones.
AI.result: Demonstrates consistent and interpretable behavior across heterogeneous evidence scenarios on forensic-style image evidence.
AI.conclusion: Improves forensic decision-making by aligning inference with evidence configuration and enhancing traceability; avoids assumptions about modality.

title: GNN-as-Judge: Unleashing the Power of LLMs for Graph Learning with GNN Feedback
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown strong performance on text-attributed graphs (TAGs) due to their superior semantic understanding ability on textual node features. However, their effectiveness as predictors in the low-resource setting, where labeled nodes are severely limited and scarce, remains constrained since fine-tuning LLMs usually requires sufficient labeled data, especially when the TAG shows complex structural patterns. In essence, this paper targets two key challenges: (i) the difficulty of generating and selecting reliable pseudo labels on TAGs for LLMs, and (ii) the need to mitigate potential label noise when fine-tuning LLMs with pseudo labels. To counter the challenges, we propose a new framework, GNN-as-Judge, which can unleash the power of LLMs for few-shot semi-supervised learning on TAGs by incorporating the structural inductive bias of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). Specifically, GNN-as-Judge introduces a collaborative pseudo-labeling strategy that first identifies the most influenced unlabeled nodes from labeled nodes, then exploits both the agreement and disagreement patterns between LLMs and GNNs to generate reliable labels. Furthermore, we develop a weakly-supervised LLM fine-tuning algorithm that can distill the knowledge from informative pseudo labels while mitigating the potential label noise. Experiments on multiple TAG datasets demonstrate that GNN-as-Judge significantly outperforms existing methods, particularly in low-resource regimes where labeled data are scarce.

AI.tldr: A GNN-guided pseudo-labeling framework, GNN-as-Judge, combines LLMs and GNNs for few-shot semi-supervised learning on text-attributed graphs, achieving strong performance and robust label handling.
AI.motivation: In text-attributed graphs (TAGs), labeled nodes are scarce and pseudo-labels from LLMs can be noisy. There is a need to leverage graph structure to identify reliable pseudo labels and to fine-tune LLMs robustly in low-resource settings.
AI.method: Collaborative pseudo-labeling: identify the most influential unlabeled nodes from labeled nodes; exploit both agreement and disagreement patterns between LLMs and GNNs to generate reliable labels. A weakly-supervised LLM fine-tuning algorithm distills knowledge from informative pseudo labels while mitigating label noise.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple TAG datasets show that GNN-as-Judge significantly outperforms existing methods, with pronounced gains in low-resource regimes where labeled data are scarce.
AI.conclusion: Integrating GNN inductive bias with LLM-based pseudo-labeling and noise-aware fine-tuning yields robust few-shot performance on TAGs, validating the proposed framework.

title: OpenKedge: Governing Agentic Mutation with Execution-Bound Safety and Evidence Chains
summary: The rise of autonomous AI agents exposes a fundamental flaw in API-centric architectures: probabilistic systems directly execute state mutations without sufficient context, coordination, or safety guarantees. We introduce OpenKedge, a protocol that redefines mutation as a governed process rather than an immediate consequence of API invocation. OpenKedge requires actors to submit declarative intent proposals, which are evaluated against deterministically derived system state, temporal signals, and policy constraints prior to execution. Approved intents are compiled into execution contracts that strictly bound permitted actions, resource scope, and time, and are enforced via ephemeral, task-oriented identities. This shifts safety from reactive filtering to preventative, execution-bound enforcement. Crucially, OpenKedge introduces an Intent-to-Execution Evidence Chain (IEEC), which cryptographically links intent, context, policy decisions, execution bounds, and outcomes into a unified lineage. This transforms mutation into a verifiable and reconstructable process, enabling deterministic auditability and reasoning about system behavior. We evaluate OpenKedge across multi-agent conflict scenarios and cloud infrastructure mutations. Results show that the protocol deterministically arbitrates competing intents and cages unsafe execution while maintaining high throughput, establishing a principled foundation for safely operating agentic systems at scale.

AI.tldr: OpenKedge formalizes mutation as a governed process via declarative intents, execution contracts, and an IEEC, enabling verifiable, reconstructable mutations and deterministic arbitration in agentic systems.
AI.motivation: Address safety, determinism, and auditability in API-driven autonomous agents by replacing immediate mutation on API calls with pre-execution governance and verifiable lineage.
AI.method: Actors submit declarative intent proposals evaluated against deterministic system state, temporal signals, and policy constraints. Approved intents are compiled into execution contracts with bounded actions, resources, and time, enforced via ephemeral identities. The Intent-to-Execution Evidence Chain cryptographically links intent, context, policy decisions, execution bounds, and outcomes.
AI.result: Deterministic arbitration of competing intents, containment of unsafe executions, and maintained high throughput across multi-agent conflict scenarios and cloud mutations, establishing a scalable safety foundation.
AI.conclusion: Safety becomes preventative and execution-bound, with a verifiable, reconstructable mutation lineage enabling auditability and reasoning about system behavior at scale.

title: Generative Simulation for Policy Learning in Physical Human-Robot Interaction
summary: Developing autonomous physical human-robot interaction (pHRI) systems is limited by the scarcity of large-scale training data to learn robust robot behaviors for real-world applications. In this paper, we introduce a zero-shot "text2sim2real" generative simulation framework that automatically synthesizes diverse pHRI scenarios from high-level natural-language prompts. Leveraging Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision-Language Models (VLMs), our pipeline procedurally generates soft-body human models, scene layouts, and robot motion trajectories for assistive tasks. We utilize this framework to autonomously collect large-scale synthetic demonstration datasets and then train vision-based imitation learning policies operating on segmented point clouds. We evaluate our approach through a user study on two physically assistive tasks: scratching and bathing. Our learned policies successfully achieve zero-shot sim-to-real transfer, attaining success rates exceeding 80% and demonstrating resilience to unscripted human motion. Overall, we introduce the first generative simulation pipeline for pHRI applications, automating simulation environment synthesis, data collection, and policy learning. Additional information may be found on our project website: https://rchi-lab.github.io/gen_phri/

AI.tldr: Introduces a zero-shot text2sim2real generative pipeline for pHRI that synthesizes diverse scenarios from prompts, trains vision-based imitation policies on synthetic data, and achieves >80% zero-shot sim-to-real transfer on scratching and bathing tasks.
AI.motivation: Scarcity of large-scale real-world pHRI data hinders robust policy learning; a scalable pipeline for generating diverse synthetic data and enabling sim-to-real transfer is highly valuable.
AI.method: A pipeline leverages LLMs and VLMs to procedurally generate soft-body human models, scene layouts, and robot trajectories from natural-language prompts; autonomously collects large synthetic demonstration datasets; trains vision-based imitation policies on segmented point clouds; evaluates with user study on two tasks.
AI.result: Policies achieve zero-shot sim-to-real transfer with success rates exceeding 80% on scratching and bathing; resilient to unscripted human motion; claims to be the first generative pipeline for pHRI automating environment synthesis, data collection, and policy learning.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of automated generative pipelines for pHRI and potential for scalable data-driven policy learning, while noting challenges in realism, generalization, and broader task applicability.

title: A Semi-Automated Framework for 3D Reconstruction of Medieval Manuscript Miniatures
summary: This paper presents a semi-automated framework for transforming two-dimensional miniatures from medieval manuscripts into three-dimensional digital models suitable for extended reality (XR), tactile 3D~printing, and web-based visualization. We evaluate seven image-to-3D methods (TripoSR, SF3D, SPAR3D, TRELLIS, Wonder3D, SAM~3D, Hi3DGen) on 69~manuscript figures from two collections using rendering-based metrics (Silhouette IoU, LPIPS, CLIP~Score) and volumetric measures (Depth Range Ratio, watertight percentage), revealing a trade-off between volumetric expansion and geometric fidelity. Hi3DGen balances topological quality with rich surface detail through its normal bridging approach, making it a good starting point for expert refinement. Our pipeline combines SAM segmentation, Hi3DGen mesh generation, expert refinement in ZBrush, and AI-assisted texturing. Two case studies on Gothic illuminations from the Decretum Gratiani (Vatican Library) and Renaissance miniatures by Giulio Clovio demonstrate applicability across artistic traditions. The resulting models can support WebXR visualization, AR overlay on physical manuscripts, and tactile 3D~prints for visually impaired users.

AI.tldr: A semi-automated 2D-to-3D reconstruction pipeline turns medieval manuscript miniatures into XR-ready, tactile, and web-visualizable models. Seven image-to-3D methods are benchmarked on 69 figures; Hi3DGen offers best topology with rich surface detail, suitable for expert refinement. Validated via Gothic and Renaissance case studies; enables WebXR, AR overlays, and tactile prints with a human-in-the-loop refinement step.
AI.motivation: To digitally preserve and disseminate 2D manuscript illuminations in 3D for extended reality, accessibility, and tactile exploration, addressing fidelity, interoperability, and preservation while enabling cross-tradition applicability.
AI.method: Dataset: 69 manuscript figures from two collections. Methods evaluated: TripoSR, SF3D, SPAR3D, TRELLIS, Wonder3D, SAM~3D, Hi3DGen. Metrics: rendering-based (Silhouette IoU, LPIPS, CLIP Score) and volumetric (Depth Range Ratio, watertight percentage). Pipeline steps: SAM segmentation, Hi3DGen mesh generation, expert refinement in ZBrush, AI-assisted texturing. Case studies: Gothic illuminations from Decretum Gratiani (Vatican Library) and Renaissance miniatures by Giulio Clovio. Outputs support WebXR visualization, AR overlay on physical manuscripts, and tactile 3D prints for visually impaired users.
AI.result: Hi3DGen achieves favorable balance between topology quality and surface detail via a normal-bridging approach; there is a trade-off observed between volumetric expansion and geometric fidelity. The approach generalizes across artistic traditions, as shown by the two case studies; the pipeline yields production-ready models suitable for XR, AR, and tactile applications, with expert refinement enhancing fidelity.
AI.conclusion: The framework is viable for producing high-fidelity, accessible 3D representations of manuscript miniatures. Hi3DGen is recommended as a starting point, given its balance of topology and detail, within a semi-automated, human-in-the-loop workflow (SAM segmentation → Hi3DGen → ZBrush refinement → AI-assisted texturing). Future work could further automate refinement, expand datasets, and refine evaluation metrics to better capture perceptual quality.

title: Memory-Guided Trust-Region Bayesian Optimization (MG-TuRBO) for High Dimensions
summary: Traffic simulation and digital-twin calibration is a challenging optimization problem with a limited simulation budget. Each trial requires an expensive simulation run, and the relationship between calibration inputs and model error is often nonconvex, and noisy. The problem becomes more difficult as the number of calibration parameters increases. We compare a commonly used automatic calibration method, a genetic algorithm (GA), with Bayesian optimization methods (BOMs): classical Bayesian optimization (BO), Trust-Region BO (TuRBO), Multi-TuRBO, and a proposed Memory-Guided TuRBO (MG-TuRBO) method. We compare performance on 2 real-world traffic simulation calibration problems with 14 and 84 decision variables, representing lower- and higher-dimensional (14D and 84D) settings. For BOMs, we study two acquisition strategies, Thompson sampling and a novel adaptive strategy. We evaluate performance using final calibration quality, convergence behavior, and consistency across runs. The results show that BOMs reach good calibration targets much faster than GA in the lower-D problem. MG-TuRBO performs comparably in our 14D setting, it demonstrates noticeable advantages in the 84D problem, particularly when paired with our adaptive strategy. Our results suggest that MG-TuRBO is especially useful for high-D traffic simulation calibration and potentially for high-D problems in general.

AI.tldr: Bayesian optimization-based calibration (including TuRBO variants and a memory-guided version) outperforms a genetic algorithm on traffic simulation calibration with limited budget, especially in high-dimensional (84D) settings; MG-TuRBO shows strong advantages in high-D and with adaptive acquisition strategies.
AI.motivation: Calibrating traffic simulations is expensive and noisy with a finite simulation budget; high-dimensional calibration (14D and 84D) poses optimization challenges. The study seeks to compare GA with several BOM variants, including a proposed memory-guided extension, to determine effective strategies for accurate, reliable calibration.
AI.method: Empirical comparison across two real-world traffic calibration problems with 14 and 84 decision variables. Algorithms compared: Genetic Algorithm (GA), classical Bayesian optimization (BO), Trust-Region BO (TuRBO), Multi-TuRBO, Memory-Guided TuRBO (MG-TuRBO). Two acquisition strategies for BOMs: Thompson sampling and an adaptive strategy. Evaluation metrics: final calibration quality, convergence behavior, consistency across runs.
AI.result: In the 14D problem, BOMs reach good calibration targets much faster than GA. MG-TuRBO performs comparably to other BOMs in 14D. In the 84D problem, MG-TuRBO shows noticeable advantages, especially when paired with the adaptive acquisition strategy. Overall, MG-TuRBO is particularly effective for high-D calibration and may generalize to other high-D optimization tasks.
AI.conclusion: Bayesian optimization frameworks, particularly the memory-guided TuRBO variant with adaptive acquisitions, offer significant gains for high-dimensional traffic simulation calibration under limited budgets, outperforming GA and providing robust high-D performance where traditional BOMs may struggle.

title: From Business Events to Auditable Decisions: Ontology-Governed Graph Simulation for Enterprise AI
summary: Existing LLM-based agent systems share a common architectural failure: they answer from the unrestricted knowledge space without first simulating how active business scenarios reshape that space for the event at hand -- producing decisions that are fluent but ungrounded and carrying no audit trail. We present LOM-action, which equips enterprise AI with \emph{event-driven ontology simulation}: business events trigger scenario conditions encoded in the enterprise ontology~(EO), which drive deterministic graph mutations in an isolated sandbox, evolving a working copy of the subgraph into the scenario-valid simulation graph $G_{\text{sim}}$; all decisions are derived exclusively from this evolved graph. The core pipeline is \emph{event $\to$ simulation $\to$ decision}, realized through a dual-mode architecture -- \emph{skill mode} and \emph{reasoning mode}. Every decision produces a fully traceable audit log. LOM-action achieves 93.82% accuracy and 98.74% tool-chain F1 against frontier baselines Doubao-1.8 and DeepSeek-V3.2, which reach only 24--36% F1 despite 80% accuracy -- exposing the \emph{illusive accuracy} phenomenon. The four-fold F1 advantage confirms that ontology-governed, event-driven simulation, not model scale, is the architectural prerequisite for trustworthy enterprise decision intelligence.

AI.tldr: LOM-action introduces event-driven ontology simulation to ground enterprise AI decisions in a sandboxed, evolving graph, delivering fully auditable decisions and strong performance gains over frontier baselines.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based agents often answer from unrestricted knowledge without simulating how active business scenarios reshape relevant knowledge, leading to fluent but ungrounded decisions and lacking auditability. This work argues for ontology-guided simulation to ensure trustworthy enterprise decision intelligence.
AI.method: An event-to-simulation-to-decision pipeline: business events trigger scenario conditions encoded in the enterprise ontology, which deterministically mutate a sandboxed subgraph to produce a scenario-valid graph G_sim. Decisions are derived exclusively from G_sim; a dual-mode architecture (skill mode and reasoning mode) provides traceable audit logs for every decision.
AI.result: The approach achieves 93.82% accuracy and 98.74% tool-chain F1 against frontier baselines Doubao-1.8 and DeepSeek-V3.2, which reach 24–36% F1 despite 80% accuracy. This four-fold F1 advantage supports the claim that ontology-driven simulation, not model scale, is key for trustworthy enterprise decision intelligence.
AI.conclusion: Ontology-governed, event-driven simulation is the architectural prerequisite for trustworthy enterprise decision intelligence, providing grounded decisions and full auditability beyond what larger models alone can achieve.

title: Task-Aware Bimanual Affordance Prediction via VLM-Guided Semantic-Geometric Reasoning
summary: Bimanual manipulation requires reasoning about where to interact with an object and which arm should perform each action, a joint affordance localization and arm allocation problem that geometry-only planners cannot resolve without semantic understanding of task intent. Existing approaches either treat affordance prediction as coarse part segmentation or rely on geometric heuristics for arm assignment, failing to jointly reason about task-relevant contact regions and arm allocation. We reframe bimanual manipulation as a joint affordance localization and arm allocation problem and propose a hierarchical framework for task-aware bimanual affordance prediction that leverages a Vision-Language Model (VLM) to generalize across object categories and task descriptions without requiring category-specific training. Our approach fuses multi-view RGB-D observations into a consistent 3D scene representation and generates global 6-DoF grasp candidates, which are then spatially and semantically filtered by querying the VLM for task-relevant affordance regions on each object, as well as for arm allocation to the individual objects, thereby ensuring geometric validity while respecting task semantics. We evaluate our method on a dual-arm platform across nine real-world manipulation tasks spanning four categories: parallel manipulation, coordinated stabilization, tool use, and human handover. Our approach achieves consistently higher task success rates than geometric and semantic baselines for task-oriented grasping, demonstrating that explicit semantic reasoning over affordances and arm allocation helps enable reliable bimanual manipulation in unstructured environments.

AI.tldr: Joint affordance localization and arm allocation for bimanual manipulation using a Vision-Language Model, enabling task-aware grasping and arm assignment with 3D scene fusion; demonstrates improved task success across nine real-world tasks.
AI.motivation: Geometry-only planners cannot resolve where to contact and which arm to use without understanding task intent; current methods either coarse segmentation or heuristics fail to jointly reason about task-relevant contact regions and arm allocation; need generalizable, task-aware reasoning across object categories and descriptions.
AI.method: Fuse multi-view RGB-D into a consistent 3D scene; generate global 6-DoF grasp candidates; query a Vision-Language Model to identify task-relevant affordance regions on each object and allocate arms to objects; filter grasps and allocations semantically while ensuring geometric validity; implement a hierarchical framework for task-aware affordance prediction.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on a dual-arm platform across nine real-world manipulation tasks (parallel manipulation, coordinated stabilization, tool use, human handover) shows consistently higher task success rates than geometric and semantic baselines for task-oriented grasping.
AI.conclusion: Explicit semantic reasoning over affordances and arm allocation improves reliability of bimanual manipulation in unstructured environments and enables generalization across object categories and task descriptions without category-specific training.

title: ViSAGE @ NTIRE 2026 Challenge on Video Saliency Prediction
summary: In this report, we present our champion solution for the NTIRE 2026 Challenge on Video Saliency Prediction held in conjunction with CVPR 2026. To exploit complementary inductive biases for video saliency, we propose Video Saliency with Adaptive Gated Experts (ViSAGE), a multi-expert ensemble framework. Each specialized decoder performs adaptive gating and modulation to refine spatio-temporal features. The complementary predictions from different experts are then fused at inference. ViSAGE thereby aggregates diverse inductive biases to capture complex spatio-temporal saliency cues in videos. On the Private Test set, ViSAGE ranked first on two out of four evaluation metrics, and outperformed most competing solutions on the other two metrics, demonstrating its effectiveness and generalization ability. Our code has been released at https://github.com/iLearn-Lab/CVPRW26-ViSAGE.

AI.tldr: A multi-expert adaptive gated ensemble (ViSAGE) for video saliency prediction that achieves state-of-the-art results on NTIRE 2026 private test, via specialized decoders with adaptive gating and fusion.
AI.motivation: To exploit complementary inductive biases in video saliency and improve generalization beyond single-model approaches by using an ensemble with adaptive gating.
AI.method: ViSAGE employs multiple expert decoders, each performing adaptive gating and feature modulation to refine spatio-temporal cues; predictions from experts are fused at inference to produce the final saliency map.
AI.result: On the Private Test set, ViSAGE ranked first on 2 of 4 metrics and surpassed most competitors on the remaining metrics, evidencing strong effectiveness and generalization.
AI.conclusion: ViSAGE demonstrates the benefits of diverse inductive biases and adaptive gating in video saliency, with released code enabling reproducibility.

title: QuanBench+: A Unified Multi-Framework Benchmark for LLM-Based Quantum Code Generation
comment: 24 pages total, 25 figures, 5 tables, including supplementary material. Accepted to the ICLR 2026 Workshop on I Can't Believe It's Not Better
summary:
Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used for code generation, yet quantum code generation is still evaluated mostly within single frameworks, making it difficult to separate quantum reasoning from framework familiarity. We introduce QuanBench+, a unified benchmark spanning Qiskit, PennyLane, and Cirq, with 42 aligned tasks covering quantum algorithms, gate decomposition, and state preparation.
  We evaluate models with executable functional tests, report Pass@1 and Pass@5, and use KL-divergence-based acceptance for probabilistic outputs. We additionally study Pass@1 after feedback-based repair, where a model may revise code after a runtime error or wrong answer. Across frameworks, the strongest one-shot scores reach 59.5% in Qiskit, 54.8% in Cirq, and 42.9% in PennyLane; with feedback-based repair, the best scores rise to 83.3%, 76.2%, and 66.7%, respectively. These results show clear progress, but also that reliable multi-framework quantum code generation remains unsolved and still depends strongly on framework-specific knowledge.

AI.tldr: QuanBench+ provides a unified cross-framework benchmark for quantum code generation across Qiskit, PennyLane, and Cirq with 42 aligned tasks. One-shot results show moderate performance, while incorporating feedback-based repair yields substantial gains, but reliable multi-framework quantum code generation remains challenging and framework-specific knowledge dominates.
AI.motivation: To disentangle quantum reasoning from framework familiarity in LLM-driven quantum code generation and enable fair cross-framework evaluation beyond single-framework benchmarks.
AI.method: A unified benchmark spanning Qiskit, PennyLane, and Cirq with 42 aligned tasks covering quantum algorithms, gate decomposition, and state preparation. Evaluation uses executable functional tests with Pass@1, Pass@5, and KL-divergence-based acceptance for probabilistic outputs. A parallel study assesses Pass@1 after feedback-based repair, where models revise code after runtime errors or incorrect results.
AI.result: One-shot strongest scores: Qiskit 59.5%, Cirq 54.8%, PennyLane 42.9%. After feedback-based repair: Qiskit 83.3%, Cirq 76.2%, PennyLane 66.7%. These results indicate progress but highlight remaining challenges in achieving reliable cross-framework quantum code generation, with performance heavily influenced by framework-specific knowledge.
AI.conclusion: While QuanBench+ demonstrates clear progress in cross-framework quantum code generation, it remains unsolved to achieve robust, framework-agnostic performance, underscoring the need for deeper integration and standardization across quantum SDKs.

title: Sustained Impact of Agentic Personalisation in Marketing: A Longitudinal Case Study
comment: To appear in the 34th ACM International Conference on User Modeling, Adaptation and Personalization (UMAP '26) Industry Track
summary:
In consumer applications, Customer Relationship Management (CRM) has traditionally relied on the manual optimisation of static, rule-based messaging strategies. While adaptive and autonomous learning systems offer the promise of scalable personalisation, it remains unclear to what extent ``human-in-the-loop'' oversight is required to sustain performance uplift over time. This paper presents a longitudinal case study analysing a real-world consumer application that leverages agentic infrastructure to personalise marketing messaging for a large-scale user base over an 11-month period.
  We compare two distinct periods: an active phase where marketers directly curated content, audiences, and strategies -- followed immediately by a passive phase where agents operated autonomously from a fixed library of components. Our results demonstrate that whilst active human management generates the highest relative lift in engagement metrics, the autonomous agents successfully sustained a positive lift during the passive period. These findings suggest a symbiotic model where human intervention drives strategic initialisation and discovery, yet autonomous agents can ensure the scalable retention and preservation of performance gains.

AI.tldr: Longitudinal case study finds human-curated campaigns provide the largest uplift, but autonomous agents sustain positive uplift in a fixed-component library, supporting a hybrid human-in-the-loop approach for scalable CRM messaging.
AI.motivation: Assess how much human supervision is required to sustain performance uplift in personalized marketing, comparing manual rule-based, human-curated, and autonomous agentic messaging in a real-world, large-scale CRM setting over 11 months.
AI.method: Two-period comparison within a real-world deployment: active phase with marketers designing content/audiences/strategies vs passive phase with agents operating autonomously from a fixed component library; analyze engagement metrics over 11 months.
AI.result: Active human management yields the highest relative lift in engagement metrics; autonomous agents achieve and maintain a positive lift during the passive period, demonstrating sustainability and scalability of gains.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid model recommended: use human intervention for initialization and discovery, then leverage autonomous agents for scalable retention and preservation of improvements.

title: Toward Hardware-Agnostic Quadrupedal World Models via Morphology Conditioning
summary: World models promise a paradigm shift in robotics, where an agent learns the underlying physics of its environment once to enable efficient planning and behavior learning. However, current world models are often hardware-locked specialists: a model trained on a Boston Dynamics Spot robot fails catastrophically on a Unitree Go1 due to the mismatch in kinematic and dynamic properties, as the model overfits to specific embodiment constraints rather than capturing the universal locomotion dynamics. Consequently, a slight change in actuator dynamics or limb length necessitates training a new model from scratch. In this work, we take a step towards a framework for training a generalizable Quadrupedal World Model (QWM) that disentangles environmental dynamics from robot morphology. We address the limitations of implicit system identification, where treating static physical properties (like mass or limb length) as latent variables to be inferred from motion history creates an adaptation lag that can compromise zero-shot safety and efficiency. Instead, we explicitly condition the generative dynamics on the robot's engineering specifications. By integrating a physical morphology encoder and a reward normalizer, we enable the model to serve as a neural simulator capable of generalizing across morphologies. This capability unlocks zero-shot control across a range of embodiments. We introduce, for the first time, a world model that enables zero-shot generalization to new morphologies for locomotion. While we carefully study the limitations of our method, QWM operates as a distribution-bounded interpolator within the quadrupedal morphology family rather than a universal physics engine, this work represents a significant step toward morphology-conditioned world models for legged locomotion.

AI.tldr: A morphology-conditioned quadrupedal world model (QWM) generalizes across robot morphologies, enabling zero-shot locomotion in a distribution-bounded manifold rather than a universal physics engine.
AI.motivation: Hardware-specific world models fail to generalize when morphology changes; there is a need to separate environmental dynamics from robot morphology to enable zero-shot planning across embodiments.
AI.method: Introduce a physical morphology encoder and reward normalizer; explicitly condition the generative dynamics on engineering specifications instead of inferring static properties; train a neural simulator that can interpolate within the quadrupedal morphology family; treat the model as a morphology-conditioned, distribution-bounded world model.
AI.result: First demonstration of zero-shot generalization to new morphologies for locomotion; shows QWM can handle morphology variations within the quadrupedal family, but is not a universal physics engine; analyzes limitations and scopes as interpolation within the morphology distribution.
AI.conclusion: QWM represents a significant step toward morphology-conditioned world models for legged locomotion and motivates further work on generalizable neural simulators across embodiment variations, while acknowledging bounds of interpolation and the need for broader generalization.

title: MARINER: A 3E-Driven Benchmark for Fine-Grained Perception and Complex Reasoning in Open-Water Environments
summary: Fine-grained visual understanding and high-level reasoning in real-world open-water environments remain under-explored due to the lack of dedicated benchmarks. We introduce MARINER, a comprehensive benchmark built under the novel Entity-Environment-Event (3E) paradigm. MARINER contains 16,629 multi-source maritime images with 63 fine-grained vessel categories, diverse adverse environments, and 5 typical dynamic maritime incidents, covering fine-grained classification, object detection, and visual question answering tasks. We conduct extensive evaluations on mainstream Multimodal Large language models (MLLMs) and establish baselines, revealing that even advanced models struggle with fine-grained discrimination and causal reasoning in complex marine scenes. As a dedicated maritime benchmark, MARINER fills the gap of realistic and cognitive-level evaluation for maritime multimodal understanding, and promotes future research on robust vision-language models for open-water applications. Appendix and supplementary materials are available at https://lxixim.github.io/MARINER.

AI.tldr: MARINER is a new maritime multimodal benchmark under the 3E (Entity-Environment-Event) paradigm, with 16,629 multi-source images across 63 fine-grained vessel categories, adverse conditions, and 5 maritime incidents, enabling fine-grained classification, object detection, and VQA; evaluations show current Multimodal LLMs struggle with fine-grained discrimination and causal reasoning in open-water scenes.
AI.motivation: Open-water understanding is under-explored due to the lack of dedicated, cognitively challenging benchmarks. The 3E paradigm targets fine-grained entities, diverse environments, and dynamic events to push maritime multimodal understanding.
AI.method: Construct a large-scale, multi-source maritime image dataset (MARINER) with 16,629 images, 63 vessel categories, 5 dynamic incidents; annotate for fine-grained classification, object detection, and VQA; evaluate mainstream Multimodal LLMs and establish baselines.
AI.result: Advanced MLLMs underperform on fine-grained discrimination and causal reasoning in complex marine scenes; MARINER provides a realistic and cognition-focused benchmark to drive future maritime vision-language research; supplementary materials available online.
AI.conclusion: MARINER fills a critical gap in maritime multimodal evaluation, enabling robust vision-language models for open-water applications and catalyzing future research in marine cognitive understanding.

title: Robust Reasoning Benchmark
summary: While Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve high performance on standard mathematical benchmarks, their underlying reasoning processes remain highly overfit to standard textual formatting. We propose a perturbation pipeline consisting of 14 techniques to evaluate robustness of LLM reasoning. We apply this pipeline to AIME 2024 dataset and evalute 8 state-of-the-art models on the resulting benchmark. While frontier models exhibit resilience, open weights reasoning models suffer catastrophic collapses (up to 55% average accuracy drops across perturbations and up to 100% on some), exposing structural fragility. To further disentangle mechanical parsing failures from downstream reasoning failures, we strictly isolate the models' working memory capacity by forcing models to solve multiple unperturbed mathematical problems sequentially within a single context window. Our results indicate that open weight models ranging from 7B to 120B parameters and Claude Opus 4.6 exhibit accuracy decay on subsequent problems. This degradation demonstrates that intermediate reasoning steps permanently pollute standard dense attention mechanisms. We argue that to achieve reliable reasoning, future reasoning architectures must integrate explicit contextual resets within a model's own Chain-of-Thought, leading to fundamental open questions regarding the optimal granularity of atomic reasoning tasks.

AI.tldr: A perturbation-based robustness benchmark reveals fragility in open-weight LLMs' mathematical reasoning; frontier models resist perturbations but internal chain-of-thought mechanisms under memory stress still degrade; suggests contextual resets are needed.
AI.motivation: Explain why high performance on standard math benchmarks does not guarantee robust, generalizable reasoning; quantify how parsing, memory, and downstream reasoning fail under perturbations across models and datasets (AIME 2024).
AI.method: A 14-technique perturbation pipeline to evaluate robustness of LLM reasoning. Apply to the AIME 2024 dataset; evaluate 8 state-of-the-art models; isolate working memory by forcing sequential solving of multiple unperturbed problems within a single context window; compare accuracy under perturbations and during memory pressure between frontier and open-weight models.
AI.result: Frontier (emerging) models show resilience to perturbations; open-weight models (7B–120B) and Claude Opus 4.6 exhibit significant accuracy decay on subsequent problems (up to 55% average drops across perturbations; up to 100% on some). Perturbations reveal structural fragility and that intermediate reasoning steps pollute dense attention; memory constraints cause degradation.
AI.conclusion: Reliable reasoning will require architectural changes such as explicit contextual resets within a model's chain-of-thought; raises questions about the appropriate granularity of atomic reasoning tasks and how to separate parsing failures from true reasoning failures.

title: RAMP: Hybrid DRL for Online Learning of Numeric Action Models
comment: Accepted as a workshop paper at the Adaptive and Learning Agents (ALA) Workshop at AAMAS 2026
summary: Automated planning algorithms require an action model specifying the preconditions and effects of each action, but obtaining such a model is often hard. Learning action models from observations is feasible, but existing algorithms for numeric domains are offline, requiring expert traces as input. We propose the Reinforcement learning, Action Model learning, and Planning (RAMP) strategy for learning numeric planning action models online via interactions with the environment. RAMP simultaneously trains a Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) policy, learns a numeric action model from past interactions, and uses that model to plan future actions when possible. These components form a positive feedback loop: the RL policy gathers data to refine the action model, while the planner generates plans to continue training the RL policy. To facilitate this integration of RL and numeric planning, we developed Numeric PDDLGym, an automated framework for converting numeric planning problems to Gym environments. Experimental results on standard IPC numeric domains show that RAMP significantly outperforms PPO, a well-known DRL algorithm, in terms of solvability and plan quality.

AI.tldr: RAMP combines online action-model learning, DRL planning, and planning in an interactive loop to learn numeric action models online, using Numeric PDDLGym; shows RAMP outperforms PPO in IPC numeric domains.
AI.motivation: Learning numeric action models online is difficult and offline expert traces are often unavailable; integrating reinforcement learning with action-model learning and planning can improve solvability and plan quality.
AI.method: RAMP concurrently trains a DRL policy, learns a numeric action model from past interactions, and uses that model to plan future actions; a positive feedback loop where data collection, model refinement, and planning reinforce each other; Numeric PDDLGym framework converts numeric planning problems to Gym environments; experiments compare against PPO in standard IPC numeric domains.
AI.result: RAMP significantly outperforms PPO in solvability and plan quality on IPC numeric domains.
AI.conclusion: Integrating online action-model learning with DRL-based planning through a feedback loop, facilitated by the Numeric PDDLGym framework, yields improved solvability and plan quality in numeric domains.

title: One Interface, Many Robots: Unified Real-Time Low-Level Motion Planning for Collaborative Arms
summary: This paper proposes a common interface for real-time low-level motion planning of collaborative robotic arms, aimed at enabling broader applicability and improved portability across heterogeneous hardware platforms. In previous work, we introduced WinGs Operating Studio (WOS), a middleware solution that abstracts diverse robotic components into uniform software resources and provides a broad suite of language-agnostic APIs. This paper specifically focuses on its minimal yet flexible interface for real-time end-effector trajectory control. By employing an n-degree polynomial interpolator in conjunction with a quadratic programming solver, the proposed method generates smooth, continuously differentiable trajectories with precise position, velocity, and acceleration profiles. We validate our approach in three distinct scenarios. First, in an offline demonstration, a collaborative arm accurately draws various geometric shapes on paper. Second, in an interruptible, low-frequency re-planning setting, a robotic manipulator grasps a dynamic object placed on a moving mobile robot. Finally, we conducted a teleoperation experiment in which one robotic arm controlled another to perform a series of dexterous manipulations, confirming the proposed method's reliability, versatility, and ease of use.

AI.tldr: Proposes a minimal, portable real-time interface for end-effector trajectory control across heterogeneous collaborative arms, using an n-degree polynomial interpolator and a quadratic-programming solver, validated in offline drawing, interruptible re-planning with a dynamic grasping scenario, and teleoperation between arms.
AI.motivation: Need for a portable, real-time low-level motion-planning interface that abstracts hardware differences and improves reuse across diverse robotic platforms.
AI.method: Introduce a minimal, flexible interface atop WinGs Operating Studio (WOS) that abstracts robotic components into uniform resources; employ an n-degree polynomial interpolator with a quadratic programming (QP) solver to generate smooth trajectories with defined position, velocity, and acceleration profiles; validate via offline drawing, interruptible low-frequency re-planning for dynamic grasping, and teleoperation experiments.
AI.result: Demonstrated three scenarios: (1) offline shape drawing with a collaborative arm, (2) interruptible re-planning where a manipulator grasps a moving object on a mobile robot, (3) teleoperation with one arm controlling another for dexterous tasks; overall showing reliability, versatility, and ease of use of the proposed interface and control pipeline.
AI.conclusion: A minimal, portable real-time end-effector control interface backed by polynomial interpolation and QP optimization can enable broad applicability of collaborative arms across heterogeneous hardware, enabling reliable, versatile, and user-friendly operation.

title: WildDet3D: Scaling Promptable 3D Detection in the Wild
summary: Understanding objects in 3D from a single image is a cornerstone of spatial intelligence. A key step toward this goal is monocular 3D object detection--recovering the extent, location, and orientation of objects from an input RGB image. To be practical in the open world, such a detector must generalize beyond closed-set categories, support diverse prompt modalities, and leverage geometric cues when available. Progress is hampered by two bottlenecks: existing methods are designed for a single prompt type and lack a mechanism to incorporate additional geometric cues, and current 3D datasets cover only narrow categories in controlled environments, limiting open-world transfer. In this work we address both gaps. First, we introduce WildDet3D, a unified geometry-aware architecture that natively accepts text, point, and box prompts and can incorporate auxiliary depth signals at inference time. Second, we present WildDet3D-Data, the largest open 3D detection dataset to date, constructed by generating candidate 3D boxes from existing 2D annotations and retaining only human-verified ones, yielding over 1M images across 13.5K categories in diverse real-world scenes. WildDet3D establishes a new state-of-the-art across multiple benchmarks and settings. In the open-world setting, it achieves 22.6/24.8 AP3D on our newly introduced WildDet3D-Bench with text and box prompts. On Omni3D, it reaches 34.2/36.4 AP3D with text and box prompts, respectively. In zero-shot evaluation, it achieves 40.3/48.9 ODS on Argoverse 2 and ScanNet. Notably, incorporating depth cues at inference time yields substantial additional gains (+20.7 AP on average across settings).

AI.tldr: WildDet3D introduces a geometry-aware monocular 3D detector that accepts text, point, and box prompts and can fuse depth cues at inference; paired with WildDet3D-Data, a large open 3D dataset, it achieves SOTA on open-world benchmarks and strong zero-shot performance; depth cues provide substantial gains.
AI.motivation: Overcome two key bottlenecks: (1) detectors designed for a single prompt modality and lacking geometric cue integration, and (2) limited open-world data covering diverse categories for generalization.
AI.method: A unified architecture that natively supports text, point, and box prompts and can ingest auxiliary depth signals at inference; data strategy to build WildDet3D-Data by generating candidate 3D boxes from 2D annotations and retaining only human-verified instances; evaluated on WildDet3D-Bench, Omni3D, Argoverse 2, and ScanNet.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance across benchmarks: 22.6/24.8 AP3D on WildDet3D-Bench (text/box prompts); 34.2/36.4 AP3D on Omni3D (text/box); zero-shot ODS of 40.3/48.9 on Argoverse 2 and ScanNet; depth cues at inference yield +20.7 AP on average.
AI.conclusion: WildDet3D demonstrates the viability of combining multiple prompt modalities with geometry-aware inference and depth cues for robust open-world monocular 3D detection, supported by a large-scale open dataset enabling broad generalization.

title: Ranked Activation Shift for Post-Hoc Out-of-Distribution Detection
summary: State-of-the-art post-hoc out-of-distribution detection methods rely on intermediate layer activation editing. However, they exhibit inconsistent performance across datasets and models. We show that this instability is driven by differences in the activation distributions, and identify a failure mode of scaling-based methods that arises when penultimate layer activations are not rectified. Motivated by this analysis, we propose \ours, a hyperparameter-free post-hoc method that replaces sorted activation magnitudes with a fixed in-distribution reference profile. Our simple plug-and-play method shows strong and consistent performance across datasets and architectures without assumptions on the penultimate layer activation function, and without requiring any hyperparameter tuning, while preserving in-distribution classification accuracy by construction. We further analyze what drives the improvement, showing that both inhibiting and exciting activation shifts independently contribute to better out-of-distribution discrimination.

AI.tldr: A hyperparameter-free post-hoc OOD detector that replaces activation-magnitude sorting with a fixed in-distribution reference, yielding robust, architecture- and dataset-agnostic OOD discrimination while preserving ID accuracy.
AI.motivation: State-of-the-art post-hoc OOD methods rely on intermediate-layer activation editing and show unstable performance across datasets/models. Scaling-based methods fail when penultimate activations are not rectified. A robust, hyperparameter-free solution is needed.
AI.method: Propose ours, a plug-and-play post-hoc method that replaces sorted activation magnitudes with a fixed in-distribution reference profile, eliminating hyperparameters and not relying on a specific penultimate activation function.
AI.result: Demonstrates strong, consistent OOD detection across datasets and architectures; maintains in-distribution accuracy by construction; analysis indicates both inhibiting and exciting activation shifts independently improve OOD discrimination.
AI.conclusion: The method provides robust, hyperparameter-free OOD detection across varied conditions and illuminates the complementary roles of activation-shift directions in improving OOD discrimination.

title: Parameterized Complexity Of Representing Models Of MSO Formulas
summary: Monadic second order logic (MSO2) plays an important role in parameterized complexity due to the Courcelle's theorem. This theorem states that the problem of checking if a given graph has a property specified by a given MSO2 formula can be solved by a parameterized linear time algorithm with respect to the treewidth of the graph and the size of the formula. We extend this result by showing that models of MSO2 formula with free variables can be represented with a decision diagram whose size is parameterized linear in the above mentioned parameter. In particular, we show a parameterized linear upper bound on the size of a sentential decision diagram (SDD) when treewidth is considered and a parameterized linear upper bound on the size of an ordered binary decision diagram (OBDD) when considering the pathwidth in the parameter. In addition, building on a lower bound on the size of OBDD by Razgon (2014), we show that there is an MSO2 formula and a class of graphs with bounded treewidth which do not admit an OBDD with the size parameterized by the treewidth. Our result offers a new perspective on the Courcelle's theorem and connects it to the area of knowledge representation.

AI.tldr: MSO2 models with free variables can be encoded efficiently as decision diagrams under treewidth/pathwidth parameters, giving linear-size bounds for SDD/OBDD; there are limits for OBDDs despite bounded treewidth; links logic, parameterized complexity, and knowledge representation.
AI.motivation: Extend Courcelle's theorem by representing MSO2 formulas with free variables using decision diagrams, and relate parameterized structural graph parameters to compact representations in knowledge representation.
AI.method: Prove parameterized linear upper bounds on the size of sentential decision diagrams (SDD) with treewidth as parameter, and on ordered binary decision diagrams (OBDD) with pathwidth as parameter; leverage Razgon (2014) lower bounds to show non-existence results for OBDDs parameterized by treewidth; construct classes of MSO2 formulas and graphs with bounded treewidth where efficient OBDD representations are impossible.
AI.result: Establish parameterized linear upper bounds on the size of SDDs (treewidth parameter) and OBDDs (pathwidth parameter); show a lower bound/limitation for OBDDs under treewidth parameterization on some bounded-treewidth graph classes; frame Courcelle's theorem in terms of decision diagrams and knowledge representation.
AI.conclusion: Provides a new perspective on Courcelle's theorem by connecting MSO2 model checking to compact decision-diagram representations; demonstrates both potential gains (efficient representations under treewidth/pathwidth) and limits (OBDDs cannot always be size-efficient under treewidth), highlighting relevance to knowledge representation.

title: Simulation of Adaptive Running with Flexible Sports Prosthesis using Reinforcement Learning of Hybrid-link System
summary: This study proposes a reinforcement learning-based adaptive running motion simulation for a unilateral transtibial amputee with the flexibility of a leaf-spring-type sports prosthesis using hybrid-link system. The design and selection of sports prostheses often rely on trial and error. A comprehensive whole-body dynamics analysis that considers the interaction between human motion and prosthetic deformation could provide valuable insights for user-specific design and selection. The hybrid-link system facilitates whole-body dynamics analysis by incorporating the Piece-wise Constant Strain model to represent the flexible deformation of the prosthesis. Based on this system, the simulation methodology generates whole-body dynamic motions of a unilateral transtibial amputee through a reinforcement learning-based approach, which combines imitation learning from motion capture data with accurate prosthetic dynamics computation. We simulated running motions under different virtual prosthetic stiffness conditions and analyzed the metabolic cost of transport obtained from the simulations, suggesting that variations in stiffness influence running performance. Our findings demonstrate the potential of this approach for simulation and analysis under virtual conditions that differ from real conditions.

AI.tldr: RL-based adaptive running motion simulation for a unilateral transtibial amputee using a leaf-spring-type prosthesis within a hybrid-link system, showing stiffness variations affect metabolic cost of transport.
AI.motivation: Design and selection of sports prostheses is largely trial-and-error; a comprehensive whole-body dynamics analysis including prosthetic deformation can inform user-specific design and selection.
AI.method: Develop a hybrid-link system incorporating the Piece-wise Constant Strain model to capture flexible prosthetic deformation. Use a reinforcement learning-based workflow that blends imitation learning from motion capture data with accurate prosthetic dynamics to generate whole-body dynamic running motions under different virtual prosthetic stiffness settings.
AI.result: Simulations indicate that changes in prosthetic stiffness influence running performance, as measured by metabolic cost of transport, demonstrating the method's potential to analyze and compare virtual prosthetic configurations.
AI.conclusion: The approach shows promise for conducting simulation and analysis under virtual conditions that diverge from real-world conditions, with potential applications in design optimization and prosthesis selection for unilateral transtibial amputees.

title: On Semiotic-Grounded Interpretive Evaluation of Generative Art
summary: Interpretation is essential to deciphering the language of art: audiences communicate with artists by recovering meaning from visual artifacts. However, current Generative Art (GenArt) evaluators remain fixated on surface-level image quality or literal prompt adherence, failing to assess the deeper symbolic or abstract meaning intended by the creator. We address this gap by formalizing a Peircean computational semiotic theory that models Human-GenArt Interaction (HGI) as cascaded semiosis. This framework reveals that artistic meaning is conveyed through three modes - iconic, symbolic, and indexical - yet existing evaluators operate heavily within the iconic mode, remaining structurally blind to the latter two. To overcome this structural blindness, we propose SemJudge. This evaluator explicitly assesses symbolic and indexical meaning in HGI via a Hierarchical Semiosis Graph (HSG) that reconstructs the meaning-making process from prompt to generated artifact. Extensive quantitative experiments show that SemJudge aligns more closely with human judgments than prior evaluators on an interpretation-intensive fine-art benchmark. User studies further demonstrate that SemJudge produces deeper, more insightful artistic interpretations, thereby paving the way for GenArt to move beyond the generation of "pretty" images toward a medium capable of expressing complex human experience. Project page: https://github.com/songrise/SemJudge.

AI.tldr: SemJudge is a Semiosis-based evaluator for Generative Art that assesses symbolic and indexical meaning using a Hierarchical Semiosis Graph, aligning with human interpretation more closely than prior, surface-focused evaluators.
AI.motivation: Current GenArt evaluators emphasize image quality and prompt fidelity, neglecting deeper Peircean meanings; a computational framework is needed to capture iconic, symbolic, and indexical meaning in human–art interaction.
AI.method: Formalizes Peircean semiotics as cascaded semiosis; identifies three meaning modes (iconic, symbolic, indexical); introduces Hierarchical Semiosis Graph (HSG) and SemJudge to reconstruct and evaluate meaning from prompt to artifact.
AI.result: Quantitative experiments show SemJudge correlates better with human judgments on interpretation-intensive tasks; user studies report deeper, more insightful interpretations than baselines.
AI.conclusion: Offers a path for GenArt to express and evaluate complex human experiences beyond aesthetic quality; provides a principled framework for meaning-centric evaluation.

title: Silhouette Loss: Differentiable Global Structure Learning for Deep Representations
summary: Learning discriminative representations is a central goal of supervised deep learning. While cross-entropy (CE) remains the dominant objective for classification, it does not explicitly enforce desirable geometric properties in the embedding space, such as intra-class compactness and inter-class separation. Existing metric learning approaches, including supervised contrastive learning (SupCon) and proxy-based methods, address this limitation by operating on pairwise or proxy-based relationships, but often increase computational cost and complexity. In this work, we introduce Soft Silhouette Loss, a novel differentiable objective inspired by the classical silhouette coefficient from clustering analysis. Unlike pairwise objectives, our formulation evaluates each sample against all classes in the batch, providing a batch-level notion of global structure. The proposed loss directly encourages samples to be closer to their own class than to competing classes, while remaining lightweight. Soft Silhouette Loss can be seamlessly combined with cross-entropy, and is also complementary to supervised contrastive learning. We propose a hybrid objective that integrates them, jointly optimizing local pairwise consistency and global cluster structure. Extensive experiments on seven diverse datasets demonstrate that: (i) augmenting CE with Soft Silhouette Loss consistently improves over CE and other metric learning baselines; (ii) the hybrid formulation outperforms SupCon alone; and (iii) the combined method achieves the best performance, improving average top-1 accuracy from 36.71% (CE) and 37.85% (SupCon2) to 39.08%, while incurring substantially lower computational overhead. These results suggest that classical clustering principles can be reinterpreted as differentiable objectives for deep learning, enabling efficient optimization of both local and global structure in representation spaces.

AI.tldr: Soft Silhouette Loss is a differentiable, batch-level objective inspired by the silhouette coefficient. It evaluates each sample against all classes in the batch to promote intra-class compactness and inter-class separation, and can be combined with cross-entropy and with supervised contrastive learning in a hybrid objective. It yields consistent accuracy gains with lower computational overhead compared to typical metric-learning approaches.
AI.motivation: Cross-entropy does not enforce favorable geometry in embedding spaces. Existing metric-learning methods (e.g., SupCon, proxy-based methods) add complexity and cost. A lightweight, global-structure objective is proposed to enforce intra-class tightness and inter-class separation at the batch level.
AI.method: Introduce Soft Silhouette Loss that compares a sample to all classes present in the batch, producing a differentiable silhouette-like score. This batch-level objective is compatible with cross-entropy and can complement SupCon. A hybrid objective combining local pairwise consistency (SupCon) with global batch-structure (Soft Silhouette) is proposed.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on seven datasets shows consistent improvements over CE and SupCon baselines. The combined method achieves the best top-1 accuracy (e.g., from 36.71% with CE and 37.85% with SupCon2 to 39.08%), with substantially lower computational overhead than typical metric-learning approaches.
AI.conclusion: Classical clustering principles can be recast as differentiable objectives for deep learning, enabling efficient optimization of both local and global structures in representation spaces. The Soft Silhouette Loss provides a lightweight, batch-aware mechanism that complements existing objectives (CE and SupCon).

title: Model Space Reasoning as Search in Feedback Space for Planning Domain Generation
comment: Accepted at ICLR 2026 the 2nd Workshop on World Models: Understanding, Modelling and Scaling
summary: The generation of planning domains from natural language descriptions remains an open problem even with the advent of large language models and reasoning models. Recent work suggests that while LLMs have the ability to assist with domain generation, they are still far from producing high quality domains that can be deployed in practice. To this end, we investigate the ability of an agentic language model feedback framework to generate planning domains from natural language descriptions that have been augmented with a minimal amount of symbolic information. In particular, we evaluate the quality of the generated domains under various forms of symbolic feedback, including landmarks, and output from the VAL plan validator. Using these feedback mechanisms, we experiment using heuristic search over model space to optimize domain quality.

AI.tldr: Agentic LLM feedback with symbolic augmentation improves planning-domain generation; uses landmarks and VAL plan validator feedback and heuristic search to optimize quality.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of generating deployable planning domains from natural language descriptions by augmenting NL inputs with symbolic cues and leveraging feedback to guide generation.
AI.method: Use an agentic LLM feedback framework with minimal symbolic information in NL descriptions; incorporate symbolic feedback from landmarks and output of the VAL plan validator; perform heuristic search over model space to optimize domain quality.
AI.result: The approach evaluates domain quality under various symbolic feedback forms and demonstrates how feedback mechanisms can steer domain generation toward higher quality; concrete quantitative results are not provided in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Combining symbolic feedback with agentic LLMs and heuristic search can guide the generation of higher-quality planning domains from natural language descriptions.

title: HTNav: A Hybrid Navigation Framework with Tiered Structure for Urban Aerial Vision-and-Language Navigation
summary: Inspired by the general Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) task, aerial VLN has attracted widespread attention, owing to its significant practical value in applications such as logistics delivery and urban inspection. However, existing methods face several challenges in complex urban environments, including insufficient generalization to unseen scenes, suboptimal performance in long-range path planning, and inadequate understanding of spatial continuity. To address these challenges, we propose HTNav, a new collaborative navigation framework that integrates Imitation Learning (IL) and Reinforcement Learning (RL) within a hybrid IL-RL framework. This framework adopts a staged training mechanism to ensure the stability of the basic navigation strategy while enhancing its environmental exploration capability. By integrating a tiered decision-making mechanism, it achieves collaborative interaction between macro-level path planning and fine-grained action control. Furthermore, a map representation learning module is introduced to deepen its understanding of spatial continuity in open domains. On the CityNav benchmark, our method achieves state-of-the-art performance across all scene levels and task difficulties. Experimental results demonstrate that this framework significantly improves navigation precision and robustness in complex urban environments.

AI.tldr: Hybrid IL-RL aerial VLN framework HTNav with staged training, tiered macro/micro decision-making, and map representation learning; achieves SOTA on CityNav across all scene levels and task difficulties.
AI.motivation: Address insufficient generalization to unseen scenes, long-range path planning, and spatial continuity understanding in urban aerial VLN.
AI.method: HTNav integrates imitation and reinforcement learning in a staged training regime; tiered decision-making for macro path planning and fine-grained control; map representation learning module to capture spatial continuity; collaborative IL-RL framework.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on CityNav across all scene levels and task difficulties; improved navigation precision and robustness in complex urban environments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed hybrid IL-RL framework effectively enhances generalization and navigation performance in aerial VLN, with the map module strengthening spatial continuity understanding.

title: 3D-VCD: Hallucination Mitigation in 3D-LLM Embodied Agents through Visual Contrastive Decoding
comment: 8 pages, 6 figures, Accepted at IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR) 2026
summary: Large multimodal models are increasingly used as the reasoning core of embodied agents operating in 3D environments, yet they remain prone to hallucinations that can produce unsafe and ungrounded decisions. Existing inference-time hallucination mitigation methods largely target 2D vision-language settings and do not transfer to embodied 3D reasoning, where failures arise from object presence, spatial layout, and geometric grounding rather than pixel-level inconsistencies. We introduce 3D-VCD, the first inference-time visual contrastive decoding framework for hallucination mitigation in 3D embodied agents. 3D-VCD constructs a distorted 3D scene graph by applying semantic and geometric perturbations to object-centric representations, such as category substitutions and coordinate or extent corruption. By contrasting predictions under the original and distorted 3D contexts, our method suppresses tokens that are insensitive to grounded scene evidence and are therefore likely driven by language priors. We evaluate 3D-VCD on the 3D-POPE and HEAL benchmarks and show that it consistently improves grounded reasoning without any retraining, establishing inference-time contrastive decoding over structured 3D representations as an effective and practical route to more reliable embodied intelligence.

AI.tldr: 3D-VCD: an inference-time visual contrastive decoding framework that mitigates hallucinations in 3D embodied agents by contrasting predictions under original and perturbed 3D scene graphs, improving grounded reasoning without retraining on 3D-POPE and HEAL.
AI.motivation: 3D embodied agents using large multimodal models often hallucinate due to mis grounding in object presence, spatial layout, and geometry. Existing 2D inference-time methods do not transfer to 3D reasoning. A retraining-free, inference-time mitigation leveraging structured 3D representations is desirable.
AI.method: Construct distorted 3D scene graphs by applying semantic and geometric perturbations to object-centric representations (e.g., category substitutions, coordinate/extents corruption). Contrast predictions under the original and distorted 3D contexts; suppress tokens that are insensitive to grounded scene evidence and therefore likely driven by language priors.
AI.result: Evaluation on 3D-POPE and HEAL benchmarks shows consistent improvements in grounded reasoning without retraining, demonstrating the effectiveness of inference-time contrastive decoding over structured 3D representations for embodied intelligence.
AI.conclusion: First inference-time visual contrastive decoding framework for 3D embodied agents; establishes a practical route to more reliable grounded reasoning by leveraging structured 3D representations without model fine-tuning.

title: Distilling Genomic Models for Efficient mRNA Representation Learning via Embedding Matching
comment: Accepted at the Tiny Papers Track for the Machine Learning for Genomics Explorations Workshop at ICLR 2026 an the Gen2 Workshop at ICLR 2026
summary: Large Genomic Foundation Models have recently achieved remarkable results and in-vivo translation capabilities. However these models quickly grow to over a few Billion of parameters and are expensive to run when compute is limited. To overcome this challenge, we present a distillation framework for transferring mRNA representations from a state of the art genomic foundation model into a much smaller model specialized for mRNA sequences, reducing the size by 200-fold. Embedding-level distillation worked better than logit based methods, which we found unstable. Benchmarking on mRNA-bench demonstrates that the distilled model achieves state-of-the-art performance among models of comparable size and competes with larger architectures for mRNA-related tasks. Our results highlight embedding-based distillation of mRNA sequences as an effective training strategy for biological foundation models. This enables similar efficient and scalable sequence modelling in genomics, particularly when large models are computationally challenging or infeasible.

AI.tldr: Embedding-level distillation from a large genomic foundation model into a 200x smaller mRNA-specific model achieves state-of-the-art performance among small models and competitive results with larger models for mRNA tasks; embedding-based distillation outperforms logit-based distillation due to stability advantages.
AI.motivation: Large genomic foundation models are powerful but computationally expensive; there is a need for compact, efficient models for mRNA sequence modelling, especially in resource-limited settings.
