AI.method: Introduce an embedding-level distillation framework that transfers representations from a state-of-the-art genomic foundation model to a much smaller, mRNA-specialized model (≈200x smaller). Compare embedding-level distillation with logit-based distillation (which is unstable). Evaluate on mRNA-bench benchmarks.
AI.result: The distilled small model achieves state-of-the-art performance among models of comparable size and competes with larger architectures on mRNA-related tasks. Embedding-based distillation outperforms logit-based methods due to stability.
AI.conclusion: Embedding-based distillation is an effective training strategy for biological foundation models, enabling efficient and scalable sequence modelling in genomics when large models are computationally challenging or infeasible.

title: Artifacts as Memory Beyond the Agent Boundary
summary: The situated view of cognition holds that intelligent behavior depends not only on internal memory, but on an agent's active use of environmental resources. Here, we begin formalizing this intuition within Reinforcement Learning (RL). We introduce a mathematical framing for how the environment can functionally serve as an agent's memory, and prove that certain observations, which we call artifacts, can reduce the information needed to represent history. We corroborate our theory with experiments showing that when agents observe spatial paths, the amount of memory required to learn a performant policy is reduced. Interestingly, this effect arises unintentionally, and implicitly through the agent's sensory stream. We discuss the implications of our findings, and show they satisfy qualitative properties previously used to ground accounts of external memory. Moving forward, we anticipate further work on this subject could reveal principled ways to exploit the environment as a substitute for explicit internal memory.

AI.tldr: We formalize how an environment can act as an agent's memory in RL, introducing 'artifacts' that reduce history complexity; experiments show spatial-path observations decrease memory requirements, implying externalized memory is feasible.
AI.motivation: Bridge situated cognition and RL by treating environmental resources as memory; determine when and how observations attenuate the information needed to represent history; provide a principled basis for using environment as external memory.
AI.method: Develop a mathematical framework in RL where the environment functionally serves as memory; define artifacts—observations that compress history information; prove information-theoretic reductions; run experiments where agents observe spatial paths and measure memory requirements for learning; show that sensory streams induce memory reductions; assess whether resultant behavior aligns with external-memory theories.
AI.result: Theoretical results show artifacts can reduce the information needed to represent history; experiments demonstrate that observing spatial paths lowers memory requirements for achieving performant policies; the memory reduction arises implicitly through the sensory stream; findings satisfy qualitative properties of external memory.
AI.conclusion: Environmentally grounded memory substitution is plausible; future work should investigate principled design of artifacts and environments to offload memory, enabling agents to rely less on internal memory while maintaining performance.

title: AssemLM: Spatial Reasoning Multimodal Large Language Models for Robotic Assembly
summary: Spatial reasoning is a fundamental capability for embodied intelligence, especially for fine-grained manipulation tasks such as robotic assembly. While recent vision-language models (VLMs) exhibit preliminary spatial awareness, they largely rely on coarse 2D perception and lack the ability to perform accurate reasoning over 3D geometry, which is crucial for precise assembly operations. To address this limitation, we propose AssemLM, a spatial multimodal large language model tailored for robotic assembly. AssemLM integrates assembly manuals, point clouds, and textual instructions to reason about and predict task-critical 6D assembly poses, enabling explicit geometric understanding throughout the assembly process. To effectively bridge raw 3D perception and high-level reasoning, we adopt a specialized point cloud encoder to capture fine-grained geometric and rotational features, which are then integrated into the multimodal language model to support accurate 3D spatial reasoning for assembly tasks. In addition, we construct AssemBench, a large-scale dataset and benchmark for assembly-oriented spatial reasoning, comprising over 900K multimodal samples with precise 6D pose annotations. AssemBench extends spatial reasoning evaluation beyond 2D and grounding tasks into full 3D geometric inference, filling a critical gap in existing embodied AI benchmarks. Extensive experiments demonstrate that AssemLM achieves state-of-the-art performance in 6D pose reasoning across diverse assembly scenarios. Furthermore, real-robot evaluations show that our model can support fine-grained and multi-step assembly execution in real-world settings, demonstrating its potential for robotic assembly applications.

AI.tldr: Proposes AssemLM, a spatial multimodal language model for robotic assembly, along with AssemBench, a 900k+ multimodal dataset with 6D pose annotations; achieves state-of-the-art 6D pose reasoning and is validated on real robots.
AI.motivation: Current vision-language models rely on coarse 2D perception and struggle with 3D geometric reasoning required for precise 6D pose estimation in assembly tasks; there is a need to bridge raw 3D perception and high-level reasoning, and to establish a benchmark for 3D spatial inference in embodied AI.
AI.method: Introduce AssemLM that fuses assembly manuals, point clouds, and textual instructions; employ a specialized point cloud encoder to capture fine-grained geometric and rotational features; integrate these into a multimodal language model to enable 3D spatial reasoning for assembly; create AssemBench with over 900K multimodal samples and precise 6D pose annotations.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance in 6D pose reasoning across diverse assembly scenarios; real-robot evaluations demonstrate capability for fine-grained, multi-step assembly execution in real-world settings.
AI.conclusion: The work bridges the gap in 3D spatial reasoning for embodied assembly AI by providing a joint model and benchmark, enabling robust 3D geometric inference and practical deployment in robotic assembly.

title: InsEdit: Towards Instruction-based Visual Editing via Data-Efficient Video Diffusion Models Adaptation
summary: Instruction-based video editing is a natural way to control video content with text, but adapting a video generation model into an editor usually appears data-hungry. At the same time, high-quality video editing data remains scarce. In this paper, we show that a video generation backbone can become a strong video editor without large scale video editing data. We present InsEdit, an instruction-based editing model built on HunyuanVideo-1.5. InsEdit combines a visual editing architecture with a video data pipeline based on Mutual Context Attention (MCA), which creates aligned video pairs where edits can begin in the middle of a clip rather than only from the first frame. With only O(100)K video editing data, InsEdit achieves state-of-the-art results among open-source methods on our video instruction editing benchmarks. In addition, because our training recipe also includes image editing data, the final model supports image editing without any modification.

AI.tldr: InsEdit is a data-efficient, instruction-based video editor built on HunyuanVideo-1.5 that uses a Mutual Context Attention pipeline to align video edits, enabling edits from mid-clip with only ~100k editing samples, achieving open-source SOTA and supporting image editing via the same training recipe.
AI.motivation: Current video editing models require large-scale editing data and typically edit from the first frame only. This work aims to repurpose a video generation backbone into an editor with limited data, enabling mid-clip edits and cross-domain editing (video and image).
AI.method: Integrates a visual editing architecture with a video data pipeline using Mutual Context Attention (MCA) to create aligned video pairs, allowing edits to start in the middle of a clip. Trains on O(100)K video editing data plus image editing data, leveraging HunyuanVideo-1.5 as the backbone.
AI.result: InsEdit achieves state-of-the-art results among open-source methods on their video instruction editing benchmarks with a modest data footprint (~100k edits). The model also inherits image editing capabilities without additional modification due to the combined training data.
AI.conclusion: A video-generation backbone can become a strong, data-efficient video editor when paired with a MCA-based data pipeline and cross-domain image editing data, enabling mid-clip edits and competitive open-source performance.

title: MolPaQ: Modular Quantum-Classical Patch Learning for Interpretable Molecular Generation
summary: Molecular generative models must jointly ensure validity, diversity, and property control, yet existing approaches typically trade off among these objectives. We present MOLPAQ, a modular quantum-classical generator that assembles molecules from quantum-generated latent patches. A \b{eta}-VAE pretrained on QM9 learns a chemically aligned latent manifold; a reduced conditioner maps molecular descriptors into this space; and a parameter-efficient quantum patch generator produces entangled node embeddings that a valence-aware aggregator reconstructs into valid molecular graphs. Adversarial fine-tuning with a latent critic and chemistry-shaped reward yields 100\% RDKit validity, 99.75\% novelty, and 0.905 diversity. Beyond aggregate metrics, the pretrained quantum generator, steered by the conditioner, improves mean QED by approx. 2.3\% and increases aromatic motif incidence by approx. 10-12\% relative to a parameter-matched classical generator, highlighting its role as a compact topology-shaping operator.

AI.tldr: MOLPAQ is a modular quantum-classical molecular generator that uses quantum-generated latent patches within a eta-VAE latent space to achieve high validity, novelty, and diversity, with improvements in chemical properties.
AI.motivation: Address the core trade-off among validity, diversity, and property control in molecular generation, and explore quantum-inspired topology shaping to enhance chemical properties while maintaining efficiency.
AI.method: A eta-VAE pretrained on QM9 learns a chemically aligned latent manifold; a reduced conditioner maps molecular descriptors into this space; a parameter-efficient quantum patch generator produces entangled node embeddings; a valence-aware aggregator reconstructs valid molecular graphs; adversarial fine-tuning with a latent critic and chemistry-shaped reward.
AI.result: 100% RDKit validity, 99.75% novelty, and 0.905 diversity; mean QED improves by ~2.3% and aromatic motif incidence increases by ~10–12% vs a parameter-matched classical generator; quantum generator acts as a compact topology-shaping operator.
AI.conclusion: The modular quantum-classical architecture delivers high validity and diversity while providing enhanced property control, illustrating effective integration of quantum components into molecular generators and potential for scalable design.

title: Hidden in Plain Sight: Visual-to-Symbolic Analytical Solution Inference from Field Visualizations
summary: Recovering analytical solutions of physical fields from visual observations is a fundamental yet underexplored capability for AI-assisted scientific reasoning. We study visual-to-symbolic analytical solution inference (ViSA) for two-dimensional linear steady-state fields: given field visualizations (and first-order derivatives) plus minimal auxiliary metadata, the model must output a single executable SymPy expression with fully instantiated numeric constants. We introduce ViSA-R2 and align it with a self-verifying, solution-centric chain-of-thought pipeline that follows a physicist-like pathway: structural pattern recognition solution-family (ansatz) hypothesis parameter derivation consistency verification. We also release ViSA-Bench, a VLM-ready synthetic benchmark covering 30 linear steady-state scenarios with verifiable analytical/symbolic annotations, and evaluate predictions by numerical accuracy, expression-structure similarity, and character-level accuracy. Using an 8B open-weight Qwen3-VL backbone, ViSA-R2 outperforms strong open-source baselines and the evaluated closed-source frontier VLMs under a standardized protocol.

AI.tldr: ViSA enables visual-to-symbolic inference of analytical solutions for 2D linear steady-state fields; introduces ViSA-R2 with self-verifying, solution-centric chain-of-thought; releases ViSA-Bench; achieves strong performance on an 8B-Qwen3-VL backbone.
AI.motivation: Addresses the gap in AI reasoning from visual field observations to exact analytical expressions, aiming to emulate physicist-style reasoning (pattern recognition, ansatz formulation, parameter derivation, and verification).
AI.method: A self-verifying, solution-centric chain-of-thought pipeline: structural pattern recognition, ansatz hypothesis, parameter derivation, and consistency verification; ViSA-R2 model; ViSA-Bench synthetic benchmark with 30 linear steady-state scenarios and verifiable annotations; evaluation on numerical accuracy, expression-structure similarity, and character-level accuracy; uses an 8B open-weight Qwen3-VL backbone.
AI.result: ViSA-R2 outperforms strong open-source baselines and evaluated closed-source frontier VLMs under a standardized protocol; ViSA-Bench provides verifiable annotations and a broad testbed.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of visual-to-symbolic analytical solving for linear PDEs using a physics-inspired reasoning pipeline; provides a scalable benchmark and architecture with strong empirical performance; potential for extension to broader physical domains and more complex equations.

title: V-CAGE: Vision-Closed-Loop Agentic Generation Engine for Robotic Manipulation
summary: Scaling Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models requires massive datasets that are both semantically coherent and physically feasible. However, existing scene generation methods often lack context-awareness, making it difficult to synthesize high-fidelity environments embedded with rich semantic information, frequently resulting in unreachable target positions that cause tasks to fail prematurely. We present V-CAGE (Vision-Closed-loop Agentic Generation Engine), an agentic framework for autonomous robotic data synthesis. Unlike traditional scripted pipelines, V-CAGE operates as an embodied agentic system, leveraging foundation models to bridge high-level semantic reasoning with low-level physical interaction. Specifically, we introduce Inpainting-Guided Scene Construction to systematically arrange context-aware layouts, ensuring that the generated scenes are both semantically structured and kinematically reachable. To ensure trajectory correctness, we integrate functional metadata with a Vision-Language Model based closed-loop verification mechanism, acting as a visual critic to rigorously filter out silent failures and sever the error propagation chain. Finally, to overcome the storage bottleneck of massive video datasets, we implement a perceptually-driven compression algorithm that achieves over 90\% filesize reduction without compromising downstream VLA training efficacy. By centralizing semantic layout planning and visual self-verification, V-CAGE automates the end-to-end pipeline, enabling the highly scalable synthesis of diverse, high-quality robotic manipulation datasets.

AI.tldr: V-CAGE is an embodied, agentic pipeline that generates semantically structured, reachable robotic scenes with self-verification and efficient video compression for scalable VLA datasets.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of context-aware scene generation that yields unreachable targets, enabling large-scale, high-fidelity data for Vision-Language-Action models.
AI.method: An autonomous agentic system using foundation models: Inpainting-Guided Scene Construction for layout planning; Vision-Language Model–based closed-loop verification with functional metadata as a critic; perceptual compression to dramatically reduce video storage while preserving downstream training efficacy.
AI.result: A complete end-to-end pipeline for automated dataset synthesis, verification, and storage optimization, enabling scalable production of diverse, high-quality robotic manipulation data.
AI.conclusion: Centralizing semantic layout planning and self-verification improves reliability and scalability of VLA data generation; success hinges on verification accuracy and generalization of compression to preserve task-relevant signals.

title: EfficientSign: An Attention-Enhanced Lightweight Architecture for Indian Sign Language Recognition
comment: Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Human-Machine Systems
summary: How do you build a sign language recognizer that works on a phone? That question drove this work. We built EfficientSign, a lightweight model which takes EfficientNet-B0 and focuses on two attention modules (Squeeze-and-Excitation for channel focus, and a spatial attention layer that focuses on the hand gestures). We tested it against five other approaches on 12,637 images of Indian Sign Language alphabets, all 26 classes, using 5-fold cross-validation. EfficientSign achieves the accuracy of 99.94% (+/-0.05%), which matches the performance of ResNet18's 99.97% accuracy, but with 62% fewer parameters (4.2M vs 11.2M). We also experimented with feeding deep features (1,280-dimensional vectors pulled from EfficientNet-B0's pooling layer) into classical classifiers. SVM achieved the accuracy of 99.63%, Logistic Regression achieved the accuracy of 99.03% and KNN achieved accuracy of 96.33%. All of these blow past the 92% that SURF-based methods managed on a similar dataset back in 2015. Our results show that attention-enhanced learning model provides an efficient and deployable solution for ISL recognition without requiring a massive model or hand-tuned feature pipelines anymore.

AI.tldr: EfficientSign: a lightweight EfficientNet-B0-based ISL recognizer with SE channel attention and a spatial attention layer; achieves ~99.94% accuracy on 12,637 ISL images (26 classes) with 4.2M parameters, comparable to ResNet-18 but smaller; deep features with SVM/LogReg/KNN also strong; outperforms 2015 SURF-based methods.
AI.motivation: Develop a deployable mobile ISL recognizer that maintains high accuracy while keeping model size small and avoiding hand-crafted feature pipelines.
AI.method: Backbone EfficientNet-B0 with two attention modules (Squeeze-and-Excitation for channel attention and a spatial attention layer). 5-fold cross-validation on 12,637 Indian Sign Language alphabet images (26 classes). Compare against five baseline approaches. Additionally, extract 1280-dimensional deep features from the pooling layer and train classical classifiers (SVM, Logistic Regression, KNN).
AI.result: Achieves 99.94% ± 0.05% accuracy. ResNet-18 achieves 99.97% but with 11.2M parameters vs EfficientSign's 4.2M (≈62% fewer). Deep-feature classifiers: SVM 99.63%, Logistic Regression 99.03%, KNN 96.33%. SURF-based methods on a similar 2015 dataset yielded ≈92%.
AI.conclusion: Attention-enhanced lightweight modeling provides an efficient, deployable ISL recognition solution suitable for mobile devices, matching state-of-the-art accuracy without large models or hand-tuned feature pipelines.

title: SPPO: Sequence-Level PPO for Long-Horizon Reasoning Tasks
summary: Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) is central to aligning Large Language Models (LLMs) in reasoning tasks with verifiable rewards. However, standard token-level PPO struggles in this setting due to the instability of temporal credit assignment over long Chain-of-Thought (CoT) horizons and the prohibitive memory cost of the value model. While critic-free alternatives like GRPO mitigate these issues, they incur significant computational overhead by requiring multiple samples for baseline estimation, severely limiting training throughput. In this paper, we introduce Sequence-Level PPO (SPPO), a scalable algorithm that harmonizes the sample efficiency of PPO with the stability of outcome-based updates. SPPO reformulates the reasoning process as a Sequence-Level Contextual Bandit problem, employing a decoupled scalar value function to derive low-variance advantage signals without multi-sampling. Extensive experiments on mathematical benchmarks demonstrate that SPPO significantly surpasses standard PPO and matches the performance of computation-heavy group-based methods, offering a resource-efficient framework for aligning reasoning LLMs.

AI.tldr: SPPO is proposed to improve PPO for LLM reasoning with long CoT horizons by treating as Sequence-Level Contextual Bandit with a decoupled scalar value, aiming for low-variance advantages without multi-sampling; it achieves better performance and throughput on math benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Tackle instability of token-level PPO due to long horizon credit assignment in reasoning tasks and the high memory costs of value models; critic-free alternatives incur heavy sampling; need a scalable, sample-efficient, stable update mechanism for verifiable rewards in LLMs.
AI.method: Reformulate reasoning as a Sequence-Level Contextual Bandit; employ a decoupled scalar value function to produce low-variance advantage signals without requiring multiple samples; align reasoning LLMs with verifiable rewards efficiently.
AI.result: Experimental results on mathematical benchmarks show SPPO significantly outperforms standard PPO and matches more computation-heavy group-based methods, while offering better throughput and resource efficiency.
AI.conclusion: SPPO provides a scalable, resource-efficient framework that combines the sample efficiency of PPO with stable, outcome-based updates for reasoning LLMs, enabling effective alignment with verifiable rewards on long-chain reasoning tasks.

title: Towards Lifelong Aerial Autonomy: Geometric Memory Management for Continual Visual Place Recognition in Dynamic Environments
summary: Robust geo-localization in changing environmental conditions is critical for long-term aerial autonomy. While visual place recognition (VPR) models perform well when airborne views match the training domain, adapting them to shifting distributions during sequential missions triggers catastrophic forgetting. Existing continual learning (CL) methods often fail here because geographic features exhibit severe intra-class variations. In this work, we formulate aerial VPR as a mission-based domain-incremental learning (DIL) problem and propose a novel heterogeneous memory framework. To respect strict onboard storage constraints, our "Learn-and-Dispose" pipeline decouples geographic knowledge into static satellite anchors (preserving global geometric priors) and a dynamic experience replay buffer (retaining domain-specific features). We introduce a spatially-constrained allocation strategy that optimizes buffer selection based on sample difficulty or feature space diversity. To facilitate systematic assessment, we provide three evaluation criteria and a comprehensive benchmark derived from 21 diverse mission sequences. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our architecture significantly boosts spatial generalization; our diversity-driven buffer selection outperforms the random baseline by 7.8% in knowledge retention. Unlike class-mean preservation methods that fail in unstructured environments, maximizing structural diversity achieves a superior plasticity-stability balance and ensures order-agnostic robustness across randomized sequences. These results prove that maintaining structural feature coverage is more critical than sample difficulty for resolving catastrophic forgetting in lifelong aerial autonomy.

AI.tldr: Aerial VPR under changing conditions is framed as mission-based DIL; introduces a heterogeneous memory with static satellite anchors and a dynamic replay buffer guided by diversity, achieving better retention and robustness with strict onboard constraints.
AI.motivation: Overcoming catastrophic forgetting in continual aerial VPR where environmental and domain shifts induce large intra-class variations, under strict memory limits for onboard deployment.
AI.method: Proposes Learn-and-Dispose: decouples knowledge into static satellite anchors (global priors) and a dynamic experience replay buffer (domain-specific features). Employs a spatially-constrained allocation strategy selecting samples by difficulty or feature-space diversity. Frames VPR as domain-incremental learning and provides three evaluation criteria plus a 21-mission benchmark.
AI.result: Diverse-buffer strategy increases knowledge retention by 7.8% over random buffer selection. Architecture improves spatial generalization and demonstrates order-agnostic robustness across randomized sequences. Structural diversity outperforms class-mean preservation in unstructured environments, indicating a better balance between plasticity and stability.
AI.conclusion: Maintaining structural feature coverage is more critical than solely sampling difficulty for mitigating forgetting in lifelong aerial VPR. The static anchors preserve global structure while the dynamic diversified buffer preserves domain-specific nuances under memory constraints.

title: Unified Multimodal Uncertain Inference
summary: We introduce Unified Multimodal Uncertain Inference (UMUI), a multimodal inference task spanning text, audio, and video, where models must produce calibrated probability estimates of hypotheses conditioned on a premise in any modality or combination. While uncertain inference has been explored in text, extension to other modalities has been limited to single-modality binary entailment judgments, leaving no framework for fine-grained probabilistic reasoning in or across other modalities. To address this, we curate a human-annotated evaluation set with scalar probability judgments across audio, visual, and audiovisual settings, and additionally evaluate on existing text and audio benchmarks. We introduce CLUE (Calibrated Latent Uncertainty Estimation), which combines self-consistent teacher calibration and distribution-based confidence probing to produce calibrated predictions. We demonstrate that our 3B-parameter model achieves equivalent or stronger performance than baselines up to 32B parameters across all modalities.

AI.tldr: Unified Multimodal Uncertain Inference (UMUI) across text, audio, and video; introduces CLUE calibration and a 3B model achieving strong, calibrated probabilistic reasoning comparable to much larger baselines across modalities.
AI.motivation: There is a lack of a formal framework for calibrated probabilistic reasoning across modalities. Uncertain inference is well-studied in text but not extended to multimodal settings beyond binary judgments; a need exists for fine-grained probabilistic estimates conditioned on a premise across modalities.
AI.method: Define the UMUI task spanning text, audio, video; curate a human-annotated evaluation set with scalar probability judgments across modalities; develop CLUE (Calibrated Latent Uncertainty Estimation) combining self-consistent teacher calibration and distribution-based confidence probing; train/evaluate a 3B-parameter model against baselines up to 32B across modalities; assess calibration and predictive performance.
AI.result: The 3B model attains equivalent or stronger performance than larger baselines (up to 32B) across text, audio, video, and cross-modal tasks; CLUE provides calibrated predictions, demonstrating effective probabilistic multimodal inference with a comparatively small model.
AI.conclusion: Calibrated probabilistic inference in multimodal settings is feasible with CLUE. The work demonstrates cross-modal uncertainty estimation with scalable models, and introduces datasets and protocols for evaluating scalar probability judgments across modalities.

title: Structured Exploration and Exploitation of Label Functions for Automated Data Annotation
summary: High-quality labeled data is critical for training reliable machine learning and deep learning models, yet manual annotation remains costly and error-prone. Programmatic labeling addresses this challenge by using label functions (LFs), i.e., heuristic rules that automatically generate weak labels for training datasets. However, existing automated LF generation methods either rely on large language models (LLMs) to synthesize surface-level heuristics or employ model-based synthesis over hand-crafted primitives. These approaches often result in limited coverage and unreliable label quality. In this paper, we introduce EXPONA, an automated framework for programmatic labeling that formulates LF generation as a principled process balancing diversity and reliability. EXPONA systematically explores multi-level LFs, spanning surface, structural, and semantic perspectives. EXPONA further applies reliability-aware mechanisms to suppress noisy or redundant heuristics while preserving complementary signals. To evaluate EXPONA, we conducted extensive experiments on eleven classification datasets across diverse domains. Experimental results show that EXPONA consistently outperformed state-of-the-art automated LF generation methods. Specifically, EXPONA achieved nearly complete label coverage (up to 98.9%), improved weak label quality by up to 87%, and yielded downstream performance gains of up to 46% in weighted F1. These results indicate that EXPONA's combination of multi-level LF exploration and reliability-aware filtering enabled more consistent label quality and downstream performance across diverse tasks by balancing coverage and precision in the generated LF set.

AI.tldr: EXPONA automates generation of programmatic labeling rules by exploring multi-level label functions (surface, structural, semantic) and applying reliability-aware filtering, achieving high coverage and strong downstream gains.
AI.motivation: High-quality labeled data is essential but costly; existing automated LF methods produce limited coverage or unreliable quality due to reliance on LLMs for surface heuristics or hand-crafted primitives; a principled, multi-level, reliability-aware approach is needed.
AI.method: EXPONA framework that systematically explores multi-level LFs (surface, structural, semantic); employs reliability-aware mechanisms to suppress noisy/redundant LFs while preserving complementary signals; evaluates across eleven classification datasets.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art automated LF generation methods; achieves up to 98.9% label coverage; up to 87% improvement in weak label quality; up to 46% gains in downstream weighted F1.
AI.conclusion: A multi-level LF exploration with reliability filtering yields more consistent label quality and downstream performance across diverse tasks by balancing coverage and precision in generated LF sets.

title: StaRPO: Stability-Augmented Reinforcement Policy Optimization
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) is effective in enhancing the accuracy of large language models in complex reasoning tasks. Existing RL policy optimization frameworks rely on final-answer correctness as feedback signals and rarely capture the internal logical structure of the reasoning process. Consequently, the models would generate fluent and semantically relevant responses but logically inconsistent, structurally erratic, or redundant. To this end, we propose StaRPO, a stability-augmented reinforcement learning framework that explicitly incorporates reasoning stability into the optimization objective. Our StaRPO decomposes stability into two computable lightweight metrics: the Autocorrelation Function (ACF) to evaluate local step-to-step coherence, and Path Efficiency (PE) to evaluate global goal-directedness of the reasoning trajectory. These stability rewards are combined with task rewards to provide complementary and process-aware feedback. We validate the effectiveness of using ACF and PE rewards by showing their correlation with logic errors on two backbone models. Experiments on four reasoning benchmarks show that StaRPO consistently outperforms compared baselines and can enhance both final-answer accuracy and logical stability.

AI.tldr: A stability-aware RL framework (StaRPO) for LLM reasoning adds lightweight stability rewards—Autocorrelation Function (ACF) for local coherence and Path Efficiency (PE) for global goal-directedness—to policy optimization, improving both final accuracy and reasoning stability.
AI.motivation: RL-based optimization for LLMs often relies solely on final-answer correctness, neglecting internal reasoning structure, leading to fluent but potentially inconsistent or erratic reasoning. A stability-aware objective aims to align process quality with final outcomes.
AI.method: Decompose stability into two computable metrics: ACF to assess step-to-step coherence and PE to assess global trajectory efficiency. Combine stability rewards with task rewards to guide RL. Validate that ACF/PE correlate with logic errors on two backbone models. Evaluate on four reasoning benchmarks; compare against baselines.
AI.result: StaRPO consistently outperforms baselines, enhancing both final-answer accuracy and logical stability. ACF and PE rewards correlate with logic errors, supporting their usefulness as process-aware feedback.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating stability metrics into RL for LLM reasoning yields more reliable and logically coherent outputs; ACF and PE provide practical, lightweight signals that improve reasoning trajectories and accuracy.

title: {\sf TriDeliver}: Cooperative Air-Ground Instant Delivery with UAVs, Couriers, and Crowdsourced Ground Vehicles
summary: Instant delivery, shipping items before critical deadlines, is essential in daily life. While multiple delivery agents, such as couriers, Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs), and crowdsourced agents, have been widely employed, each of them faces inherent limitations (e.g., low efficiency/labor shortages, flight control, and dynamic capabilities, respectively), preventing them from meeting the surging demands alone. This paper proposes {\sf TriDeliver}, the first hierarchical cooperative framework, integrating human couriers, UAVs, and crowdsourced ground vehicles (GVs) for efficient instant delivery. To obtain the initial scheduling knowledge for GVs and UAVs as well as improve the cooperative delivery performance, we design a Transfer Learning (TL)-based algorithm to extract delivery knowledge from couriers' behavioral history and transfer their knowledge to UAVs and GVs with fine-tunings, which is then used to dispatch parcels for efficient delivery. Evaluated on one-month real-world trajectory and delivery datasets, it has been demonstrated that 1) by integrating couriers, UAVs, and crowdsourced GVs, {\sf TriDeliver} reduces the delivery cost by $65.8\%$ versus state-of-the-art cooperative delivery by UAVs and couriers; 2) {\sf TriDeliver} achieves further improvements in terms of delivery time ($-17.7\%$), delivery cost ($-9.8\%$), and impacts on original tasks of crowdsourced GVs ($-43.6\%$), even with the representation of the transferred knowledge by simple neural networks, respectively.

AI.tldr: TriDeliver is a hierarchical framework that fuses human couriers, UAVs, and crowdsourced ground vehicles for instant delivery, using a Transfer Learning-based approach to emulate courier knowledge in autonomous agents, yielding substantial efficiency gains on real-world data.
AI.motivation: Single-delivery modalities struggle with efficiency, capacity, and dynamic constraints. Integrating multiple agents and transferring human scheduling knowledge can improve responsiveness and reduce costs.
AI.method: Proposes TriDeliver, a hierarchical cooperative framework. Introduces a Transfer Learning–based algorithm that extracts delivery patterns from couriers’ history and fine-tunes them for UAVs and crowdsourced GVs to dispatch parcels. Evaluation on one-month real-world trajectory and delivery datasets.
AI.result: Delivery cost reduced by 65.8% compared with state-of-the-art UAVs+couriers. Additional improvements: delivery time down 17.7%, delivery cost down 9.8%, and reduced negative impact on crowdsourced GVs tasks by 43.6%, achieved even with simple neural networks for the transferred knowledge.
AI.conclusion: TriDeliver demonstrates the benefit of hierarchical cooperation and transfer learning in instant delivery, achieving substantial efficiency gains by leveraging human behavior to guide autonomous agents.

title: RS-OVC: Open-Vocabulary Counting for Remote-Sensing Data
summary: Object-Counting for remote-sensing (RS) imagery is attracting increasing research interest due to its crucial role in a wide and diverse set of applications. While several promising methods for RS object-counting have been proposed, existing methods focus on a closed, pre-defined set of object classes. This limitation necessitates costly re-annotation and model re-training to adapt current approaches for counting of novel objects that have not been seen during training, and severely inhibits their application in dynamic, real-world monitoring scenarios. To address this gap, in this work we propose RS-OVC - the first Open Vocabulary Counting (OVC) model for Remote-Sensing and aerial imagery. We show that our model is capable of accurate counting of novel object classes, that were unseen during training, based solely on textual and/or visual conditioning.

AI.tldr: Introduces RS-OVC, the first open-vocabulary counting model for remote-sensing imagery, enabling accurate counting of unseen object classes from textual and/or visual conditioning.
AI.motivation: Current RS counting methods are restricted to a predefined class set, requiring costly re-annotation and retraining to count novel objects, hindering deployment in dynamic, real-world monitoring.
AI.method: RS-OVC uses open vocabulary conditioning (textual and/or visual) to count objects without prior exposure to the target classes, enabling zero-shot or few-shot adaptation to new categories.
AI.result: Empirically demonstrates accurate counting for novel object classes unseen during training when guided by textual and/or visual conditioning.
AI.conclusion: Open Vocabulary Counting for RS imagery addresses the adaptability gap, facilitating flexible counting for dynamic scenarios.

title: On the Spectral Geometry of Cross-Modal Representations: A Functional Map Diagnostic for Multimodal Alignment
comment: Under review at ACMMM Brave New Ideas Track
summary: We study cross-modal alignment between independently pretrained vision (DINOv2) and language (all-MiniLM-L6-v2) encoders using the functional map framework from computational geometry, which represents correspondence between representation manifolds as a compact linear operator between graph Laplacian eigenbases. While the framework underperforms Procrustes alignment and relative representations for cross-modal retrieval across all supervision budgets, it reveals a structural property of multimodal representations. We find that the Laplacian eigenvalue spectra of the two encoders are quantitatively similar (normalized spectral distance 0.043), indicating that independently trained models develop manifolds of comparable intrinsic complexity. However, the functional map exhibits near-zero diagonal dominance (mean below 0.05) and large orthogonality error (70.15), showing that the eigenvector bases are effectively unaligned. We term this decoupling the spectral complexity--orientation gap: models converge in how much structure they capture but not in how they organize it. This gap defines a boundary condition for spectral alignment methods and motivates three diagnostic quantities : diagonal dominance, orthogonality deviation, and Laplacian commutativity error for characterizing cross-modal representation compatibility.

AI.tldr: Independent pretrained vision (DINOv2) and language (all-MiniLM-L6-v2) encoders show similar Laplacian spectral complexity but misaligned eigenvectors, limiting spectral alignment; introduces the spectral complexity–orientation gap and three diagnostic metrics.
AI.motivation: To understand cross-modal alignment feasibility and uncover structural properties of independently trained multimodal representation manifolds using the functional map framework.
AI.method: Represent representation manifolds with graph Laplacians, compute Laplacian eigenbases, and model cross-modal correspondence as a functional map (linear operator). Compare spectral properties (diagonal dominance, orthogonality, commutativity) and retrieval performance across supervision budgets.
AI.result: Laplacian spectra of the two encoders are similar (normalized spectral distance = 0.043). The functional map has near-zero diagonal dominance (mean < 0.05) and large orthogonality error (70.15), indicating unaligned eigenvector bases. Cross-modal retrieval under this framework underperforms compared to Procrustes alignment and relative representations.
AI.conclusion: The spectral complexity–orientation gap quantifies decoupled structure between modalities and defines a boundary for spectral alignment methods. It motivates three diagnostic quantities—diagonal dominance, orthogonality deviation, and Laplacian commutativity error—for assessing cross-modal representation compatibility.

title: Enhancing LLM Problem Solving via Tutor-Student Multi-Agent Interaction
comment: 7 pages, 3 figures, This work is under review for conference appearance
summary: Human cognitive development is shaped not only by individual effort but by structured social interaction, where role-based exchanges such as those between a tutor and a learner, enable solutions that neither could achieve alone. Inspired by these developmental principles, we ask the question whether a tutor-student multi-agent system can create a synergistic effect by pushing Large Language Model (LLM) beyond what it can do within existing frameworks. To test the idea, we adopt autonomous coding problem domain where two agents instantiated from the same LLM assigned asymmetric roles: a student agent generates and iteratively refines solutions, while a tutor agent provides structured evaluative feedback without access to ground-truth answers. In our proposed framework (PETITE), we aim to extract better problem-solving performance from one model by structuring its interaction through complementary roles, rather than relying on stronger supervisory models or heterogeneous ensembles. Our model is evaluated on the APPS coding benchmark against state-of-the-art approaches of Self-Consistency, Self-Refine, Multi-Agent Debate, and Multi-Agent Review. The results show that our model achieves similar or higher accuracy while consuming significantly fewer tokens. These results suggest that developmentally grounded role-differentiated interaction structures provide a principled and resource-efficient paradigm for enhancing LLM problem-solving through structured peer-like interactions. Index Terms- Peer Tutoring, Scaffolding, Large Language Models, Multi-Agent Systems, Code Generation

AI.tldr: PETITE uses a tutor-student duo instantiated from a single LLM to push problem-solving beyond standard single-agent prompting by structured peer-like interaction; evaluated on APPS; achieves similar or better accuracy with fewer tokens.
AI.motivation: Leverage developmental principles (role-based scaffolding) to improve LLM problem solving without stronger supervisory models or ensembles.
AI.method: Two asymmetric roles, student generates/refines solutions; tutor provides structured evaluative feedback without ground-truth access; operate within autonomous coding problem domain; test on APPS; compare to Self-Consistency, Self-Refine, Multi-Agent Debate, Multi-Agent Review.
AI.result: Similar or higher accuracy; significantly fewer tokens consumed.
AI.conclusion: Developmentally grounded role-differentiated interaction structures can enhance LLM problem solving efficiently in a resource-constrained setting.

title: On the Terminology and Geometric Aspects of Redundant Parallel Manipulators
summary: Parallel kinematics machines (PKM) can exhibit kinematic as well as actuation redundancy. While the meaning of kinematic redundancy has been clarified already for serial manipulators, actuation redundancy, that is only possible in PKM, is differently classified in the literature. In this paper a consistent terminology for general redundant PKM is proposed. A kinematic model is introduced with the configuration space (c-space) as central part. The notion of kinematic redundancy is recalled for PKM. C-space, output, and input singularities are distinguished. The significance of the c-space geometry is emphasized, and it is pointed out geometrically that input singularities can be avoided by redundant actuation schemes. In order to distinguish different actuation schemes of PKM a non-linear control system is introduced whose dynamics evolves on the c-space. The degree of actuation (DOA) is introduced as the number of independent control vector fields, and PKM are classified as full-actuated and underactuated. Relating this DOA to the degree of freedom (DOF) allows to classify the actuation redundancy.

AI.tldr: Proposes a consistent terminology and c-space-based framework to analyze actuation redundancy in parallel kinematic machines (PKM). Introduces degree of actuation (DOA) as the number of independent control vector fields, and classifies PKMs as full-actuated or underactuated; links DOA to DOF, and shows input singularities can be mitigated by redundant actuation; emphasizes c-space geometry and distinguishes c-space, output, and input singularities.
AI.motivation: There is confusion in the PKM literature about actuation redundancy; a unified terminology and mathematical framework is needed to study redundancy, singularities, and control strategies.
AI.method: Develops a nonlinear control system evolving on the configuration space (c-space); defines DOA, distinguishes singularities, and classifies PKM by actuation; relates DOA to DOF; considers kinematic redundancy and the role of redundant actuation to avoid input singularities; proposes a consistent terminology.
AI.result: A classification scheme for actuation redundancy (full-actuated vs underactuated); a c-space–based model and DOA concept; demonstrates that input singularities can be avoided with redundancy; clarifies relationships among c-space, input, and output singularities.
AI.conclusion: Provides a unified terminology and modeling framework for PKM redundancy, enabling systematic analysis of actuation schemes and singularities through c-space geometry and the DOA/DOF linkage.

title: Deep Learning-Based Tracking and Lineage Reconstruction of Ligament Breakup
summary: The disintegration of liquid sheets into ligaments and droplets involves highly transient, multi-scale dynamics that are difficult to quantify from high-speed shadowgraphy images. Identifying droplets, ligaments, and blobs formed during breakup, along with tracking across frames, is essential for spray analysis. However, conventional multi-object tracking frameworks impose strict one-to-one temporal associations and cannot represent one-to-many fragmentation events. In this study, we present a two-stage deep learning framework for object detection and temporal relationship modeling across frames. The framework captures ligament deformation, fragmentation, and parent-child lineage during liquid sheet disintegration. In the first stage, a Faster R-CNN with a ResNet-50 backbone and Feature Pyramid Network detects and classifies ligaments and droplets in high-speed shadowgraphy recordings of an impinging Carbopol gel jet. A morphology-preserving synthetic data generation strategy augments the training set without introducing physically implausible configurations, achieving a held-out F1 score of up to 0.872 across fourteen original-to-synthetic configurations. In the second stage, a Transformer-augmented multilayer perceptron classifies inter-frame associations into continuation, fragmentation (one-to-many), and non-association using physics-informed geometric features. Despite severe class imbalance, the model achieves 86.1% accuracy, 93.2% precision, and perfect recall (1.00) for fragmentation events. Together, the framework enables automated reconstruction of fragmentation trees, preservation of parent-child lineage, and extraction of breakup statistics such as fragment multiplicity and droplet size distributions. By explicitly identifying children droplets formed from ligament fragmentation, the framework provides automated analysis of the primary atomization mode.

AI.tldr: A two-stage deep learning framework is proposed to detect ligaments and droplets in high-speed shadowgraphy and to model inter-frame associations for fragmentation events, enabling reconstruction of fragmentation trees and extraction of breakup statistics. It tackles one-to-many fragmentation by using a Transformer-augmented MLP with physics-informed features, plus synthetic data augmentation to boost detection performance.
AI.motivation: Quantify highly transient, multi-scale breakup dynamics in liquid-sheet disintegration and overcome the limitations of conventional one-to-one tracking, which cannot capture fragmentation and parent–child lineage.
AI.method: Stage 1: Detection — Faster R-CNN with ResNet-50 back- bone + FPN detects and classifies ligaments and droplets in high-speed shadowgraphy of Carbopol gel jets. Stage 2: Temporal relationships — Transformer-augmented multilayer perceptron classifies inter-frame associations as continuation, fragmentation (one-to-many), or non-association using physics-informed geometric features. A morphology-preserving synthetic data generation strategy augments training with fourteen original-to-synthetic configurations. Outcome: A framework that reconstructs fragmentation trees, preserves parent–child lineage, and extracts breakup statistics (fragment multiplicity, droplet size distributions), enabling analysis of primary atomization by identifying children droplets from ligament fragmentation.
AI.result: Held-out F1 score up to 0.872 for fourteen synthetic configurations; fragmentation events reach 86.1% accuracy, 93.2% precision, and 1.00 recall; capable of automated fragmentation-tree reconstruction and extraction of breakup statistics.
AI.conclusion: The proposed two-stage framework provides robust detection and temporally-aware association modeling to capture fragmentation, preserving lineage and enabling automated analysis of atomization, including identification of child droplets and fragmentation trees.

title: Fully Autonomous Z-Score-Based TinyML Anomaly Detection on Resource-Constrained MCUs Using Power Side-Channel Data
summary: This paper presents a fully autonomous Tiny Machine Learning (TinyML) Z-Score-based anomaly detection system deployed on a low-power microcontroller for real-time monitoring of appliance behavior using power side-channel data. Unlike existing Internet of Things (IoT) anomaly detection approaches that rely on offline training or cloud-assisted analytics, the proposed system performs both model training and inference directly on a resource-constrained microcontroller without external computation or connectivity. The system continuously samples current consumption, computes Root Mean Square (RMS) values on-device, and derives statistical parameters during an initial training phase. Anomalies are detected using lightweight Z-Score thresholds, enabling interpretable and computationally efficient inference suitable for embedded deployment. The architecture was implemented on an STM32-based platform and evaluated using a 14-day dataset collected from a household mini-fridge under normal operation and controlled anomaly conditions. Results demonstrate perfect detection performance, with Precision and Recall of 1.00, inference latencies on the order of tens of microseconds, and a total memory footprint of approximately 3.3 KB SRAM and 63 KB Flash. These results confirm that robust and fully autonomous TinyML anomaly detection can be achieved on low-cost microcontrollers. Future work includes extending the framework to incorporate additional lightweight models and multi-device learning scenarios.

AI.tldr: Autonomous TinyML Z-Score anomaly detection on an STM32 MCU using on-device RMS-derived features; zero-cloud training/inference with perfect detection and tiny footprint.
AI.motivation: Address privacy, latency, and connectivity concerns by performing both training and inference entirely on resource-constrained devices without external computation.
AI.method: On-device RMS of current, initial training to estimate statistical parameters, and Z-Score thresholding for anomaly detection; lightweight, interpretable inference suitable for embedded deployment; implemented on STM32 hardware.
AI.result: Perfect detection (Precision=Recall=1.00); inference latency in tens of microseconds; memory footprint ~3.3 KB SRAM and 63 KB Flash; evaluated on 14-day dataset from a mini-fridge with normal and anomalous conditions.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of fully autonomous TinyML anomaly detection on low-cost MCUs; highlights a compact, efficient approach and suggests extending with additional lightweight models and multi-device learning.

title: PilotBench: A Benchmark for General Aviation Agents with Safety Constraints
comment: Accepted at the 2026 IEEE International Joint Conference on Neural Networks (IJCNN 2026). 6 pages, 7 figures
summary: As Large Language Models (LLMs) advance toward embodied AI agents operating in physical environments, a fundamental question emerges: can models trained on text corpora reliably reason about complex physics while adhering to safety constraints? We address this through PilotBench, a benchmark evaluating LLMs on safety-critical flight trajectory and attitude prediction. Built from 708 real-world general aviation trajectories spanning nine operationally distinct flight phases with synchronized 34-channel telemetry, PilotBench systematically probes the intersection of semantic understanding and physics-governed prediction through comparative analysis of LLMs and traditional forecasters. We introduce Pilot-Score, a composite metric balancing 60% regression accuracy with 40% instruction adherence and safety compliance. Comparative evaluation across 41 models uncovers a Precision-Controllability Dichotomy: traditional forecasters achieve superior MAE of 7.01 but lack semantic reasoning capabilities, while LLMs gain controllability with 86--89% instruction-following at the cost of 11--14 MAE precision. Phase-stratified analysis further exposes a Dynamic Complexity Gap-LLM performance degrades sharply in high-workload phases such as Climb and Approach, suggesting brittle implicit physics models. These empirical discoveries motivate hybrid architectures combining LLMs' symbolic reasoning with specialized forecasters' numerical precision. PilotBench provides a rigorous foundation for advancing embodied AI in safety-constrained domains.

AI.tldr: PilotBench benchmarks LLMs against traditional forecasters for safety-critical flight trajectory and attitude prediction, introducing Pilot-Score (60% regression accuracy, 40% instruction adherence). Findings show a precision-controllability trade-off: forecasters excel in MAE (~7.01) but lack semantic reasoning; LLMs offer higher instruction-following (86–89%) with higher MAE (11–14). Phase-by-phase analysis reveals a Dynamic Complexity Gap where LLMs struggle in high-workload phases, motivating hybrid architectures that combine symbolic reasoning with numeric precision.
AI.motivation: Assess whether LLMs trained on text can reliably reason about physics and safety constraints in embodied AI, specifically for flight trajectory and attitude prediction, and to establish a benchmark for comparing semantic reasoning and physics-based forecasting.
AI.method: Dataset of 708 real-world general aviation trajectories across 9 flight phases with 34 telemetry channels. Evaluation across 41 models (LLMs and traditional forecasters). Introduction of Pilot-Score with weights 60% regression accuracy and 40% instruction adherence/safety. Phase-stratified analysis to examine performance under varying dynamic complexity.
AI.result: Traditional forecasters achieve lower MAE (7.01) but limited semantic reasoning; LLMs show higher instruction-following (86–89%) and higher MAE (11–14). Pilot-Score balances accuracy and safety conformity. LLM performance deteriorates in high-workload phases (e.g., Climb, Approach), revealing brittleness of implicit physics models. The study suggests hybrid architectures to combine symbolic reasoning with numerical precision.
AI.conclusion: PilotBench provides a rigorous, safety-focused foundation for evaluating embodied AI in aviation and advocates hybrid systems that leverage LLMs for semantics and forecasters for precise physics-based predictions.

title: Soft Electroadhesive Feet for Micro Aerial Robots Perching on Smooth and Curved Surfaces
summary: Electroadhesion (EA) provides electrically switchable adhesion and is a promising mechanism for perching micro aerial robots on smooth surfaces. However, practical implementations of soft and stretchable EA pads for aerial perching remain limited. This work presents (i) an efficient workflow for fabricating soft, stretchable electroadhesive pads with sinusoidal wave and concentric-circle electrodes in multiple sizes, (ii) a controlled experimental comparison of normal and shear adhesion under inactive (0 kV) and active (4.8 kV) conditions using an Instron-based setup, and (iii) a perching demonstration using a Crazyflie quadrotor equipped with electroadhesive feet on flat and curved substrates. Experimental results show that shear adhesion dominates, reaching forces on the order of 3 N with partial pad contact, while normal adhesion is comparatively small and strongly dependent on substrate properties. The Crazyflie prototype demonstrates repeatable attachment on smooth plastic surfaces, including curved geometries, as well as rapid detachment when the voltage is removed. These results highlight the potential of soft electroadhesive feet for lightweight and reliable perching in micro aerial vehicles (MAVs).

AI.tldr: Soft, stretchable electroadhesive pads with sinusoidal and concentric-circle electrode patterns enable strong shear adhesion for MAV perching; the study provides a fabrication workflow, a controlled comparison of normal vs shear adhesion under 0 and 4.8 kV, and a perching demonstration on flat and curved surfaces, highlighting shear-dominated adhesion and rapid voltage-triggered detachment.
AI.motivation: Despite the promise of electroadhesion for perching, soft, stretchable EA pads that work reliably on practical MAV surfaces remain underdeveloped. This work aims to (i) provide an efficient fabrication workflow for soft, stretchable EA pads with varied electrode geometries, (ii) quantify the relative contributions of normal and shear adhesion under off/on voltages, and (iii) validate perching on flat and curved substrates using a small MAV.
AI.method: (i) Develop a fabrication workflow for soft, stretchable EA pads incorporating sinusoidal-wave and concentric-circle electrodes across multiple sizes. (ii) Perform controlled adhesion tests on the pads using an Instron-based setup to compare normal and shear adhesion at 0 kV (inactive) and 4.8 kV (active). (iii) Demonstrate perching with a Crazyflie quadrotor equipped with electroadhesive feet on smooth and curved substrates.
AI.result: Shear adhesion dominates, reaching about 3 N under partial pad contact at 4.8 kV; normal adhesion is comparatively small and strongly substrate-dependent. The Crazyflie prototype exhibits repeatable attachment on smooth plastic surfaces, including curved geometries, and rapid detachment when voltage is removed.
AI.conclusion: Soft, stretchable electroadhesive feet show strong potential for lightweight, reliable perching in micro aerial vehicles; future work could focus on reducing actuation voltage, improving contact reliability, and extending performance to a wider range of surface properties.

title: What Matters in Virtual Try-Off? Dual-UNet Diffusion Model For Garment Reconstruction
summary: Virtual Try-On (VTON) has seen rapid advancements, providing a strong foundation for generative fashion tasks. However, the inverse problem, Virtual Try-Off (VTOFF)-aimed at reconstructing the canonical garment from a draped-on image-remains a less understood domain, distinct from the heavily researched field of VTON. In this work, we seek to establish a robust architectural foundation for VTOFF by studying and adapting various diffusion-based strategies from VTON and general Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs). We focus our investigation on the Dual-UNet Diffusion Model architecture and analyze three axes of design: (i) Generation Backbone: comparing Stable Diffusion variants; (ii) Conditioning: ablating different mask designs, masked/unmasked inputs for image conditioning, and the utility of high-level semantic features; and (iii) Losses and Training Strategies: evaluating the impact of the auxiliary attention-based loss, perceptual objectives and multi-stage curriculum schedules. Extensive experiments reveal trade-offs across various configuration options. Evaluated on VITON-HD and DressCode datasets, our framework achieves state-of-the-art performance with a drop of 9.5\% on the primary metric DISTS and competitive performance on LPIPS, FID, KID, and SSIM, providing both stronger baselines and insights to guide future Virtual Try-Off research.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based Dual-UNet framework for Virtual Try-Off (VTOFF) that analyzes generation backbones, conditioning schemes, and training losses; achieves state-of-the-art on VITON-HD and DressCode with ~9.5% DISTS improvement and competitive LPIPS/FID/KID/SSIM.
AI.motivation: To address the underexplored inverse problem of Virtual Try-On (VTON) by reconstructing canonical garments from draped-on images, using diffusion-based strategies from VTON and latent diffusion models to establish a robust VTOFF architecture.
AI.method: Adopts a Dual-UNet Diffusion Model and conducts systematic ablations along three axes: (1) Generation Backbone (Stable Diffusion variants); (2) Conditioning (mask designs, masked/unmasked inputs, and high-level semantic features); (3) Losses and Training (auxiliary attention-based loss, perceptual objectives, and multi-stage curriculum schedules). Extensive experiments with ablations on VITON-HD and DressCode.
AI.result: The approach reveals trade-offs across configurations and achieves state-of-the-art performance on VITON-HD and DressCode, with a 9.5% relative improvement in the primary metric DISTS and competitive results on LPIPS, FID, KID, and SSIM.
AI.conclusion: Provides stronger baselines and actionable design insights to guide future VTOFF research; diffusion-based strategies with careful conditioning and curriculum training yield robust VTOFF performance.

title: Multivariate Time Series Anomaly Detection via Dual-Branch Reconstruction and Autoregressive Flow-based Residual Density Estimation
summary: Multivariate Time Series Anomaly Detection (MTSAD) is critical for real-world monitoring scenarios such as industrial control and aerospace systems. Mainstream reconstruction-based anomaly detection methods suffer from two key limitations: first, overfitting to spurious correlations induced by an overemphasis on cross-variable modeling; second, the generation of misleading anomaly scores by simply summing up multivariable reconstruction errors, which makes it difficult to distinguish between hard-to-reconstruct samples and genuine anomalies. To address these issues, we propose DBR-AF, a novel framework that integrates a dual-branch reconstruction (DBR) encoder and an autoregressive flow (AF) module. The DBR encoder decouples cross-variable correlation learning and intra-variable statistical property modeling to mitigate spurious correlations, while the AF module employs multiple stacked reversible transformations to model the complex multivariate residual distribution and further leverages density estimation to accurately identify normal samples with large reconstruction errors. Extensive experiments on seven benchmark datasets demonstrate that DBR-AF achieves state-of-the-art performance, with ablation studies validating the indispensability of its core components.

AI.tldr: DBR-AF advances multivariate time series anomaly detection by decoupling cross-variable correlations from intra-variable statistics through a dual-branch reconstruction encoder, and by modeling the residual distribution with an autoregressive flow for improved anomaly scoring.
AI.motivation: Reconstruction-based MTSAD methods often overfit to spurious cross-variable correlations and rely on simple aggregate reconstruction errors, making it hard to distinguish hard-to-reconstruct normals from real anomalies.
AI.method: A dual-branch reconstruction (DBR) encoder separates cross-variable correlation learning from intra-variable statistical property modeling. An autoregressive flow (AF) with multiple stacked reversible transformations models the joint residual distribution and enables density-based anomaly scoring, improving discrimination for large reconstruction errors.
AI.result: Reported state-of-the-art performance on seven benchmark datasets; ablation studies confirm that the core components (DBR encoder and AF module) are indispensable for performance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed DBR-AF framework effectively mitigates overfitting to spurious correlations and improves anomaly detection by combining decoupled reconstruction with flow-based density estimation, yielding more reliable anomaly scores.

title: SEA-Eval: A Benchmark for Evaluating Self-Evolving Agents Beyond Episodic Assessment
summary: Current LLM-based agents demonstrate strong performance in episodic task execution but remain constrained by static toolsets and episodic amnesia, failing to accumulate experience or optimize strategies across task boundaries. While the Self-Evolving Agent (SEA) paradigm has been previously proposed, this paper contributes a new formal definition of SEA grounded in digital embodiment and continuous cross-task evolution, and introduces SEA-Eval, the first benchmark designed to evaluate SEA characteristics across two dimensions, intra-task execution reliability and long-term evolutionary performance. By organizing tasks into sequential streams and analyzing Success Rate and Token Consumption over time, SEA-Eval quantifies evolutionary gain and structural stability in ways that existing episodic benchmarks cannot. Empirical evaluations reveal a significant evolutionary bottleneck in current state-of-the-art frameworks, where identical success rates mask up to 31.2 times differences in token consumption and divergent evolutionary trajectories under sequential analysis. SEA-Eval provides a rigorous scientific foundation for advancing agents from mere task executors toward genuinely self-evolving digital entities.

AI.tldr: Proposes SEA-Eval, a benchmark to assess Self-Evolving Agents (SEA) across intra-task reliability and long-term evolutionary performance, addressing episodic amnesia and static toolsets in LLM agents.; demonstrates substantial evolutionary bottlenecks where identical success rates mask large token-efficiency differences (up to 31.2x) and divergent evolutionary trajectories.
AI.motivation: LLM-driven agents typically operate episodically with fixed toolkits and suffer from memory and cross-task generalization limits. The work formalizes SEA and introduces a benchmark to evaluate genuine cross-task evolution and digital embodiment.
AI.method: Formally define SEA in a digital-embodiment, continuous-evolution framework. Design SEA-Eval with sequential task streams and metrics (Success Rate, Token Consumption) to quantify evolutionary gain and structural stability over time.
AI.result: Empirical results reveal a major evolutionary bottleneck: the same success rate can correspond to very different token consumption and divergent long-term evolutionary paths across tasks.
AI.conclusion: SEA-Eval provides a rigorous framework for advancing agents from task executors to self-evolving digital entities and highlights the need to measure cross-task evolution beyond episodic performance.

title: Characterizing Lidar Range-Measurement Ambiguity due to Multiple Returns
comment: Proceedings of the 38th International Technical Meeting of the Satellite Division of The Institute of Navigation (ION GNSS+ 2025), Baltimore, Maryland, September 2025, pp. 1949-1963
summary: Reliable position and attitude sensing is critical for highly automated vehicles that operate on conventional roadways. Lidar sensors are increasingly incorporated into pose-estimation systems. Despite its great utility, lidar is a complex sensor, and its performance in roadway environments is not yet well understood. For instance, it is often assumed in lidar-localization algorithms that a lidar will always identify a unique surface along a given raypath. However, this assumption is not always true, as ample prior evidence exists to suggest that lidar units may generate measurements probabilistically when more than one scattering surface appears within the lidar's conical beam. In this paper, we analyze lidar datasets to characterize cases with probabilistic returns along particular raypaths. Our contribution is to present representative cumulative distribution functions (CDFs) for raypaths observed by two different mechanically rotating lidar units with stationary bases. In subsequent discussion, we outline a qualitative methodology to assess the effect of probabilistic multi-return cases on lidar-based localization.

AI.tldr: Presents empirical CDFs for probabilistic multi-return raypaths from two rotating lidars and a qualitative framework to assess their impact on lidar-based localization.
AI.motivation: Lidar pose estimation often assumes a unique surface along a ray; multiple surfaces in the conical beam yield probabilistic returns, potentially degrading localization accuracy in roadway environments.
AI.method: Analyze datasets from two mechanically rotating lidars with stationary bases; extract raypath returns; compute representative CDFs of returns; outline a qualitative methodology to evaluate the effect of probabilistic multi-returns on localization.
AI.result: Represents CDFs for raypaths observed by the two lidars and provides a qualitative methodology to assess how probabilistic multi-returns could affect lidar-based localization.
AI.conclusion: Offers empirical characterization of probabilistic returns and a framework to incorporate such behavior into localization analyses, highlighting the need for methods that handle multi-return scenarios in lidar SLAM/pose estimation.

title: Accelerating Transformer-Based Monocular SLAM via Geometric Utility Scoring
summary: Geometric Foundation Models (GFMs) have recently advanced monocular SLAM by providing robust, calibration-free 3D priors. However, deploying these models on dense video streams introduces significant computational redundancy. Current GFM-based SLAM systems typically rely on post hoc keyframe selection. Because of this, they must perform expensive dense geometric decoding simply to determine whether a frame contains novel geometry, resulting in late rejection and wasted computation. To mitigate this inefficiency, we propose LeanGate, a lightweight feed-forward frame-gating network. LeanGate predicts a geometric utility score to assess a frame's mapping value prior to the heavy GFM feature extraction and matching stages. As a predictive plug-and-play module, our approach bypasses over 90% of redundant frames. Evaluations on standard SLAM benchmarks demonstrate that LeanGate reduces tracking FLOPs by more than 85% and achieves a 5x end-to-end throughput speedup. Furthermore, it maintains the tracking and mapping accuracy of dense baselines.

AI.tldr: LeanGate is a lightweight, plug-and-play frame-gating network that pre-emptively predicts a frame's geometric utility to skip redundant frames before heavy GFM processing, achieving large computational savings without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.motivation: Geometric Foundation Models (GFMs) improve monocular SLAM but add heavy computation on dense video streams. Current workflows rely on post hoc keyframe selection and expensive dense decoding to decide if a frame contains novel geometry, leading to wasted computation.
AI.method: Introduce LeanGate, a lightweight feed-forward network that outputs a geometric utility score indicating a frame's mapping value. If the score is low, the frame is skipped prior to GFM feature extraction and matching, effectively gating frames in a predictive, plug-and-play manner.
AI.result: On standard SLAM benchmarks, LeanGate reduces tracking FLOPs by over 85% and delivers about a 5× increase in end-to-end throughput, while preserving the tracking and mapping accuracy of dense baselines.
AI.conclusion: LeanGate significantly improves the practicality of GFM-based SLAM for dense video streams by dramatically reducing redundant computation with negligible accuracy loss, enabling real-time deployment and suggesting broader applicability of predictive gating in SLAM pipelines.

title: CSAttention: Centroid-Scoring Attention for Accelerating LLM Inference
summary: Long-context LLMs increasingly rely on extended, reusable prefill prompts for agents and domain Q&A, pushing attention and KV-cache to become the dominant decode-time bottlenecks. While sparse attention reduces computation and transfer costs, it often struggles to maintain accuracy at high sparsity levels due to the inherent distribution shift between Queries and Keys. We propose Centroid-Scoring Attention (CSAttention), a training-free sparse attention method optimized for high-throughput serving of reusable contexts. CSAttention adopts a storage-for-computation strategy tailored to the offline-prefill/online-decode setting: it front-loads computation into a one-time offline prefill phase that can be amortized across multiple queries, while aggressively optimizing per-step decoding latency. Specifically, CSAttention constructs query-centric lookup tables during offline prefill, whose size remains fixed during decoding, and enables online decoding to replace full-context scans with efficient table lookups and GPU-friendly score accumulation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CSAttention achieves near-identical accuracy to full attention. Under high sparsity (95%) and long-context settings (32K-128K), CSAttention consistently outperforms state-of-the-art sparse attention methods in both model accuracy and inference speed, achieving up to 4.6x inference speedup over the most accurate baseline at a context length of 128K.

AI.tldr: CSAttention is a training-free sparse attention method with offline prefill to accelerate long-context decoding; it preserves accuracy similar to full attention and achieves up to 4.6x speed at 95% sparsity on 32K–128K contexts.
AI.motivation: Decode-time bottlenecks in long-context LLMs arise from attention over extended contexts; sparse attention helps but suffers from Q–K distribution shift and accuracy loss at high sparsity. A storage-for-computation approach aims to amortize computation by offline prefill.
AI.method: Offline prefill constructs query-centric lookup tables with fixed size that facilitate online decoding via table lookups and efficient score accumulation, replacing full-context scans. The method is training-free and optimized for the offline-prefill/online-decode setting, enabling amortized computations across queries.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show near-identical accuracy to full attention. At high sparsity (95%) and long-contexts (32K-128K), CSAttention outperforms state-of-the-art sparse methods in both accuracy and speed, achieving up to 4.6x inference speedup over the most accurate baseline at context length 128K.
AI.conclusion: CSAttention provides a practical, training-free sparse attention mechanism tailored for reusable-context serving, delivering strong accuracy/throughput tradeoffs and enabling efficient long-context decoding with substantial speedups.

title: Hypergraph Neural Networks Accelerate MUS Enumeration
summary: Enumerating Minimal Unsatisfiable Subsets (MUSes) is a fundamental task in constraint satisfaction problems (CSPs). Its major challenge is the exponential growth of the search space, which becomes particularly severe when satisfiability checks are expensive. Recent machine learning approaches reduce this cost for Boolean satisfiability problems but rely on explicit variable-constraint relationships, limiting their application domains. This paper proposes a domain-agnostic method to accelerate MUS enumeration using Hypergraph Neural Networks (HGNNs). The proposed method incrementally builds a hypergraph with constraints as vertices and MUSes enumerated until the current step as hyperedges, and employs an HGNN-based agent trained via reinforcement learning to minimize the number of satisfiability checks required to obtain an MUS. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach in accelerating MUS enumeration, showing that our method can enumerate more MUSes within the same satisfiability check budget compared to conventional methods.

AI.tldr: A domain-agnostic framework using Hypergraph Neural Networks (HGNNs) and reinforcement learning to accelerate Minimal Unsatisfiable Subsets (MUS) enumeration by incrementally building a constraint hypergraph and guiding SAT checks, achieving more MUSes within the same compute budget than baseline methods.
AI.motivation: MUS enumeration is NP-hard and grows exponentially; SAT checks are expensive, and existing ML approaches for SAT rely on explicit variable-constraint relations, limiting domain applicability. A domain-agnostic, learning-driven approach could generalize to CSPs and reduce SAT inference costs.
AI.method: Constructs a hypergraph incrementally with constraints as vertices; MUSes found so far form hyperedges. Trains an HGNN-based agent via reinforcement learning to decide actions that minimize the number of SAT calls needed to discover a new MUS, thereby accelerating enumeration across diverse CSP domains.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows the HGNN-RL method enumerates more MUSes within a fixed SAT-check budget than conventional methods, demonstrating effectiveness and improved efficiency over baselines.
AI.conclusion: HGNN-based reinforcement learning provides a domain-agnostic, scalable approach to accelerate MUS enumeration by reducing expensive SAT checks, with promising generalization to various CSP settings and potential for further optimization.

title: A Benchmark of Dexterity for Anthropomorphic Robotic Hands
summary: Dexterity is a central yet ambiguously defined concept in the design and evaluation of anthropomorphic robotic hands.
