In practice, the term is often used inconsistently, with different systems evaluated under disparate criteria, making meaningful comparisons across designs difficult. This highlights the need for a unified, performance-based definition of dexterity grounded in measurable outcomes rather than proxy metrics. In this work, we introduce POMDAR, a comprehensive dexterity benchmark that formalizes dexterity as task performance across a structured set of manipulation and grasping motions. The benchmark was systematically derived from established taxonomies in human motor control. It is implemented in both real-world and simulation and includes four manipulation configurations: vertical and horizontal configurations, continuous rotation, and pure grasping. The task designs contain mechanical scaffolding to constrain task motion, suppress compensatory strategies, and enable metrics to be measured unambiguously. We define a quantitative scoring metric combining task correctness and execution speed, effectively measuring dexterity as throughput. This enables objective, reproducible, and interpretable evaluation across different hand designs. POMDAR provides an open-source, standardized, and taxonomy-grounded benchmark for consistent comparison and evaluation of anthropomorphic robot hands to facilitate a systematic advancement of dexterous manipulation platforms. CAD, simulation files, and evaluation videos are publicly available at https://srl-ethz.github.io/POMDAR/.

AI.tldr: Introduces POMDAR, a unified dexterity benchmark for anthropomorphic robot hands, with four configurations, measured by throughput, implemented both in real and simulated settings, and released as open-source.
AI.motivation: Dexterity is ambiguously defined and evaluated using disparate criteria, hindering cross-design comparisons; a unified, measurable, performance-based benchmark is needed, grounded in human motor control taxonomy.
AI.method: Derives manipulation tasks from established human motor control taxonomies; includes four configurations (vertical, horizontal, continuous rotation, pure grasping); uses mechanical scaffolding to constrain motion and enable unambiguous metric measurement; defines a throughput-based score combining task correctness and execution speed; implemented in both real-world and simulation; provides CAD, simulation files, and evaluation videos.
AI.result: Provides an objective, reproducible, and interpretable framework for comparing different hand designs across platforms; enables standardized evaluation of dexterous manipulation performance.
AI.conclusion: POMDAR offers an open-source, taxonomy-grounded benchmark to systematically advance dexterous manipulation, with resources available at the project site.

title: LMGenDrive: Bridging Multimodal Understanding and Generative World Modeling for End-to-End Driving
summary: Recent years have seen remarkable progress in autonomous driving, yet generalization to long-tail and open-world scenarios remains a major bottleneck for large-scale deployment. To address this challenge, some works use LLMs and VLMs for vision-language understanding and reasoning, enabling vehicles to interpret rare and safety-critical situations when generating actions. Others study generative world models to capture the spatio-temporal evolution of driving scenes, allowing agents to imagine possible futures before acting. Inspired by human intelligence, which unifies understanding and imagination, we explore a unified model for autonomous driving. We present LMGenDrive, the first framework that combines LLM-based multimodal understanding with generative world models for end-to-end closed-loop driving. Given multi-view camera inputs and natural-language instructions, LMGenDrive generates both future driving videos and control signals. This design provides complementary benefits: video prediction improves spatio-temporal scene modeling, while the LLM contributes strong semantic priors and instruction grounding from large-scale pretraining. We further propose a progressive three-stage training strategy, from vision pretraining to multi-step long-horizon driving, to improve stability and performance. LMGenDrive supports both low-latency online planning and autoregressive offline video generation. Experiments show that it significantly outperforms prior methods on challenging closed-loop benchmarks, with clear gains in instruction following, spatio-temporal understanding, and robustness to rare scenarios. These results suggest that unifying multimodal understanding and generation is a promising direction for more generalizable and robust embodied decision-making systems.

AI.tldr: LMGenDrive unifies LLM-based multimodal understanding with generative world models to enable end-to-end, closed-loop autonomous driving, predicting future driving videos and control signals from multi-view inputs and instructions, with a progressive training strategy and improved performance in long-horizon, rare-scenario settings.
AI.motivation: To overcome long-tail and open-world generalization bottlenecks in autonomous driving by combining semantic reasoning with imaginative scene modeling, leveraging large-scale pretraining to ground actions in both understanding and imagined futures.
AI.method: Introduce LMGenDrive, a framework that fuses LLM-driven multimodal understanding with generative world models. It processes multi-view camera inputs and natural-language instructions to produce future driving videos and control commands. A three-stage progressive training pipeline—vision pretraining, multi-step long-horizon driving, and end-to-end fine-tuning—stabilizes training and enhances long-horizon performance. Supports both low-latency online planning and autoregressive offline video generation.
AI.result: Empirical results show substantial improvements over prior methods on challenging closed-loop benchmarks, with notable gains in instruction following, spatio-temporal understanding, and robustness to rare scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Unifying multimodal understanding and generation is a promising direction for more generalizable and robust embodied decision-making systems, and LMGenDrive demonstrates the feasibility and benefits of this integrated approach for autonomous driving.

title: FluidFlow: a flow-matching generative model for fluid dynamics surrogates on unstructured meshes
summary: Computational fluid dynamics (CFD) provides high-fidelity simulations of fluid flows but remains computationally expensive for many-query applications. In recent years deep learning (DL) has been used to construct data-driven fluid-dynamic surrogate models. In this work we consider a different learning paradigm and embrace generative modelling as a framework for constructing scalable fluid-dynamics surrogate models. We introduce FluidFlow, a generative model based on conditional flow-matching, a recent alternative to diffusion models that learns deterministic transport maps between noise and data distributions. FluidFlow is specifically designed to operate directly on CFD data defined on both structured and unstructured meshes alike, without the needs to perform any mesh interpolation pre-processing and preserving geometric fidelity. We assess the capabilities of FluidFlow using two different core neural network architectures, a U-Net and diffusion transformer (DiT), and condition their learning on physically meaningful parameters. The methodology is validated on two benchmark problems of increasing complexity: prediction of pressure coefficients along an airfoil boundary across different operating conditions, and prediction of pressure and friction coefficients over a full three-dimensional aircraft geometry discretized on a large unstructured mesh. In both cases, FluidFlow outperform strong multilayer perceptron baselines, achieving significantly lower error metrics and improved generalisation across operating conditions. Notably, the transformer-based architecture enables scalable learning on large unstructured datasets while maintaining high predictive accuracy. These results demonstrate that flow-matching generative models provide an effective and flexible framework for surrogate modelling in fluid dynamics, with potential for realistic engineering and scientific applications.

AI.tldr: FluidFlow introduces a flow-matching generative surrogate for CFD data on both structured and unstructured meshes, using U-Net and Diffusion Transformer architectures, conditioning on physical parameters. It claims superior accuracy to MLP baselines and scalable learning on large unstructured meshes, validated on airfoil and full aircraft geometries.
AI.motivation: Reduce computational cost of CFD for many-query problems by leveraging generative models that preserve geometric fidelity and operate directly on CFD meshes, avoiding mesh interpolation.
AI.method: Propose FluidFlow, a conditional flow-matching generative model. It learns deterministic transport maps from noise to CFD data, handling both structured and unstructured meshes without interpolation. Two network architectures (U-Net and Diffusion Transformer) are evaluated, with conditioning on physical operating parameters. Training and evaluation on two benchmarks: 2D airfoil pressure along boundary across operating conditions, and 3D aircraft geometry on a large unstructured mesh. Baseline comparisons against multilayer perceptrons (MLPs).
AI.result: FluidFlow outperforms strong MLP baselines in both benchmarks, with significantly lower error metrics and better generalization across operating conditions. The transformer-based architecture enables scalable learning on large unstructured datasets while maintaining high predictive accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Flow-matching generative models constitute an effective and flexible framework for CFD surrogate modelling, capable of handling meshes directly and supporting scalable, accurate predictions across varying operating conditions; promising for engineering and scientific applications.

title: Advantage-Guided Diffusion for Model-Based Reinforcement Learning
summary: Model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) with autoregressive world models suffers from compounding errors, whereas diffusion world models mitigate this by generating trajectory segments jointly. However, existing diffusion guides are either policy-only, discarding value information, or reward-based, which becomes myopic when the diffusion horizon is short. We introduce Advantage-Guided Diffusion for MBRL (AGD-MBRL), which steers the reverse diffusion process using the agent's advantage estimates so that sampling concentrates on trajectories expected to yield higher long-term return beyond the generated window. We develop two guides: (i) Sigmoid Advantage Guidance (SAG) and (ii) Exponential Advantage Guidance (EAG). We prove that a diffusion model guided through SAG or EAG allows us to perform reweighted sampling of trajectories with weights increasing in state-action advantage-implying policy improvement under standard assumptions. Additionally, we show that the trajectories generated from AGD-MBRL follow an improved policy (that is, with higher value) compared to an unguided diffusion model. AGD integrates seamlessly with PolyGRAD-style architectures by guiding the state components while leaving action generation policy-conditioned, and requires no change to the diffusion training objective. On MuJoCo control tasks (HalfCheetah, Hopper, Walker2D and Reacher), AGD-MBRL improves sample efficiency and final return over PolyGRAD, an online Diffuser-style reward guide, and model-free baselines (PPO/TRPO), in some cases by a margin of 2x. These results show that advantage-aware guidance is a simple, effective remedy for short-horizon myopia in diffusion-model MBRL.

AI.tldr: Advantage-Guided Diffusion for MBRL (AGD-MBRL) uses Sigmoid and Exponential Advantage Guidance to steer diffusion-based world models via long-horizon value information, improving policy value and sample efficiency on MuJoCo tasks without altering the diffusion objective.
AI.motivation: MBRL with autoregressive diffusion suffers from compounding errors or short-horizon myopia. Policy-only or reward-based diffusion guides fail to leverage value information over longer horizons. A mechanism to inject advantage estimates into diffusion sampling could align trajectory generation with long-term returns.
AI.method: Introduce two guides—Sigmoid Advantage Guidance (SAG) and Exponential Advantage Guidance (EAG)—that reweight diffusion-sampled trajectories by state-action advantage estimates. Prove that reweighted sampling increases weights with advantage, implying policy improvement under standard assumptions. AGD integrates with PolyGRAD-style architectures by guiding state components while leaving action generation policy-conditioned and does not modify the diffusion training objective.
AI.result: Theoretical results: guided diffusion trajectories yield higher-value policies than unguided ones; sampling weights correlate with state-action advantage, enabling policy improvement guarantees under common assumptions. Empirical results on MuJoCo tasks (HalfCheetah, Hopper, Walker2D, Reacher) show improved sample efficiency and final returns over PolyGRAD, online Diffuser reward guides, and model-free baselines (PPO/TRPO), sometimes by up to 2x.
AI.conclusion: Advantage-aware guidance effectively mitigates short-horizon myopia in diffusion-based MBRL, offering a simple, effective augmentation that preserves diffusion training objectives and integrates with existing architectures.

title: Online Intention Prediction via Control-Informed Learning
summary: This paper presents an online intention prediction framework for estimating the goal state of autonomous systems in real time, even when intention is time-varying, and system dynamics or objectives include unknown parameters. The problem is formulated as an inverse optimal control / inverse reinforcement learning task, with the intention treated as a parameter in the objective. A shifting horizon strategy discounts outdated information, while online control-informed learning enables efficient gradient computation and online parameter updates. Simulations under varying noise levels and hardware experiments on a quadrotor drone demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves accurate, adaptive intention prediction in complex environments.

AI.tldr: An online, real-time intention prediction framework for autonomous systems using inverse optimal control/inverse reinforcement learning, with shifting horizon and online gradient updates, validated on simulations and quadrotor hardware.
AI.motivation: Estimate time-varying goals of autonomous agents under unknown dynamics/parameters to enable proactive and safe autonomous decision making in dynamic environments.
AI.method: Model intention as a parameter in an IOC/IRL objective. Employ a shifting horizon to discount outdated data and online control-informed learning to compute gradients efficiently for real-time parameter updates. Validate via simulations with noise and hardware experiments on a quadrotor.
AI.result: Demonstrates accurate, adaptive intention prediction in complex environments with real-time update capability and robustness to noise, supported by both simulations and real hardware tests.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables real-time, adaptive intention estimation under time-varying goals and unknown dynamics, showing practical potential; effectiveness hinges on appropriate horizon tuning and gradient computation efficiency, with room for broader validation and extension.

title: AI Driven Soccer Analysis Using Computer Vision
summary: Sport analysis is crucial for team performance since it provides actionable data that can inform coaching decisions, improve player performance, and enhance team strategies. To analyze more complex features from game footage, a computer vision model can be used to identify and track key entities from the field. We propose the use of an object detection and tracking system to predict player positioning throughout the game. To translate this to positioning in relation to the field dimensions, we use a point prediction model to identify key points on the field and combine these with known field dimensions to extract actual distances. For the player-identification model, object detection models like YOLO and Faster R-CNN are evaluated on the accuracy of our custom video footage using multiple different evaluation metrics. The goal is to identify the best model for object identification to obtain the most accurate results when paired with SAM2 (Segment Anything Model 2) for segmentation and tracking. For the key point detection model, we use a CNN model to find consistent locations in the soccer field. Through homography, the positions of points and objects in the camera perspective will be transformed to a real-ground perspective. The segmented player masks from SAM2 are transformed from camera perspective to real-world field coordinates through homography, regardless of camera angle or movement. The transformed real-world coordinates can be used to calculate valuable tactical insights including player speed, distance covered, positioning heatmaps, and more complex team statistics, providing coaches and players with actionable performance data previously unavailable from standard video analysis.

AI.tldr: A computer vision pipeline for soccer analytics that detects and tracks players, predicts key field points, and maps camera coordinates to real-world field coordinates using homography, with SAM2 for segmentation to derive tactical metrics.
AI.motivation: To derive actionable, geometry-aware performance metrics from game footage and overcome perspective limitations in standard video analysis, by identifying an optimal object detector and integrating segmentation with real-world coordinate mapping.
AI.method: Evaluate object detectors (YOLO and Faster R-CNN) on custom soccer footage for identification; use a CNN-based key-point predictor to identify consistent field locations; apply homography to convert camera views to real-field coordinates; use SAM2 for player segmentation and transform masks to field coordinates; compute metrics such as speed, distance, and heatmaps.
AI.result: No empirical results are reported in the abstract; the work aims to identify the best object-detection model to pair with SAM2 and to demonstrate feasibility of the end-to-end pipeline for tactical analytics.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework can produce real-world coordinate mappings and rich tactical metrics, potentially enabling analytics not available from standard video analysis, but requires empirical validation and careful handling of calibration, camera motion, and occlusions.

title: Act or Escalate? Evaluating Escalation Behavior in Automation with Language Models
summary: Effective automation hinges on deciding when to act and when to escalate. We model this as a decision under uncertainty: an LLM forms a prediction, estimates its probability of being correct, and compares the expected costs of acting and escalating. Using this framework across five domains of recorded human decisions-demand forecasting, content recommendation, content moderation, loan approval, and autonomous driving-and across multiple model families, we find marked differences in the implicit thresholds models use to trade off these costs. These thresholds vary substantially and are not predicted by architecture or scale, while self-estimates are miscalibrated in model-specific ways. We then test interventions that target this decision process by varying cost ratios, providing accuracy signals, and training models to follow the desired escalation rule. Prompting helps mainly for reasoning models. SFT on chain-of-thought targets yields the most robust policies, which generalize across datasets, cost ratios, prompt framings, and held-out domains. These results suggest that escalation behavior is a model-specific property that should be characterized before deployment, and that robust alignment benefits from training models to reason explicitly about uncertainty and decision costs.

AI.tldr: Formalizes escalation as a decision under uncertainty for LLMs, showing model-specific thresholds for acting vs. escalating across five decision domains; thresholds do not track architecture/scale and self-estimates are miscalibrated; interventions (cost ratios, accuracy signals, training to follow escalation rules) reveal prompting helps for reasoning models and that SFT with chain-of-thought yields the most robust, generalizable policies across datasets and held-out domains.
AI.motivation: To understand and improve when LLMs should act, abstain, or escalate in high-stakes decisions by analyzing how models estimate uncertainty, calibrate their own predictions, and trade off action costs against escalation costs.
AI.method: Empirical study across five domains (demand forecasting, content recommendation, content moderation, loan approval, autonomous driving) and multiple model families. Measure implicit decision thresholds for acting vs. escalating, examine self-estimation calibration, and test interventions: varying cost ratios, providing accuracy signals, and training models to follow an escalation rule. Compare prompting strategies and SFT with chain-of-thought prompting.
AI.result: Observed substantial variability in escalation thresholds across models; not predicted by architecture or scale. Self-estimates are miscalibrated in model-specific ways. Prompting improves reasoning models; SFT on chain-of-thought targets yields the most robust escalation policies that generalize across datasets, cost ratios, prompt framings, and held-out domains.
AI.conclusion: Escalation behavior is a model-specific property requiring characterization before deployment. Robust alignment benefits from training models to explicitly reason about uncertainty and decision costs (e.g., SFT with chain-of-thought).

title: Overhang Tower: Resource-Rational Adaptation in Sequential Physical Planning
summary: Humans effortlessly navigate the physical world by predicting how objects behave under gravity and contact forces, yet how such judgments support sequential physical planning under resource constraints remains poorly understood. Research on intuitive physics debates whether prediction relies on the Intuitive Physics Engine (IPE) or fast, cue-based heuristics; separately, decision-making research debates deliberative lookahead versus myopic strategies. These debates have proceeded in isolation, leaving the cognitive architecture of sequential physical planning underspecified. How physical prediction mechanisms and planning strategies jointly adapt under limited cognitive resources remains an open question. Here we show that humans exhibit a dual transition under resource pressure, simultaneously shifting both physical prediction mechanism and planning strategy to match cognitive budget. Using Overhang Tower, a construction task requiring participants to maximize horizontal overhang while maintaining stability, we find that IPE-based simulation dominates early stages while CNN-based visual heuristics prevail as complexity grows; concurrently, time pressure truncates deliberative lookahead, shifting planning toward shallower horizons: a dual transition unpredicted by prior single-mechanism accounts. These findings reveal a hierarchical, resource-rational architecture that flexibly trades computational cost against predictive fidelity. Our results unify two long-standing debates (simulation vs. heuristics and myopic vs. deliberative planning) as a dynamic repertoire reconfigured by cognitive budget.

AI.tldr: Under cognitive resource constraints, humans adapt both predictive mechanism and planning horizon during sequential physical tasks: early reliance on intuitive physics engine (IPE) simulation shifts to CNN-based heuristics as complexity grows, while time pressure truncates deliberative lookahead to shallower horizons. This supports a resource-rational, hierarchical architecture that trades computation cost for predictive fidelity and unifies simulation/heuristics with myopic/deliberative planning debates.
AI.motivation: Clarify how physical prediction and planning adapt under limited cognitive resources and reconcile two long-standing debates: (i) intuitive physics engines vs. fast heuristics, and (ii) deliberative lookahead vs. myopic planning.
AI.method: Experimental task using Overhang Tower: participants aim to maximize horizontal overhang while maintaining stability. Manipulations include varying task complexity and imposing time pressure to observe shifts in predictive strategy (IPE vs CNN heuristics) and planning horizon (deliberative vs shallow). Behavioral signatures and performance metrics are used to infer underlying mechanisms and planning depth.
AI.result: A dual transition emerged: (1) prediction mechanism shifts from IPE-based simulation to CNN-based visual heuristics as complexity increases; (2) planning shifts from deliberative lookahead to shallower horizons under time pressure. These transitions occur concurrently, revealing a hierarchical, resource-rational adaptation rather than a single-mechanism account.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a resource-rational, hierarchical architecture that flexibly allocates computational resources to maintain predictive accuracy within budget. Reconciles simulation vs. heuristics and myopic vs. deliberative planning as a dynamic repertoire reconfigured by cognitive constraints.

title: Robust Adaptive Backstepping Impedance Control of Robots in Unknown Environments
summary: This paper presents a Robust Adaptive Backstepping Impedance Control (RABIC) strategy for robots operating in contact-rich and uncertain environments. The proposed control strategy considers the complete coupled dynamics of the system and explicitly accounts for key sources of uncertainty, including external disturbances and unmodeled dynamics, while not requiring the robot's dynamic parameters in implementation. We propose a backstepping-based adaptive impedance control scheme for the inner loop to track the reference impedance model. To handle uncertainties, we employ a Taylor series-based estimator for system dynamics and an adaptive estimator for determining the upper bound of external forces. Stability analysis demonstrates the semi-global practical finite-time stability of the overall system. To demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method, a simulated mobile manipulator scenario and experimental evaluations on a real Franka Emika Panda robot were conducted. The proposed approach exhibits safer performance compared to PD control while ensuring trajectory tracking and force monitoring. Overall, the RABIC framework provides a solid basis for future research on adaptive and learning-based impedance control for coupled mobile and fixed serially linked manipulators.

AI.tldr: Robust Adaptive Backstepping Impedance Control (RABIC) for contact-rich robots, combining backstepping, Taylor-series dynamics estimation, and adaptive force upper-bound estimation; achieves semi-global practical finite-time stability without exact dynamics; validated on a Panda robot, outperforming PD in safety while preserving tracking and force monitoring.
AI.motivation: Address instability and safety concerns in impedance control for uncertain, contact-rich environments where full dynamic parameters are unavailable or impractical to obtain.
AI.method: Develop a backstepping-based adaptive impedance control for the inner loop that tracks a reference impedance model. Use a Taylor-series-based estimator for system dynamics and an adaptive estimator to bound external forces. Prove semi-global practical finite-time stability. Validate via simulation on a mobile manipulator and experiments on a Franka Emika Panda robot.
AI.result: Demonstrates safer performance than PD control while achieving trajectory tracking and force monitoring; confirms effectiveness of the RABIC framework for coupled mobile and fixed manipulators.
AI.conclusion: RABIC provides a solid foundation for future adaptive and learning-based impedance control in coupled robotic systems, with robustness to uncertainties and without requiring exact dynamic parameters in implementation.

title: LPLCv2: An Expanded Dataset for Fine-Grained License Plate Legibility Classification
summary: Modern Automatic License Plate Recognition (ALPR) systems achieve outstanding performance in controlled, well-defined scenarios. However, large-scale real-world usage remains challenging due to low-quality imaging devices, compression artifacts, and suboptimal camera installation. Identifying illegible license plates (LPs) has recently become feasible through a dedicated benchmark; however, its impact has been limited by its small size and annotation errors. In this work, we expand the original benchmark to over three times the size with two extra capture days, revise its annotations and introduce novel labels. LP-level annotations include bounding boxes, text, and legibility level, while vehicle-level annotations comprise make, model, type, and color. Image-level annotations feature camera identity, capture conditions (e.g., rain and faulty cameras), acquisition time, and day ID. We present a novel training procedure featuring an Exponential Moving Average-based loss function and a refined learning rate scheduler, addressing common mistakes in testing. These improvements enable a baseline model to achieve an 89.5% F1-score on the test set, considerably surpassing the previous state of the art. We further introduce a novel protocol to explicitly addresses camera contamination between training and evaluation splits, where results show a small impact. Dataset and code are publicly available at https://github.com/lmlwojcik/LPLCv2-Dataset.

AI.tldr: Expanded LPLCv2 benchmark for illegible license plates with richer annotations and a novel EMA-based training scheme, achieving state-of-the-art 89.5% F1 and a camera-contamination protocol; dataset and code released.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of prior ALPR illegible-plate benchmarks: small size, annotation errors, and real-world imaging challenges (low quality, compression, and camera setup).
AI.method: Extend the dataset to >3x size with extra capture days, revise and add LP- and vehicle-level/image-level annotations, and introduce EMA-based loss with refined learning-rate schedule. Propose a protocol to measure camera contamination between training and evaluation splits to assess robustness.
AI.result: Baseline model attains 89.5% F1 on the test set, substantially surpassing prior state of the art; the contamination protocol shows only a small impact on results.
AI.conclusion: Public release of dataset and code enables robust evaluation and further research into real-world ALPR under challenging conditions.

title: EngageTriBoost: Predictive Modeling of User Engagement in Digital Mental Health Intervention Using Explainable Machine Learning
summary: Mental health challenges among young adults, are on the rise, necessitating effective solutions such as digital mental health interventions (DMHIs). Despite their promise, DMHIs face significant adoption barriers, including low initial uptake and high dropout rates. This study leverages machine learning (ML) to analyze behavioral patterns of users of a DMHI, eBridge, designed to increase the utilization of professional mental health services among at-risk college students through motivational interviewing-based online counseling. Our ensemble model, EngageTriBoost, achieved up to 84% accuracy in predicting engagement, measured by sign-ins and counselor interactions. We then applied the Shapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) analysis which provided clear, interpretable insights into key factors influencing user engagement such as emotional dysregulation and perceived stigma, highlighting their critical effect on DMHI adoption. This study demonstrates the power of explainable ML for better understanding user engagement with DMHI to improve their adoption and achievable impact on mental health outcomes.

AI.tldr: An explainable ML approach predicts user engagement with a digital mental health intervention and identifies drivers of adoption, informing improvements in uptake.
AI.motivation: DMHIs suffer from low uptake and high dropout; understanding engagement drivers is critical for effectiveness.
AI.method: Developed EngageTriBoost ensemble model to predict engagement (sign-ins and counselor interactions) and used SHAP to interpret feature importance; applied to eBridge platform for at-risk college students.
AI.result: Engagement prediction accuracy up to 84%; SHAP highlights emotional dysregulation and perceived stigma as key influences on DMHI adoption.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates value of explainable ML to elucidate engagement drivers and to inform strategies to boost adoption and potential mental health outcomes.

title: Camera Artist: A Multi-Agent Framework for Cinematic Language Storytelling Video Generation
summary: We propose Camera Artist, a multi-agent framework that models a real-world filmmaking workflow to generate narrative videos with explicit cinematic language. While recent multi-agent systems have made substantial progress in automating filmmaking workflows from scripts to videos, they often lack explicit mechanisms to structure narrative progression across adjacent shots and deliberate use of cinematic language, resulting in fragmented storytelling and limited filmic quality. To address this, Camera Artist builds upon established agentic pipelines and introduces a dedicated Cinematography Shot Agent, which integrates recursive storyboard generation to strengthen shot-to-shot narrative continuity and cinematic language injection to produce more expressive, film-oriented shot designs. Extensive quantitative and qualitative results demonstrate that our approach consistently outperforms existing baselines in narrative consistency, dynamic expressiveness, and perceived film quality.

AI.tldr: Camera Artist introduces a multi-agent framework with a dedicated Cinematography Shot Agent and recursive storyboard generation to improve narrative continuity and explicit cinematic language in video synthesis, outperforming baselines on narrative consistency, expressiveness, and perceived film quality.
AI.motivation: Fill gaps in existing multi-agent filmmaking systems, which often produce fragmented storytelling and weak filmic style due to lack of explicit mechanisms for narrative progression and cinematic language.
AI.method: Extend an agentic filmmaking pipeline with a dedicated Cinematography Shot Agent that performs recursive storyboard generation and coordinates with other agents to enforce shot-to-shot narrative continuity and filmic style; integrates cinematic language injection into shot design; uses multi-agent collaboration and evaluation.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows consistent improvement over baselines in narrative consistency, dynamic expressiveness, and perceived film quality, across quantitative and qualitative assessments.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling cinematography processes via specialized agents and recursive storyboard generation enhances coherence and filmic quality, yielding more expressive and coherent narrative videos than prior approaches.

title: Multimodal Anomaly Detection for Human-Robot Interaction
summary: Ensuring safety and reliability in human-robot interaction (HRI) requires the timely detection of unexpected events that could lead to system failures or unsafe behaviours. Anomaly detection thus plays a critical role in enabling robots to recognize and respond to deviations from normal operation during collaborative tasks. While reconstruction models have been actively explored in HRI, approaches that operate directly on feature vectors remain largely unexplored. In this work, we propose MADRI, a framework that first transforms video streams into semantically meaningful feature vectors before performing reconstruction-based anomaly detection. Additionally, we augment these visual feature vectors with the robot's internal sensors' readings and a Scene Graph, enabling the model to capture both external anomalies in the visual environment and internal failures within the robot itself. To evaluate our approach, we collected a custom dataset consisting of a simple pick-and-place robotic task under normal and anomalous conditions. Experimental results demonstrate that reconstruction on vision-based feature vectors alone is effective for detecting anomalies, while incorporating other modalities further improves detection performance, highlighting the benefits of multimodal feature reconstruction for robust anomaly detection in human-robot collaboration.

AI.tldr: MADRI introduces a multimodal, feature-vector reconstruction framework for anomaly detection in human-robot interaction (HRI) by turning video into semantic features and augmenting with robot sensor data and a Scene Graph; demonstrates that vision-based reconstruction works and is improved when adding internal and Scene Graph modalities on a custom pick-and-place dataset.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of reconstruction-based anomaly detection in HRI that rely on raw images or single modalities, by using semantically meaningful feature vectors and integrating internal robot signals plus a Scene Graph to capture external and internal anomalies.
AI.method: A two-stage approach: (1) transform video streams into semantically meaningful feature vectors; (2) augment these vectors with robot internal sensor readings and a Scene Graph; apply reconstruction-based anomaly detection on the multimodal feature space; evaluated on a custom pick-and-place task with normal and anomalous conditions, comparing vision-only vs multimodal configurations.
AI.result: Reconstruction on vision-based feature vectors effectively detects anomalies; incorporating additional modalities improves detection performance, indicating that multimodal feature reconstruction yields more robust anomaly detection in HRI.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal feature reconstruction is beneficial for robust anomaly detection in HRI; semantic feature extraction and Scene Graph integration help capture external environment anomalies and internal robot faults; future work could broaden modalities, scale to more complex tasks, and study real-time deployment.

title: SIC3D: Style Image Conditioned Text-to-3D Gaussian Splatting Generation
summary: Recent progress in text-to-3D object generation enables the synthesis of detailed geometry from text input by leveraging 2D diffusion models and differentiable 3D representations. However, the approaches often suffer from limited controllability and texture ambiguity due to the limitation of the text modality. To address this, we present SIC3D, a controllable image-conditioned text-to-3D generation pipeline with 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS). There are two stages in SIC3D. The first stage generates the 3D object content from text with a text-to-3DGS generation model. The second stage transfers style from a reference image to the 3DGS. Within this stylization stage, we introduce a novel Variational Stylized Score Distillation (VSSD) loss to effectively capture both global and local texture patterns while mitigating conflicts between geometry and appearance. A scaling regularization is further applied to prevent the emergence of artifacts and preserve the pattern from the style image. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SIC3D enhances geometric fidelity and style adherence, outperforming prior approaches in both qualitative and quantitative evaluations.

AI.tldr: Two-stage text-to-3D generation using 3D Gaussian Splatting with image-conditioned stylization. Introduces VSSD loss and scaling regularization to improve geometry fidelity and texture/style adherence over prior work.
AI.motivation: Overcome text modality limitations that hinder controllability and texture clarity in text-to-3D synthesis; enable image-based style control to guide appearance while preserving 3D geometry.
AI.method: Stage 1: text-to-3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) generation from text. Stage 2: stylize the 3DGS using a reference image. Introduces Variational Stylized Score Distillation (VSSD) loss to capture global and local texture patterns and mitigate geometry-appearance conflicts; scaling regularization to prevent artifacts and preserve style.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show enhanced geometric fidelity and stronger adherence to the reference style, outperforming prior approaches in both qualitative and quantitative evaluations.
AI.conclusion: SIC3D provides a controllable, image-conditioned text-to-3D generation pipeline that improves geometry and texture transfer quality, validating the effectiveness of VSSD and scaling regularization in 3D synthesis.

title: AlphaLab: Autonomous Multi-Agent Research Across Optimization Domains with Frontier LLMs
summary: We present AlphaLab, an autonomous research harness that leverages frontier LLM agentic capabilities to automate the full experimental cycle in quantitative, computation-intensive domains. Given only a dataset and a natural-language objective, AlphaLab proceeds through three phases without human intervention: (1) it adapts to the domain and explores the data, writing analysis code and producing a research report; (2) it constructs and adversarially validates its own evaluation framework; and (3) it runs large-scale GPU experiments via a Strategist/Worker loop, accumulating domain knowledge in a persistent playbook that functions as a form of online prompt optimization. All domain-specific behavior is factored into adapters generated by the model itself, so the same pipeline handles qualitatively different tasks without modification. We evaluate AlphaLab with two frontier LLMs (GPT-5.2 and Claude Opus 4.6) on three domains: CUDA kernel optimization, where it writes GPU kernels that run 4.4x faster than torch.compile on average (up to 91x); LLM pretraining, where the full system achieves 22% lower validation loss than a single-shot baseline using the same model; and traffic forecasting, where it beats standard baselines by 23-25% after researching and implementing published model families from the literature. The two models discover qualitatively different solutions in every domain (neither dominates uniformly), suggesting that multi-model campaigns provide complementary search coverage. We additionally report results on financial time series forecasting in the appendix, and release all code at https://brendanhogan.github.io/alphalab-paper/.

AI.tldr: An autonomous research harness, AlphaLab, uses frontier LLMs to automate the full experimental cycle across multiple quantitative domains via adapters and a Strategist/Worker loop, achieving substantial performance gains and showing complementary model policies.
AI.motivation: To reduce human labor in data-driven scientific experimentation by enabling autonomous domain adaptation, evaluation design, and large-scale experimentation, thereby accelerating discovery.
AI.method: A three-phase pipeline: (1) domain adaptation and data exploration with code generation and research reporting; (2) adversarial construction and validation of its evaluation framework; (3) large-scale GPU experiments via a Strategist/Worker loop, accumulating domain knowledge in a persistent playbook and producing adapters auto-generated by the model; evaluation across CUDA kernel optimization, LLM pretraining, and traffic forecasting with two frontier LLMs (GPT-5.2 and Claude Opus 4.6).
AI.result: In CUDA kernel optimization, AlphaLab writes GPU kernels achieving average speedups of 4.4x over torch.compile and up to 91x in some cases. In LLM pretraining, it achieves 22% lower validation loss than a single-shot baseline using the same model. In traffic forecasting, it outperforms standard baselines by 23-25% after researching and implementing published model families. The two models exhibit qualitatively different solutions, indicating complementary search coverage; additional results on financial time series forecasting are in the appendix; code release provided.
AI.conclusion: Autonomous research harnesses can autonomously adapt to domains, design and validate evaluation frameworks, and run large-scale experiments, with complementary strengths across frontier models. The work suggests a viable path toward automated scientific workflows and highlights the benefits of model-driven adapters and multi-model campaigns; future work includes broader domain coverage, deeper ablations, and reproducibility considerations.

title: DRBENCHER: Can Your Agent Identify the Entity, Retrieve Its Properties and Do the Math?
summary: Deep research agents increasingly interleave web browsing with multi-step computation, yet existing benchmarks evaluate these capabilities in isolation, creating a blind spot in assessing real-world performance. We introduce DRBENCHER, a synthetic benchmark generator for questions that require both browsing and computation. It enforces four criteria: verifiability (gold answers are computed by executing parameterized code over knowledge-graph values), complexity (multi-hop entity identification, property retrieval, and domain-specific computation), difficulty (a two-stage verification cascade filters out questions solvable by the generating model), and diversity (a greedy max-min embedding filter maximizes coverage). These criteria are realized via a unified answer-first pipeline spanning five domains: biochemistry, financial, geophysical, security, and history. Human evaluation shows 76% validity (84% excluding stale data), with 35% of errors due to outdated knowledge-graph entries, highlighting an inherent limitation of systems that reason over evolving data. Automatic evaluation shows that the strongest frontier model achieves only 20% answer accuracy. Compared to manually constructed benchmarks (BrowseComp+, MATH-500, GPQA), DRBENCHER achieves the highest semantic diversity.

AI.tldr: DRBENCHER is a synthetic benchmark generator for questions requiring both browsing and computation, enforcing verifiability, complexity, difficulty, and diversity across five domains; current models show limited accuracy, but the benchmark achieves high semantic diversity compared with baselines.
AI.motivation: Real-world AI agents increasingly blend web browsing with multi-step computation. Existing benchmarks evaluate these skills in isolation, leaving a gap in assessing integrated performance. DRBENCHER aims to create a unified, verifiable, and diverse benchmark for such tasks.
AI.method: An answer-first pipeline generates questions whose gold answers are produced by parameterized code operating on knowledge-graph values. Four criteria are enforced: verifiability (answers computed by code over knowledge graphs), complexity (multi-hop entity identification, property retrieval, domain-specific computation), difficulty (two-stage verification cascade filters out trivially solvable questions), and diversity (greedy max-min embedding filter to maximize coverage). The evaluation spans five domains: biochemistry, financial, geophysical, security, and history. Human evaluation and automatic evaluation compare against manual benchmarks.
AI.result: Human evaluation shows 76% validity (84% excluding stale data), with 35% errors due to outdated knowledge-graph entries. Automatic evaluation shows the strongest frontier model achieving only 20% answer accuracy. Compared to manually constructed benchmarks (BrowseComp+, MATH-500, GPQA), DRBENCHER exhibits the highest semantic diversity.
AI.conclusion: DRBENCHER demonstrates the feasibility and value of a unified, verifiable benchmark for tasks requiring both browsing and computation, while highlighting the challenges posed by evolving knowledge graphs. The approach emphasizes diversity and structured difficulty, suggesting future work on keeping knowledge graphs up to date and expanding domain coverage to further stress-test integrated reasoning.

title: VAG: Dual-Stream Video-Action Generation for Embodied Data Synthesis
summary: Recent advances in robot foundation models trained on large-scale human teleoperation data have enabled robots to perform increasingly complex real-world tasks. However, scaling these systems remains difficult because collecting task-specific demonstrations is expensive and labor-intensive. Synthetic data, especially generated videos, offer a promising direction, but existing World Models (WMs) are not directly suitable for policy learning since they do not provide paired action trajectories. World-Action (WA) models partially address this by predicting actions with visual outputs, yet often lack strong video-action alignment, while two-stage pipelines that generate video first and then infer actions introduce inefficiency and error accumulation. To address these limitations, we propose VAG, a unified flow-matching-based dual-stream framework that jointly generates video and action under visual and language conditioning. By synchronizing denoising in both branches and using an adaptive 3D pooling mechanism to transfer compact global video context to the action branch, VAG improves cross-modal consistency during generation. Across both simulated and real-world settings, VAG produces aligned video-action pairs with competitive prediction quality, supports executable trajectory replay, and provides useful synthetic pretraining data that improves downstream policy generalization, indicating its potential as a practical world-action model for embodied data synthesis.

AI.tldr: A unified flow-matching dual-stream model (VAG) jointly generates video and action conditioned on visual and language inputs, achieving aligned video-action pairs to enable executable trajectories and better downstream policy generalization for embodied tasks.
AI.motivation: Scale robot foundation models by leveraging synthetic data. Current world models lack paired action trajectories and cross-modal alignment; existing WA approaches have weak video-action alignment or inefficient two-stage pipelines. A unified, consistent generation framework could reduce data collection costs and improve policy learning.
AI.method: VAG uses a flow-matching-based dual-stream architecture with two synchronized generation branches: one for video and one for actions. Visual and language conditioning guide generation. An adaptive 3D pooling mechanism transfers compact global video context to the action branch. Denoising is synchronized across branches to improve cross-modal consistency.
AI.result: In both simulated and real-world settings, VAG yields aligned video-action pairs with competitive action/policy prediction quality. It supports executable trajectory replay and provides useful synthetic pretraining data that improves downstream policy generalization.
AI.conclusion: VAG demonstrates potential as a practical world-action model for embodied data synthesis, offering improved cross-modal consistency, executable trajectories, and data-efficient pretraining for policy learning.

title: From Dispersion to Attraction: Spectral Dynamics of Hallucination Across Whisper Model Scales
comment: This paper has been submitted to Interspeech 2026 for review
summary: Hallucinations in large ASR models present a critical safety risk. In this work, we propose the \textit{Spectral Sensitivity Theorem}, which predicts a phase transition in deep networks from a dispersive regime (signal decay) to an attractor regime (rank-1 collapse) governed by layer-wise gain and alignment. We validate this theory by analyzing the eigenspectra of activation graphs in Whisper models (Tiny to Large-v3-Turbo) under adversarial stress. Our results confirm the theoretical prediction: intermediate models exhibit \textit{Structural Disintegration} (Regime I), characterized by a $13.4\%$ collapse in Cross-Attention rank. Conversely, large models enter a \textit{Compression-Seeking Attractor} state (Regime II), where Self-Attention actively compresses rank ($-2.34\%$) and hardens the spectral slope, decoupling the model from acoustic evidence.

AI.tldr: Proposes Spectral Sensitivity Theorem predicting a phase transition in deep ASR networks from dispersive to attractor dynamics, validated on Whisper via activation-graph eigenspectra; finds regime-dependent rank changes that relate to hallucination risk.
AI.motivation: To understand and mitigate hallucinations in large ASR models by grounding them in spectral dynamics of activation graphs under adversarial stress.
AI.method: Theoretically derive the theorem; empirically analyze activation-graph eigenspectra across Whisper models from Tiny to Large-v3-Turbo under adversarial stress; quantify Cross-Attention rank (Regime I) and Self-Attention rank (Regime II) changes and spectral slope.
AI.result: Intermediate models exhibit 13.4% Cross-Attention rank collapse (Structural Disintegration, Regime I); large models exhibit -2.34% Self-Attention rank compression and hardened spectral slope (Compression-Seeking Attractor, Regime II), decoupling from acoustic evidence.
AI.conclusion: Validates a phase-transition framework for spectral dynamics in ASR that links layer-wise gain/alignment to information processing and susceptibility to hallucination, with implications for model design and safety.

title: SAGE: A Service Agent Graph-guided Evaluation Benchmark
summary: The development of Large Language Models (LLMs) has catalyzed automation in customer service, yet benchmarking their performance remains challenging. Existing benchmarks predominantly rely on static paradigms and single-dimensional metrics, failing to account for diverse user behaviors or the strict adherence to structured Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) required in real-world deployments. To bridge this gap, we propose SAGE (Service Agent Graph-guided Evaluation), a universal multi-agent benchmark for automated, dual-axis assessment. SAGE formalizes unstructured SOPs into Dynamic Dialogue Graphs, enabling precise verification of logical compliance and comprehensive path coverage. We introduce an Adversarial Intent Taxonomy and a modular Extension Mechanism, enabling low-cost deployment across domains and facilitating automated dialogue data synthesis. Evaluation is conducted via a framework where Judge Agents and a Rule Engine analyze interactions between User and Service Agents to generate deterministic ground truth. Extensive experiments on 27 LLMs across 6 industrial scenarios reveal a significant ``Execution Gap'' where models accurately classify intents but fail to derive correct subsequent actions. We also observe ``Empathy Resilience'', a phenomenon where models maintain polite conversational facades despite underlying logical failures under high adversarial intensity. Code and resources are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/SAGE-Bench-4CD3/.

AI.tldr: A universal multi-agent benchmark (SAGE) for SOP-compliant LLM evaluation using Dynamic Dialogue Graphs, Adversarial Intent Taxonomy, and Judge-Agent/Rule-Engine ground truth, revealing an execution gap and empathy resilience across 27 LLMs in 6 domains; supports automated data synthesis and cross-domain deployment.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks rely on static, single-mmetric evaluations and overlook diverse user behaviors and strict SOP adherence; there is a need for a dynamic, multi-agent, domain-adaptable framework to assess both intent understanding and action generation in real-world, SOP-driven customer service.
AI.method: Translate SOPs into Dynamic Dialogue Graphs; define an Adversarial Intent Taxonomy; provide an Extension Mechanism for domain deployment; use Judge Agents and a Rule Engine to generate deterministic ground truth; evaluate 27 LLMs across 6 industrial scenarios; test with adversarial intensity to study resilience; enable automated dialogue data synthesis.
AI.result: Across 27 LLMs and 6 scenarios, models exhibit a notable Execution Gap—high intent classification accuracy but poor downstream action derivation; Empathy Resilience observed under high adversarial intensity; SAGE enables cross-domain deployment and automated ground-truth generation; code/resources available.
AI.conclusion: SAGE provides a formal, reusable benchmark for SOP-compliant, multi-agent evaluation of conversational AI; dynamic graphs allow precise verification of compliance and path coverage; its Extension Mechanism supports domain adaptation and automated data synthesis; highlights key evaluation gaps to address in real-world deployments.

title: Musculoskeletal Motion Imitation for Learning Personalized Exoskeleton Control Policy in Impaired Gait
summary: Designing generalizable control policies for lower-limb exoskeletons remains fundamentally constrained by exhaustive data collection or iterative optimization procedures, which limit accessibility to clinical populations. To address this challenge, we introduce a device-agnostic framework that combines physiologically plausible musculoskeletal simulation with reinforcement learning to enable scalable personalized exoskeleton assistance for both able-bodied and clinical populations. Our control policies not only generate physiologically plausible locomotion dynamics but also capture clinically observed compensatory strategies under targeted muscular deficits, providing a unified computational model of both healthy and pathological gait. Without task-specific tuning, the resulting exoskeleton control policies produce assistive torque profiles at the hip and ankle that align with state-of-the-art profiles validated in human experiments, while consistently reducing metabolic cost across walking speeds. For simulated impaired-gait models, the learned control policies yield asymmetric, deficit-specific exoskeleton assistance that improves both energetic efficiency and bilateral kinematic symmetry without explicit prescription of the target gait pattern. These results demonstrate that physiologically plausible musculoskeletal simulation via reinforcement learning can serve as a scalable foundation for personalized exoskeleton control across both able-bodied and clinical populations, eliminating the need for extensive physical trials.

AI.tldr: A device-agnostic framework merges physiologically plausible musculoskeletal simulation with reinforcement learning to produce scalable, personalized exoskeleton control for both able-bodied and clinical populations; it generalizes without task-specific tuning and reduces metabolic cost, offering deficit-specific assistance in impaired gait.
AI.motivation: To overcome data collection and optimization bottlenecks that constrain generalizable exoskeleton control in clinical populations; seeks a unified model of healthy and pathological gait and reduced need for extensive trials.
AI.method: Integrates physiologically plausible musculoskeletal simulation with reinforcement learning to learn hip/ankle torque profiles across speeds; device-agnostic policy trained to reproduce physiological gait and compensatory strategies under muscular deficits; no task-specific tuning.
AI.result: Learned policies generate physiologically plausible gait and clinically observed compensations; hip/ankle assistive torque profiles align with state-of-the-art profiles validated in humans; metabolic cost reduced across walking speeds; in impaired gait, policies provide asymmetric, deficit-specific assistance improving energy efficiency and bilateral symmetry.
AI.conclusion: Physiologically grounded musculoskeletal simulation via reinforcement learning provides a scalable foundation for personalized exoskeleton control across healthy and clinical populations, reducing or eliminating the need for extensive physical trials.

title: InstrAct: Towards Action-Centric Understanding in Instructional Videos
summary: Understanding instructional videos requires recognizing fine-grained actions and modeling their temporal relations, which remains challenging for current Video Foundation Models (VFMs). This difficulty stems from noisy web supervision and a pervasive "static bias", where models rely on objects rather than motion cues. To address this, we propose InstrAction, a pretraining framework for instructional videos' action-centric representations. We first introduce a data-driven strategy, which filters noisy captions and generates action-centric hard negatives to disentangle actions from objects during contrastive learning. At the visual feature level, an Action Perceiver extracts motion-relevant tokens from redundant video encodings. Beyond contrastive learning, we introduce two auxiliary objectives: Dynamic Time Warping alignment (DTW-Align) for modeling sequential temporal structure, and Masked Action Modeling (MAM) for strengthening cross-modal grounding. Finally, we introduce the InstrAct Bench to evaluate action-centric understanding, where our method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art VFMs on semantic reasoning, procedural logic, and fine-grained retrieval tasks.

AI.tldr: InstrAction is a pretraining framework that learns action-centric representations from instructional videos by filtering noisy captions, generating action-focused hard negatives, applying an Action Perceiver to extract motion-relevant tokens, and using DTW-Align and MAM as auxiliary objectives; evaluated on the InstrAct Bench with superior performance vs. state-of-the-art VFMs in semantic reasoning, procedural logic, and fine-grained retrieval.
AI.motivation: Address noisy web supervision and the static bias of relying on objects rather than motion cues in Video Foundation Models, to better capture fine-grained actions and their temporal structure in instructional videos.
AI.method: 1) Data-driven caption filtering and action-centric hard-negative generation to disentangle actions from objects during contrastive learning. 2) Action Perceiver to extract motion-relevant tokens from redundant video encodings. 3) Auxiliary objectives: Dynamic Time Warping alignment (DTW-Align) for sequential temporal modeling and Masked Action Modeling (MAM) for cross-modal grounding. 4) InstrAct Bench for evaluating action-centric understanding.
AI.result: Consistent outperformance of state-of-the-art VFMs on tasks involving semantic reasoning, procedural logic, and fine-grained retrieval on the InstrAct Bench.
AI.conclusion: Action-centric pretraining improves instructional-video understanding and temporal-action reasoning; the combination of data-driven caption filtering, motion-focused visual encoding, and auxiliary objectives yields stronger action-centric representations, with the InstrAct Bench providing a robust evaluation platform.

title: Reservoir observer enhanced with residual calibration and attention mechanism
summary: Reservoir observers provide a data-driven approach to the inference of unmeasured variables from observed ones for nonlinear dynamical systems. While previous studies have demonstrated wide applicability, their performance may vary considerably with different input variables, even compromising reliability in the worst cases. To enhance the performance of inference, we integrate residual calibration and attention mechanism into the reservoir observer design. The residual calibration module leverages information from the estimation residuals to refine the observer output, and the attention mechanism exploits the temporal dependencies of the data to enrich the representation of reservoir internal dynamics. Experiments on typical chaotic systems demonstrate that our method substantially improves inference accuracy, especially for the worst cases resulting from the traditional reservoir observers. We also invoke the notion of transfer entropy to explain the reason for the input-dependent observation discrepancy and the effectiveness of the proposed method.

AI.tldr: Enhances reservoir observers with residual calibration and attention to improve worst-case inference in nonlinear chaotic systems; uses transfer entropy to explain input-dependent discrepancies.
AI.motivation: Address variability and unreliability of input-dependent inference performance in data-driven reservoir observers for nonlinear dynamics.
AI.method: Introduce a residual calibration module that uses estimation residuals to refine the observer output and an attention mechanism to capture temporal dependencies, enriching reservoir dynamics.
AI.result: Significant improvements in inference accuracy, especially for worst-case inputs, demonstrated on chaotic systems.
AI.conclusion: Residual calibration plus attention mitigate input-dependent observation gaps; transfer entropy provides explanatory insight into the observed discrepancies and method effectiveness.

title: Constraint-Aware Corrective Memory for Language-Based Drug Discovery Agents
summary: Large language models are making autonomous drug discovery agents increasingly feasible, but reliable success in this setting is not determined by any single action or molecule. It is determined by whether the final returned set jointly satisfies protocol-level requirements such as set size, diversity, binding quality, and developability. This creates a fundamental control problem: the agent plans step by step, while task validity is decided at the level of the whole candidate set. Existing language-based drug discovery systems therefore tend to rely on long raw history and under-specified self-reflection, making failure localization imprecise and planner-facing agent states increasingly noisy. We present CACM (Constraint-Aware Corrective Memory), a language-based drug discovery framework built around precise set-level diagnosis and a concise memory write-back mechanism. CACM introduces protocol auditing and a grounded diagnostician, which jointly analyze multimodal evidence spanning task requirements, pocket context, and candidate-set evidence to localize protocol violations, generate actionable remediation hints, and bias the next action toward the most relevant correction. To keep planning context compact, CACM organizes memory into static, dynamic, and corrective channels and compresses them before write-back, thereby preserving persistent task information while exposing only the most decision-relevant failures. Our experimental results show that CACM improves the target-level success rate by 36.4% over the state-of-the-art baseline. The results show that reliable language-based drug discovery benefits not only from more powerful molecular tools, but also from more precise diagnosis and more economical agent states.

AI.tldr: CACM: Constraint-Aware Corrective Memory for language-based drug discovery; improves target success by 36.4% via protocol auditing, grounded diagnostician, and memory channels.
AI.motivation: Need to address set-level (protocol-level) requirements in LLM-driven drug discovery, where overall candidate-set validity drives success, not just single-step actions; current systems struggle with failure localization due to long histories.
AI.method: Proposes CACM framework with protocol auditing and grounded diagnostician; memory organized into static, dynamic, corrective channels; memory write-back compressed; uses multimodal evidence (task requirements, pocket context, candidate-set evidence) to diagnose protocol violations, generate remediation hints, and bias next actions; action sequence remains planner-facing.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows 36.4% improvement in target-level success rate over a state-of-the-art baseline.
AI.conclusion: Precise diagnosis and economical agent state management, via constrained memory and set-level auditing, enhances reliability of language-based drug discovery beyond using more powerful molecular tools alone.

title: Adaptor: Advancing Assistive Teleoperation with Few-Shot Learning and Cross-Operator Generalization
comment: Accepted to the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA 2026)
summary: Assistive teleoperation enhances efficiency via shared control, yet inter-operator variability, stemming from diverse habits and expertise, induces highly heterogeneous trajectory distributions that undermine intent recognition stability. We present Adaptor, a few-shot framework for robust cross-operator intent recognition. The Adaptor bridges the domain gap through two stages: (i) preprocessing, which models intent uncertainty by synthesizing trajectory perturbations via noise injection and performs geometry-aware keyframe extraction; and (ii) policy learning, which encodes the processed trajectories with an Intention Expert and fuses them with the pre-trained vision-language model context to condition an Action Expert for action generation. Experiments on real-world and simulated benchmarks demonstrate that Adaptor achieves state-of-the-art performance, improving success rates and efficiency over baselines. Moreover, the method exhibits low variance across operators with varying expertise, demonstrating robust cross-operator generalization.

AI.tldr: Adaptor is a few-shot cross-operator intent recognition framework for assistive teleoperation with a two-stage design (preprocessing: perturbation-based intent uncertainty modeling and geometry-aware keyframe extraction; policy learning: an Intention Expert + Vision-Language fusion to condition an Action Expert). It achieves state-of-the-art performance and robust cross-operator generalization.
AI.motivation: Inter-operator variability in teleoperation creates highly heterogeneous trajectory distributions, destabilizing intent recognition and reducing shared-control efficiency. A robust, few-shot method is needed to generalize across operators with diverse habits and expertise.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: (i) preprocessing that models intent uncertainty via trajectory perturbations (noise injection) and selects geometry-aware keyframes; (ii) policy learning that encodes processed trajectories with an Intention Expert and fuses them with a pre-trained vision-language model to condition an Action Expert for action generation.
AI.result: Experiments on real-world and simulated benchmarks show state-of-the-art performance, with higher success rates and efficiency over baselines and reduced variance across operators of varying expertise.
AI.conclusion: Adaptor effectively bridges domain gaps caused by operator variability, enabling robust cross-operator intent recognition in assistive teleoperation and illustrating the benefit of combining perturbation-based preprocessing with cross-modal fusion for policy learning.

title: R2G: A Multi-View Circuit Graph Benchmark Suite from RTL to GDSII
comment: Accepted as a poster by CVPR2026
summary: Graph neural networks (GNNs) are increasingly applied to physical design tasks such as congestion prediction and wirelength estimation, yet progress is hindered by inconsistent circuit representations and the absence of controlled evaluation protocols. We present R2G (RTL-to-GDSII), a multi-view circuit-graph benchmark suite that standardizes five stage-aware views with information parity (every view encodes the same attribute set, differing only in where features attach) over 30 open-source IP cores (up to $10^6$ nodes/edges). R2G provides an end-to-end DEF-to-graph pipeline spanning synthesis, placement, and routing stages, together with loaders, unified splits, domain metrics, and reproducible baselines. By decoupling representation choice from model choice, R2G isolates a confound that prior EDA and graph-ML benchmarks leave uncontrolled. In systematic studies with GINE, GAT, and ResGatedGCN, we find: (i) view choice dominates model choice, with Test R$^2$ varying by more than 0.3 across representations for a fixed GNN; (ii) node-centric views generalize best across both placement and routing; and (iii) decoder-head depth (3--4 layers) is the primary accuracy driver, turning divergent training into near-perfect predictions (R$^2$$>$0.99). Code and datasets are available at https://github.com/ShenShan123/R2G.

AI.tldr: R2G (RTL-to-GDSII) provides a standardized, multi-view circuit-graph benchmark with five stage-aware views across 30 IP cores, coupled with an end-to-end DEF-to-graph pipeline and reproducible baselines for GNN-based physical design tasks.
AI.motivation: Inconsistent circuit representations and missing controlled evaluation hinder progress in applying GNNs to physical design. A unified benchmark with controlled representations and baselines is needed.
AI.method: Introduce R2G: five stage-aware, parity-preserving views for graph representations, an end-to-end DEF-to-graph pipeline (synthesis, placement, routing), loaders, unified splits, domain metrics, and reproducible baselines. Evaluate GNNs (GINE, GAT, ResGatedGCN) on placement and routing tasks over up to 1e6 nodes/edges from 30 IP cores.
AI.result: View choice dominates model choice (Test R^2 varies >0.3 across representations for a fixed GNN); node-centric views generalize best across placement and routing; decoder-head depth (3–4 layers) is the main accuracy driver, yielding R^2 > 0.99 with proper design.
AI.conclusion: Standardized representations are crucial to fair evaluation in GNN-based physical design. R2G isolates representation effects, enabling reproducible comparisons and revealing that representation choice can trump model choice; proper decoder design enables near-perfect predictive performance.

title: Joint Interference Detection and Identification via Adversarial Multi-task Learning
comment: 13 pages, 13 figures. Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Cognitive Communications and Networking
summary: Precise interference detection and identification are crucial for enhancing the survivability of communication systems in non-cooperative wireless environments. While deep learning (DL) has advanced this field, existing single-task learning (STL) approaches neglect inherent task correlations. Furthermore, emerging multi-task learning (MTL) methods often lack a theoretical foundation for quantifying and modeling task relationships. To bridge this gap, we establish a theoretically grounded MTL framework for joint interference detection, modulation identification, and interference identification. First, we derive an upper bound for the weighted expected loss in MTL frameworks. This bound explicitly connects MTL performance to task similarity, quantified by the Wasserstein distance and learnable task relation coefficients. Guided by this theory, we present the adversarial multi-task interference detection and identification network (AMTIDIN), which integrates adversarial training to minimize distributional discrepancies across tasks and uses adaptive coefficients to model task correlations dynamically. Crucially, we conducted a quantitative analysis of task similarity to reveal intrinsic task relationships, specifically that modulation identification and interference identification share a substantial feature overlap distinct from interference detection. Extensive comparative experiments demonstrate that AMTIDIN significantly outperforms both its task-specific STL baseline and state-of-the-art MTL baselines in robustness and generalization, particularly under challenging conditions with limited training data, short signal lengths, and low signal-to-noise ratios (SNRs).

AI.tldr: A theoretically grounded MT learning framework, AMTIDIN, for joint interference detection, modulation identification, and interference identification in non-cooperative wireless environments, leveraging Wasserstein-based task similarity and adversarial training to improve robustness; outperforms STL and state-of-the-art MT baselines, especially with limited data and low SNR.
AI.motivation: To address neglected task correlations in single-task learning and the lack of a theoretical foundation for task relationships in multi-task learning for interference-related problems.
AI.method: Derive an upper bound on the weighted expected loss in MT learning that connects performance to task similarity via Wasserstein distance and learnable relation coefficients. Propose AMTIDIN with adversarial training to minimize distributional discrepancies across tasks and adaptive coefficients to model dynamic task correlations; perform quantitative analysis of task similarity to uncover intrinsic relationships.
AI.result: AMTIDIN significantly outperforms task-specific STL and state-of-the-art MT baselines in robustness and generalization, notably under limited training data, short signal lengths, and low SNR. Modulation identification and interference identification share substantial feature overlap distinct from interference detection.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a theoretical link between MT performance and task similarity; dynamically modeled task relationships via AMTIDIN improve joint interference-related tasks and offer insights into how tasks relate.

title: Mind the Gap Between Spatial Reasoning and Acting! Step-by-Step Evaluation of Agents With Spatial-Gym
summary: Spatial reasoning is central to navigation and robotics, yet measuring model capabilities on these tasks remains difficult. Existing benchmarks evaluate models in a one-shot setting, requiring full solution generation in a single response, unlike humans, who work in interactive environments step-by-step. We introduce Spatial-Gym, a Gymnasium environment that isolates spatial constraint reasoning by testing pathfinding in 2D-grid puzzles as a sequential decision task with optional backtracking. We evaluate eight models in three settings (one-shot, step-by-step, step-by-step with backtracking) against human, random, and A* baselines on 500 episodes. The best model, GPT-OSS 120B, achieves a solve rate of 16.0%, 82 points below the human baseline (98.0%). Step-by-step format helps weaker models (up to +5.4%) by removing formatting errors, but hurts stronger models (up to 5.6%) by constraining global planning. Backtracking improves episode completion, but increases solve rate only for weaker models; stronger models rarely backtrack and do not benefit from it. Our experiments have three key findings: (1) models fail to scale reasoning effort with difficulty, (2) vision models receiving images of the spatial environment reduce solve rate by 73%, and (3) extended chain-of-thought reasoning retains a 3-5x accuracy advantage over standard inference even in the step-by-step setting. Spatial-Gym enables diagnosis of model limitations and provides a framework for improving spatial reasoning through reinforcement learning.

AI.tldr: Spatial-Gym is a sequential decision framework for 2D grid pathfinding that reveals how current models struggle with spatial reasoning, with stepwise reasoning and backtracking providing mixed benefits and extended chain-of-thought offering a sizable accuracy boost.
AI.motivation: Current spatial reasoning benchmarks evaluate one-shot performance, which conflates planning and execution. There is a need for interactive, stepwise evaluation to diagnose reasoning failures and guide improvements in navigation and robotics models.
AI.method: Introduce Spatial-Gym in Gymnasium to isolate spatial constraint reasoning via 2D-grid pathfinding treated as a sequential decision task with optional backtracking. Evaluate eight models across three settings (one-shot, step-by-step, step-by-step with backtracking) against human, random, and A* baselines on 500 episodes, and assess performance with image versus non-image inputs and extended chain-of-thought prompting.
