AI.result: The best model (GPT-OSS 120B) achieves 16.0% solve rate, far below human (98.0%). Step-by-step helps weaker models (up to +5.4%) but hurts stronger models (up to -5.6%). Backtracking improves completion only for weaker models. Vision-based inputs reduce solve rate by 73%. Extended chain-of-thought maintains a 3-5x accuracy advantage over standard inference even in step-by-step settings.
AI.conclusion: Spatial-Gym enables diagnostic assessment of spatial reasoning capabilities and offers a pathway to improvement via reinforcement learning and prompting strategies, with chain-of-thought prompting showing promising gains for reasoning in sequential tasks.

title: SafeMind: A Risk-Aware Differentiable Control Framework for Adaptive and Safe Quadruped Locomotion
summary: Learning-based quadruped controllers achieve impressive agility but typically lack formal safety guarantees under model uncertainty, perception noise, and unstructured contact conditions. We introduce SafeMind, a differentiable stochastic safety-control framework that unifies probabilistic Control Barrier Functions with semantic context understanding and meta-adaptive risk calibration. SafeMind explicitly models epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty through a variance-aware barrier constraint embedded in a differentiable quadratic program, thereby preserving gradient flow for end-to-end training. A semantics-to-constraint encoder modulates safety margins using perceptual or language cues, while a meta-adaptive learner continuously adjusts risk sensitivity across environments. We provide theoretical conditions for probabilistic forward invariance, feasibility, and stability under stochastic dynamics. SafeMind is deployed on Unitree A1 and ANYmal C at 200~Hz and validated across 12 terrain types, dynamic obstacles, morphology perturbations, and semantically defined tasks. Experiments show that SafeMind reduces safety violations by 3--10x and energy consumption by 10--15% relative to state-of-the-art CBF, MPC, and hybrid RL baselines, while maintaining real-time control performance.

AI.tldr: SafeMind is a differentiable, uncertainty-aware safety-control framework for quadruped robots that integrates probabilistic control barrier functions with semantic context and meta-adaptive risk, enabling real-time, safer, and more energy-efficient locomotion across diverse environments.
AI.motivation: Learning-based legged controllers offer agility but lack formal safety guarantees under model uncertainty, perception noise, and unstructured contact; existing safety methods (CBF, MPC, RL) struggle to provide gradient-friendly, uncertainty-aware safety with semantic guidance across varied terrains and tasks.
AI.method: Introduces a differentiable stochastic safety-control framework (SafeMind) that combines variance-aware probabilistic CBFs embedded in a differentiable QP to preserve gradient flow; uses a semantics-to-constraint encoder to modulate safety margins from perceptual/language cues; employs a meta-adaptive learner to adjust risk sensitivity across environments; provides theoretical guarantees for probabilistic forward invariance, feasibility, and stability under stochastic dynamics; deployed at 200 Hz on Unitree A1 and ANYmal C, validated across 12 terrains, dynamic obstacles, morphology perturbations, and semantically defined tasks.
AI.result: Experimental results show SafeMind reduces safety violations by 3–10x and energy consumption by 10–15% relative to state-of-the-art CBF, MPC, and hybrid RL baselines, while preserving real-time control performance across diverse conditions.
AI.conclusion: SafeMind offers a scalable, uncertainty-aware safety-control approach that unifies probabilistic CBFs with semantic cues and meta-adaptive risk, providing formal guarantees and practical gains for real-time legged robotics in varied environments.

title: Towards Responsible Multimodal Medical Reasoning via Context-Aligned Vision-Language Models
summary: Medical vision-language models (VLMs) show strong performance on radiology tasks but often produce fluent yet weakly grounded conclusions due to over-reliance on a dominant modality. We introduce a context-aligned reasoning framework that enforces agreement across heterogeneous clinical evidence before generating diagnostic conclusions. The proposed approach augments a frozen VLM with structured contextual signals derived from radiomic statistics, explainability activations, and vocabulary-grounded semantic cues. Instead of producing free-form responses, the model generates structured outputs containing supporting evidence, uncertainty estimates, limitations, and safety notes. We observe that auxiliary signals alone provide limited benefit; performance gains emerge only when these signals are integrated through contextual verification. Experiments on chest X-ray datasets demonstrate that context alignment improves discriminative performance (AUC 0.918 to 0.925) while maintaining calibrated uncertainty. The framework also substantially reduces hallucinated keywords (1.14 to 0.25) and produces more concise reasoning explanations (19.4 to 15.3 words) without increasing model confidence (0.70 to 0.68). Cross-dataset evaluation on CheXpert further reveals that modality informativeness significantly influences reasoning behavior. These results suggest that enforcing multi-evidence agreement improves both reliability and trustworthiness in medical multimodal reasoning, while preserving the underlying model architecture.

AI.tldr: A context-aligned reasoning framework augments a frozen medical VLM with structured contextual signals to enforce cross-evidence agreement, yielding improved AUC and reduced hallucinations with calibrated uncertainty.
AI.motivation: Address the tendency of medical vision-language models to over-rely on a dominant modality, causing weak grounding; investigate whether multi-evidence alignment improves reliability and trustworthiness in medical multimodal reasoning.
AI.method: Integrate structured signals from radiomic statistics, explainability activations, and vocabulary-grounded cues into a frozen VLM; enforce agreement across heterogeneous evidence before producing structured outputs (evidence, uncertainty, limitations, safety notes); evaluate on chest X-ray datasets and cross-dataset CheXpert to assess generalization.
AI.result: AUC improved from 0.918 to 0.925; uncertainty remained calibrated; hallucinated keywords reduced from 1.14 to 0.25; reasoning length shortened from 19.4 to 15.3 words; cross-dataset results indicate modality informativeness influences reasoning behavior.
AI.conclusion: Context alignment via multi-evidence verification improves reliability and trustworthiness of medical multimodal reasoning without altering the core architecture; auxiliary signals alone offer limited benefit unless integrated through contextual verification.

title: From Selection to Scheduling: Federated Geometry-Aware Correction Makes Exemplar Replay Work Better under Continual Dynamic Heterogeneity
summary: Exemplar replay has become an effective strategy for mitigating catastrophic forgetting in federated continual learning (FCL) by retaining representative samples from past tasks. Existing studies focus on designing sample-importance estimation mechanisms to identify information-rich samples. However, they typically overlook strategies for effectively utilizing the selected exemplars, which limits their performance under continual dynamic heterogeneity across clients and tasks. To address this issue, this paper proposes a Federated gEometry-Aware correcTion method, termed FEAT, which alleviates imbalance-induced representation collapse that drags rare-class features toward frequent classes across clients. Specifically, it consists of two key modules: 1) the Geometric Structure Alignment module performs structural knowledge distillation by aligning the pairwise angular similarities between feature representations and their corresponding Equiangular Tight Frame prototypes, which are fixed and shared across clients to serve as a class-discriminative reference structure. This encourages geometric consistency across tasks and helps mitigate representation drift; 2) the Energy-based Geometric Correction module removes task-irrelevant directional components from feature embeddings, which reduces prediction bias toward majority classes. This improves sensitivity to minority classes and enhances the model's robustness under class-imbalanced distributions.

AI.tldr: Federated gEometry-Aware correcTion (FEAT) leverages geometry-aware exemplar replay to counter representation drift and class imbalance in federated continual learning, using fixed Equiangular Tight Frame prototypes and an energy-based feature correction.
AI.motivation: In federated continual learning, exemplar replay helps mitigate forgetting, but existing work inadequately exploits exemplars under non-iid and dynamic task shifts, leading to imbalance-induced representation collapse and weak minority-class performance.
AI.method: Two modules: (1) Geometric Structure Alignment aligns pairwise angular similarities between features and fixed Equiangular Tight Frame prototypes shared across clients to enforce a common geometry; (2) Energy-based Geometric Correction removes task-irrelevant directional components from embeddings to reduce bias toward majority classes.
AI.result: The approach is claimed to improve representation stability, reduce drift, and enhance minority-class sensitivity and robustness under heterogeneous continual tasks.
AI.conclusion: FEAT integrates a fixed geometric reference with energy-based feature correction to maintain discriminative geometry and mitigate imbalance-driven drift in federated continual learning.

title: HiL-Bench (Human-in-Loop Benchmark): Do Agents Know When to Ask for Help?
summary:
Frontier coding agents solve complex tasks when given complete context but collapse when specifications are incomplete or ambiguous. The bottleneck is not raw capability, but judgment: knowing when to act autonomously and when to ask for help. Current benchmarks are blind to this failure mode. They supply unambiguous detailed instructions and solely reward execution correctness, so an agent that makes a lucky guess for a missing requirement will score identically to one that would have asked to be certain.
  We present HiL-Bench (Human-in-the-Loop Benchmark) to measure this selective escalation skill. Each task contains human-validated blockers (missing information, ambiguous requests, contradictory information) that surface only through progressive exploration, not upfront inspection. Our core metric, Ask-F1, the harmonic mean of question precision and blocker recall, captures the tension between over-asking and silent guessing; its structure architecturally prevents gaming through question spam.
  Evaluation across SWE and text-to-SQL domains reveals a large universal judgment gap: no frontier model recovers more than a fraction of its full-information performance when deciding whether to ask. Failure analysis identifies three key help-seeking patterns: overconfident wrong beliefs with no gap detection; high uncertainty detection yet persistent errors; broad, imprecise escalation without self-correction. These consistent patterns confirm poor help-seeking is a model-level flaw, not task-specific. RL training on shaped Ask-F1 reward shows judgment is trainable: a 32B model improves both help-seeking quality and task pass rate, with gains that transfer across domains. The model does not learn domain-specific heuristics for when to ask; it learns to detect unresolvable uncertainty and act on it.

AI.tldr: HiL-Bench introduces a human-in-the-loop benchmark to measure selective escalation in frontier agents. It uses a novel Ask-F1 metric to balance asking for information vs. guessing, and demonstrates that current models struggle to decide when to consult humans. RL training with Ask-F1 rewards improves help-seeking and task success, with cross-domain transfer in SWE and text-to-SQL.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks reward correct execution under complete information, obscuring the model's judgment about when to seek help. There is a critical need to quantify and improve selective escalation to build safer, more reliable agents that can surface blockers and defer to humans when information is missing or ambiguous.
AI.method: Introduce HiL-Bench tasks with human-validated blockers that surface only through progressive exploration. Define Ask-F1 as the harmonic mean of question precision and blocker recall to penalize both over-questioning and silent guessing. Evaluate frontier models in SWE and text-to-SQL, perform failure analyses, and train RL agents with a shaped Ask-F1 reward to enhance help-seeking. Assess cross-domain transfer and generalization of the learned avoidance of domain-specific heuristics.
AI.result: A universal judgment gap is observed: no frontier model recovers more than a fraction of full-information performance when deciding to ask. Failure modes include overconfident incorrect beliefs with no gap detection, high-uncertainty yet wrong results, and broad yet imprecise escalation. RL-trained 32B models improve help-seeking quality and task pass rate, with cross-domain transfer. Learned behavior focuses on detecting unresolvable uncertainty rather than exploiting domain cues, indicating domain-agnostic improvement of judgment.
AI.conclusion: Judgment and help-seeking are trainable, not fixed traits. A robust benchmark and reward signal (Ask-F1) enable safer, more reliable agents that seek human input when necessary. The approach generalizes across domains and demonstrates that improving selective escalation should be a core objective for frontier models.

title: Sim-to-Real Transfer for Muscle-Actuated Robots via Generalized Actuator Networks
summary: Tendon drives paired with soft muscle actuation enable faster and safer robots while potentially accelerating skill acquisition. Still, these systems are rarely used in practice due to inherent nonlinearities, friction, and hysteresis, which complicate modeling and control. So far, these challenges have hindered policy transfer from simulation to real systems. To bridge this gap, we propose a sim-to-real pipeline that learns a neural network model of this complex actuation and leverages established rigid body simulation for the arm dynamics and interactions with the environment. Our method, called Generalized Actuator Network (GeAN), enables actuation model identification across a wide range of robots by learning directly from joint position trajectories rather than requiring torque sensors. Using GeAN on PAMY2, a tendon-driven robot powered by pneumatic artificial muscles, we successfully deploy precise goal-reaching and dynamic ball-in-a-cup policies trained entirely in simulation. To the best of our knowledge, this result constitutes the first successful sim-to-real transfer for a four-degrees-of-freedom muscle-actuated robot arm.

AI.tldr: GeAN: a neural actuator model learned from joint trajectories to enable sim-to-real transfer for tendon-driven soft actuators, demonstrated on a 4-DOF PAMY2 arm achieving precise goal-reaching and a dynamic ball-in-a-cup task, first of its kind.
AI.motivation: Nonlinearities, friction, and hysteresis in tendon-driven soft actuators hinder modeling/control and policy transfer from simulation to real robots, limiting practical use.
AI.method: Train a neural actuation model (GeAN) from joint position trajectories and integrate with a rigid-body simulator for arm dynamics; apply to PAMY2 (tendon-driven with pneumatic artificial muscles); policies trained in simulation transferred to real hardware without torque sensors.
AI.result: Successful sim-to-real transfer for a four-DOF muscle-actuated arm; demonstrated precise goal-reaching and dynamic ball-in-a-cup behavior on PAMY2, with policies trained entirely in simulation.
AI.conclusion: GeAN generalizes actuation model identification across diverse robots using position trajectories and bridges the sim-to-real gap for tendon-driven actuators, enabling practical deployment without torque sensing.

title: SenBen: Sensitive Scene Graphs for Explainable Content Moderation
summary: Content moderation systems classify images as safe or unsafe but lack spatial grounding and interpretability: they cannot explain what sensitive behavior was detected, who is involved, or where it occurs. We introduce the Sensitive Benchmark (SenBen), the first large-scale scene graph benchmark for sensitive content, comprising 13,999 frames from 157 movies annotated with Visual Genome-style scene graphs (25 object classes, 28 attributes including affective states such as pain, fear, aggression, and distress, 14 predicates) and 16 sensitivity tags across 5 categories. We distill a frontier VLM into a compact 241M student model using a multi-task recipe that addresses vocabulary imbalance in autoregressive scene graph generation through suffix-based object identity, Vocabulary-Aware Recall (VAR) Loss, and a decoupled Query2Label tag head with asymmetric loss, yielding a +6.4 percentage point improvement in SenBen Recall over standard cross-entropy training. On grounded scene graph metrics, our student model outperforms all evaluated VLMs except Gemini models and all commercial safety APIs, while achieving the highest object detection and captioning scores across all models, at $7.6\times$ faster inference and $16\times$ less GPU memory.

AI.tldr: Introduces SenBen, the first large-scale scene graph benchmark for sensitive content, and a compact 241M student model trained with a multi-task recipe to address vocabulary imbalance in autoregressive scene graph generation. The approach yields +6.4pp SenBen Recall over CE training, outperforms most VLMs and safety APIs on grounded metrics (except Gemini), and is significantly faster and more memory-efficient.
AI.motivation: Current content moderation systems lack spatial grounding and interpretability. A large, richly annotated benchmark (SenBen) with scene graphs and sensitivity tags is needed to drive models that can explain what, where, and who is involved in sensitive content. A compact student model aims to balance accuracy with efficiency for scalable deployment.
AI.method: Construct SenBen: 13,999 frames from 157 movies annotated with Visual Genome-style scene graphs (25 object classes, 28 attributes including affective states, 14 predicates) and 16 sensitivity tags across 5 categories. Train a frontier Vision-Language Model into a 241M student using a multi-task setup that mitigates vocabulary imbalance in autoregressive scene graph generation via suffix-based object identity, Vocabulary-Aware Recall (VAR) Loss, and a decoupled Query2Label tag head with asymmetric loss.
AI.result: SenBen Recall improves by 6.4 percentage points over standard cross-entropy training. The student achieves top object detection and captioning scores among models, outperforms all evaluated VLMs except Gemini models and all commercial safety APIs on grounded scene graph metrics, and offers 7.6x faster inference with 16x less GPU memory.
AI.conclusion: The proposed SenBen framework demonstrates strong, interpretable grounding for sensitive-content moderation with high efficiency. While Gemini models remain strongest on some metrics, the approach provides competitive performance with substantial efficiency gains, enabling scalable deployment and improved explainability.

title: StructRL: Recovering Dynamic Programming Structure from Learning Dynamics in Distributional Reinforcement Learning
summary: Reinforcement learning is typically treated as a uniform, data-driven optimization process, where updates are guided by rewards and temporal-difference errors without explicitly exploiting global structure. In contrast, dynamic programming methods rely on structured information propagation, enabling efficient and stable learning. In this paper, we provide evidence that such structure can be recovered from the learning dynamics of distributional reinforcement learning. By analyzing the temporal evolution of return distributions, we identify signals that capture when and where learning occurs in the state space. In particular, we introduce a temporal learning indicator t*(s) that reflects when a state undergoes its strongest learning update during training. Empirically, this signal induces an ordering over states that is consistent with a dynamic programming-style propagation of information. Building on this observation, we propose StructRL, a framework that exploits these signals to guide sampling in alignment with the emerging propagation structure. Our preliminary results suggest that distributional learning dynamics provide a mechanism to recover and exploit dynamic programming-like structure without requiring an explicit model. This offers a new perspective on reinforcement learning, where learning can be interpreted as a structured propagation process rather than a purely uniform optimization procedure.

AI.tldr: Distributional RL dynamics reveal a dynamic-programming-like propagation structure, identified by a temporal learning indicator t*(s). The authors propose StructRL to guide sampling along this latent structure, yielding DP-consistent state ordering with preliminary positive results, without requiring a model.
AI.motivation: To reconcile the apparent uniform, data-driven nature of RL with the structured information propagation of dynamic programming by uncovering latent structure embedded in distributional learning dynamics.
AI.method: Analyze the temporal evolution of return distributions during training to extract a state-specific learning signal t*(s) indicating when a state undergoes its strongest update; introduce StructRL that leverages these signals to bias sampling according to the emergent propagation structure.
AI.result: Identification of a DP-like ordering of states driven by learning dynamics; initial empirical evidence that StructRL can exploit this propagation structure to improve sampling and learning efficiency without an explicit model.
AI.conclusion: Distributional RL learning dynamics can recover and utilize dynamic programming–style structure, offering a new perspective where structured propagation emerges from learning rather than requiring explicit modeling.

title: Do We Really Need to Approach the Entire Pareto Front in Many-Objective Bayesian Optimisation?
summary: Many-objective optimisation, a subset of multi-objective optimisation, involves optimisation problems with more than three objectives. As the number of objectives increases, the number of solutions needed to adequately represent the entire Pareto front typically grows substantially. This makes it challenging, if not infeasible, to design a search algorithm capable of effectively exploring the entire Pareto front. This difficulty is particularly acute in the Bayesian optimisation paradigm, where sample efficiency is critical and only a limited number of solutions (often a few hundred) are evaluated. Moreover, after the optimisation process, the decision-maker eventually selects just one solution for deployment, regardless of how many high-quality, diverse solutions are available. In light of this, we argue an idea that under a very limited evaluation budget, it may be more useful to focus on finding a single solution of the highest possible quality for the decision-maker, rather than aiming to approximate the entire Pareto front as existing many-/multi-objective Bayesian optimisation methods typically do. Bearing this idea in mind, this paper proposes a \underline{s}ingle \underline{p}oint-based \underline{m}ulti-\underline{o}bjective search framework (SPMO) that aims to improve the quality of solutions along a direction that leads to a good tradeoff between objectives. Within SPMO, we present a simple acquisition function, called expected single-point improvement (ESPI), working under both noiseless and noisy scenarios. We show that ESPI can be optimised effectively with gradient-based methods via the sample average approximation (SAA) approach and theoretically prove its convergence guarantees under the SAA. We also empirically demonstrate that the proposed SPMO is computationally tractable and outperforms state-of-the-arts on a wide range of benchmark and real-world problems.

AI.tldr: A single-point-based multi-objective Bayesian optimization framework (SPMO) with an ESPI acquisition to efficiently search for a single high-quality solution under limited evaluations, with convergence guarantees and superior empirical performance.
AI.motivation: In many-objective optimisation, representing the Pareto front becomes impractical under strict evaluation budgets; for Bayesian BO where evaluations are costly and decision-makers ultimately select one solution, focusing on a single high-quality point may be more useful.
AI.method: Proposes SPMO; defines ESPI (expected single-point improvement) acquisition; optimizes it using gradient-based methods via sample average approximation (SAA); provides convergence guarantees under SAA; handles both noiseless and noisy observations.
AI.result: Theoretical convergence under SAA; empirical results show SPMO is computationally tractable and outperforms state-of-the-art methods on diverse benchmarks and real-world problems.
AI.conclusion: Under tight budgets, concentrating search on a single, high-quality solution is viable and beneficial; SPMO with ESPI offers a principled, efficient approach with provable guarantees and strong empirical performance.

title: Physics-Informed Reinforcement Learning of Spatial Density Velocity Potentials for Map-Free Racing
summary: Autonomous racing without prebuilt maps is a grand challenge for embedded robotics that requires kinodynamic planning from instantaneous sensor data at the acceleration and tire friction limits. Out-Of-Distribution (OOD) generalization to various racetrack configurations utilizes Machine Learning (ML) to encode the mathematical relation between sensor data and vehicle actuation for end-to-end control, with implicit localization. These comprise Behavioral Cloning (BC) that is capped to human reaction times and Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) which requires large-scale collisions for comprehensive training that can be infeasible without simulation but is arduous to transfer to reality, thus exhibiting greater performance than BC in simulation, but actuation instability on hardware. This paper presents a DRL method that parameterizes nonlinear vehicle dynamics from the spectral distribution of depth measurements with a non-geometric, physics-informed reward, to infer vehicle time-optimal and overtaking racing controls with an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) that utilizes less than 1% of the computation of BC and model-based DRL. Slaloming from simulation to reality transfer and variance-induced conservatism are eliminated with the combination of a physics engine exploit-aware reward and the replacement of an explicit collision penalty with an implicit truncation of the value horizon. The policy outperforms human demonstrations by 12% in OOD tracks on proportionally scaled hardware, by maximizing the friction circle with tire dynamics that resemble an empirical Pacejka tire model. System identification illuminates a functional bifurcation where the first layer compresses spatial observations to extract digitized track features with higher resolution in corner apexes, and the second encodes nonlinear dynamics.

AI.tldr: A DRL-based autonomous racing method learns nonlinear vehicle dynamics from depth-spectral features using a physics-informed reward, achieving time-optimal and overtaking controls with low computation, strong sim-to-real transfer, and 12% human performance gains on OOD tracks; two-layer network captures track features and nonlinear dynamics.
AI.motivation: Address autonomous racing without prebuilt maps, with robust OOD generalization, and reduce BC latency and DRL training difficulties while enabling real-world deployment.
AI.method: DRL framework parameterizing nonlinear dynamics from spectral depth measurements; physics-informed reward (engine/exploit-aware) and implicit horizon truncation instead of explicit collision penalties; ANN with minimal computation; leverages a physics engine-inspired reward; system identification reveals a two-layer network: first layer extracts spatial track features with higher corner apex resolution, second encodes nonlinear dynamics.
AI.result: Policy outperforms human demonstrations by 12% on OOD tracks using proportionally scaled hardware; aligns tire dynamics with empirical Pacejka-like behavior; demonstrates efficient computation (<1% of BC/model-based DRL) and improved sim-to-real transfer via physics-informed design.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a lightweight, robust DRL approach for time-optimal autonomous racing with strong sim-to-real transfer; reveals a functional bifurcation in the network where early layers focus on track feature extraction and later layers on dynamics.

title: CatalogStitch: Dimension-Aware and Occlusion-Preserving Object Compositing for Catalog Image Generation
comment: CVPR 2026 HiGen Workshop. Project page, https://catalogstitch.github.io
summary: Generative object compositing methods have shown remarkable ability to seamlessly insert objects into scenes. However, when applied to real-world catalog image generation, these methods require tedious manual intervention: users must carefully adjust masks when product dimensions differ, and painstakingly restore occluded elements post-generation. We present CatalogStitch, a set of model-agnostic techniques that automate these corrections, enabling user-friendly content creation. Our dimension-aware mask computation algorithm automatically adapts the target region to accommodate products with different dimensions; users simply provide a product image and background, without manual mask adjustments. Our occlusion-aware hybrid restoration method guarantees pixel-perfect preservation of occluding elements, eliminating post-editing workflows. We additionally introduce CatalogStitch-Eval, a 58-example benchmark covering aspect-ratio mismatch and occlusion-heavy catalog scenarios, together with supplementary PDF and HTML viewers. We evaluate our techniques with three state-of-the-art compositing models (ObjectStitch, OmniPaint, and InsertAnything), demonstrating consistent improvements across diverse catalog scenarios. By reducing manual intervention and automating tedious corrections, our approach transforms generative compositing into a practical, human-friendly tool for production catalog workflows.

AI.tldr: CatalogStitch automates image compositing for catalogs with two key components: a dimension-aware masking system that adapts to product size and an occlusion-aware restoration to preserve occluders, plus a 58-sample benchmark (CatalogStitch-Eval) for evaluation across three state-of-the-art models.
AI.motivation: Manual mask adjustment and post-edit restoration are bottlenecks in real-world catalog image generation; automation is needed to reduce labor and streamline production workflows.
AI.method: Two main modules: (1) dimension-aware mask computation that adapts the target region to product dimensions without manual mask edits; (2) occlusion-aware hybrid restoration that preserves occluding elements pixel-perfect. Also introduce CatalogStitch-Eval, a 58-example benchmark focusing on aspect-ratio mismatch and occlusion-heavy scenarios, with viewers in PDF/HTML.
AI.result: Consistent improvements across diverse catalog scenarios and three models (ObjectStitch, OmniPaint, InsertAnything), reducing manual intervention and automating corrections, demonstrating practical improvements for production catalogs.
AI.conclusion: CatalogStitch enables model-agnostic, automated corrections in generative catalog compositing, making the technology more usable in production workflows and lowering manual editing requirements.

title: Practical Bayesian Inference for Speech SNNs: Uncertainty and Loss-Landscape Smoothing
summary: Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) are naturally suited for speech processing tasks due to their specific dynamics, which allows them to handle temporal data. However, the threshold-based generation of spikes in SNNs intuitively causes an angular or irregular predictive landscape. We explore the effect of using the Bayesian learning approach for the weights on the irregular predictive landscape. For the surrogate-gradient SNNs, we also explore the application of the Improved Variational Online Newton (IVON) approach, which is an efficient variational approach. The performance of the proposed approach is evaluated on the Heidelberg Digits and Speech Commands datasets. The hypothesis is that the Bayesian approach will result in a smoother and more regular predictive landscape, given the angular nature of the deterministic predictive landscape. The experimental evaluation of the proposed approach shows improved performance on the negative log-likelihood and Brier score. Furthermore, the proposed approach has resulted in a smoother and more regular predictive landscape compared to the deterministic approach, based on the one-dimensional slices of the weight space

AI.tldr: Bayesian weight learning (with IVON variational method) smooths the predictive landscape of surrogate-gradient SNNs for speech tasks, yielding better probabilistic metrics and a more regular weight-space landscape than deterministic baselines.
AI.motivation: SNNs' spike-threshold dynamics create angular/irregular predictive landscapes; Bayesian weights and efficient variational approaches can regularize learning and improve probabilistic calibration for temporal speech data.
AI.method: Apply Bayesian learning to SNN weights and use the Improved Variational Online Newton (IVON) approach in surrogate-gradient SNNs; evaluate on Heidelberg Digits and Speech Commands; analyze one-dimensional weight-space slices to assess landscape smoothness.
AI.result: Improved performance on negative log-likelihood and Brier score; observed a smoother and more regular predictive landscape in weight space compared to the deterministic approach.
AI.conclusion: Bayesian-weighted SNNs with IVON training enhance probabilistic calibration and produce smoother optimization landscapes in speech-related tasks.

title: E3-TIR: Enhanced Experience Exploitation for Tool-Integrated Reasoning
summary: While Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated significant potential in Tool-Integrated Reasoning (TIR), existing training paradigms face significant limitations: Zero-RL suffers from inefficient exploration and mode degradation due to a lack of prior guidance, while SFT-then-RL is limited by high data costs and capability plateaus caused by low-entropy collapse. To address these challenges, we propose E3-TIR (Enhanced Experience Exploitation), a warm-up paradigm for the early stages of agent training. Specifically, we formulate training as the dynamic integration of three experience types: Expert Prefixes, Expert Guided, and Self-Exploration. By executing diverse branching exploration around expert "anchors" and employing a mix policy optimization mechanism, we effectively mitigate distribution shifts and resolve optimization conflicts arising from shared prefixes. Our method dynamically adapts the model's knowledge boundaries, effectively balancing exploration diversity with training efficiency.Experimental results demonstrate that E3-TIR achieves a 6 performance improvement over traditional paradigms on tool-use tasks, while requiring less than 10 of the synthetic data. Furthermore, in terms of ROI, a comprehensive metric integrating performance, data cost, and training efficiency we achieve a 1.46x gain compared to baselines. Code is available at https://github.com/yuki-younai/E3-TIR.

AI.tldr: E3-TIR proposes Enhanced Experience Exploitation for early-stage tool-use training in LLMs, integrating three experience types (Expert Prefixes, Expert Guided, Self-Exploration) with branching exploration around expert anchors and mix-policy optimization to reduce distribution shifts and resolve conflicts from shared prefixes; reports 6 performance improvement on tool-use tasks with under 10% synthetic data and 1.46x ROI; code available.
AI.motivation: Current TIR training paradigms (Zero-RL and SFT-then-RL) struggle with inefficient exploration, mode degradation, high data costs, and low-entropy-induced plateaus. E3-TIR targets early-stage training warm-up to balance exploration, data efficiency, and stable optimization.
AI.method: Training is formulated as the dynamic integration of three experience types: Expert Prefixes, Expert Guided, and Self-Exploration. The approach uses diverse branching exploration around expert anchors and a mix policy optimization mechanism to mitigate distribution shifts and conflicts from shared prefixes. It adaptively adjusts the model's knowledge boundaries to balance exploration diversity with training efficiency.
AI.result: Empirical results show E3-TIR achieving a 6 performance improvement on tool-use tasks with less than 10% synthetic data. Additionally, a ROI metric (combining performance, data cost, and training efficiency) yields a 1.46x gain over baselines.
AI.conclusion: E3-TIR effectively accelerates early-stage tool-use training in LLMs by combining diverse experiential signals and adaptive knowledge boundary management, improving performance and data efficiency while maintaining training stability; code is publicly available.

title: DeFakeQ: Enabling Real-Time Deepfake Detection on Edge Devices via Adaptive Bidirectional Quantization
summary: Deepfake detection has become a fundamental component of modern media forensics. Despite significant progress in detection accuracy, most existing methods remain computationally intensive and parameter-heavy, limiting their deployment on resource-constrained edge devices that require real-time, on-site inference. This limitation is particularly critical in an era where mobile devices are extensively used for media-centric applications, including online payments, virtual meetings, and social networking. Meanwhile, due to the unique requirement of capturing extremely subtle forgery artifacts for deepfake detection, state-of-the-art quantization techniques usually underperform for such a challenging task. These fine-grained cues are highly sensitive to model compression and can be easily degraded during quantization, leading to noticeable performance drops. This challenge highlights the need for quantization strategies specifically designed to preserve the discriminative features essential for reliable deepfake detection. To address this gap, we propose DefakeQ, the first quantization framework tailored for deepfake detectors, enabling real-time deployment on edge devices. Our approach introduces a novel adaptive bidirectional compression strategy that simultaneously leverages feature correlations and eliminates redundancy, achieving an effective balance between model compactness and detection performance. Extensive experiments across five benchmark datasets and eleven state-of-the-art backbone detectors demonstrate that DeFakeQ consistently surpasses existing quantization and model compression baselines. Furthermore, we deploy DefakeQ on mobile devices in real-world scenarios, demonstrating its capability for real-time deepfake detection and its practical applicability in edge environments.

AI.tldr: Adaptive bidirectional compression (DefakeQ) quantizes deepfake detectors for edge devices, balancing compactness and detection accuracy while outperforming baselines across benchmarks and enabling real-time mobile deployment.
AI.motivation: Current deepfake detectors are computationally heavy; quantization often degrades the subtle forgery cues required for detection, hindering real-time edge deployment on mobile devices.
AI.method: Propose DefakeQ: an adaptive bidirectional compression framework that exploits feature correlations and removes redundancy to produce compact, discriminative models. Evaluated across five datasets and eleven backbones; deployed in real-world mobile scenarios.
AI.result: DefakeQ consistently surpasses existing quantization and model compression baselines across multiple datasets and backbone detectors; enables real-time deepfake detection on edge/mobile devices.
AI.conclusion: Specialized quantization for deepfake detectors can enable practical edge deployment without substantial loss in detection performance; suggests direction for further optimizations and broader adoption in mobile media-forensics.

title: Evidential Transformation Network: Turning Pretrained Models into Evidential Models for Post-hoc Uncertainty Estimation
summary: Pretrained models have become standard in both vision and language, yet they typically do not provide reliable measures of confidence. Existing uncertainty estimation methods, such as deep ensembles and MC dropout, are often too computationally expensive to deploy in practice. Evidential Deep Learning (EDL) offers a more efficient alternative, but it requires models to be trained to output evidential quantities from the start, which is rarely true for pretrained networks. To enable EDL-style uncertainty estimation in pretrained models, we propose the Evidential Transformation Network (ETN), a lightweight post-hoc module that converts a pretrained predictor into an evidential model. ETN operates in logit space: it learns a sample-dependent affine transformation of the logits and interprets the transformed outputs as parameters of a Dirichlet distribution for uncertainty estimation. We evaluate ETN on image classification and large language model question-answering benchmarks under both in-distribution and out-of-distribution settings. ETN consistently improves uncertainty estimation over post-hoc baselines while preserving accuracy and adding only minimal computational overhead.

AI.tldr: ETN is a lightweight post-hoc module that applies a sample-dependent affine transformation in logit space to pretrained predictors, turning outputs into Dirichlet parameters for uncertainty estimation. It improves uncertainty calibration over post-hoc baselines with minimal overhead on vision and language tasks, under both IID and OOD conditions.
AI.motivation: Pretrained models lack reliable confidence estimates; while methods like deep ensembles and MC dropout exist, they are computationally expensive. Evidential Deep Learning (EDL) offers efficiency but requires models to output evidential quantities during training, which is uncommon for pretrained networks. A post-hoc approach is needed to enable EDL-like uncertainty estimation without retraining.
AI.method: Introduce Evidential Transformation Network (ETN), a lightweight post-hoc module that learns a sample-dependent affine transformation of the logits in logit space. The transformed logits are interpreted as Dirichlet parameters, enabling uncertainty estimation. The module is evaluated on image classification and large language model QA benchmarks under both in-distribution and out-of-distribution settings, comparing against post-hoc baselines and observing minimal overhead and preserved accuracy.
AI.result: ETN consistently improves uncertainty estimation over post-hoc baselines while preserving accuracy and adding only minimal computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: ETN provides a practical, lightweight means to retrofit pretrained models with EDL-style uncertainty estimation, broadening applicability to both vision and language tasks without retraining or extensive computation.

title: Process Reward Agents for Steering Knowledge-Intensive Reasoning
summary: Reasoning in knowledge-intensive domains remains challenging as intermediate steps are often not locally verifiable: unlike math or code, evaluating step correctness may require synthesizing clues across large external knowledge sources. As a result, subtle errors can propagate through reasoning traces, potentially never to be detected. Prior work has proposed process reward models (PRMs), including retrieval-augmented variants, but these methods operate post hoc, scoring completed trajectories, which prevents their integration into dynamic inference procedures. Here, we introduce Process Reward Agents (PRA), a test-time method for providing domain-grounded, online, step-wise rewards to a frozen policy. In contrast to prior retrieval-augmented PRMs, PRA enables search-based decoding to rank and prune candidate trajectories at every generation step. Experiments on multiple medical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that PRA consistently outperforms strong baselines, achieving 80.8% accuracy on MedQA with Qwen3-4B, a new state of the art at the 4B scale. Importantly, PRA generalizes to unseen frozen policy models ranging from 0.5B to 8B parameters, improving their accuracy by up to 25.7% without any policy model updates. More broadly, PRA suggests a paradigm in which frozen reasoners are decoupled from domain-specific reward modules, allowing the deployment of new backbones in complex domains without retraining.

AI.tldr: PRA provides online, step-wise rewards to a frozen policy at test time, enabling search-based decoding across steps; achieves state-of-the-art MedQA performance on a 4B model and generalizes across unseen backbones without retraining.
AI.motivation: Reasoning in knowledge-intensive domains is hard due to non-local verifiability of intermediate steps; prior post-hoc process reward models cannot guide inference in real time.
AI.method: Introduce Process Reward Agents (PRA) that supply domain-grounded rewards during generation to a frozen policy, enabling pruning/ranking of candidate trajectories at each generation step via search-based decoding.
AI.result: On medical reasoning benchmarks, PRA achieves 80.8% accuracy on MedQA with Qwen3-4B (state-of-the-art at 4B). generalizes to unseen frozen policies from 0.5B to 8B, boosting performance by up to 25.7% without policy updates.
AI.conclusion: PRA decouples domain-specific rewards from the frozen reasoner, allowing new backbones to be deployed without retraining and enabling dynamic, step-wise guidance during inference.

title: BIAS: A Biologically Inspired Algorithm for Video Saliency Detection
summary: We present BIAS, a fast, biologically inspired model for dynamic visual saliency detection in continuous video streams. Building on the Itti--Koch framework, BIAS incorporates a retina-inspired motion detector to extract temporal features, enabling the generation of saliency maps that integrate both static and motion information. Foci of attention (FOAs) are identified using a greedy multi-Gaussian peak-fitting algorithm that balances winner-take-all competition with information maximization. BIAS detects salient regions with millisecond-scale latency and outperforms heuristic-based approaches and several deep-learning models on the DHF1K dataset, particularly in videos dominated by bottom-up attention. Applied to traffic accident analysis, BIAS demonstrates strong real-world utility, achieving state-of-the-art performance in cause-effect recognition and anticipating accidents up to 0.72 seconds before manual annotation with reliable accuracy. Overall, BIAS bridges biological plausibility and computational efficiency to achieve interpretable, high-speed dynamic saliency detection.

AI.tldr: BIAS: a fast, biologically inspired dynamic saliency model that integrates static and motion cues, identifies attention foci via peak-fitting, and achieves rapid, accurate saliency and early accident anticipation on DHF1K and traffic analysis.
AI.motivation: address the need for fast, interpretable dynamic saliency detection with biological plausibility and practical applicability.
AI.method: extend Itti–Koch framework with a retina-inspired motion detector; identify foas via a greedy multi-Gaussian peak-fitting algorithm balancing winner-take-all with information maximization; evaluate on DHF1K; apply to traffic accident analysis for cause-effect recognition and anticipation.
AI.result: demonstrates millisecond-scale latency; outperforms heuristic methods and several deep-learning models on DHF1K, especially in videos dominated by bottom-up attention; achieves state-of-the-art in cause-effect recognition for traffic accidents, anticipating up to 0.72 seconds before manual annotation with reliable accuracy.
AI.conclusion: BIAS combines biological plausibility with computational efficiency to deliver fast, interpretable dynamic saliency detection suitable for bottom-up dominated video streams and real-world analytics.

title: VOLTA: The Surprising Ineffectiveness of Auxiliary Losses for Calibrated Deep Learning
summary: Uncertainty quantification (UQ) is essential for deploying deep learning models in safety critical applications, yet no consensus exists on which UQ method performs best across different data modalities and distribution shifts. This paper presents a comprehensive benchmark of ten widely used UQ baselines including MC Dropout, SWAG, ensemble methods, temperature scaling, energy based OOD, Mahalanobis, hyperbolic classifiers, ENN, Taylor Sensus, and split conformal prediction against a simplified yet highly effective variant of VOLTA that retains only a deep encoder, learnable prototypes, cross entropy loss, and post hoc temperature scaling. We evaluate all methods on CIFAR 10 (in distribution), CIFAR 100, SVHN, uniform noise (out of distribution), CIFAR 10 C (corruptions), and Tiny ImageNet features (tabular). VOLTA achieves competitive or superior accuracy (up to 0.864 on CIFAR 10), significantly lower expected calibration error (0.010 vs. 0.044 to 0.102 for baselines), and strong OOD detection (AUROC 0.802). Statistical testing over three random seeds shows that VOLTA matches or outperforms most baselines, with ablation studies confirming the importance of adaptive temperature and deep encoders. Our results establish VOLTA as a lightweight, deterministic, and well calibrated alternative to more complex UQ approaches.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive benchmark compares ten UQ baselines with a VOLTA variant; VOLTA shows competitive accuracy, exceptionally low calibration error, and solid OOD detection, suggesting a lightweight, deterministic alternative to more complex UQ methods.
AI.motivation: There is no consensus on which UQ method consistently performs best across data modalities and distribution shifts; a fair, comprehensive, and scalable benchmark is needed, along with a lightweight method.
AI.method: Evaluate ten UQ baselines (MC Dropout, SWAG, ensembles, temperature scaling, energy-based OOD, Mahalanobis, hyperbolic classifiers, ENN, Taylor Sensus, split conformal prediction) against a simplified VOLTA variant retaining a deep encoder, learnable prototypes, cross-entropy loss, and post hoc temperature scaling. Experiments span CIFAR-10 (in-distribution), CIFAR-100, SVHN, uniform noise (OOD), CIFAR-10-C, and Tiny ImageNet features (tabular). Metrics include accuracy, expected calibration error (ECE), and AUROC for OOD detection; statistical testing over three seeds; ablations on adaptive temperature and deep encoder components.
AI.result: VOLTA achieves competitive or superior accuracy (up to 0.864 on CIFAR-10), markedly lower ECE (0.010 vs. 0.044 to 0.102 for baselines), and solid OOD AUROC (0.802). Results are stable across seeds; ablations confirm the importance of adaptive temperature and deep encoders.
AI.conclusion: A lightweight, deterministic, and well-calibrated alternative to complex UQ approaches. VOLTA can match or outperform many baselines with simpler architecture and post-hoc calibration.

title: Strategic Algorithmic Monoculture:Experimental Evidence from Coordination Games
summary: AI agents increasingly operate in multi-agent environments where outcomes depend on coordination. We distinguish primary algorithmic monoculture -- baseline action similarity -- from strategic algorithmic monoculture, whereby agents adjust similarity in response to incentives. We implement a simple experimental design that cleanly separates these forces, and deploy it on human and large language model (LLM) subjects. LLMs exhibit high levels of baseline similarity (primary monoculture) and, like humans, they regulate it in response to coordination incentives (strategic monoculture). While LLMs coordinate extremely well on similar actions, they lag behind humans in sustaining heterogeneity when divergence is rewarded.

AI.tldr: LLMs show strong baseline action similarity (primary monoculture) and adjust similarity in response to coordination incentives (strategic monoculture). They coordinate well on similar actions but struggle to sustain heterogeneity when divergence is rewarded, unlike humans.
AI.motivation: Disentangle how default action similarity and incentive-driven strategic adjustment shape coordination in AI agents, comparing humans and LLMs.
AI.method: Experimental design that cleanly separates primary monoculture from strategic monoculture, applied to both human participants and LLM subjects; measure baseline similarity, changes under incentives, and coordination/heterogeneity outcomes.
AI.result: LLMs exhibit high baseline similarity and regulate it in response to incentives; they coordinate extremely well on similar actions but lag humans in sustaining heterogeneity when divergence is rewarded.
AI.conclusion: Distinguishing primary vs strategic monoculture reveals distinct coordination dynamics: LLMs converge on common actions but have difficulty maintaining diverse strategies under reward for divergence; humans sustain heterogeneity better. Implications for designing AI agents in mixed human–AI teams.

title: Harnessing Weak Pair Uncertainty for Text-based Person Search
summary: In this paper, we study the text-based person search, which is to retrieve the person of interest via natural language description. Prevailing methods usually focus on the strict one-to-one correspondence pair matching between the visual and textual modality, such as contrastive learning. However, such a paradigm unintentionally disregards the weak positive image-text pairs, which are of the same person but the text descriptions are annotated from different views (cameras). To take full use of weak positives, we introduce an uncertainty-aware method to explicitly estimate image-text pair uncertainty, and incorporate the uncertainty into the optimization procedure in a smooth manner. Specifically, our method contains two modules: uncertainty estimation and uncertainty regularization. (1) Uncertainty estimation is to obtain the relative confidence on the given positive pairs; (2) Based on the predicted uncertainty, we propose the uncertainty regularization to adaptively adjust loss weight. Additionally, we introduce a group-wise image-text matching loss to further facilitate the representation space among the weak pairs. Compared with existing methods, the proposed method explicitly prevents the model from pushing away potentially weak positive candidates. Extensive experiments on three widely-used datasets, .e.g, CUHK-PEDES, RSTPReid and ICFG-PEDES, verify the mAP improvement of our method against existing competitive methods +3.06%, +3.55% and +6.94%, respectively.

AI.tldr: Uncertainty-aware weak-positive handling for text-based person search; uses uncertainty estimation and adaptive loss weighting plus group-wise matching; shows mAP gains on three datasets.
AI.motivation: Address the neglect of weak positive image-text pairs in strict one-to-one contrastive learning; exploit cross-view positives to improve retrieval.
AI.method: Two modules: (1) uncertainty estimation to assign confidence to image-text positive pairs; (2) uncertainty regularization to adaptively weight loss based on predicted uncertainty; plus a group-wise image-text matching loss to reinforce weak positives and better align representations across views.
AI.result: Outperforms baselines with notable mAP gains: CUHK-PEDES +3.06%, RSTPReid +3.55%, ICFG-PEDES +6.94%.
AI.conclusion: An uncertainty-aware framework effectively leverages weak positives in cross-modal person search, improving cross-view alignment and overall retrieval performance; potential applicability to other cross-modal tasks with uncertain positives.

title: Creator Incentives in Recommender Systems: A Cooperative Game-Theoretic Approach for Stable and Fair Collaboration in Multi-Agent Bandits
comment: Accepted in AISTATS 2026 as an Oral Presentation
summary:
User interactions in online recommendation platforms create interdependencies among content creators: feedback on one creator's content influences the system's learning and, in turn, the exposure of other creators' contents. To analyze incentives in such settings, we model collaboration as a multi-agent stochastic linear bandit problem with a transferable utility (TU) cooperative game formulation, where a coalition's value equals the negative sum of its members' cumulative regrets.
  We show that, for identical (homogenous) agents with fixed action sets, the induced TU game is convex under mild algorithmic conditions, implying a non-empty core that contains the Shapley value and ensures both stability and fairness. For heterogeneous agents, the game still admits a non-empty core, though convexity and Shapley value core-membership are no longer guaranteed. To address this, we propose a simple regret-based payout rule that satisfies three out of the four Shapley axioms and also lies in the core. Experiments on MovieLens-100k dataset illustrate when the empirical payout aligns with -- and diverges from -- the Shapley fairness across different settings and algorithms.

AI.tldr: TU cooperative game for multi-agent bandits; core non-empty for homogeneous agents (convexity) and non-empty for heterogeneous; Shapley value in core for homogeneous; proposed regret-based payout approximates Shapley and lies in core; MovieLens experiments show varying alignment with Shapley fairness.
AI.motivation: Understand incentives and fairness in collaborative content creation under feedback loops in online recommendation platforms; ensure stability via game-theoretic core and Shapley fairness.
AI.method: Model collaboration as a transferable-utility cooperative game where a coalition’s value equals the negative sum of member regrets in a multi-agent stochastic linear bandit. Prove convexity and non-empty core for homogeneous agents; show non-emptiness of core for heterogeneous agents; propose a regret-based payout rule satisfying 3/4 Shapley axioms and lying in the core; validate with experiments on MovieLens-100k across algorithms.
AI.result: For homogeneous agents, the induced TU game is convex with a non-empty core that contains the Shapley value, ensuring stability and fairness. For heterogeneous agents, the core remains non-empty but convexity and Shapley-core-membership are not guaranteed. A regret-based payout rule is proposed that satisfies three of four Shapley axioms and lies in the core. Empirical results show varying alignment with Shapley fairness across settings and algorithms.
AI.conclusion: The work provides conditions under which stability and fairness (via core/Shapley) can be guaranteed in cooperative bandit settings; homogeneity yields stronger guarantees (convexity and Shapley in core), while heterogeneity necessitates approximate fairness with a payout rule that remains core-compatible; experiments illustrate the practical relevance and limitations of Shapley-based fairness.

title: On Divergence Measures for Training GFlowNets
summary: Generative Flow Networks (GFlowNets) are amortized inference models designed to sample from unnormalized distributions over composable objects, with applications in generative modeling for tasks in fields such as causal discovery, NLP, and drug discovery. Traditionally, the training procedure for GFlowNets seeks to minimize the expected log-squared difference between a proposal (forward policy) and a target (backward policy) distribution, which enforces certain flow-matching conditions. While this training procedure is closely related to variational inference (VI), directly attempting standard Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence minimization can lead to proven biased and potentially high-variance estimators. Therefore, we first review four divergence measures, namely, Renyi-$α$'s, Tsallis-$α$'s, reverse and forward KL's, and design statistically efficient estimators for their stochastic gradients in the context of training GFlowNets. Then, we verify that properly minimizing these divergences yields a provably correct and empirically effective training scheme, often leading to significantly faster convergence than previously proposed optimization. To achieve this, we design control variates based on the REINFORCE leave-one-out and score-matching estimators to reduce the variance of the learning objectives' gradients. Our work contributes by narrowing the gap between GFlowNets training and generalized variational approximations, paving the way for algorithmic ideas informed by the divergence minimization viewpoint.

AI.tldr: Minimize generalized divergences (Renyi and Tsallis, plus forward/reverse KL) for training GFlowNets using variance-reduced stochastic gradients, yielding faster, provably correct learning and tighter connections to variational inference.
AI.motivation: Standard GFlowNet training via log-squared forward/backward policy differences is related to VI but KL-based optimization can be biased/high-variance. A divergence-minimization view with efficient estimators can improve convergence and theoretical guarantees.
AI.method: Derive statistically efficient stochastic gradient estimators for Renyi-α, Tsallis-α, forward KL, and reverse KL divergences in the GFlowNet setting. Develop control variates based on REINFORCE leave-one-out and score-matching to reduce gradient variance. Validate that divergence-minimizing training yields correct, fast-converging GFlowNets and empirically outperform prior optimization approaches.
AI.result: The proposed divergent-minimization framework provides provable correctness and empirically faster convergence for training GFlowNets. Variance reduction via REINFORCE LO and score-matching control variates significantly improves gradient estimates, narrowing the gap between GFlowNets and generalized variational methods.
AI.conclusion: Minimizing a spectrum of divergences within GFlowNets offers a principled training paradigm with better convergence and theoretical guarantees, enabling algorithmic ideas from generalized variational inference to inform GFlowNet design and optimization.

title: MASS: Mesh-inellipse Aligned Deformable Surfel Splatting for Hand Reconstruction and Rendering from Egocentric Monocular Video
comment: This paper has been accepted to CVM 2026 Journal Track and is under consideration for publication in IEEE TVCG
summary: Reconstructing high-fidelity 3D hands from egocentric monocular videos remains a challenge due to the limitations in capturing high-resolution geometry, hand-object interactions, and complex objects on hands. Additionally, existing methods often incur high computational costs, making them impractical for real-time applications. In this work, we propose Mesh-inellipse Aligned deformable Surfel Splatting (MASS) to address these challenges by leveraging a deformable 2D Gaussian Surfel representation. We introduce the mesh-aligned Steiner Inellipse and fractal densification for mesh-to-surfel conversion that initiates high-resolution 2D Gaussian surfels from coarse parametric hand meshes, providing surface representation with photorealistic rendering potential. Second, we propose Gaussian Surfel Deformation, which enables efficient modeling of hand deformations and personalized features by predicting residual updates to surfel attributes and introducing an opacity mask to refine geometry and texture without adaptive density control. In addition, we propose a two-stage training strategy and a novel binding loss to improve the optimization robustness and reconstruction quality. Extensive experiments on the ARCTIC dataset, the Hand Appearance dataset, and the Interhand2.6M dataset demonstrate that our model achieves superior reconstruction performance compared to state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: MASS presents a deformable 2D Gaussian surfel framework to reconstruct high-fidelity 3D hands from egocentric monocular videos, using mesh-aligned inellipse and fractal densification for mesh-to-surfel conversion, Gaussian surfel deformation with residual updates and an opacity mask, plus a two-stage training strategy and a binding loss; it achieves state-of-the-art reconstruction across ARCTIC, Hand Appearance, and Interhand2.6M datasets.
AI.motivation: Reconstructing high-fidelity 3D hands from egocentric monocular videos is challenging due to limited high-resolution geometry capture, intricate hand-object interactions, and high computational cost, which hinders real-time applicability.
AI.method: Introduce MASS: a mesh-aligned Steiner Inellipse and fractal densification to convert coarse parametric hand meshes into high-resolution 2D Gaussian surfels; predict residual surfel attribute updates (Gaussian Surfel Deformation) and apply an opacity mask to refine geometry/textures without adaptive density control; employ a two-stage training strategy and a novel binding loss to improve optimization robustness and reconstruction quality.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on ARCTIC, Hand Appearance, and Interhand2.6M datasets shows MASS achieves superior reconstruction quality compared with state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: The MASS framework provides an effective, efficient deformable surfel-based representation for high-fidelity 3D hand reconstruction from monocular egocentric videos, with robust optimization and potential for photorealistic rendering.

title: Precise Shield: Explaining and Aligning VLLM Safety via Neuron-Level Guidance
summary: In real-world deployments, Vision-Language Large Models (VLLMs) face critical challenges from multilingual and multimodal composite attacks: harmful images paired with low-resource language texts can easily bypass defenses designed for high-resource language scenarios, exposing structural blind spots in current cross-lingual and cross-modal safety methods. This raises a mechanistic question: where is safety capability instantiated within the model, and how is it distributed across languages and modalities? Prior studies on pure-text LLMs have identified cross-lingual shared safety neurons, suggesting that safety may be governed by a small subset of critical neurons. Leveraging this insight, we propose Precise Shield, a two-stage framework that first identifies safety neurons by contrasting activation patterns between harmful and benign inputs, and then constrains parameter updates strictly within this subspace via gradient masking with affecting fewer than 0.03% of parameters. This strategy substantially improves safety while preserving multilingual and multimodal generalization. Further analysis reveals a moderate overlap of safety neurons across languages and modalities, enabling zero-shot cross-lingual and cross-modal transfer of safety capabilities, and offering a new direction for neuron-level, transfer-based safety enhancement.

AI.tldr: Precise Shield identifies a small set of safety neurons by contrasting activations for harmful vs. benign inputs, and locks updates to this subspace via gradient masking (<0.03% of parameters). It improves safety in Vision-Language LLMs against multilingual/multimodal attacks while preserving multilingual/multimodal performance, and enables zero-shot transfer of safety across languages and modalities.
AI.motivation: Address multilingual and multimodal safety gaps in Vision-Language LLMs (VLLMs). Prior work shows cross-lingual safety neurons in pure-text LLMs; it is unknown how safety capabilities distribute across languages and modalities in VLLMs and under composite attacks.
AI.method: A two-stage approach: (1) identify safety neurons by contrasting activation patterns between harmful and benign inputs; (2) constrain updates to the identified subspace using gradient masking so that fewer than 0.03% of parameters are affected.
AI.result: The approach yields substantial safety improvements without sacrificing multilingual and multimodal generalization. There is a moderate overlap of safety neurons across languages and modalities, enabling zero-shot cross-lingual and cross-modal transfer of safety capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Safety capabilities in VLLMs appear concentrated in a small neuron subset; neuron-level, transfer-based strategies can enhance cross-lingual and cross-modal safety, suggesting a scalable path for multi-language, multi-modal safety without broad parameter changes.

title: PRAGMA: Revolut Foundation Model
summary: Modern financial systems generate vast quantities of transactional and event-level data that encode rich economic signals. This paper presents PRAGMA, a family of foundation models for multi-source banking event sequences. Our approach pre-trains a Transformer-based architecture with masked modelling on a large-scale, heterogeneous banking event corpus using a self-supervised objective tailored to the discrete, variable-length nature of financial records. The resulting model supports a wide range of downstream tasks such as credit scoring, fraud detection, and lifetime value prediction: strong performance can be achieved by training a simple linear model on top of the extracted embeddings and can be further improved with lightweight fine-tuning. Through extensive evaluation on downstream tasks, we demonstrate that PRAGMA achieves superior performance across multiple domains directly from raw event sequences, providing a general-purpose representation layer for financial applications.

AI.tldr: PRAGMA is a Transformer-based foundation model for multi-source banking event sequences, pre-trained with masked self-supervision on large heterogeneous data to create embeddings applicable to credit scoring, fraud detection, and lifetime value prediction; strong performance with linear heads and lightweight fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: To unlock rich predictive signals from raw, multi-source banking event data and provide a general-purpose representation for diverse financial tasks without task-specific models.
AI.method: Pre-train a Transformer with masked modelling on a large-scale, heterogeneous banking event corpus; tailor objective for discrete, variable-length financial records; evaluate by training simple linear models on embeddings and perform lightweight fine-tuning.
AI.result: PRAGMA embeddings yield strong performance across downstream tasks; linear models suffice, with additional gains from fine-tuning; demonstrates a versatile representation layer for financial applications.
AI.conclusion: PRAGMA offers a broad, general-purpose foundation model for financial event data, enabling efficient transfer to multiple tasks directly from raw sequences.

title: TouchAnything: Diffusion-Guided 3D Reconstruction from Sparse Robot Touches
summary: Accurate object geometry estimation is essential for many downstream tasks, including robotic manipulation and physical interaction. Although vision is the dominant modality for shape perception, it becomes unreliable under occlusions or challenging lighting conditions. In such scenarios, tactile sensing provides direct geometric information through physical contact. However, reconstructing global 3D geometry from sparse local touches alone is fundamentally underconstrained. We present TouchAnything, a framework that leverages a pretrained large-scale 2D vision diffusion model as a semantic and geometric prior for 3D reconstruction from sparse tactile measurements. Unlike prior work that trains category-specific reconstruction networks or learns diffusion models directly from tactile data, we transfer the geometric knowledge encoded in pretrained visual diffusion models to the tactile domain. Given sparse contact constraints and a coarse class-level description of the object, we formulate reconstruction as an optimization problem that enforces tactile consistency while guiding solutions toward shapes consistent with the diffusion prior. Our method reconstructs accurate geometries from only a few touches, outperforms existing baselines, and enables open-world 3D reconstruction of previously unseen object instances. Our project page is https://grange007.github.io/touchanything .

AI.tldr: TouchAnything uses a pretrained large-scale 2D vision diffusion model as a prior to reconstruct 3D geometry from very sparse tactile measurements. It transfers visual diffusion priors to the tactile domain, avoiding category-specific training or diffusion modeling on tactile data, and formulates an optimization that enforces tactile consistency while aligning with the diffusion prior.
AI.motivation: Vision-based shape perception struggles under occlusion and poor lighting; tactile sensing provides local geometry but is underconstrained for global 3D reconstruction. A strong, general prior is needed to regularize sparse tactile data into plausible 3D shapes, enabling open-world reconstruction without task-specific training.
AI.method: Given sparse contact constraints and a coarse object class description, formulate an optimization that enforces tactile consistency and steers solutions toward shapes that are compatible with a pretrained 2D diffusion model's priors. The diffusion model is used as a semantic and geometric prior transferred from vision to touch; unlike prior works, there is no category-specific tactile reconstruction network or diffusion training on tactile data.
AI.result: The method reconstructs accurate geometries from only a few touches, outperforming existing baselines, and enables open-world 3D reconstruction of previously unseen object instances.
AI.conclusion: Cross-modal diffusion priors learned from vision can be effectively leveraged for tactile-based 3D reconstruction, enabling robust, open-world object geometry recovery from sparse tactile data with minimal tactile input.

title: HM-Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Multimodal Large Language Models in Hyperspectral Remote Sensing
summary: While multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have made significant strides in natural image understanding, their ability to perceive and reason over hyperspectral image (HSI) remains underexplored, which is a vital modality in remote sensing. The high dimensionality and intricate spectral-spatial properties of HSI pose unique challenges for models primarily trained on RGB data.To address this gap, we introduce Hyperspectral Multimodal Benchmark (HM-Bench), the first benchmark designed specifically to evaluate MLLMs in HSI understanding. We curate a large-scale dataset of 19,337 question-answer pairs across 13 task categories, ranging from basic perception to spectral reasoning. Given that existing MLLMs are not equipped to process raw hyperspectral cubes natively, we propose a dual-modality evaluation framework that transforms HSI data into two complementary representations: PCA-based composite images and structured textual reports. This approach facilitates a systematic comparison of different representation for model performance. Extensive evaluations on 18 representative MLLMs reveal significant difficulties in handling complex spatial-spectral reasoning tasks. Furthermore, our results demonstrate that visual inputs generally outperform textual inputs, highlighting the importance of grounding in spectral-spatial evidence for effective HSI understanding. Dataset and appendix can be accessed at https://github.com/HuoRiLi-Yu/HM-Bench.

AI.tldr: HM-Bench: first benchmark for evaluating multimodal LLMs on hyperspectral imagery; uses PCA-based images and structured textual reports; evaluates 18 models; visuals outperform text; dataset at GitHub.
AI.motivation: HSI's high dimensionality and spectral-spatial complexity are poorly supported by existing MLLMs; a standardized benchmark is needed to study perception and reasoning on hyperspectral data.
AI.method: Create HM-Bench dataset with 19,337 QA pairs across 13 task categories; propose dual-modality representations (PCA composite images and structured textual reports) to enable MLLMs to process HSI; evaluate 18 representative MLLMs; analyze results.
AI.result: Significant difficulties in complex spatial-spectral reasoning; visual inputs outperform textual inputs; grounding in spectral-spatial evidence is critical for HSI understanding.
AI.conclusion: HM-Bench provides a systematic framework and dataset for evaluating MLLMs on HSI, highlighting current gaps in spectral-spatial reasoning and grounding; dataset and appendix available at the project repository.

title: Skip-Connected Policy Optimization for Implicit Advantage
summary: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) has proven effective in RLVR by using outcome-based rewards. While fine-grained dense rewards can theoretically improve performance, we reveal that under practical sampling budgets, Monte Carlo estimation yields high-variance and sign-inconsistent advantages for early reasoning tokens, paradoxically underperforming outcome-only GRPO. We propose Skip-Connected Optimization (SKPO), which decomposes reasoning into upstream and downstream phases: upstream receives dense rewards from downstream Monte Carlo sampling with single-stream optimization; downstream maintains group-relative optimization, where a skip connection concatenates the upstream segment with the original problem, enabling the model to leverage helpful upstream reasoning while preserving the freedom to bypass flawed reasoning through direct problem access. Experiments demonstrate improvements of 3.91% and 6.17% relative gains over the strongest baselines on Qwen2.5-Math-7B and Llama-3.2-3B respectively across mathematical benchmarks and out-of-domain tasks including general reasoning and code generation. Further analysis reveals an implicit advantage: SKPO generates trajectories with higher intermediate-step quality even when matched for final correctness.

AI.tldr: SKPO introduces a skip-connected optimization that splits reasoning into an upstream dense-reward phase and a downstream group-relative phase, improving stability and intermediate-step quality, and achieving gains on math and general-reasoning benchmarks over GRPO baselines.
AI.motivation: To address high-variance, sign-inconsistent advantages of early reasoning tokens under practical sampling budgets when using dense rewards in GRPO, by enabling useful upstream reasoning while preserving the ability to bypass flawed steps.
AI.method: Decompose reasoning into two phases: upstream receives dense rewards via downstream Monte Carlo sampling with single-stream optimization; downstream uses group-relative optimization with a skip connection that concatenates the upstream segment with the original problem, enabling effective use of upstream reasoning while retaining access to the raw problem.
AI.result: Empirical gains of 3.91% and 6.17% relative over strongest baselines on Qwen2.5-Math-7B and Llama-3.2-3B across math benchmarks and out-of-domain tasks (general reasoning and code generation); SKPO also yields higher intermediate-step quality trajectories when final correctness is matched.
