AI.result: Fixed per-step computation regardless of program length/history; fixed parameter budget; supports running any compiled program with a single fixed-weight model; demonstrates practical capability via Sudoku solver in a compact config; source code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: Loom demonstrates that fixed-weight neural architectures can act as universal interpreters for compiled programs, delivering predictable compute costs and programmability; the approach is open-source for further experimentation and evaluation.

title: How Should Video LLMs Output Time? An Analysis of Efficient Temporal Grounding Paradigms
summary: While Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have advanced Video Temporal Grounding (VTG), existing methods often couple output paradigms with different backbones, datasets, and training protocols. This makes it challenging to isolate the specific impact of the output design. Additionally, as VTG systems are increasingly considered for resource-constrained edge deployment, the trade-off between output formulation and system-level efficiency requires systematic investigation. In this paper, we present a controlled empirical study comparing three dominant VTG output paradigms: Text Numeral Generation, Temporal Token Generation, and Continuous Temporal Decoding. We evaluate these paradigms across identical compact VLMs (SmolVLM2, FastVLM, and Molmo2) using consistent datasets and LoRA fine-tuning protocols. Evaluations on Charades-STA, QVHighlights, and YouCook2 measure both localization accuracy and system efficiency, including inference latency, training throughput, and parameter overhead. Our results demonstrate that the choice of output formulation significantly affects both grounding accuracy and computational cost, independent of model scale. Specifically, the continuous distribution paradigm consistently achieves the most favorable efficiency-accuracy trade-off on the Pareto frontier, delivering robust localization with minimal latency overhead. These findings provide objective empirical guidelines for designing efficient, deployment-ready VTG systems.

AI.tldr: The paper conducts a controlled empirical study comparing three VTG output paradigms—Text Numeral Generation, Temporal Token Generation, and Continuous Temporal Decoding—across identical compact VLMs (SmolVLM2, FastVLM, Molmo2) on Charades-STA, QVHighlights, and YouCook2. It evaluates both grounding accuracy and system efficiency (inference latency, training throughput, parameter overhead). The continuous temporal decoding paradigm consistently offers the best efficiency-accuracy trade-off on the Pareto frontier, independent of model scale, providing guidelines for deployment-ready VTG systems.
AI.motivation: Existing VTG literature often mixes output paradigms with varying backbones, datasets, and training protocols, making it hard to isolate the effect of output design; with edge deployment in mind, it is crucial to understand the efficiency-accuracy trade-offs of different output formulations.
AI.method: A controlled experiment using identical compact VLMs (SmolVLM2, FastVLM, Molmo2) with LoRA fine-tuning across three VTG output paradigms (Text Numeral Generation, Temporal Token Generation, Continuous Temporal Decoding) evaluated on Charades-STA, QVHighlights, and YouCook2. Metrics include localization accuracy and system efficiency: inference latency, training throughput, and parameter overhead.
AI.result: Continuous Temporal Decoding (continuous distribution paradigm) yields the most favorable efficiency-accuracy Pareto frontier, delivering robust localization with minimal latency overhead. The output formulation materially affects both accuracy and computational cost, independent of model scale.
AI.conclusion: The study provides objective empirical guidelines for designing efficient, deployment-ready VTG systems, with continuous temporal decoding recommended for favorable trade-offs; suggests further work to expand model/dataset coverage and deeper ablations on the output design.

title: HiFloat4 Format for Language Model Pre-training on Ascend NPUs
summary: Large foundation models have become central to modern machine learning, with performance scaling predictably with model size and data. However, training and deploying such models incur substantial computational and memory costs, motivating the development of low-precision training techniques. Recent work has demonstrated that 4-bit floating-point (FP4) formats--such as MXFP4 and NVFP4--can be successfully applied to linear GEMM operations in large language models (LLMs), achieving up to 4x improvements in compute throughput and memory efficiency compared to higher-precision baselines. In this work, we investigate the recently proposed HiFloat4 FP4 format for Huawei Ascend NPUs and systematically compare it with MXFP4 in large-scale training settings. All experiments are conducted on Ascend NPU clusters, with linear and expert GEMM operations performed entirely in FP4 precision. We evaluate both dense architectures (e.g., Pangu and LLaMA-style models) and mixture-of-experts (MoE) models, where both standard linear layers and expert-specific GEMMs operate in FP4. Furthermore, we explore stabilization techniques tailored to FP4 training that significantly reduce numerical degradation, maintaining relative error within 1% of full-precision baselines while preserving the efficiency benefits of 4-bit computation. Our results provide a comprehensive empirical study of FP4 training on NPUs and highlight the practical trade-offs between FP4 formats in large-scale dense and MoE models.

AI.tldr: Empirical comparison of FP4 formats (HiFloat4 vs MXFP4) for training large models on Ascend NPUs, including dense and MoE; shows stabilization can keep errors near FP32 and delivers up to 4x throughput/memory efficiency gains vs higher precision.
AI.motivation: Large foundation models incur high compute/memory costs; FP4 formats offer substantial efficiency. The work provides a systematic, hardware-specific comparison (HiFloat4 vs MXFP4) and stabilization techniques to guide FP4 training on NPUs across dense and Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models.
AI.method: Experiments on Ascend NPU clusters with FP4-only linear and expert GEMMs. Compare HiFloat4 and MXFP4 in dense models (Pangu, LLaMA-like) and MoE models. Apply stabilization techniques to reduce numerical degradation and measure relative error against full-precision baselines; report compute throughput and memory efficiency.
AI.result: FP4 formats enable significant efficiency gains (up to ~4x) over higher-precision baselines. Stabilization techniques reduce numerical degradation, keeping relative error within ~1% of FP32 baselines. The study provides comprehensive empirical comparisons of FP4 formats for both dense and MoE training on Huawei Ascend NPUs.
AI.conclusion: FP4 training on NPUs is viable with proper stabilization. The choice between HiFloat4 and MXFP4 involves trade-offs that depend on model type and hardware specifics, but both formats deliver substantial compute/memory benefits for dense and MoE models while maintaining acceptable accuracy.

title: ActFER: Agentic Facial Expression Recognition via Active Tool-Augmented Visual Reasoning
summary: Recent advances in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have created new opportunities for facial expression recognition (FER), moving it beyond pure label prediction toward reasoning-based affect understanding. However, existing MLLM-based FER methods still follow a passive paradigm: they rely on externally prepared facial inputs and perform single-pass reasoning over fixed visual evidence, without the capability for active facial perception. To address this limitation, we propose ActFER, an agentic framework that reformulates FER as active visual evidence acquisition followed by multimodal reasoning. Specifically, ActFER dynamically invokes tools for face detection and alignment, selectively zooms into informative local regions, and reasons over facial Action Units (AUs) and emotions through a visual Chain-of-Thought. To realize such behavior, we further develop Utility-Calibrated GRPO (UC-GRPO), a reinforcement learning algorithm tailored to agentic FER. UC-GRPO uses AU-grounded multi-level verifiable rewards to densify supervision, query-conditional contrastive utility estimation to enable sample-aware dynamic credit assignment for local inspection, and emotion-aware EMA calibration to reduce noisy utility estimates while capturing emotion-wise inspection tendencies. This algorithm enables ActFER to learn both when local inspection is beneficial and how to reason over the acquired evidence. Comprehensive experiments show that ActFER trained with UC-GRPO consistently outperforms passive MLLM-based FER baselines and substantially improves AU prediction accuracy.

AI.tldr: ActFER introduces an agentic, active-perception framework for facial expression recognition (FER), leveraging dynamic tool use and AU-grounded reasoning with a reinforcement learning algorithm (UC-GRPO) to improve AU prediction and overall FER accuracy over passive MLLM baselines.
AI.motivation: Traditional MLLM-based FER methods rely on passive, single-pass reasoning over fixed visual evidence, limiting their ability to adaptively perceive facial cues. The work aims to enable active facial perception and evidence-driven reasoning to improve FER performance and AU estimation.
AI.method: ActFER enables dynamic invocation of face-detection/alignment tools, selective zooming into informative local facial regions, and reasoning over facial Action Units (AUs) and emotions using a visual Chain-of-Thought. UC-GRPO (utility-calibrated GRPO) reinforces learning with AU-grounded multi-level verifiable rewards, query-conditional contrastive utility estimation for dynamic credit assignment, and emotion-aware EMA calibration to reduce noisy utility estimates while capturing emotion-wise inspection tendencies.
AI.result: Experiments show ActFER trained with UC-GRPO consistently surpasses passive MLLM-based FER baselines and substantially improves AU prediction accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Agentic FER with active perception and calibrated RL demonstrates strong potential for robust multimodal affect understanding, suggesting broader applicability to interactive vision-language tasks where evidence acquisition guides reasoning.

title: Post-Hoc Guidance for Consistency Models by Joint Flow Distribution Learning
summary: Classifier-free Guidance (CFG) lets practitioners trade-off fidelity against diversity in Diffusion Models (DMs). The practicality of CFG is however hindered by DMs sampling cost. On the other hand, Consistency Models (CMs) generate images in one or a few steps, but existing guidance methods require knowledge distillation from a separate DM teacher, limiting CFG to Consistency Distillation (CD) methods. We propose Joint Flow Distribution Learning (JFDL), a lightweight alignment method enabling guidance in a pre-trained CM. With a pre-trained CM as an ordinary differential equation (ODE) solver, we verify with normality tests that the variance-exploding noise implied by the velocity fields from unconditional and conditional distributions is Gaussian. In practice, JFDL equips CMs with the familiar adjustable guidance knob, yielding guided images with similar characteristics to CFG. Applied to an original Consistency Trained (CT) CM that could only do conditional sampling, JFDL unlocks guided generation and reduces FID on both CIFAR-10 and ImageNet 64x64 datasets. This is the first time that CMs are able to receive effective guidance post-hoc without a DM teacher, thus, bridging a key gap in current methods for CMs.

AI.tldr: Introduces Joint Flow Distribution Learning (JFDL) to enable CFG-like guidance for Consistency Models without a DM teacher, aligning unconditional and conditional flow distributions; provides an adjustable guidance knob and improves FID on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet 64x64.
AI.motivation: DGMs offer high fidelity/diversity via classifier-free guidance but are costly to sample; Consistency Models sample fast but lack post-hoc guidance without distillation from a DM teacher; need a lightweight method to enable guidance directly in pre-trained CMs.
AI.method: Joint Flow Distribution Learning (JFDL) aligns the flow distributions (velocity fields) of unconditional and conditional distributions by treating a pre-trained CM as an ordinary differential equation (ODE) solver. The authors verify that the implied variance-exploding noise is Gaussian via normality tests. JFDL introduces an adjustable guidance knob, enabling CFG-like control in CMs and is applied to a Consistency Trained CM that previously supported only conditional sampling.
AI.result: JFDL yields guided images with CFG-like characteristics and reduces FID on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet 64x64. It enables effective post-hoc guidance for CMs without a DM teacher, and demonstrates practical CFG-like control for CMs.
AI.conclusion: This work is the first to enable effective post-hoc CFG-like guidance for Consistency Models without a diffusion-model teacher, bridging a key practical gap and expanding CM usability.

title: PinpointQA: A Dataset and Benchmark for Small Object-Centric Spatial Understanding in Indoor Videos
summary: Small object-centric spatial understanding in indoor videos remains a significant challenge for multimodal large language models (MLLMs), despite its practical value for object search and assistive applications. Although existing benchmarks have advanced video spatial intelligence, embodied reasoning, and diagnostic perception, no existing benchmark directly evaluates whether a model can localize a target object in video and express its position with sufficient precision for downstream use. In this work, we introduce PinpointQA, the first dataset and benchmark for small object-centric spatial understanding in indoor videos. Built from ScanNet++ and ScanNet200, PinpointQA comprises 1,024 scenes and 10,094 QA pairs organized into four progressively challenging tasks: Target Presence Verification (TPV), Nearest Reference Identification (NRI), Fine-Grained Spatial Description (FSD), and Structured Spatial Prediction (SSP). The dataset is built from intermediate spatial representations, with QA pairs generated automatically and further refined through quality control. Experiments on representative MLLMs reveal a consistent capability gap along the progressive chain, with SSP remaining particularly difficult. Supervised fine-tuning on PinpointQA yields substantial gains, especially on the harder tasks, demonstrating that PinpointQA serves as both a diagnostic benchmark and an effective training dataset. The dataset and project page are available at https://rainchowz.github.io/PinpointQA.

AI.tldr: PinpointQA introduces a dataset/benchmark for small object-centric spatial understanding in indoor videos, enabling diagnostics and training for precise localization tasks across four progressively harder tasks.
AI.motivation: There is a gap in evaluating models' ability to localize small objects in video with precise spatial descriptions; existing benchmarks focus on broader spatial reasoning but not precise localization for downstream use.
AI.method: Construct PinpointQA from ScanNet++, ScanNet200; 1,024 scenes, 10,094 QA pairs; four tasks: TPV, NRI, FSD, SSP; QA generated from intermediate spatial representations and refined via quality control; evaluate representative MLLMs; supervised fine-tuning on PinpointQA.
AI.result: Models show a consistent capability gap along the task chain; SSP is particularly challenging; supervised fine-tuning yields substantial gains, especially on harder tasks; PinpointQA functions as both a diagnostic benchmark and a training dataset.
AI.conclusion: PinpointQA fills a gap in small object-centric spatial evaluation for indoor videos and demonstrates utility as both a benchmark and a training resource; project page provided.

title: Hierarchical Kernel Transformer: Multi-Scale Attention with an Information-Theoretic Approximation Analysis
comment: 20 pages, 3 figures, 8 tables submitted to Neurocomputing
summary: The Hierarchical Kernel Transformer (HKT) is a multi-scale attention mechanism that processes sequences at L resolution levels via trainable causal downsampling, combining level-specific score matrices through learned convex weights. The total computational cost is bounded by 4/3 times that of standard attention, reaching 1.3125x for L = 3. Four theoretical results are established. (i) The hierarchical score matrix defines a positive semidefinite kernel under a sufficient condition on the symmetrised bilinear form (Proposition 3.1). (ii) The asymmetric score matrix decomposes uniquely into a symmetric part controlling reciprocal attention and an antisymmetric part controlling directional attention; HKT provides L independent such pairs across scales, one per resolution level (Propositions 3.5-3.6). (iii) The approximation error decomposes into three interpretable components with an explicit non-Gaussian correction and a geometric decay bound in L (Theorem 4.3, Proposition 4.4). (iv) HKT strictly subsumes single-head standard attention and causal convolution (Proposition 3.4). Experiments over 3 random seeds show consistent gains over retrained standard attention baselines: +4.77pp on synthetic ListOps (55.10+-0.29% vs 50.33+-0.12%, T = 512), +1.44pp on sequential CIFAR-10 (35.45+-0.09% vs 34.01+-0.19%, T = 1,024), and +7.47pp on IMDB character-level sentiment (70.19+-0.57% vs 62.72+-0.40%, T = 1,024), all at 1.31x overhead.

AI.tldr: HKT offers a multi-scale, trainable-downsampling attention mechanism with hierarchical score matrices, achieving 1.3125x cost at L=3 and improving over standard attention across three tasks; it provides four theoretical guarantees (kernel PSDness, decomposition into symmetric/antisymmetric parts, interpretable error decomposition with non-Gaussian correction and geometric decay, and strict subsumption of standard attention and causal convolution) alongside empirical gains on ListOps, CIFAR-10, and IMDB.
AI.motivation: To create a more expressive yet computationally bounded attention mechanism by integrating hierarchical, multi-scale processing and kernel interpretations, enabling directional and reciprocal attention while preserving interpretability and theoretical guarantees.
AI.method: Introduce Hierarchical Kernel Transformer (HKT) with L resolution levels and trainable causal downsampling. Each level has a score matrix; combine via learned convex weights. Prove PSD condition for the hierarchical kernel; decompose the asymmetric score into symmetric (reciprocal attention) and antisymmetric (directional attention) parts, yielding L independent pairs; decompose approximation error into three components with explicit non-Gaussian correction and a geometric decay bound in L; show HKT subsumes single-head standard attention and causal convolution. Empirical evaluation across three seeds on ListOps, sequential CIFAR-10, and IMDB at 1.31x overhead.
AI.result: Theoretical contributions: (i) hierarchical score matrix defines PSD kernel under a symmetrised bilinear form condition; (ii) decomposition of asymmetric score into symmetric and antisymmetric parts with L independent pairs at each scale; (iii) error decomposes into three interpretable components with non-Gaussian correction and geometric decay in L; (iv) HKT strictly subsumes standard attention and causal convolution. Empirical results show consistent gains: ListOps +4.77 percentage points (55.10±0.29% vs 50.33±0.12%), CIFAR-10 +1.44pp (35.45±0.09% vs 34.01±0.19%), IMDB +7.47pp (70.19±0.57% vs 62.72±0.40%), all at 1.31x overhead (L=3).
AI.conclusion: HKT provides a principled, efficient enhancement to attention by combining multi-scale hierarchical processing with kernel interpretations, offering strong theoretical guarantees and consistent empirical gains across diverse tasks, while strictly encompassing standard attention and causal convolution.

title: Matrix-Game 3.0: Real-Time and Streaming Interactive World Model with Long-Horizon Memory
summary: With the advancement of interactive video generation, diffusion models have increasingly demonstrated their potential as world models. However, existing approaches still struggle to simultaneously achieve memory-enabled long-term temporal consistency and high-resolution real-time generation, limiting their applicability in real-world scenarios. To address this, we present Matrix-Game 3.0, a memory-augmented interactive world model designed for 720p real-time longform video generation. Building upon Matrix-Game 2.0, we introduce systematic improvements across data, model, and inference. First, we develop an upgraded industrial-scale infinite data engine that integrates Unreal Engine-based synthetic data, large-scale automated collection from AAA games, and real-world video augmentation to produce high-quality Video-Pose-Action-Prompt quadruplet data at scale. Second, we propose a training framework for long-horizon consistency: by modeling prediction residuals and re-injecting imperfect generated frames during training, the base model learns self-correction; meanwhile, camera-aware memory retrieval and injection enable the base model to achieve long horizon spatiotemporal consistency. Third, we design a multi-segment autoregressive distillation strategy based on Distribution Matching Distillation (DMD), combined with model quantization and VAE decoder pruning, to achieve efficient real-time inference. Experimental results show that Matrix-Game 3.0 achieves up to 40 FPS real-time generation at 720p resolution with a 5B model, while maintaining stable memory consistency over minute-long sequences. Scaling up to a 2x14B model further improves generation quality, dynamics, and generalization. Our approach provides a practical pathway toward industrial-scale deployable world models.

AI.tldr: Matrix-Game 3.0 is a memory-augmented world model enabling 720p real-time long-form video generation with 40 FPS at 5B, leveraging enhanced data, training for long-horizon consistency, and efficient inference via distillation and pruning.
AI.motivation: There is a need for memory-enabled long-term temporal consistency alongside high-resolution real-time video generation in interactive world models; current approaches struggle to balance memory, stability, and speed.
AI.method: An upgraded data engine integrating Unreal Engine-based synthetic data, large-scale automated collection from AAA games, and real-world video augmentation to produce Video-Pose-Action-Prompt quadruplets; a training framework that learns from prediction residuals and re-injects imperfect frames to train self-correction; camera-aware memory retrieval and injection for long-horizon spatiotemporal consistency; a multi-segment autoregressive distillation strategy based on Distribution Matching Distillation, with model quantization and VAE decoder pruning for efficiency.
AI.result: Real-time generation at 720p up to 40 FPS with a 5B model, with stable memory consistency over minute-long sequences; scaling to a 14B model further improves quality, dynamics, and generalization.
AI.conclusion: Provides a practical pathway toward industrial-scale deployable world models.

title: Discrete Meanflow Training Curriculum
summary: Flow-based image generative models exhibit stable training and produce high quality samples when using multi-step sampling procedures. One-step generative models can produce high quality image samples but can be difficult to optimize as they often exhibit unstable training dynamics. Meanflow models exhibit excellent few-step sampling performance and tantalizing one-step sampling performance. Notably, MeanFlow models that achieve this have required extremely large training budgets. We significantly decrease the amount of computation and data budget it takes to train Meanflow models by noting and exploiting a particular discretization of the Meanflow objective that yields a consistency property which we formulate into a ``Discrete Meanflow'' (DMF) Training Curriculum. Initialized with a pretrained Flow Model, DMF curriculum reaches one-step FID 3.36 on CIFAR-10 in only 2000 epochs. We anticipate that faster training curriculums of Meanflow models, specifically those fine-tuned from existing Flow Models, drives efficient training methods of future one-step examples.

AI.tldr: Discrete Meanflow (DMF) Training Curriculum reduces training cost for MeanFlow models by exploiting a discretized Meanflow objective that enforces a consistency property. Initialized from a pretrained Flow model, DMF achieves one-step FID 3.36 on CIFAR-10 in 2000 epochs, suggesting faster, data-efficient training for one-step image generation.
AI.motivation: MeanFlow models can enable high-quality one-step sampling but typically require very large training budgets. The work seeks a more efficient training strategy that preserves or improves sampling quality, enabling practical one-step generation.
AI.method: Identify a discretization of the Meanflow objective that yields a consistency property. Formulate this as a training curriculum (Discrete Meanflow). Start from a pretrained Flow model and fine-tune using the DMF objective, evaluating progress with one-step FID on CIFAR-10.
AI.result: The DMF curriculum achieves one-step FID of 3.36 on CIFAR-10 within 2000 training epochs, substantially reducing the computational and data requirements relative to prior MeanFlow training.
AI.conclusion: A fast, data-efficient training approach for MeanFlow models. Beginning from Flow models, the DMF curriculum enables practical one-step sampling with competitive FID, indicating potential for broader application to efficient one-step generative modeling.

title: StreamMeCo: Long-Term Agent Memory Compression for Efficient Streaming Video Understanding
summary: Vision agent memory has shown remarkable effectiveness in streaming video understanding. However, storing such memory for videos incurs substantial memory overhead, leading to high costs in both storage and computation. To address this issue, we propose StreamMeCo, an efficient Stream Agent Memory Compression framework. Specifically, based on the connectivity of the memory graph, StreamMeCo introduces edge-free minmax sampling for the isolated nodes and an edge-aware weight pruning for connected nodes, evicting the redundant memory nodes while maintaining the accuracy. In addition, we introduce a time-decay memory retrieval mechanism to further eliminate the performance degradation caused by memory compression. Extensive experiments on three challenging benchmark datasets (M3-Bench-robot, M3-Bench-web and Video-MME-Long) demonstrate that under 70% memory graph compression, StreamMeCo achieves a 1.87* speedup in memory retrieval while delivering an average accuracy improvement of 1.0%. Our code is available at https://github.com/Celina-love-sweet/StreamMeCo.

AI.tldr: Efficient memory compression for vision agent memory via graph-aware sampling and pruning, with time-decay retrieval; achieves 1.87x retrieval speedup and ~1% accuracy gain under 70% compression on three benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Reduce memory/storage/computation overhead of streaming video agent memory by compressing the memory graph while preserving accuracy.
AI.method: Leverage memory graph connectivity: apply edge-free minmax sampling to isolated nodes; apply edge-aware weight pruning to connected nodes to remove redundant memory slots; introduce a time-decay memory retrieval mechanism to mitigate performance loss from compression; evaluate under 70% memory graph compression on three datasets.
AI.result: With 70% compression, StreamMeCo achieves 1.87x speedup in memory retrieval and an average accuracy improvement of 1.0% across M3-Bench-robot, M3-Bench-web, and Video-MME-Long; code released at provided repository.
AI.conclusion: StreamMeCo offers an efficient graph-guided memory compression strategy for vision agents, balancing memory reduction with retrieval speed and accuracy gains, demonstrated on multiple benchmarks.

title: Spectral Geometry of LoRA Adapters Encodes Training Objective and Predicts Harmful Compliance
comment: 15 pages, 8 figures, pre-registered experiment, data at https://github.com/roip/task-geometry-experiment-results
summary: We study whether low-rank spectral summaries of LoRA weight deltas can identify which fine-tuning objective was applied to a language model, and whether that geometric signal predicts downstream behavioral harm. In a pre-registered experiment on \texttt{Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct}, we manufacture 38 LoRA adapters across four categories: healthy SFT baselines, DPO on inverted harmlessness preferences, DPO on inverted helpfulness preferences, and activation-steering-derived adapters, and extract per-layer spectral features (norms, stable rank, singular-value entropy, effective rank, and singular-vector cosine alignment to a healthy centroid). Within a single training method (DPO), a logistic regression classifier achieves AUC~1.00 on binary drift detection, all six pairwise objective comparisons, and near-perfect ordinal severity ranking ($ρ\geq 0.956$). Principal component analysis on flattened weight deltas reveals that training objective is PC1 (AUC~1.00 for objective separation), orthogonal to training duration on PC2. Query-projection weights detect that drift occurred; value-projection weights identify which objective. Cross-method generalization fails completely: a DPO-trained classifier assigns every steering adapter a lower drift score than every DPO adapter (AUC~0.00). In a behavioral evaluation phase, DPO-inverted-harmlessness adapters show elevated harmful compliance on HEx-PHI prompts (mean ASR 0.266 vs.\ healthy 0.112, $Δ= +0.154$), with near-perfect dose--response ($ρ= 0.986$). The geometry-to-behavior rank correlation is $ρ= 0.72$ across 24 non-steered adapters. These results establish that within a controlled manufacturing regime, LoRA weight-space geometry carries objective identity, intensity ordering, and a coarse link to harmful compliance, and that cross-method monitoring requires per-method calibration.

AI.tldr: Low-rank spectral features of LoRA weight deltas can identify the fine-tuning objective within a single method and modestly relate to downstream harm; however, cross-method generalization fails, so monitoring requires per-method calibration.
AI.motivation: To determine whether the geometry of LoRA weight-space encodes the applied fine-tuning objective and whether this geometry predicts downstream behavioral harm.
AI.method: Manufacture 38 LoRA adapters across four categories on Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct; extract per-layer spectral features (norms, stable rank, singular-value entropy, effective rank, singular-vector cosine alignment to a healthy centroid); train a logistic regression to detect drift and predict objective within a single method; use PCA on flattened weight deltas to identify principal components associated with objective vs duration; analyze query/value projection weights to locate drift and objective signals; conduct a behavioral evaluation using HEx-PHI prompts to assess harmful compliance.
AI.result: Within a single training method, a logistic regression classifier achieves AUC ~1.00 for binary drift detection, all six pairwise objective comparisons, and near-perfect ordinal severity ranking (ρ ≥ 0.956). PCA shows training objective aligns with PC1 (AUC ~1.00 for objective separation) and is orthogonal to training duration on PC2. Cross-method generalization fails: a DPO-trained classifier assigns lower drift to steering adapters than to DPO adapters (AUC ~0.00). Behavioral evaluation shows DPO-inverted-harmlessness adapters have higher harmful compliance (mean ASR 0.266 vs 0.112, Δ=+0.154) with near-perfect dose–response (ρ=0.986). Geometry-to-behavior correlation is ρ=0.72 across 24 non-steered adapters.
AI.conclusion: LoRA weight-space geometry encodes objective identity and intensity ordering within a controlled regime and shows a coarse link to harmful behavior, but cross-method monitoring requires per-method calibration; geometry alone is insufficient for generalizable detection without method-specific calibrations.

title: Robust by Design: A Continuous Monitoring and Data Integration Framework for Medical AI
comment: Accepted at IEEE ISBI 2026. Chandra Mohan and Hien Van Nguyen jointly supervised this work
summary: Adaptive medical AI models often face performance drops in dynamic clinical environments due to data drift. We propose an autonomous continuous monitoring and data integration framework that maintains robust performance over time. Focusing on glomerular pathology image classification (proliferative vs. non-proliferative lupus nephritis), our three-stage method uses multi-metric feature analysis and Monte Carlo dropout-based uncertainty gating to decide when to retrain on new data. Only images statistically similar to the training distribution (via Euclidean, cosine, Mahalanobis metrics) and with low predictive entropy are integrated. The model is then incrementally retrained with these images under strict performance safeguards (no metric degradation >5%). In experiments with a ResNet18 ensemble on a multi-center dataset, the framework prevents performance degradation: new images were added without significant change in AUC (~0.92) or accuracy (~89%). This approach addresses data shift and avoids catastrophic forgetting, enabling sustained learning in medical imaging AI.

AI.tldr: A framework for autonomous continuous monitoring and selective data integration to combat data drift in medical imaging, using uncertainty gating and metric-based similarity to retrain incrementally; demonstrated on lupus nephritis pathology images with stable AUC ~0.92 and accuracy ~89%.
AI.motivation: Data drift in adaptive medical AI leads to performance degradation; autonomous, safe continual learning is needed to sustain performance without forgetting.
AI.method: Three-stage approach: (1) multi-metric feature analysis and Monte Carlo dropout-based uncertainty gating to decide when to retrain; (2) integrate only images statistically similar to the training distribution using Euclidean, cosine, and Mahalanobis distances and with low predictive entropy; (3) incremental retraining under safeguards (no metric degradation >5%).
AI.result: Using a ResNet18 ensemble on a multi-center dataset, the framework prevents performance degradation; new images added without significant change in AUC (~0.92) or accuracy (~89%).
AI.conclusion: Addresses data shift and mitigates catastrophic forgetting, enabling sustained learning in medical imaging AI.

title: Dictionary-Aligned Concept Control for Safeguarding Multimodal LLMs
comment: Accepted in CVPR 2026. Project page: https://peterljq.github.io/project/daco
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have been shown to be vulnerable to malicious queries that can elicit unsafe responses. Recent work uses prompt engineering, response classification, or finetuning to improve MLLM safety. Nevertheless, such approaches are often ineffective against evolving malicious patterns, may require rerunning the query, or demand heavy computational resources. Steering the activations of a frozen model at inference time has recently emerged as a flexible and effective solution. However, existing steering methods for MLLMs typically handle only a narrow set of safety-related concepts or struggle to adjust specific concepts without affecting others. To address these challenges, we introduce Dictionary-Aligned Concept Control (DACO), a framework that utilizes a curated concept dictionary and a Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) to provide granular control over MLLM activations. First, we curate a dictionary of 15,000 multimodal concepts by retrieving over 400,000 caption-image stimuli and summarizing their activations into concept directions. We name the dataset DACO-400K. Second, we show that the curated dictionary can be used to intervene activations via sparse coding. Third, we propose a new steering approach that uses our dictionary to initialize the training of an SAE and automatically annotate the semantics of the SAE atoms for safeguarding MLLMs. Experiments on multiple MLLMs (e.g., QwenVL, LLaVA, InternVL) across safety benchmarks (e.g., MM-SafetyBench, JailBreakV) show that DACO significantly improves MLLM safety while maintaining general-purpose capabilities.

AI.tldr: DACO introduces dictionary-aligned concept control for MLLMs using a 15k concept dictionary derived from DACO-400K, paired with a Sparse Autoencoder to steer activations at inference for safety, achieving improved safety across multiple models and benchmarks while preserving general capabilities.
AI.motivation: Current safety approaches for multimodal LLMs struggle against evolving malicious prompts and often incur reruns or heavy computation. Steering activations in a frozen model offers a flexible solution, but existing methods lack granularity and generality over safety concepts.
AI.method: Construct a concept dictionary of 15,000 multimodal concepts by mining 400,000 caption–image stimuli (DACO-400K) and distilling their activations into concept directions. Use the dictionary to enable sparse coding-based intervention of activations. Initialize and train a Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) with the dictionary, and automatically annotate the semantics of the SAE atoms for safeguarding MLLMs.
AI.result: DACO improves safety across several MLLMs (e.g., QwenVL, LLaVA, InternVL) on safety benchmarks such as MM-SafetyBench and JailBreakV, while maintaining general-purpose capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Dictionary-aligned concept control via SAE and sparse coding provides granular, scalable safety interventions for MLLMs, enabling interpretable semantics per SAE atom and broad applicability across models and benchmarks.

title: Domain-generalizable Face Anti-Spoofing with Patch-based Multi-tasking and Artifact Pattern Conversion
comment: The published version is available at DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.patcog.2026.113640
summary: Face Anti-Spoofing (FAS) algorithms, designed to secure face recognition systems against spoofing, struggle with limited dataset diversity, impairing their ability to handle unseen visual domains and spoofing methods. We introduce the Pattern Conversion Generative Adversarial Network (PCGAN) to enhance domain generalization in FAS. PCGAN effectively disentangles latent vectors for spoof artifacts and facial features, allowing to generate images with diverse artifacts. We further incorporate patch-based and multi-task learning to tackle partial attacks and overfitting issues to facial features. Our extensive experiments validate PCGAN's effectiveness in domain generalization and detecting partial attacks, giving a substantial improvement in facial recognition security.

AI.tldr: A GAN-based framework (PCGAN) enhances domain generalization in face anti-spoofing by disentangling spoof artifacts from facial features to generate diverse training images; includes patch-based and multi-task learning to handle partial attacks, improving domain robustness and security.
AI.motivation: FAS models suffer from limited dataset diversity, leading to poor generalization to unseen domains and spoofing methods.
AI.method: Proposes Pattern Conversion GAN (PCGAN) that disentangles latent spaces for spoof artifacts and facial features to synthesize images with varied artifacts; integrates patch-based training and multi-task learning to address partial attacks and reduce feature overfitting.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate improved domain generalization and effectiveness in detecting partial attacks, yielding substantial improvements in facial recognition security.
AI.conclusion: PCGAN's disentangled latent representation plus auxiliary strategies enhances FAS generalization and robustness against partial spoofs, contributing to more secure face recognition systems.

title: Finite-Sample Analysis of Nonlinear Independent Component Analysis:Sample Complexity and Identifiability Bounds
summary: Independent Component Analysis (ICA) is a fundamental unsupervised learning technique foruncovering latent structure in data by separating mixed signals into their independent sources. While substantial progress has been made in establishing asymptotic identifiability guarantees for nonlinear ICA, the finite-sample statistical properties of learning algorithms remain poorly understood. This gap poses significant challenges for practitioners who must determine appropriate sample sizes for reliable source recovery. This paper presents a comprehensive finite-sample analysis of nonlinear ICA with neural network encoders, providing the first complete characterization with matching upper and lower bounds. Our theoretical development introduces three key technical contributions. First, we establish a direct relationship between excess risk and identification error that bypasses parameter-space arguments, thereby avoiding the rate degradation that would otherwise yield suboptimal scaling. Second, we prove matching information-theoretic lower bounds that confirm the optimality of our sample complexity results. Third, we extend our analysis to practical SGD optimization, showing that the same sample efficiency can be achieved with finite-iteration gradient descent under standard landscape assumptions. We validate our theoretical predictions through carefully designed simulation experiments. This gap points toward valuable future research on finite-sample behavior of neural network training and highlights the importance of our validated scaling laws for dimension and diversity.

AI.tldr: The paper provides the first finite-sample characterization of nonlinear ICA with neural network encoders, establishing matching upper and lower bounds on sample complexity, a direct excess risk–identification error relation, information-theoretic lower bounds proving optimality, and SGD-optimization results under standard assumptions, validated by simulations.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of finite-sample guarantees for nonlinear ICA with neural network encoders, enabling practitioners to determine required sample sizes for reliable source recovery and understanding how dimension and data diversity affect learning.
AI.method: Develop a three-fold theoretical framework: (1) derive a direct link between excess risk and identification error to avoid parameter-space rate degradation; (2) establish matching information-theoretic lower bounds to prove optimal sample complexity; (3) extend analysis to practical SGD optimization, showing finite-iteration gradient descent achieves the same sample efficiency under standard landscape assumptions; plus empirical simulations to validate predictions.
AI.result: First complete finite-sample bounds for nonlinear ICA with neural-network encoders, with matching upper and lower bounds and an explicit sample-complexity characterization. The analysis yields optimal rates and demonstrates that SGD with finite iterations attains these rates under standard assumptions; simulations corroborate the theoretical scaling in dimension and diversity.
AI.conclusion: The work closes the finite-sample gap for nonlinear ICA with neural encoders, providing optimal sample-complexity results and practical SGD implications, and points to future research on finite-sample behaviors of neural-network training and the role of data diversity in learning latent structure.

title: BlendFusion -- Scalable Synthetic Data Generation for Diffusion Model Training
summary: With the rapid adoption of diffusion models, synthetic data generation has emerged as a promising approach for addressing the growing demand for large-scale image datasets. However, images generated purely by diffusion models often exhibit visual inconsistencies, and training models on such data can create an autophagous feedback loop that leads to model collapse, commonly referred to as Model Autophagy Disorder (MAD). To address these challenges, we propose BlendFusion, a scalable framework for synthetic data generation from 3D scenes using path tracing. Our pipeline incorporates an object-centric camera placement strategy, robust filtering mechanisms, and automatic captioning to produce high-quality image-caption pairs. Using this pipeline, we curate FineBLEND, an image-caption dataset constructed from a diverse set of 3D scenes. We empirically analyze the quality of FineBLEND and compare it to several widely used image-caption datasets. We also demonstrate the effectiveness of our object-centric camera placement strategy relative to object-agnostic sampling approaches. Our open-source framework is designed for high configurability, enabling the community to create their own datasets from 3D scenes.

AI.tldr: BlendFusion offers a scalable 3D-scene path-tracing pipeline to generate high-quality image-caption data (FineBLEND), addressing diffusion-model MAD by minimizing visual inconsistencies and autophagic feedback loops; includes object-centric camera placement and open-source configurability.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-only synthetic data can introduce inconsistencies and trigger Model Autophagy Disorder (MAD) in training loops; there's a need for high-quality, controlled synthetic datasets derived from 3D scenes to improve data fidelity and training stability.
AI.method: A pipeline using path-tracing rendering from diverse 3D scenes; object-centric camera placement; robust filters; automatic captioning; dataset curation (FineBLEND); empirical comparisons to existing image-caption datasets; evaluation of object-centric sampling vs object-agnostic sampling; open-source, highly configurable.
AI.result: Produced FineBLEND; demonstrated data quality comparable to widely used image-caption datasets; showed advantage of object-centric sampling; provided open-source framework for dataset creation.
AI.conclusion: BlendFusion enables scalable, customizable synthetic data generation from 3D scenes to improve image-caption datasets and mitigate MAD risks; facilitates community-driven dataset creation.

title: Temporal Dropout Risk in Learning Analytics: A Harmonized Survival Benchmark Across Dynamic and Early-Window Representations
comment: 34 pages, 14 figures, 18 tables. Includes appendix with reliability diagrams, sensitivity analyses, and dataset audit tables
summary: Student dropout is a persistent concern in Learning Analytics, yet comparative studies frequently evaluate predictive models under heterogeneous protocols, prioritizing discrimination over temporal interpretability and calibration. This study introduces a survival-oriented benchmark for temporal dropout risk modelling using the Open University Learning Analytics Dataset (OULAD). Two harmonized arms are compared: a dynamic weekly arm, with models in person-period representation, and a comparable continuous-time arm, with an expanded roster of families -- tree-based survival, parametric, and neural models. The evaluation protocol integrates four analytical layers: predictive performance, ablation, explainability, and calibration. Results are reported within each arm separately, as a single cross-arm ranking is not methodologically warranted. Within the comparable arm, Random Survival Forest leads in discrimination and horizon-specific Brier scores; within the dynamic arm, Poisson Piecewise-Exponential leads narrowly on integrated Brier score within a tight five-family cluster. No-refit bootstrap sampling variability qualifies these positions as directional signals rather than absolute superiority. Ablation and explainability analyses converged, across all families, on a shared finding: the dominant predictive signal was not primarily demographic or structural, but temporal and behavioral. Calibration corroborated this pattern in the better-discriminating models, with the exception of XGBoost AFT, which exhibited systematic bias. These results support the value of a harmonized, multi-dimensional benchmark in Learning Analytics and situate dropout risk as a temporal-behavioral process rather than a function of static background attributes.

AI.tldr: A survival-oriented, two-arm benchmark for dropout risk on OULAD; temporal-behavioral drivers predominate; RSF best for discrimination in the continuous-time arm; Poisson Piecewise-Exponential best for the dynamic arm; results show directional signals due to sampling variability; supports multi-dimensional benchmarking.
AI.motivation: Address heterogeneity in evaluation protocols for dropout prediction and emphasize temporal interpretability and calibration, not just discrimination.
AI.method: Two harmonized arms: a dynamic weekly arm using person-period representation, and a continuous-time arm with an expanded set of model families (tree-based survival, parametric, and neural models). Evaluation includes four layers: predictive performance, ablation, explainability, and calibration. Data source: Open University Learning Analytics Dataset (OULAD). Cross-arm reporting is avoided; results are presented per arm.
AI.result: In the comparable arm, Random Survival Forest leads in discrimination and horizon-specific Brier scores; in the dynamic arm, Poisson Piecewise-Exponential leads narrowly on integrated Brier score within a tight five-family cluster. No-refit bootstrap sampling variability suggests these are directional signals, not absolute wins. Ablation and explainability analyses converge on temporal/behavioral signals as dominant predictors over static demographics or structure. Calibration supports this pattern for better-discriminating models, with XGBoost AFT showing systematic bias.
AI.conclusion: A harmonized, multi-dimensional benchmarking approach yields insights into dropout as a temporal-behavioral process rather than a function of static attributes; such benchmarks provide a robust framework for comparing models across different temporal representations and evaluation metrics, while acknowledging model-specific calibration biases and variability.

title: CAD 100K: A Comprehensive Multi-Task Dataset for Car Related Visual Anomaly Detection
summary:
Multi-task visual anomaly detection is critical for car-related manufacturing quality assessment. However, existing methods remain task-specific, hindered by the absence of a unified benchmark for multi-task evaluation. To fill in this gap, We present the CAD Dataset, a large-scale and comprehensive benchmark designed for car-related multi-task visual anomaly detection. The dataset contains over 100
  images crossing 7 vehicle domains and 3 tasks, providing models a comprehensive view for car-related anomaly detection. It is the first car-related anomaly dataset specialized for multi-task learning(MTL), while combining synthesis data augmentation for few-shot anomaly images. We implement a multi-task baseline and conduct extensive empirical studies. Results show MTL promotes task interaction and knowledge transfer, while also exposing challenging conflicts between tasks. The CAD dataset serves as a standardized platform to drive future advances in car-related multi-task visual anomaly detection.

AI.tldr: Introduces the CAD Dataset, a large-scale benchmark for car-related multi-task visual anomaly detection, and demonstrates that multi-task learning can enhance task interaction and knowledge transfer while revealing cross-task conflicts.
AI.motivation: Addresses the lack of a unified benchmark for multi-task visual anomaly detection in car manufacturing and enables standardized evaluation with multi-task learning, including few-shot anomaly data via synthesis augmentation.
AI.method: Proposes CAD Dataset with over 100 images spanning 7 vehicle domains and 3 tasks; implements a multi-task baseline; applies synthesis data augmentation for few-shot anomaly images; conducts extensive empirical studies to analyze task interactions and transfer.
AI.result: MTL promotes knowledge transfer and task interaction but also reveals conflicting demands among tasks; CAD provides a standardized platform to drive future advances in car-related multi-task anomaly detection.
AI.conclusion: CAD establishes a standardized, multi-task benchmark for car-related anomaly detection and baseline models, enabling future research into task coordination and transfer learning in this domain.

title: How does Chain of Thought decompose complex tasks?
summary: Many language tasks can be modeled as classification problems where a large language model (LLM) is given a prompt and selects one among many possible answers. We show that the classification error in such problems scales as a power law in the number of classes. This has a dramatic consequence: the prediction error can be reduced substantially by splitting the overall task into a sequence of smaller classification problems, each with the same number of classes ("degree"). This tree-structured decomposition models chain-of-thought (CoT). It has been observed that CoT-based predictors perform better when they "think'", i.e., when they develop a deeper tree, thus decomposing the problem into a larger number of steps. We identify a critical threshold for the degree, below which thinking is detrimental, and above which there exists an optimal depth that minimizes the error. It is impossible to surpass this minimal error by increasing the depth of thinking.

AI.tldr: Classification error scales as a power law with the number of classes; organizing tasks as a tree (degree and depth) reduces error up to an optimal depth; there is a critical degree threshold; beyond optimal depth, error cannot be improved by deeper thinking.
AI.motivation: Investigate how hierarchical decomposition (chain-of-thought) impacts accuracy in large language model classification tasks and identify limits of error reduction with depth.
AI.method: Theoretically derive error scaling as a power law in class count; model task as a sequence of smaller classification problems with fixed degree; analyze depth vs error, identify threshold for degree and optimal depth; show asymptotic lower bound on error independent of depth.
AI.result: Find a critical threshold for the per-node degree below which deeper reasoning hurts; above the threshold there exists an optimal depth that minimizes error; this minimum error cannot be surpassed by increasing depth.
AI.conclusion: For LLM classification tasks, tree-structured CoT can reduce error but only up to a fundamental limit; beyond the optimal depth further expansion does not improve and cannot beat the minimal achievable error.

title: Leave My Images Alone: Preventing Multi-Modal Large Language Models from Analyzing Images via Visual Prompt Injection
summary: Multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) have emerged as powerful tools for analyzing Internet-scale image data, offering significant benefits but also raising critical safety and societal concerns. In particular, open-weight MLLMs may be misused to extract sensitive information from personal images at scale, such as identities, locations, or other private details. In this work, we propose ImageProtector, a user-side method that proactively protects images before sharing by embedding a carefully crafted, nearly imperceptible perturbation that acts as a visual prompt injection attack on MLLMs. As a result, when an adversary analyzes a protected image with an MLLM, the MLLM is consistently induced to generate a refusal response such as "I'm sorry, I can't help with that request." We empirically demonstrate the effectiveness of ImageProtector across six MLLMs and four datasets. Additionally, we evaluate three potential countermeasures, Gaussian noise, DiffPure, and adversarial training, and show that while they partially mitigate the impact of ImageProtector, they simultaneously degrade model accuracy and/or efficiency. Our study focuses on the practically important setting of open-weight MLLMs and large-scale automated image analysis, and highlights both the promise and the limitations of perturbation-based privacy protection.

AI.tldr: ImageProtector proposes a user-side perturbation that acts as a visual prompt injection to protect images from open-weight MLLMs, causing analyses to refuse. It is effective across six MLLMs and four datasets, but countermeasures (Gaussian noise, DiffPure, adversarial training) offer limited mitigation with trade-offs in accuracy/efficiency.
AI.motivation: Open-weight MLLMs enable large-scale extraction of sensitive information from personal images. There is a need for proactive, scalable client-side defenses that protect privacy before data sharing while preserving usability.
AI.method: Introduce ImageProtector, a nearly imperceptible perturbation embedded in images to induce visual prompt injections that trigger refusal responses in MLLMs during analysis. Evaluate on six MLLMs and four datasets. Compare three countermeasures—Gaussian noise, DiffPure, and adversarial training—and assess their impact on defense effectiveness and model performance.
AI.result: The perturbation consistently induces refusal responses from MLLMs when analyzing protected images, validated across six models and four datasets. Countermeasures provide partial mitigation but at the cost of decreased accuracy and/or efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Perturbation-based, user-side privacy protections can effectively impede sensitive analysis by open-weight MLLMs but come with limitations and trade-offs. Robust defenses remain challenging, highlighting the need for further work on privacy-preserving strategies that balance protection with model utility.

title: A Mathematical Framework for Temporal Modeling and Counterfactual Policy Simulation of Student Dropout
comment: Approx. 20 pages, 9 figures. Code and reproducibility package available at https://github.com/rafa-rodriguess/TCM-Student-Dropout This work introduces a temporal survival framework with counterfactual policy simulation
summary: This study proposes a temporal modeling framework with a counterfactual policy-simulation layer for student dropout in higher education, using LMS engagement data and administrative withdrawal records. Dropout is operationalized as a time-to-event outcome at the enrollment level; weekly risk is modeled in discrete time via penalized, class-balanced logistic regression over person--period rows. Under a late-event temporal holdout, the model attains row-level AUCs of 0.8350 (train) and 0.8405 (test), with aggregate calibration acceptable but sparsely supported in the highest-risk bins. Ablation analyses indicate performance is sensitive to feature set composition, underscoring the role of temporal engagement signals. A scenario-indexed policy layer produces survival contrasts $ΔS(T)$ under an explicit trigger/schedule contract: positive contrasts are confined to the shock branch ($T_{\rm policy}=18$: 0.0102, 0.0260, 0.0819), while the mechanism-aware branch is negative ($ΔS_{\rm mech}(18)=-0.0078$, $ΔS_{\rm mech}(38)=-0.0134$). A subgroup analysis by gender quantifies scenario-induced survival gaps via bootstrap; contrasts are directionally stable but small. Results are not causally identified; they demonstrate the framework's capacity for internal structural scenario comparison under observational data constraints.

AI.tldr: Temporal, counterfactual policy-simulation framework for student dropout using LMS data; enables scenario-based policy contrasts with competitive predictive performance; not causal.
AI.motivation: Address time-to-event dropout modeling with rich temporal engagement signals and provide internal, scenario-based policy evaluation under observational data.
AI.method: Discrete-time hazard modeling via penalized, class-balanced logistic regression on person–period data; late-event temporal holdout; weekly risk; ablation analysis; scenario-indexed policy layer producing survival contrasts ΔS(T); bootstrap subgroup analysis by gender.
AI.result: Row-level AUCs: train 0.8350, test 0.8405; calibration acceptable but sparse in highest-risk bins; policy shocks yield positive ΔS(T) on policy branch (e.g., ΔS(18)=0.0102, 0.0260, 0.0819); mechanism-aware branch negative (ΔS_mech(18)=-0.0078, ΔS_mech(38)=-0.0134); gender subgroups show directionally stable but small effects; results are observational and not causal.
AI.conclusion: Framework supports internal structural scenario comparison under observational data; useful for policy design and risk stratification, with limitations including non-causal identification and reliance on feature signals; improvements may focus on feature set and calibration in high-risk groups.

title: Skill-Conditioned Visual Geolocation for Vision-Language
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) have shown a promising ability in image geolocation, but they still lack structured geographic reasoning and the capacity for autonomous self-evolution. Existing methods predominantly rely on implicit parametric memory, which often exploits outdated knowledge and generates hallucinated reasoning. Furthermore, current inference is a "one-off" process, lacking the feedback loops necessary for self-evolution based on reasoning outcomes. To address these issues, we propose GeoSkill, a training-free framework based on an evolving Skill-Graph. We first initialize the graph by refining human expert trajectories into atomic, natural-language skills. For execution, GeoSkill employs an inference model to perform direct reasoning guided by the current Skill-Graph. For continuous growth, an Autonomous Evolution mechanism leverages a larger model to conduct multiple reasoning rollouts on image-coordinate pairs sourced from web-scale data and verified real-world reasoning. By analyzing both successful and failed trajectories from these rollouts, the mechanism iteratively synthesizes and prunes skills, effectively expanding the Skill-Graph and correcting geographic biases without any parameter updates. Experiments demonstrate that GeoSkill achieves promising performance in both geolocation accuracy and reasoning faithfulness on GeoRC, while maintaining superior generalization across diverse external datasets. Furthermore, our autonomous evolution fosters the emergence of novel, verifiable skills, significantly enhancing the system's cognition of real-world geographic knowledge beyond isolated case studies.

AI.tldr: GeoSkill is a training-free framework that builds an evolving Skill-Graph for vision-language geolocation, enabling structured geographic reasoning and autonomous self-evolution without parameter updates, improving accuracy and generalization.
AI.motivation: Current VLM geolocation relies on implicit, outdated parametric memory and often yields hallucinated or biased reasoning. There is a lack of feedback loops for self-improvement, limiting sustained, reliable geographic understanding.
AI.method: Initialize a Skill-Graph by refining human expert trajectories into atomic, natural-language skills. Use an inference model to perform direct reasoning guided by the current Skill-Graph. Employ Autonomous Evolution: a larger model conducts multiple reasoning rollouts on image-coordinate pairs from web-scale data; analyze successful and failed rollouts to synthesize and prune skills, expanding the graph without parameter updates.
AI.result: GeoSkill achieves promising geolocation accuracy and reasoning faithfulness on GeoRC and demonstrates strong generalization across external datasets. Autonomous Evolution enables emergence of novel, verifiable skills that enhance real-world geographic knowledge beyond isolated cases.
AI.conclusion: GeoSkill offers a training-free, self-evolving framework for structured geographic reasoning in VLMs, yielding improved accuracy, faithfulness, and knowledge growth through skill synthesis and graph pruning without standard parameter updates.

title: Revisiting the Capacity Gap in Chain-of-Thought Distillation from a Practical Perspective
summary: Chain-of-thought (CoT) distillation transfers reasoning behaviors from a strong teacher to a smaller student, but prior work reports a capacity gap: distillation may fail when the teacher-student capability mismatch is large. We revisit the capacity gap from a practical perspective by re-examining commonly used experimental settings. Notably, we find that CoT distillation often degrades performance compared to the student's pre-distillation baseline, an issue obscured when only post-distillation comparisons are reported. We therefore propose a more realistic evaluation protocol and find that the impact of capacity gap effects does not consistently dominate across tasks and settings, especially when candidate teachers differ substantially in performance. Our results offer practical guidance for selecting teacher-student pairs in CoT distillation.

AI.tldr: CoT distillation shows a capacity gap issue, but the effect is not universal; prior findings may be biased by evaluation protocols that miss pre-distillation baselines.
AI.motivation: Clarify the real impact of teacher-student capacity gaps in Chain-of-Thought distillation and improve evaluation practices.
AI.method: Re-examine common experimental setups, compare pre- and post-distillation baselines, and assess performance across tasks with varying teacher quality.
AI.result: CoT distillation often lowers performance relative to the student’s pre-distillation baseline; the capacity-gap effect is not consistently dominant, especially when teachers vary in performance.
AI.conclusion: Provide practical guidelines for selecting teacher-student pairs and advocate a realistic evaluation protocol that includes pre-distillation baselines to avoid misleading conclusions.

title: NTIRE 2026 The 3rd Restore Any Image Model (RAIM) Challenge: Multi-Exposure Image Fusion in Dynamic Scenes (Track 2)
summary: This paper presents NTIRE 2026, the 3rd Restore Any Image Model (RAIM) challenge on multi-exposure image fusion in dynamic scenes. We introduce a benchmark that targets a practical yet difficult HDR imaging setting, where exposure bracketing must be fused under scene motion, illumination variation, and handheld camera jitter. The challenge data contains 100 training sequences with 7 exposure levels and 100 test sequences with 5 exposure levels, reflecting real-world scenarios that frequently cause misalignment and ghosting artefacts. We evaluate submissions with a leaderboard score derived from PSNR, SSIM, and LPIPS, while also considering perceptual quality, efficiency, and reproducibility during the final review. This track attracted 114 participating teams and received 987 submissions. The winning methods significantly improved the ability to remove artifacts from multi-exposure fusion and recover fine details. The dataset and the code of each team can be found at the repository: https://github.com/qulishen/RAIM-HDR.

AI.tldr: NTIRE 2026 RAIM: a challenge and benchmark for multi-exposure HDR fusion in dynamic scenes, featuring 100 training sequences (7 exposures) and 100 test sequences (5 exposures); evaluation via PSNR/SSIM/LPIPS with perceptual/efficiency/repro considerations; 114 teams and 987 submissions; methods improved artifact removal and detail recovery; dataset and code available at RAIM-HDR repo.
AI.motivation: Address robust HDR fusion under real-world challenges: misalignment, ghosting, motion, illumination changes, and handheld jitter in exposure-bracketed sequences; provide a realistic benchmark and protocol to advance methods.
AI.method: Introduce a benchmark and challenge framework with a practical HDR setting, a curated dataset (100 train, 100 test sequences with varied exposure levels), and a multi-criteria leaderboard (PSNR, SSIM, LPIPS) augmented by perceptual quality, efficiency, and reproducibility considerations; public dataset and code to enable reproducibility.
AI.result: High engagement: 114 participating teams and 987 submissions. Winning methods significantly improve artifact removal and detail recovery in multi-exposure fusion under motion. Dataset and code available at the RAIM-HDR GitHub repository.
AI.conclusion: The NTIRE 2026 RAIM track provides a realistic, reproducible platform to push advances in robust multi-exposure HDR fusion in dynamic scenes, emphasizing both artifact suppression and detail preservation, with public data and code to accelerate progress.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Transformers: Conformal Prediction for Language Models
summary: Transformers have had a profound impact on the field of artificial intelligence, especially on large language models and their variants. However, as was the case with neural networks, their black-box nature limits trust and deployment in high-stakes settings. For models to be genuinely useful and trustworthy in critical applications, they must provide more than just predictions: they must supply users with a clear understanding of the reasoning that underpins their decisions. This article presents an uncertainty quantification framework for transformer-based language models. This framework, called CONFIDE (CONformal prediction for FIne-tuned DEep language models), applies conformal prediction to the internal embeddings of encoder-only architectures, like BERT and RoBERTa, while enabling hyperparameter tuning. CONFIDE uses either [CLS] token embeddings or flattened hidden states to construct class-conditional nonconformity scores, enabling statistically valid prediction sets with instance-level explanations. Empirically, CONFIDE improves test accuracy by up to 4.09% on BERT-tiny and achieves greater correct efficiency (i.e., the expected size of the prediction set conditioned on it containing the true label) compared to prior methods, including NM2 and VanillaNN. We show that early and intermediate transformer layers often yield better-calibrated and more semantically meaningful representations for conformal prediction. In resource-constrained models and high-stakes tasks with ambiguous labels, CONFIDE offers robustness and interpretability where softmax-based uncertainty fails. We position CONFIDE as a framework for practical diagnostic and efficiency/robustness improvement over prior conformal baselines.

AI.tldr: A conformal prediction framework (CONFIDE) for encoder-only transformer models applies conformal prediction to internal embeddings to produce valid, interpretable prediction sets; shows accuracy gains and better calibration, especially in resource-constrained and high-stakes settings.
AI.motivation: To provide trustworthy, interpretable uncertainty estimates for transformer-based language models in high-stakes applications, addressing their black-box nature and the need for statistically valid predictions with explanations.
AI.method: Apply conformal prediction to class-conditional nonconformity scores derived from CLS embeddings or flattened hidden states of encoder-only architectures (e.g., BERT, RoBERTa); enable hyperparameter tuning; compare performance against NM2 and VanillaNN; investigate layer-wise calibration.
AI.result: Up to 4.09% improvement in test accuracy on BERT-tiny; improved correct efficiency compared to prior methods; early/intermediate layers yield better calibration and semantics for conformal prediction; robustness and interpretability in resource-constrained and high-stakes tasks where softmax uncertainty fails.
AI.conclusion: CONFIDE provides a practical diagnostic and efficiency/robustness enhancement over prior conformal baselines, offering instance-level explanations and more reliable uncertainty estimates for transformer-based models.

title: SiMing-Bench: Evaluating Procedural Correctness from Continuous Interactions in Clinical Skill Videos
summary: Current video benchmarks for multimodal large language models (MLLMs) focus on event recognition, temporal ordering, and long-context recall, but overlook a harder capability required for expert procedural judgment: tracking how ongoing interactions update the procedural state and thereby determine the correctness of later actions. We introduce SiMing-Bench, the first benchmark for evaluating this capability from full-length clinical skill videos. It targets rubric-grounded process-level judgment of whether interaction-driven state updates preserve procedural correctness across an entire workflow. SiMing-Bench is instantiated with SiMing-Score, a physician-annotated dataset of real clinical skill examination videos spanning cardiopulmonary resuscitation, automated external defibrillator operation, and bag-mask ventilation, each paired with a standardized step-wise rubric and dual-expert labels. Across diverse open- and closed-source MLLMs, we observe consistently weak agreement with physician judgments. Moreover, weak performance on rubric-defined intermediate steps persists even when overall procedure-level correlation appears acceptable, suggesting that coarse global assessment substantially overestimates current models' procedural judgment ability. Additional analyses with binary step judgment and step-aligned clips indicate that the bottleneck is not merely fine-grained scoring or temporal localization, but modeling how continuous interactions update procedural state over time.

AI.tldr: SiMing-Bench introduces a benchmark to assess procedural state-tracking in medical procedures within full-length videos; current MLLMs fail to align with physician judgments on state updates and rubric-based intermediate steps, despite reasonable global procedure correlations.
AI.motivation: Existing video benchmarks target event recognition and long-context recall but neglect how ongoing interactions update procedural state to determine correctness; a capability crucial for expert procedural judgment in medical workflows.
AI.method: SiMing-Score comprises real clinical skill exam videos (CPR, AED, BMV) annotated with standardized stepwise rubrics and dual-expert labels; evaluation spans open- and closed-source MLLMs; includes binary step judgments and step-aligned clips to dissect where models fail.
AI.result: Models show consistently weak agreement with physician judgments; coarse procedure-level correlations overstate capability; models underperform on rubric-defined intermediate steps; bottleneck lies in modeling continuous state updates over time, not just fine-grained scoring or localization.
AI.conclusion: A clinically grounded benchmark reveals substantial gaps in procedural judgment for MLLMs, emphasizing the need to develop architectures and training strategies that track state updates across an entire workflow rather than relying on coarse final judgments.

title: A Closer Look at the Application of Causal Inference in Graph Representation Learning
summary: Modeling causal relationships in graph representation learning remains a fundamental challenge. Existing approaches often draw on theories and methods from causal inference to identify causal subgraphs or mitigate confounders. However, due to the inherent complexity of graph-structured data, these approaches frequently aggregate diverse graph elements into single causal variables, an operation that risks violating the core assumptions of causal inference. In this work, we prove that such aggregation compromises causal validity. Building on this conclusion, we propose a theoretical model grounded in the smallest indivisible units of graph data to ensure that the causal validity is guaranteed. With this model, we further analyze the costs of achieving precise causal modeling in graph representation learning and identify the conditions under which the problem can be simplified. To empirically support our theory, we construct a controllable synthetic dataset that reflects realworld causal structures and conduct extensive experiments for validation. Finally, we develop a causal modeling enhancement module that can be seamlessly integrated into existing graph learning pipelines, and we demonstrate its effectiveness through comprehensive comparative experiments.

AI.tldr: Aggregating diverse graph elements into single causal variables undermines causal validity; the paper advocates modeling with smallest indivisible graph units, analyzes the costs, validates on synthetic data, and provides an integrable causal modeling module with empirical gains.
AI.motivation: Causal inference in graphs risks violating core assumptions when heterogeneous graph elements are collapsed into coarse causal variables; a principled smallest-unit approach is needed to guarantee causal validity and practical applicability.
AI.method: Develop a theoretical model based on indivisible graph units; perform formal analysis of causal validity and cost of precise modeling; create a controllable synthetic dataset mirroring real-world causal structures; design a causal modeling enhancement module; integrate into existing graph learning pipelines; conduct extensive comparative experiments.
AI.result: Proved that aggregation harms causal validity; the smallest-unit model guarantees causal validity; empirical validation on synthetic data supports theory; the enhancement module yields improvements over baselines in comparative experiments; identified conditions that simplify the problem.
AI.conclusion: A principled causal modeling framework for graphs based on indivisible units, with analysis of trade-offs and a practical, integrable module that improves performance in graph representation learning.

title: Scene-Agnostic Object-Centric Representation Learning for 3D Gaussian Splatting
summary: Recent works on 3D scene understanding leverage 2D masks from visual foundation models (VFMs) to supervise radiance fields, enabling instance-level 3D segmentation. However, the supervision signals from foundation models are not fundamentally object-centric and often require additional mask pre/post-processing or specialized training and loss design to resolve mask identity conflicts across views. The learned identity of the 3D scene is scene-dependent, limiting generalizability across scenes. Therefore, we propose a dataset-level, object-centric supervision scheme to learn object representations in 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS). Building on a pre-trained slot attention-based Global Object Centric Learning (GOCL) module, we learn a scene-agnostic object codebook that provides consistent, identity-anchored representations across views and scenes. By coupling the codebook with the module's unsupervised object masks, we can directly supervise the identity features of 3D Gaussians without additional mask pre-/post-processing or explicit multi-view alignment. The learned scene-agnostic codebook enables object supervision and identification without per-scene fine-tuning or retraining. Our method thus introduces unsupervised object-centric learning (OCL) into 3DGS, yielding more structured representations and better generalization for downstream tasks such as robotic interaction, scene understanding, and cross-scene generalization.

AI.tldr: Proposes dataset-level, object-centric supervision for 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) using a scene-agnostic codebook learned via a GOCL module; enables consistent object identity across views and scenes without per-scene fine-tuning, improving generalization.
AI.motivation: Current supervision from 2D foundation-model masks is not object-centric and suffers from view-specific identity conflicts, causing scene-dependent representations and poor cross-scene generalization.
