AI.method: Extend a pre-trained slot-attention Global Object Centric Learning (GOCL) module to learn a scene-agnostic object codebook; couple it with the module's unsupervised object masks to supervise identity features of 3D Gaussians directly; no mask pre-/post-processing or multi-view alignment; no per-scene retraining.
AI.result: Yields more structured, identity-consistent object representations and improved generalization for downstream tasks such as robotic interaction, scene understanding, and cross-scene generalization.
AI.conclusion: Introduces unsupervised object-centric learning into 3D Gaussian Splatting, enabling robust object identities and better generalization without per-scene fine-tuning.

title: Adaptive Candidate Point Thompson Sampling for High-Dimensional Bayesian Optimization
summary: In Bayesian optimization, Thompson sampling selects the evaluation point by sampling from the posterior distribution over the objective function maximizer. Because this sampling problem is intractable for Gaussian process (GP) surrogates, the posterior distribution is typically restricted to fixed discretizations (i.e., candidate points) that become exponentially sparse as dimensionality increases. While previous works aim to increase candidate point density through scalable GP approximations, our orthogonal approach increases density by adaptively reducing the search space during sampling. Specifically, we introduce Adaptive Candidate Thompson Sampling (ACTS), which generates candidate points in subspaces guided by the gradient of a surrogate model sample. ACTS is a simple drop-in replacement for existing TS methods -- including those that use trust regions or other local approximations -- producing better samples of maxima and improved optimization across synthetic and real-world benchmarks.

AI.tldr: ACTS adaptively narrows the search space to generate dense, gradient-guided candidate points for Thompson sampling, improving maxima sampling and optimization performance.
AI.motivation: In Bayesian optimization with GP surrogates, posterior sampling of the maximizer is intractable; fixed discretizations become exponentially sparse in high dimensions; existing work increases candidate density via scalable GP approximations. ACTS offers an orthogonal approach by adaptively reducing the search space during sampling.
AI.method: Adaptive Candidate Thompson Sampling (ACTS) generates candidate points in subspaces guided by the gradient of a sample from the surrogate model's posterior, effectively focusing search where improvements are likely. It is a drop-in replacement for TS and compatible with trust regions or other local approximations.
AI.result: Empirically yields better samples of maximizers and improved optimization performance across synthetic and real-world benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Gradient-guided subspace sampling provides a simple, compatible enhancement to Thompson sampling that increases candidate density without resorting to heavy GP approximations or global discretization.

title: Text-Conditioned Multi-Expert Regression Framework for Fully Automated Multi-Abutment Design
summary: Dental implant abutments serve as the geometric and biomechanical interface between the implant fixture and the prosthetic crown, yet their design relies heavily on manual effort and is time-consuming. Although deep neural networks have been proposed to assist dentists in designing abutments, most existing approaches remain largely manual or semi-automated, requiring substantial clinician intervention and lacking scalability in multi-abutment scenarios. To address these limitations, we propose TEMAD, a fully automated, text-conditioned multi-expert architecture for multi-abutment design. This framework integrates implant site localization and implant system, compatible abutment parameter regression into a unified pipeline. Specifically, we introduce an Implant Site Identification Network (ISIN) to automatically localize implant sites and provide this information to the subsequent multi-abutment regression network. We further design a Tooth-Conditioned Feature-wise Linear Modulation (TC-FiLM) module, which adaptively calibrates mesh representations using tooth embeddings to enable position-specific feature modulation. Additionally, a System-Prompted Mixture-of-Experts (SPMoE) mechanism leverages implant system prompts to guide expert selection, ensuring system-aware regression. Extensive experiments on a large-scale abutment design dataset show that TEMAD achieves state-of-the-art performance compared to existing methods, particularly in multi-abutment settings, validating its effectiveness for fully automated dental implant planning.

AI.tldr: TEMAD is a fully automated, text-conditioned multi-expert pipeline for designing multiple abutments in dental implants, integrating implant site localization, tooth-conditioned feature modulation, and system-aware expert selection; it achieves state-of-the-art results, especially for multi-abutment scenarios.
AI.motivation: Manual, time-consuming abutment design and limited scalability of existing deep-learning approaches; need end-to-end automation that can handle multi-abutment configurations and system-specific variations.
AI.method: Introduce an Implant Site Identification Network (ISIN) for automatic site localization; use Tooth-Conditioned FiLM (TC-FiLM) to modulate mesh features via tooth embeddings; deploy a System-Prompted Mixture-of-Experts (SPMoE) to guide expert selection with implant-system prompts; integrate site localization and system-aware abutment parameter regression in a unified pipeline for fully automated design.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on a large-scale abutment design dataset show TEMAD achieves state-of-the-art performance, particularly in multi-abutment settings.
AI.conclusion: TEMAD enables fully automated, scalable dental implant planning with improved performance, reducing clinician intervention and enabling robust multi-abutment design.

title: Using Synthetic Data for Machine Learning-based Childhood Vaccination Prediction in Narok, Kenya
summary: Background: Limited data utilization in low-resource settings poses a barrier to the vaccine delivery ecosystem, undermining efforts to achieve equitable immunization coverage. In nomadic populations, individuals face an increased risk of missing crucial vaccination doses as children. One such population is the Maasai in Narok County, Kenya, where the absence of high-volume, quality data hampers accurate coverage estimates, impedes efficient resource allocation, and weakens the ability to deliver timely interventions. Additionally, data privacy concerns are heightened in groups with limited sensitive data. Objectives: First, we aim to identify children at risk of missing key vaccines across a large population to provide timely, evidence-based interventions that support increased vaccination coverage. Second, we aim to better protect the privacy of sensitive health data in a vulnerable population. Methods: We digitized 8 years of child vaccination records from the MOH 510 registry (n=6,913) and applied machine learning models (Logistic Regression and XGBoost) to identify children at risk. Additionally, we utilize a novel approach to tabular diffusion-based synthetic data generation (TabSyn) to protect patient privacy within the models. Results: Our findings show that classification techniques can reliably and successfully predict children at risk of missing a vaccine, with recall, precision, and F1-scores exceeding 90% for some vaccines modeled. Additionally, training these models with synthetic data rather than real data, thus preserving the privacy of individuals within the original dataset, does not lead to a loss in predictive performance. Conclusion: These results support the use of synthetic data implementation in health informatics strategies for clinics with limited digital infrastructure, enabling privacy-preserving, scalable forecasting for childhood immunization coverage.

AI.tldr: ML models (Logistic Regression and XGBoost) identify children at risk of missing key vaccines in Maasai populations using 8 years of MOH vaccination data; TabSyn synthetic data enables privacy-preserving modeling with no loss in predictive performance.
AI.motivation: Low-resource, nomadic settings have sparse data and heightened privacy concerns, hindering accurate vaccine coverage estimates and timely interventions.
AI.method: Dataset: MOH 510 registry, n=6,913, across 8 years. Models: Logistic Regression and XGBoost. Privacy: TabSyn, a tabular diffusion-based synthetic data generator, evaluated for maintaining predictive performance when training on synthetic data.
AI.result: Classification models reliably predict at-risk children with high recall/precision/F1 (exceeding 90% for some vaccines). Training on synthetic TabSyn data preserves predictive accuracy compared to models trained on real data.
AI.conclusion: Synthetic data approaches can enable privacy-preserving, scalable vaccination-coverage forecasting in clinics with limited digital infrastructure.

title: Bridging SFT and RL: Dynamic Policy Optimization for Robust Reasoning
summary: Post-training paradigms for Large Language Models (LLMs), primarily Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning (RL), face a fundamental dilemma: SFT provides stability (low variance) but suffers from high fitting bias, while RL enables exploration (low bias) but grapples with high gradient variance. Existing unified optimization strategies often employ naive loss weighting, overlooking the statistical conflict between these distinct gradient signals. In this paper, we provide a rigorous theoretical analysis of this bias-variance trade-off and propose \textbf{DYPO} (Dynamic Policy Optimization), a unified framework designed to structurally mitigate this conflict. DYPO integrates three core components: (1) a \textit{Group Alignment Loss (GAL)} that leverages intrinsic group dynamics to significantly reduce RL gradient variance; (2) a \textit{Multi-Teacher Distillation} mechanism that corrects SFT fitting bias via diverse reasoning paths; and (3) a \textit{Dynamic Exploitation-Exploration Gating} mechanism that adaptively arbitrates between stable SFT and exploratory RL based on reward feedback. Theoretical analysis confirms that DYPO linearly reduces fitting bias and minimizes overall variance. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DYPO significantly outperforms traditional sequential pipelines, achieving an average improvement of 4.8\% on complex reasoning benchmarks and 13.3\% on out-of-distribution tasks. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/Tocci-Zhu/DYPO.

AI.tldr: Introduces DYPO, a unified dynamic policy optimization framework for post-training LLMs that mitigates the bias-variance conflict between SFT (low variance but high bias) and RL (low bias but high variance) through Group Alignment Loss, Multi-Teacher Distillation, and Dynamic Exploitation-Exploration Gating; claims theoretical bias and variance reductions and empirical gains on reasoning and OOD tasks, with code release.
AI.motivation: Address fundamental bias-variance trade-off in post-training LLM optimization; existing methods use naive loss weighting and fail to account for conflict between gradient signals from SFT and RL.
AI.method: Propose DYPO with (1) GAL to reduce RL gradient variance using intrinsic group dynamics; (2) Multi-Teacher Distillation to correct SFT bias via diverse reasoning paths; (3) Dynamic Exploitation-Exploration Gating to adapt between SFT and RL based on reward feedback; theoretical analysis showing linear reduction in bias and variance minimization; extensive experiments showing improvements; code available.
AI.result: Average improvements of 4.8% on complex reasoning benchmarks and 13.3% on out-of-distribution tasks; DYPO outperforms sequential pipelines.
AI.conclusion: DYPO provides a principled, dynamic framework to balance SFT and RL signals, achieving better generalization and robustness in post-training LLM optimization.

title: Tora3: Trajectory-Guided Audio-Video Generation with Physical Coherence
summary: Audio-video (AV) generation has recently made strong progress in perceptual quality and multimodal coherence, yet generating content with plausible motion-sound relations remains challenging. Existing methods often produce object motions that are visually unstable and sounds that are only loosely aligned with salient motion or contact events, largely because they lack an explicit motion-aware structure shared by video and audio generation. We present Tora3, a trajectory-guided AV generation framework that improves physical coherence by using object trajectories as a shared kinematic prior. Rather than treating trajectories as a video-only control signal, Tora3 uses them to jointly guide visual motion and acoustic events. Specifically, we design a trajectory-aligned motion representation for video, a kinematic-audio alignment module driven by trajectory-derived second-order kinematic states, and a hybrid flow matching scheme that preserves trajectory fidelity in trajectory-conditioned regions while maintaining local coherence elsewhere. We further curate PAV, a large-scale AV dataset emphasizing motion-relevant patterns with automatically extracted motion annotations. Extensive experiments show that Tora3 improves motion realism, motion-sound synchronization, and overall AV generation quality over strong open-source baselines.

AI.tldr: Tora3 is a trajectory-guided AV generation framework that uses object trajectories as a shared kinematic prior to jointly drive video motion and audio events, with a trajectory-aligned motion representation, a kinematic-audio alignment module based on second-order kinematics, and a hybrid flow matching scheme. It introduces PAV, a motion-centric AV dataset. Experiments report improved motion realism and sound-visual synchronization over baselines.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of an explicit motion-aware, shared structure between video and audio generation, which leads to unstable motions and weak audio-visual alignment.
AI.method: Three main components: (1) trajectory-aligned motion representation for video; (2) kinematic-audio alignment module driven by trajectory-derived second-order (acceleration) states; (3) hybrid flow matching that preserves trajectory fidelity in trajectory-conditioned regions while maintaining local coherence. Also collects PAV, a large motion-focused AV dataset with automatic motion annotations.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show that Tora3 improves motion realism, motion-sound synchronization, and overall AV generation quality compared to strong open-source baselines.
AI.conclusion: A trajectory-guided framework with a shared kinematic prior can substantially enhance cross-modal coherence in AV generation; the PAV dataset supports motion-centric evaluation and may enable further advances in physically grounded AV synthesis.

title: Delve into the Applicability of Advanced Optimizers for Multi-Task Learning
summary: Multi-Task Learning (MTL) is a foundational machine learning problem that has seen extensive development over the past decade. Recently, various optimization-based MTL approaches have been proposed to learn multiple tasks simultaneously by altering the optimization trajectory. Although these methods strive to de-conflict and re-balance tasks, we empirically identify that their effectiveness is often undermined by an overlooked factor when employing advanced optimizers: the instant-derived gradients play only a marginal role in the actual parameter updates. This discrepancy prevents MTL frameworks from fully releasing its power on learning dynamics. Furthermore, we observe that Muon-a recently emerged advanced optimizer-inherently functions as a multi-task learner, which underscores the critical importance of the gradients used for its orthogonalization. To address these issues, we propose APT (Applicability of advanced oPTimizers), a framework featuring a simple adaptive momentum mechanism designed to balance the strengths between advanced optimizers and MTL. Additionally, we introduce a light direction preservation method to facilitate Muon's orthogonalization. Extensive experiments across four mainstream MTL datasets demonstrate that APT consistently augments existing MTL approaches, yielding substantial performance improvements.

AI.tldr: APT improves multi-task learning by balancing advanced optimizers with MTL and preserving gradient directions, yielding consistent gains across datasets.
AI.motivation: To address the observation that instant-derived gradients in optimization-based MTL have limited influence on parameter updates when using advanced optimizers, and to leverage optimizer-inherent multi-task learning capabilities (e.g., Muon) through improved gradient handling.
AI.method: Introduce APT, an adaptive momentum mechanism that balances the strengths of advanced optimizers and MTL; add a light direction preservation technique to support Muon's orthogonalization.
AI.result: Empirical validation on four mainstream MTL datasets shows that integrating APT with existing MTL methods yields substantial performance improvements.
AI.conclusion: APT provides a simple, effective framework to enhance MTL with advanced optimizers by improving gradient usage and orthogonalization, leading to better learning dynamics and performance.

title: Learning Vision-Language-Action World Models for Autonomous Driving
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have recently achieved notable progress in end-to-end autonomous driving by integrating perception, reasoning, and control within a unified multimodal framework. However, they often lack explicit modeling of temporal dynamics and global world consistency, which limits their foresight and safety. In contrast, world models can simulate plausible future scenes but generally struggle to reason about or evaluate the imagined future they generate. In this work, we present VLA-World, a simple yet effective VLA world model that unifies predictive imagination with reflective reasoning to improve driving foresight. VLA-World first uses an action-derived feasible trajectory to guide the generation of the next-frame image, capturing rich spatial and temporal cues that describe how the surrounding environment evolves. The model then reasons over this self-generated future imagined frame to refine the predicted trajectory, achieving higher performance and better interpretability. To support this pipeline, we curate nuScenes-GR-20K, a generative reasoning dataset derived from nuScenes, and employ a three-stage training strategy that includes pretraining, supervised fine-tuning, and reinforcement learning. Extensive experiments demonstrate that VLA-World consistently surpasses state-of-the-art VLA and world-model baselines on both planning and future-generation benchmarks. Project page: https://vlaworld.github.io

AI.tldr: A unified Vision-Language-Action (VLA) world model (VLA-World) integrates predictive imagination with reflective reasoning to improve driving foresight, using action-guided next-frame generation and imagined-frame reasoning to refine trajectories; introduces nuScenes-GR-20K for generative reasoning; achieves state-of-the-art on planning and future-generation with a three-stage training pipeline.
AI.motivation: Address the weaknesses of existing VLA models (limited temporal/global world consistency) and world models (weak reasoning about imagined futures). Propose a hybrid VLA-world approach that combines predictive imagination with reflective reasoning to enhance foresight and safety in autonomous driving.
AI.method: 1) Use an action-derived feasible trajectory to guide next-frame image generation to capture spatial-temporal evolution. 2) Reason over the self-generated imagined frame to refine the predicted trajectory (reflective reasoning). 3) Curate nuScenes-GR-20K, a generative reasoning dataset derived from nuScenes. 4) Train in three stages: pretraining, supervised fine-tuning, reinforcement learning.
AI.result: VLA-World consistently outperforms state-of-the-art VLA and world-model baselines on planning and future-generation benchmarks across extensive experiments.
AI.conclusion: Integrating predictive imagination with reflective reasoning improves driving foresight and interpretability. The proposed three-stage training and new dataset support robust generative reasoning, advancing end-to-end autonomous driving systems.

title: Predictive Entropy Links Calibration and Paraphrase Sensitivity in Medical Vision-Language Models
summary: Medical Vision Language Models VLMs suffer from two failure modes that threaten safe deployment mis calibrated confidence and sensitivity to question rephrasing. We show they share a common cause, proximity to the decision boundary, by benchmarking five uncertainty quantification methods on MedGemma 4BIT across in distribution MIMIC CXR and outof distribution PadChest chest X ray datasets, with cross architecture validation on LLaVA RAD7B. For well calibrated single model methods, predictive entropy from one forward pass predicts which samples will flip under rephrasing AUROC 0.711 on MedGemma, 0.878 on LLaVARAD p 10 4, enabling a single entropy threshold to flag both unreliable and rephrase sensitive predictions. A five member LoRA ensemble fails under the MIMIC PadChest shift 42.9 ECE, 34.1 accuracy, though LLaVA RAD s ensemble does not collapse 69.1. MC Dropout achieves the best calibration ECE 4.3 and selective prediction coverage 21.5 at 5 risk, yet total entropy from a single forward pass outperforms the ensemble for both error detection AUROC 0.743 vs 0.657 and paraphrase screening. Simple methods win.

AI.tldr: Single forward-pass predictive entropy often surpasses ensembles for detecting unreliable or paraphrase-sensitive predictions in medical VLMs; simple methods achieve strong calibration; ensembles can fail under distribution shift.
AI.motivation: Identify the root causes of miscalibration and sensitivity to question rephrasing in medical vision-language models and evaluate uncertainty quantification (UQ) methods across datasets and architectures to inform safe deployment.
AI.method: Benchmark five UQ methods on MedGemma 4BIT across in-distribution MIMIC CXR and out-of-distribution PadChest chest X-ray datasets, with cross-architecture validation on LLaVA RAD7B. Assess whether predictive entropy from a single forward pass can predict samples that flip under rephrasing (AUROC). Compare a five-member LoRA ensemble, LLaVA RADs ensemble, and MC Dropout across metrics such as AUROC, ECE, accuracy, and selective prediction coverage.
AI.result: Predictive entropy from a single forward pass predicts rephrase-induced flips with AUROC 0.711 on MedGemma and 0.878 on LLaVA-RAD (p<1e-4). A five-member LoRA ensemble collapses under MIMIC–PadChest shift (ECE 42.9, accuracy 34.1); LLaVA RADs ensemble remains stable (69.1). MC Dropout achieves best calibration (ECE 4.3) and selective prediction coverage (21.5 at 5% risk), yet total entropy from a single forward pass outperforms the ensemble for error detection (AUROC 0.743 vs 0.657) and paraphrase screening. Overall, simple methods win.“ ,
AI.conclusion: In medical VLMs, simple single-pass uncertainty signals (predictive entropy) often outperform ensembles for error detection and paraphrase screening. Calibration and proximity to the decision boundary under distribution shifts are key factors; ensemble approaches may not reliably mitigate these issues, suggesting a preference for efficient single-pass UQ in deployment.

title: Nested Radially Monotone Polar Occupancy Estimation: Clinically-Grounded Optic Disc and Cup Segmentation for Glaucoma Screening
summary: Valid segmentation of the optic disc (OD) and optic cup (OC) from fundus photographs is essential for glaucoma screening. Unfortunately, existing deep learning methods do not guarantee clinical validness including star-convexity and nested structure of OD and OC, resulting corruption in diagnostic metric, especially under cross-dataset domain shift. To adress this issue, this paper proposed NPS-Net (Nested Polar Shape Network), the first framework that formulates the OD/OC segmentation as nested radially monotone polar occupancy estimation.This output representation can guarantee the aforementioned clinical validness and achieve high accuracy. Evaluated across seven public datasets, NPS-Net shows strong zero-shot generalization. On RIM-ONE, it maintains 100% anatomical validity and improves Cup Dice by 12.8% absolute over the best baseline, reducing vCDR MAE by over 56%. On PAPILA, it achieves Disc Dice of 0.9438 and Disc HD95 of 2.78 px, an 83% reduction over the best competing method.

AI.tldr: NPS-Net introduces a nested radially monotone polar occupancy representation for optic disc/cup segmentation, guaranteeing star-convexity and nested structure, achieving strong zero-shot generalization and notable improvements across RIM-ONE and PAPILA datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing deep learning methods do not guarantee clinical validity (star-convexity and nested structure) of OD/OC segmentation, making diagnostic metrics unreliable under cross-dataset domain shifts.
AI.method: Formulates OD/OC segmentation as nested radially monotone polar occupancy estimation, outputting a polar occupancy representation that enforces star-convex and nested geometry and thus clinical validity.
AI.result: Evaluated on seven public datasets with strong zero-shot generalization. RIM-ONE: 100% anatomical validity; Cup Dice improved by 12.8 percentage points; vCDR MAE reduced by >56%. PAPILA: Disc Dice 0.9438; Disc HD95 2.78 px; 83% reduction over best competing method.
AI.conclusion: NPS-Net achieves clinically valid segmentation with high accuracy and cross-domain robustness; claims of being the first framework for nested polar occupancy-based OD/OC segmentation. Future work could address computational efficiency, ablations, and broader validation across more datasets.

title: Cross-Modal Knowledge Distillation from Spatial Transcriptomics to Histology
comment: Accepted to the CVMI Workshop at CVPR 2026. Project page: https://cross-modal-distillation.github.io/
summary: Spatial transcriptomics provides a molecularly rich description of tissue organization, enabling unsupervised discovery of tissue niches -- spatially coherent regions of distinct cell-type composition and function that are relevant to both biological research and clinical interpretation. However, spatial transcriptomics remains costly and scarce, while H&E histology is abundant but carries a less granular signal. We propose to leverage paired spatial transcriptomics and H&E data to transfer transcriptomics-derived niche structure to a histology-only model via cross-modal distillation. Across multiple tissue types and disease contexts, the distilled model achieves substantially higher agreement with transcriptomics-derived niche structure than unsupervised morphology-based baselines trained on identical image features, and recovers biologically meaningful neighborhood composition as confirmed by cell-type analysis. The resulting framework leverages paired spatial transcriptomic and H&E data during training, and can then be applied to held-out tissue regions using histology alone, without any transcriptomic input at inference time.

AI.tldr: Cross-modal distillation transfers tissue niche structure learned from spatial transcriptomics to histology-only models (H&E), enabling niche discovery from histology without transcriptomics at inference.
AI.motivation: Spatial transcriptomics provides rich molecular context but is costly; H&E histology is abundant but lacks granular signal. Leveraging paired data aims to enable transcriptomics-derived niche structure to be inferred from histology images at inference time.
AI.method: Train on paired spatial transcriptomics and H&E data to distill transcriptomics-derived niche structure into a histology-based model via cross-modal distillation. Evaluate across multiple tissue types and disease contexts. Deploy on held-out tissue regions using histology alone, without transcriptomic input at inference.
AI.result: The distilled histology-based model achieves substantially higher agreement with transcriptomics-derived niche structure than unsupervised morphology-based baselines trained on identical image features and recovers biologically meaningful neighborhood composition via cell-type analysis. The approach generalizes across tissue types and disease contexts.
AI.conclusion: Pairing spatial transcriptomic and H&E data during training enables histology-only inference of transcriptomics-like niche structure, expanding access to tissue-niche insights without requiring transcriptomic data at inference.

title: Efficient Hierarchical Implicit Flow Q-learning for Offline Goal-conditioned Reinforcement Learning
summary: Offline goal-conditioned reinforcement learning (GCRL) is a practical reinforcement learning paradigm that aims to learn goal-conditioned policies from reward-free offline data. Despite recent advances in hierarchical architectures such as HIQL, long-horizon control in offline GCRL remains challenging due to the limited expressiveness of Gaussian policies and the inability of high-level policies to generate effective subgoals. To address these limitations, we propose the goal-conditioned mean flow policy, which introduces an average velocity field into hierarchical policy modeling for offline GCRL. Specifically, the mean flow policy captures complex target distributions for both high-level and low-level policies through a learned average velocity field, enabling efficient action generation via one-step sampling. Furthermore, considering the insufficiency of goal representation, we introduce a LeJEPA loss that repels goal representation embeddings during training, thereby encouraging more discriminative representations and improving generalization. Experimental results show that our method achieves strong performance across both state-based and pixel-based tasks in the OGBench benchmark.

AI.tldr: Proposes a goal-conditioned mean flow policy for offline GCRL with a LeJEPA loss to improve goal representations; demonstrates strong results on OGBench across state- and pixel-based tasks.
AI.motivation: Address limited expressiveness of Gaussian policies and ineffective subgoal generation in long-horizon offline GCRL (e.g., HIQL). A richer policy distribution and discriminative goal embeddings are needed to enable long-horizon planning from reward-free offline data.
AI.method: Introduce a mean flow policy that incorporates a learned average velocity field to model target distributions for both high- and low-level policies, enabling efficient one-step action sampling. Add LeJEPA loss to repel goal representation embeddings to enhance discriminativeness and generalization.
AI.result: Empirical results show strong performance on both state-based and pixel-based tasks within the OGBench benchmark.
AI.conclusion: The combination of mean flow policy and discriminative goal representations improves expressiveness and generalization for offline GCRL, particularly for long-horizon tasks, enabling effective offline planning and control.

title: Memory-Efficient Transfer Learning with Fading Side Networks via Masked Dual Path Distillation
summary: Memory-efficient transfer learning (METL) approaches have recently achieved promising performance in adapting pre-trained models to downstream tasks. They avoid applying gradient backpropagation in large backbones, thus significantly reducing the number of trainable parameters and high memory consumption during fine-tuning. However, since they typically employ a lightweight and learnable side network, these methods inevitably introduce additional memory and time overhead during inference, which contradicts the ultimate goal of efficient transfer learning. To address the above issue, we propose a novel approach dubbed Masked Dual Path Distillation (MDPD) to accelerate inference while retaining parameter and memory efficiency in fine-tuning with fading side networks. Specifically, MDPD develops a framework that enhances the performance by mutually distilling the frozen backbones and learnable side networks in fine-tuning, and discard the side network during inference without sacrificing accuracy. Moreover, we design a novel feature-based knowledge distillation method for the encoder structure with multiple layers. Extensive experiments on distinct backbones across vision/language-only and vision-and-language tasks demonstrate that our method not only accelerates inference by at least 25.2\% while keeping parameter and memory consumption comparable, but also remarkably promotes the accuracy compared to SOTA approaches. The source code is available at https://github.com/Zhang-VKk/MDPD.

AI.tldr: MDPD speeds up inference in memory-efficient transfer learning by distilling knowledge between a frozen backbone and a lightweight side network during training and removing the side network at inference, yielding at least 25.2% speedup with comparable parameters/memory and improved accuracy.
AI.motivation: METL reduces trainable parameters by avoiding backpropagation through large backbones, but current side networks used during fine-tuning add inference-time overhead. There is a need to maintain memory/parameter efficiency while achieving fast inference.
AI.method: Introduce Masked Dual Path Distillation (MDPD): a framework that jointly distills knowledge between a frozen backbone and a learnable side network during fine-tuning, with a masked/dual-path mechanism to leverage both paths; also a feature-based knowledge distillation method applied to multi-layer encoders; the side network is discarded during inference (fading) without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across vision-only, language-only, and vision-language tasks show inference acceleration of at least 25.2%, with parameter and memory consumption comparable to METL baselines, and improved accuracy relative to SOTA methods.
AI.conclusion: MDPD delivers fast, resource-efficient transfer learning by leveraging mutual distillation during training and discarding the side network at inference, with strong, cross-domain performance and available source code.

title: Modality-Aware Zero-Shot Pruning and Sparse Attention for Efficient Multimodal Edge Inference
summary: Edge devices increasingly run multimodal sensing pipelines that must remain accurate despite fluctuating power budgets and unpredictable sensor dropout. Existing pruning methods fail under these conditions: they generally require fine-tuning after compression, consuming over $10\times$ the deployment energy, and they assign static importance scores that are blind to which sensors are present. We present the SentryFuse framework, which addresses both challenges jointly through two key components. First, SentryGate learns modality-conditioned importance scores during training via first-order saliency supervision and then prunes attention heads and feed-forward channels at deployment without fine-tuning. Second, SentryAttend replaces dense self-attention, a key bottleneck in contemporary multimodal architectures, with sparse grouped-query attention, yielding a net 15% reduction in GFLOPs across three different multimodal architectures. Across three applications and multimodal backbones, SentryGate achieves a 12.7% average accuracy improvement over the strongest pruning baseline, and upto to 18% under modality dropout conditions. Together, SentryFuse reduces memory by 28.2% and lowers latency by up to $1.63\times$ without further fine-tuning, establishing modality-aware zero-shot compression as a practical path to multimodal intelligence on heterogeneous edge hardware.

AI.tldr: SentryFuse enables modality-aware zero-shot compression for multimodal edge models via SentryGate and SentryAttend, achieving substantial efficiency gains with accuracy gains under modality dropout without fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Edge multimodal systems face fluctuating power budgets and sensor dropout; existing pruning methods require fine-tuning and use static, modality-agnostic importance scores, hindering robustness.
AI.method: Two components: SentryGate learns modality-conditioned importance scores during training using first-order saliency supervision and prunes attention heads and feed-forward channels at deployment without fine-tuning; SentryAttend substitutes dense self-attention with sparse grouped-query attention to reduce FLOPs in multimodal backbones.
AI.result: Average accuracy improves by 12.7% over the strongest pruning baseline, up to 18% under modality dropout; memory reduced by 28.2%; latency reduced up to 1.63×; net 15% GFLOPs reduction across three architectures.
AI.conclusion: Modality-aware zero-shot compression is a practical path to robust multimodal intelligence on heterogeneous edge hardware.

title: Off-the-shelf Vision Models Benefit Image Manipulation Localization
summary: Image manipulation localization (IML) and general vision tasks are typically treated as two separate research directions due to the fundamental differences between manipulation-specific and semantic features. In this paper, however, we bridge this gap by introducing a fresh perspective: these two directions are intrinsically connected, and general semantic priors can benefit IML. Building on this insight, we propose a novel trainable adapter (named ReVi) that repurposes existing off-the-shelf general-purpose vision models (e.g., image generation and segmentation networks) for IML. Inspired by robust principal component analysis, the adapter disentangles semantic redundancy from manipulation-specific information embedded in these models and selectively enhances the latter. Unlike existing IML methods that require extensive model redesign and full retraining, our method relies on the off-the-shelf vision models with frozen parameters and only fine-tunes the proposed adapter. The experimental results demonstrate the superiority of our method, showing the potential for scalable IML frameworks.

AI.tldr: A trainable adapter (ReVi) enables using off-the-shelf vision models for image manipulation localization by disentangling semantic redundancy and enhancing manipulation-specific signals; backbone models remain frozen and only the adapter is tuned to achieve scalable IML.
AI.motivation: IML and general semantic vision tasks are intrinsically connected; leveraging general semantic priors from pre-trained models can improve manipulation localization. A lightweight, adapter-based approach offers scalable integration without full retraining.
AI.method: Introduce ReVi, a trainable adapter inserted into frozen off-the-shelf vision models (e.g., image generation and segmentation networks). Inspired by robust PCA, ReVi separates semantic redundancy from manipulation-specific information and amplifies the latter. Only the adapter is trained; backbones remain fixed, enabling efficient fine-tuning and scalability.
AI.result: Empirical results indicate superiority of ReVi over baselines, demonstrating strong IML performance with reduced training cost and better scalability when using existing vision models.
AI.conclusion: ReVi shows that leveraging semantic priors from general vision models via a lightweight adapter enables scalable, effective IML without full-model retraining, suggesting a versatile framework for integrating manipulation localization with general vision tasks.

title: Neighbourhood Transformer: Switchable Attention for Monophily-Aware Graph Learning
summary: Graph neural networks (GNNs) have been widely adopted in engineering applications such as social network analysis, chemical research and computer vision. However, their efficacy is severely compromised by the inherent homophily assumption, which fails to hold for heterophilic graphs where dissimilar nodes are frequently connected. To address this fundamental limitation in graph learning, we first draw inspiration from the recently discovered monophily property of real-world graphs, and propose Neighbourhood Transformers (NT), a novel paradigm that applies self-attention within every local neighbourhood instead of aggregating messages to the central node as in conventional message-passing GNNs. This design makes NT inherently monophily-aware and theoretically guarantees its expressiveness is no weaker than traditional message-passing frameworks. For practical engineering deployment, we further develop a neighbourhood partitioning strategy equipped with switchable attentions, which reduces the space consumption of NT by over 95% and time consumption by up to 92.67%, significantly expanding its applicability to larger graphs. Extensive experiments on 10 real-world datasets (5 heterophilic and 5 homophilic graphs) show that NT outperforms all current state-of-the-art methods on node classification tasks, demonstrating its superior performance and cross-domain adaptability. The full implementation code of this work is publicly available at https://github.com/cf020031308/MoNT to facilitate reproducibility and industrial adoption.

AI.tldr: NT proposes neighborhood transformers that apply self-attention within local neighborhoods, modeling monophily and addressing heterophily; includes neighborhood partitioning and switchable attentions to reduce memory and time; achieves state-of-the-art on node classification across 10 real-world graphs.
AI.motivation: Limitations of classic GNNs due to homophily assumption; monophily property in real-world graphs; desire to handle heterophily effectively, improve expressiveness and scalability.
AI.method: Introduce Neighbourhood Transformers (NT) that compute self-attention within each local neighborhood rather than aggregating to central node; ensure expressiveness at least as powerful as message-passing GNNs; add neighborhood partitioning with switchable attentions to reduce space/time; provide theoretical guarantees; implement and evaluate on 10 datasets; provide code.
AI.result: NT outperforms state-of-the-art methods on node classification across both heterophilic and homophilic graphs; significant reductions in space (>95%) and time (up to 92.67%); robust cross-domain performance.
AI.conclusion: NT is a monophily-aware GNN paradigm with strong theoretical guarantees and practical efficiency improvements; broad applicability and availability of code for reproducibility.

title: Identification and Anonymization of Named Entities in Unstructured Information Sources for Use in Social Engineering Detection
summary: This study addresses the challenge of creating datasets for cybercrime analysis while complying with the requirements of regulations such as the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and Organic Law 10/1995 of the Penal Code. To this end, a system is proposed for collecting information from the Telegram platform, including text, audio, and images; the implementation of speech-to-text transcription models incorporating signal enhancement techniques; and the evaluation of different Named Entity Recognition (NER) solutions, including Microsoft Presidio and AI models designed using a transformer-based architecture. Experimental results indicate that Parakeet achieves the best performance in audio transcription, while the proposed NER solutions achieve the highest f1-score values in detecting sensitive information. In addition, anonymization metrics are presented that allow evaluation of the preservation of structural coherence in the data, while simultaneously guaranteeing the protection of personal information and supporting cybersecurity research within the current legal framework.

AI.tldr: Proposes a GDPR-compliant pipeline to collect Telegram data for cybercrime analysis, evaluating speech-to-text (Parakeet) and NER (Microsoft Presidio and transformer models), and introduces anonymization metrics to preserve data utility while protecting privacy.
AI.motivation: Need for compliant datasets enabling cybercrime research without exposing personal data.
AI.method: Collect Telegram text, audio, and images; apply speech-to-text with signal enhancement; evaluate NER solutions including Microsoft Presidio and transformer-based models; propose anonymization metrics for data utility and privacy.
AI.result: Parakeet yields best audio transcription; proposed NER solutions achieve highest F1 for detecting sensitive information; anonymization metrics indicate preserved structural coherence and effective privacy protection; supports cybersecurity research within current legal framework.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of GDPR-compliant dataset generation for cybercrime analysis with effective transcription and NER, complemented by anonymization metrics to balance data utility and privacy.

title: Detecting Diffusion-generated Images via Dynamic Assembly ForestsDetecting Diffusion-generated Images via Dynamic Assembly Forests
summary: Diffusion models are known for generating high-quality images, causing serious security concerns. To combat this, most efforts rely on deep neural networks (e.g., CNNs and Transformers), while largely overlooking the potential of traditional machine learning models. In this paper, we freshly investigate such alternatives and proposes a novel Dynamic Assembly Forest model (DAF) to detect diffusion-generated images. Built upon the deep forest paradigm, DAF addresses the inherent limitations in feature learning and scalable training, making it an effective diffusion-generated image detector. Compared to existing DNN-based methods, DAF has significantly fewer parameters, much lower computational cost, and can be deployed without GPUs, while achieving competitive performance under standard evaluation protocols. These results highlight the strong potential of the proposed method as a practical substitute for heavyweight DNN models in resource-constrained scenarios. Our code and models are available at https://github.com/OUC-VAS/DAF.

AI.tldr: A Dynamic Assembly Forest (DAF) is a lightweight, CPU-friendly diffusion image detector built on the deep forest paradigm, offering competitive accuracy with far fewer parameters and no GPU requirement, serving as a practical alternative to DNN detectors.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-model generated images pose security concerns. DNN detectors dominate, but are resource-intensive. There is a need for accurate, lightweight, and hardware-free detectors.
AI.method: Introduce the Dynamic Assembly Forest (DAF), a deep-forest-based detector that mitigates feature-learning limitations and scales training. It emphasizes efficiency (fewer parameters, lower compute) and CPU/GPU-free deployment, contrasting with CNN/Transformer-based detectors.
AI.result: DAF achieves competitive detection performance under standard benchmarks while using substantially fewer parameters and lower computational cost, and can be deployed without GPUs. Code/models are released at the provided repository.
AI.conclusion: DAF demonstrates that strong diffusion-generated image detection is achievable with traditional machine learning frameworks, offering a practical substitute for heavyweight DNN detectors in resource-constrained environments.

title: The nextAI Solution to the NeurIPS 2023 LLM Efficiency Challenge
summary: The rapid evolution of Large Language Models (LLMs) has significantly impacted the field of natural language processing, but their growing complexity raises concerns about resource usage and transparency. Addressing these challenges, we participated in the NeurIPS LLM Efficiency Challenge, aiming to fine-tune a foundation model within stringent constraints. Our focus was the LLaMa2 70 billion model, optimized on a single A100 40GB GPU within a 24-hour limit. Our methodology hinged on a custom dataset, carefully assembled from diverse open-source resources and benchmark tests, aligned with the challenge's open-source ethos. Our approach leveraged Quantized-Low Rank Adaptation (QLoRA) Fine tuning, integrated with advanced attention mechanisms like Flash Attention 2. We experimented with various configurations of the LoRA technique, optimizing the balance between computational efficiency and model accuracy. Our fine-tuning strategy was underpinned by the creation and iterative testing of multiple dataset compositions, leading to the selection of a version that demonstrated robust performance across diverse tasks and benchmarks. The culmination of our efforts was an efficiently fine-tuned LLaMa2 70B model that operated within the constraints of a single GPU, showcasing not only a significant reduction in resource utilization but also high accuracy across a range of QA benchmarks. Our study serves as a testament to the feasibility of optimizing large-scale models in resource-constrained environments, emphasizing the potential of LLMs in real-world applications.

AI.tldr: Efficiently fine-tuned LLaMa2 70B on a single A100 GPU within 24 hours using QLoRA and Flash Attention 2, via a carefully crafted open-source dataset; achieved strong QA performance with significantly reduced resource usage, illustrating feasibility of resource-constrained LLM fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Address resource usage and transparency concerns in large language models; demonstrate that high-performance LLMs can be fine-tuned under strict hardware constraints, contributing to open, reproducible methodologies.
AI.method: QLoRA fine-tuning of LLaMa2 70B on a single NVIDIA A100 40GB GPU within 24 hours; use of Flash Attention 2; exploration of various LoRA configurations; construction of a bespoke, open-source dataset from diverse resources and benchmarks; iterative dataset testing and model evaluation focused on QA benchmarks.
AI.result: An efficiently fine-tuned LLaMa2 70B model that runs on a single GPU with markedly reduced resource usage and robust QA performance across benchmarks, validating the practicality of constrained-resource fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: This work demonstrates the feasibility of optimizing large-scale models in resource-constrained environments and highlights the potential for real-world deployment of capable, efficient LLMs, aided by open-source data and methods.

title: FIRE-CIR: Fine-grained Reasoning for Composed Fashion Image Retrieval
summary: Composed image retrieval (CIR) aims to retrieve a target image that depicts a reference image modified by a textual description. While recent vision-language models (VLMs) achieve promising CIR performance by embedding images and text into a shared space for retrieval, they often fail to reason about what to preserve and what to change. This limitation hinders interpretability and yields suboptimal results, particularly in fine-grained domains like fashion. In this paper, we introduce FIRE-CIR, a model that brings compositional reasoning and interpretability to fashion CIR. Instead of relying solely on embedding similarity, FIRE-CIR performs question-driven visual reasoning: it automatically generates attribute-focused visual questions derived from the modification text, and verifies the corresponding visual evidence in both reference and candidate images. To train such a reasoning system, we automatically construct a large-scale fashion-specific visual question answering dataset, containing questions requiring either single- or dual-image analysis. During retrieval, our model leverages this explicit reasoning to re-rank candidate results, filtering out images inconsistent with the intended modifications. Experimental results on the Fashion IQ benchmark show that FIRE-CIR outperforms state-of-the-art methods in retrieval accuracy. It also provides interpretable, attribute-level insights into retrieval decisions.

AI.tldr: FIRE-CIR couples compositional reasoning with fashion image retrieval by generating attribute-focused visual questions from the modification text, verifying evidence in reference and candidate images, and re-ranking results; it achieves SOTA on Fashion IQ with interpretable attribute-level explanations.
AI.motivation: Current vision-language CIR methods rely on embedding similarity and struggle to decide what to preserve vs. modify, leading to limited interpretability in fine-grained domains like fashion.
AI.method: 1) Automatically generate attribute-focused visual questions from the modification text. 2) Verify the corresponding visual evidence in both reference and candidate images. 3) Train on a large-scale fashion-specific VQA dataset containing single- and dual-image questions. 4) Use explicit reasoning to re-rank candidate results during retrieval, filtering inconsistent images.
AI.result: FIRE-CIR achieves state-of-the-art retrieval accuracy on Fashion IQ and provides interpretable, attribute-level insights into retrieval decisions.
AI.conclusion: By incorporating question-driven visual reasoning into CIR, FIRE-CIR enhances both interpretability and accuracy in fashion retrieval, with potential applicability to other domains.

title: U-Cast: A Surprisingly Simple and Efficient Frontier Probabilistic AI Weather Forecaster
comment: Our code is available at: https://github.com/Rose-STL-Lab/u-cast
summary: AI-based weather forecasting now rivals traditional physics-based ensembles, but state-of-the-art (SOTA) models rely on specialized architectures and massive computational budgets, creating a high barrier to entry. We demonstrate that such complexity is unnecessary for frontier performance. We introduce U-Cast, a probabilistic forecaster built on a standard U-Net backbone trained with a simple recipe: deterministic pre-training on Mean Absolute Error followed by short probabilistic fine-tuning on the Continuous Ranked Probability Score (CRPS) using Monte Carlo Dropout for stochasticity. As a result, our model matches or exceeds the probabilistic skill of GenCast and IFS ENS at 1.5$^\circ\$ resolution while reducing training compute by over 10$\times$ compared to leading CRPS-based models and inference latency by over 10$\times$ compared to diffusion-based models. U-Cast trains in under 12 H200 GPU-days and generates a 60-step ensemble forecast in 11 seconds. These results suggest that scalable, general-purpose architectures paired with efficient training curricula can match complex domain-specific designs at a fraction of the cost, opening the training of frontier probabilistic weather models to the broader community. Our code is available at: https://github.com/Rose-STL-Lab/u-cast.

AI.tldr: A standard U-Net with a two-stage training recipe (deterministic MAE pretraining, then short probabilistic CRPS fine-tuning with MC Dropout) achieves frontier probabilistic weather forecasting at 1.5° resolution, matching/exceeding GenCast and IFS ENS while dramatically reducing compute and latency.
AI.motivation: High barrier to entry for frontier probabilistic weather models due to reliance on specialized architectures and massive compute. There is a need for scalable, general-purpose architectures with efficient training to democratize access while preserving performance.
AI.method: U-Cast uses a standard U-Net backbone. It performs deterministic pre-training optimized for Mean Absolute Error, followed by a short probabilistic fine-tuning phase optimizing CRPS with Monte Carlo Dropout to introduce stochasticity. The model produces 60-step ensemble forecasts and is trained with a lightweight curriculum.
AI.result: Matches or exceeds the probabilistic skill of GenCast and IFS ENS at 1.5° resolution. Training compute reduced by over 10× compared to leading CRPS-based models; inference latency reduced by over 10× relative to diffusion-based models. Training completed in under 12 H100 GPU-days; 60-step ensemble forecast generated in 11 seconds.
AI.conclusion: Scalable, general-purpose architectures paired with efficient training curricula can reach frontier performance in probabilistic weather forecasting at a fraction of the cost, enabling broader community access to advanced models. Code is publicly available.

title: Few-Shot Personalized Age Estimation
summary: Existing age estimation methods treat each face as an independent sample, learning a global mapping from appearance to age. This ignores a well-documented phenomenon: individuals age at different rates due to genetics, lifestyle, and health, making the mapping from face to age identity-dependent. When reference images of the same person with known ages are available, we can exploit this context to personalize the estimate. The only existing benchmark for this task (NIST FRVT) is closed-source and limited to a single reference image. In this work, we introduce OpenPAE, the first open benchmark for $N$-shot personalized age estimation with strict evaluation protocols. We establish a hierarchy of increasingly sophisticated baselines: from arithmetic offset, through closed-form Bayesian linear regression, to a conditional attentive neural process. Our experiments show that personalization consistently improves performance, that the gains are not merely domain adaptation, and that nonlinear methods significantly outperform simpler alternatives. We release all models, code, protocols, and evaluation splits.

AI.tldr: OpenPAE introduces the first open benchmark for N-shot personalized age estimation, showing that personalization via an increasing hierarchy of baselines (arithmetic offset, Bayesian linear regression, and conditional attentive neural processes) consistently improves age estimation accuracy over non-personalized methods, and that nonlinear personalization yields the best gains; all code, models, and evaluation splits are released.
AI.motivation: Address the limitations of treating faces as independent samples in age estimation and the lack of open benchmarks for personalized aging models. The work argues that individual aging rates vary due to genetics, lifestyle, and health, and that leveraging reference images per person can personalize estimates. It fills the gap left by the closed-source NIST FRVT benchmark by providing an open, standardized evaluation for N-shot personalization.
AI.method: Establish a hierarchy of personalization baselines: (1) arithmetic offset, (2) closed-form Bayesian linear regression, (3) a conditional attentive neural process. Propose OpenPAE, an open benchmark with strict evaluation protocols for N-shot personalized age estimation. Release all models, code, protocols, and evaluation splits.
AI.result: Personalization consistently improves performance across experiments. Gains are not solely due to domain adaptation. Nonlinear methods significantly outperform simpler baselines, with conditional attentive neural processes providing the strongest performance. The authors release the full experimental framework and data splits to enable reproducible research.
AI.conclusion: Personalized age estimation is beneficial and scalable with N-shot references. OpenPAE enables standardized, rigorous evaluation of personalization approaches, and nonlinear personalization models offer the best performance gains among the proposed baselines.

title: PDE-regularized Dynamics-informed Diffusion with Uncertainty-aware Filtering for Long-Horizon Dynamics
summary: Long-horizon spatiotemporal prediction remains a challenging problem due to cumulative errors, noise amplification, and the lack of physical consistency in existing models. While diffusion models provide a probabilistic framework for modeling uncertainty, conventional approaches often rely on mean squared error objectives and fail to capture the underlying dynamics governed by physical laws. In this work, we propose PDYffusion, a dynamics-informed diffusion framework that integrates PDE-based regularization and uncertainty-aware forecasting for stable long-term prediction. The proposed method consists of two key components: a PDE-regularized interpolator and a UKF-based forecaster. The interpolator incorporates a differential operator to enforce physically consistent intermediate states, while the forecaster leverages the Unscented Kalman Filter to explicitly model uncertainty and mitigate error accumulation during iterative prediction. We provide theoretical analyses showing that the proposed interpolator satisfies PDE-constrained smoothness properties, and that the forecaster converges under the proposed loss formulation. Extensive experiments on multiple dynamical datasets demonstrate that PDYffusion achieves superior performance in terms of CRPS and MSE, while maintaining stable uncertainty behavior measured by SSR. We further analyze the inherent trade-off between prediction accuracy and uncertainty, showing that our method provides a balanced and robust solution for long-horizon forecasting.

AI.tldr: PDYffusion blends PDE-based regularization with a UKF-based forecaster to enable stable, uncertainty-aware long-horizon diffusion forecasting; achieves improved CRPS/MSE and SSR.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon spatiotemporal prediction suffers from accumulating errors, noise amplification, and physical-compatibility issues. Diffusion models with MSE losses fail to enforce physics; a dynamics-informed approach is needed to enforce PDE consistency and explicitly model uncertainty.
AI.method: Two components: (1) PDE-regularized interpolator that uses a differential operator to enforce PDE-consistent intermediate states; (2) UKF-based forecaster for uncertainty propagation and mitigation of error accumulation. The paper provides theoretical results: PDE-constrained smoothness for the interpolator and convergence for the forecaster. Evaluation on dynamical datasets using CRPS, MSE, and SSR.
AI.result: PDYffusion achieves superior performance in CRPS and MSE, while maintaining stable uncertainty behavior as measured by SSR. The authors also analyze the trade-off between prediction accuracy and uncertainty, showing a balanced and robust solution for long-horizon forecasting.
AI.conclusion: A dynamics-informed diffusion framework that integrates physics-based regularization with uncertainty-aware forecasting offers a robust solution for long-horizon spatiotemporal prediction, maintaining physical consistency and stable uncertainty dynamics.

title: FaceLiVTv2: An Improved Hybrid Architecture for Efficient Mobile Face Recognition
summary: Lightweight face recognition is increasingly important for deployment on edge and mobile devices, where strict constraints on latency, memory, and energy consumption must be met alongside reliable accuracy. Although recent hybrid CNN-Transformer architectures have advanced global context modeling, striking an effective balance between recognition performance and computational efficiency remains an open challenge. In this work, we present FaceLiVTv2, an improved version of our FaceLiVT hybrid architecture designed for efficient global--local feature interaction in mobile face recognition. At its core is Lite MHLA, a lightweight global token interaction module that replaces the original multi-layer attention design with multi-head linear token projections and affine rescale transformations, reducing redundancy while preserving representational diversity across heads. We further integrate Lite MHLA into a unified RepMix block that coordinates local and global feature interactions and adopts global depthwise convolution for adaptive spatial aggregation in the embedding stage. Under our experimental setup, results on LFW, CA-LFW, CP-LFW, CFP-FP, AgeDB-30, and IJB show that FaceLiVTv2 consistently improves the accuracy-efficiency trade-off over existing lightweight methods. Notably, FaceLiVTv2 reduces mobile inference latency by 22% relative to FaceLiVTv1, achieves speedups of up to 30.8% over GhostFaceNets on mobile devices, and delivers 20-41% latency improvements over EdgeFace and KANFace across platforms while maintaining higher recognition accuracy. These results demonstrate that FaceLiVTv2 offers a practical and deployable solution for real-time face recognition. Code is available at https://github.com/novendrastywn/FaceLiVT.

AI.tldr: FaceLiVTv2 is an efficient lightweight hybrid CNN-Transformer for mobile face recognition, improving latency and accuracy via Lite MHLA (lightweight global token interaction) and RepMix blocks with global depthwise conv, achieving notable latency reductions while maintaining accuracy across standard datasets.
AI.motivation: Edge/mobile face recognition requires low latency, limited memory, and energy efficiency without sacrificing recognition accuracy. Prior hybrid CNN-Transformer approaches struggle to balance performance and efficiency.
AI.method: Introduce Lite MHLA: a lightweight global token interaction module employing multi-head linear token projections and affine rescale transformations to replace heavy multi-layer attention. Integrate into a unified RepMix block coordinating local/global feature interactions, with global depthwise convolution for adaptive spatial aggregation in the embedding stage.
AI.result: FaceLiVTv2 achieves improved accuracy-efficiency trade-offs on standard benchmarks (LFW, CA-LFW, CP-LFW, CFP-FP, AgeDB-30, IJB). Mobile inference latency is reduced by 22% relative to FaceLiVTv1; up to 30.8% speedup over GhostFaceNets on mobile; 20-41% latency improvements over EdgeFace and KANFace across platforms, while maintaining higher recognition accuracy.
AI.conclusion: FaceLiVTv2 provides a practical, deployable solution for real-time mobile face recognition, combining efficient global-local interaction with strong accuracy, and is supported by publicly available code.

title: Feature-Label Modal Alignment for Robust Partial Multi-Label Learning
summary: In partial multi-label learning (PML), each instance is associated with a set of candidate labels containing both ground-truth and noisy labels. The presence of noisy labels disrupts the correspondence between features and labels, degrading classification performance. To address this challenge, we propose a novel PML method based on feature-label modal alignment (PML-MA), which treats features and labels as two complementary modalities and restores their consistency through systematic alignment. Specifically, PML-MA first employs low-rank orthogonal decomposition to generate pseudo-labels that approximate the true label distribution by filtering noisy labels. It then aligns features and pseudo-labels through both global projection into a common subspace and local preservation of neighborhood structures. Finally, a multi-peak class prototype learning mechanism leverages the multi-label nature where instances simultaneously belong to multiple categories, using pseudo-labels as soft membership weights to enhance discriminability. By integrating modal alignment with prototype-guided refinement, PML-MA ensures pseudo-labels better reflect the true distribution while maintaining robustness against label noise. Extensive experiments on both real-world and synthetic datasets demonstrate that PML-MA significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving superior classification accuracy and noise robustness.

AI.tldr: PML-MA introduces feature-label modal alignment with noise-robust pseudo-labels via low-rank filtering, global-local alignment, and multi-peak prototype learning for multi-label classification.
AI.motivation: Noisy candidate labels in partial multi-label learning disrupt feature-label correspondence and degrade accuracy; robust pseudo-label generation and aligned representation are needed.
AI.method: 1) Use low-rank orthogonal decomposition to filter noisy labels and generate pseudo-labels; 2) Align features and pseudo-labels globally in a shared subspace and locally by preserving neighborhood structure; 3) Apply multi-peak class prototype learning using pseudo-labels as soft membership weights to enhance discriminability.
AI.result: Experiments on real-world and synthetic data show that PML-MA achieves superior classification accuracy and robustness to label noise, outperforming state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: Modal alignment between features and pseudo-labels with prototype-guided refinement yields pseudo-labels closer to the true distribution and improved robustness in partial multi-label learning.

title: Geometry Reinforced Efficient Attention Tuning Equipped with Normals for Robust Stereo Matching
summary: Despite remarkable advances in image-driven stereo matching over the past decade, Synthetic-to-Realistic Zero-Shot (Syn-to-Real) generalization remains an open challenge. This suboptimal generalization performance mainly stems from cross-domain shifts and ill-posed ambiguities inherent in image textures, particularly in occluded, textureless, repetitive, and non-Lambertian (specular/transparent) regions. To improve Syn-to-Real generalization, we propose GREATEN, a framework that incorporates surface normals as domain-invariant, object-intrinsic, and discriminative geometric cues to compensate for the limitations of image textures. The proposed framework consists of three key components. First, a Gated Contextual-Geometric Fusion (GCGF) module adaptively suppresses unreliable contextual cues in image features and fuses the filtered image features with normal-driven geometric features to construct domain-invariant and discriminative contextual-geometric representations. Second, a Specular-Transparent Augmentation (STA) strategy improves the robustness of GCGF against misleading visual cues in non-Lambertian regions. Third, sparse attention designs preserve the fine-grained global feature extraction capability of GREAT-Stereo for handling occlusion and texture-related ambiguities while substantially reducing computational overhead, including Sparse Spatial (SSA), Sparse Dual-Matching (SDMA), and Simple Volume (SVA) attentions. Trained exclusively on synthetic data such as SceneFlow, GREATEN-IGEV achieves outstanding Syn-to-Real performance. Specifically, it reduces errors by 30% on ETH3D, 8.5% on the non-Lambertian Booster, and 14.1% on KITTI-2015, compared to FoundationStereo, Monster-Stereo, and DEFOM-Stereo, respectively. In addition, GREATEN-IGEV runs 19.2% faster than GREAT-IGEV and supports high-resolution (3K) inference on Middlebury with disparity ranges up to 768.

AI.tldr: GREATEN enhances synthetic-to-real stereo generalization by combining image features with surface-normal cues through a gated fusion, augmenting with specular/transparent robustness, and using sparse attentions to maintain fine-grained features, achieving strong cross-domain performance and faster, high-resolution inference.
AI.motivation: Cross-domain shifts and ill-posed texture ambiguities (occlusion, textureless, repetitive, non-Lambertian regions) hinder Syn-to-Real stereo generalization; surface normals offer domain-invariant, intrinsic geometry to mitigate this.
AI.method: Gated Contextual-Geometric Fusion (GCGF) that fuses image features with normal-driven geometric features via gating; Specular-Transparent Augmentation (STA) to bolster robustness in non-Lambertian regions; Sparse attention schemes (SSA, SDMA, SVA) to preserve global-feature extraction with lower computational cost; trained solely on synthetic data (SceneFlow).
AI.result: GREATEN-IGEV achieves notable Syn-to-Real gains: ~30% error reduction on ETH3D, ~8.5% on the non-Lambertian Booster, and ~14.1% on KITTI-2015, relative to baseline methods; runs ~19.2% faster than GREAT-IGEV; supports high-resolution (3K) Middlebury inference with disparity up to 768.
AI.conclusion: Integrating surface-normal geometry with adaptive fusion and targeted augmentation yields robust domain-invariant representations for stereo matching, enabling improved generalization from synthetic data to real-world scenarios, while maintaining efficiency and compatibility with high-resolution disparity estimation.

title: Beyond Isolated Clients: Integrating Graph-Based Embeddings into Event Sequence Models
comment: Short paper accepted at ACM Web Conference 2026 (WWW '26)
summary: Large-scale digital platforms generate billions of timestamped user-item interactions (events) that are crucial for predicting user attributes in, e.g., fraud prevention and recommendations. While self-supervised learning (SSL) effectively models the temporal order of events, it typically overlooks the global structure of the user-item interaction graph. To bridge this gap, we propose three model-agnostic strategies for integrating this structural information into contrastive SSL: enriching event embeddings, aligning client representations with graph embeddings, and adding a structural pretext task. Experiments on four financial and e-commerce datasets demonstrate that our approach consistently improves the accuracy (up to a 2.3% AUC) and reveals that graph density is a key factor in selecting the optimal integration strategy.

AI.tldr: Enhance SSL for timestamped user-item events by incorporating interaction graph structure via three model-agnostic strategies; yields up to 2.3% AUC improvement; graph density guides strategy choice.
AI.motivation: Self-supervised learning (SSL) captures temporal order but often neglects the global user-item interaction graph. Integrating graph structure can provide complementary signals for predicting user attributes (e.g., fraud risk, recommendations).
AI.method: Propose three model-agnostic strategies to inject structural information into contrastive SSL: (1) enrich event embeddings with graph-aware features; (2) align user/client representations with graph embeddings; (3) add a structural pretext task reflecting the graph topology. Evaluate across four financial and e-commerce datasets to test compatibility with existing SSL frameworks.
AI.result: Consistent improvements in accuracy, up to 2.3% in AUC across datasets. Findings indicate that graph density is a key factor in choosing the optimal integration strategy.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating the interaction graph structure into SSL for large-scale timestamped events improves predictive performance. Practitioners should consider graph density when selecting among the proposed strategies, and the approaches are model-agnostic and broadly applicable to contrastive SSL settings.

title: Deep Light Pollution Removal in Night Cityscape Photographs
summary: Nighttime photography is severely degraded by light pollution induced by pervasive artificial lighting in urban environments. After long-range scattering and spatial diffusion, unwanted artificial light overwhelms natural night luminance, generates skyglow that washes out the view of stars and celestial objects and produces halos and glow artifacts around light sources. Unlike nighttime dehazing, which aims to improve detail legibility through thick air, the objective of light pollution removal is to restore the pristine night appearance by neutralizing the radiative footprint of ground lighting. In this paper we introduce a physically-based degradation model that adds to the previous ones for nighttime dehazing two critical aspects; (i) anisotropic spread of directional light sources, and (ii) skyglow caused by invisible surface lights behind skylines. In addition, we construct a training strategy that leverages large generative model and synthetic-real coupling to compensate for the scarcity of paired real data and enhance generalization. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed formulation and learning framework substantially reduce light pollution artifacts and better recover authentic night imagery than prior nighttime restoration methods.

AI.tldr: A physically-based nighttime light pollution removal framework that adds anisotropic light spread and skyglow modeling, plus a training strategy using synthetic-real coupling with generative models; reports improved restoration over prior methods.
AI.motivation: Combat the degraded fidelity of night photography due to artificial lighting by extending nighttime dehazing to account for directional lighting spread and hidden-skyglow, addressing data scarcity with synthetic-real training.
AI.method: Extend degradation model to include anisotropic spread of directional light sources and skyglow from invisible surface lights behind skylines; propose a training pipeline leveraging a large generative model and synthetic-real data coupling to compensate for limited paired real data.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show the proposed formulation and learning framework substantially reduce light pollution artifacts and better recover authentic night imagery than prior nighttime restoration methods.
AI.conclusion: The work advances nighttime restoration by incorporating physically grounded light pollution processes and robust data strategies, enabling more authentic nighttime imagery with broader generalization.

title: Synthesizing real-world distributions from high-dimensional Gaussian Noise with Fully Connected Neural Network
summary: The use of synthetic data in machine learning applications and research offers many benefits, including performance improvements through data augmentation, privacy preservation of original samples, and reliable method assessment with fully synthetic data. This work proposes a time-efficient synthetic data generation method based on a fully connected neural network and a randomized loss function that transforms a random Gaussian distribution to approximate a target real-world dataset. The experiments conducted on 25 diverse tabular real-world datasets confirm that the proposed solution surpasses the state-of-the-art generative methods and achieves reference MMD scores orders of magnitude faster than modern deep learning solutions. The experiments involved analyzing distributional similarity, assessing the impact on classification quality, and using PCA for dimensionality reduction, which further enhances data privacy and can boost classification quality while reducing time and memory complexity.

AI.tldr: A fast, FCN-based synthetic data generator uses a randomized loss to map Gaussian inputs to a target real-world tabular distribution, achieving competitive distributional similarity (MMD) with state-of-the-art generative models at orders-of-magnitude lower runtime; PCA-based dimensionality reduction enhances privacy and can improve classification while reducing time/memory.
AI.motivation: Provide a time-efficient, privacy-preserving synthetic data generation approach for augmentation, evaluation, and method benchmarking, addressing computational bottlenecks of current deep generative models on tabular data.
AI.method: A fully connected neural network serves as the generator. A randomized loss function guides transformation of a Gaussian input distribution to approximate a target real-world dataset. Evaluations on 25 diverse tabular datasets. PCA is employed for dimensionality reduction to enhance privacy and potentially improve classification while reducing resource use.
AI.result: The proposed method surpasses state-of-the-art generative methods in experiments. It achieves reference MMD scores orders of magnitude faster than modern deep learning solutions. Across experiments, distributional similarity, classification impact, and PCA-based reduction were analyzed; PCA further enhances data privacy and can boost classification quality with lower time/memory requirements.
AI.conclusion: A time-efficient synthetic data generation approach using a FCN and a randomized loss can achieve competitive distributional fidelity with substantial speed advantages and privacy benefits.
