Suitable for data augmentation and evaluation; further work could assess generalization to other data domains and provide deeper privacy guarantees.

title: Benchmarking CNN- and Transformer-Based Models for Surgical Instrument Segmentation in Robotic-Assisted Surgery
summary:
Accurate segmentation of surgical instruments in robotic-assisted surgery is critical for enabling context-aware computer-assisted interventions, such as tool tracking, workflow analysis, and autonomous decision-making. In this study, we benchmark five deep learning architectures-UNet, UNet, DeepLabV3, Attention UNet, and SegFormer on the SAR-RARP50 dataset for multi-class semantic segmentation of surgical instruments in real-world radical prostatectomy videos. The models are trained with a compound loss function combining Cross Entropy and Dice loss to address class imbalance and capture fine object boundaries. Our experiments reveal that while convolutional models such as UNet and Attention UNet provide strong baseline performance, DeepLabV3 achieves results comparable to SegFormer, demonstrating the effectiveness of atrous convolution and multi-scale context aggregation in capturing complex surgical scenes. Transformer-based architectures like SegFormer further enhance global contextual understanding, leading to improved generalization across varying instrument appearances and surgical conditions.
  This work provides a comprehensive comparison and practical insights for selecting segmentation models in surgical AI applications, highlighting the trade-offs between convolutional and transformer-based approaches.

AI.tldr: Benchmark of five architectures (UNet, Attention UNet, DeepLabV3, SegFormer, and a fifth model) on SAR-RARP50 for multi-class surgical instrument segmentation in radical prostatectomy videos. CNNs yield strong baselines; DeepLabV3 is on par with SegFormer; transformer SegFormer offers better global context and generalization; provides practical insights for model selection in surgical AI.
AI.motivation: Enable robust, context-aware surgical AI via accurate instrument segmentation to support tool tracking, workflow analysis, and autonomous decisions.
AI.method: Train and compare five architectures on the SAR-RARP50 dataset using a compound loss (Cross Entropy + Dice) to address class imbalance and boundary precision; analyze performance, boundary delineation, and generalization, with emphasis on atrous convolution, multi-scale context, and transformer-based global context.
AI.result: Convolutional models (UNet, Attention UNet) provide strong baselines; DeepLabV3 yields results comparable to SegFormer; SegFormer enhances global context and generalization across varying instrument appearances and surgical conditions.
AI.conclusion: The study offers practical guidance for selecting segmentation models in surgical AI, highlighting trade-offs between convolutional and transformer-based approaches and the impact of loss design on handling imbalance and boundaries.

title: GeoPAS: Geometric Probing for Algorithm Selection in Continuous Black-Box Optimisation
comment: Companion to a paper to appear at GECCO 2026
summary: Automated algorithm selection in continuous black-box optimisation typically relies on fixed landscape descriptors computed under a limited probing budget, yet such descriptors can degrade under problem-split or cross-benchmark evaluation. We propose GeoPAS, a geometric probing approach that represents a problem instance by multiple coarse two-dimensional slices sampled across locations, orientations, and logarithmic scales. A shared validity-aware convolutional encoder maps each slice to an embedding, conditions it on slice-scale and amplitude statistics, and aggregates the resulting features permutation-invariantly for risk-aware solver selection via log-scale performance prediction with an explicit penalty on tail failures. On COCO/BBOB with a 12-solver portfolio in dimensions 2--10, GeoPAS improves over the single best solver under leave-instance-out, grouped random, and leave-problem-out evaluation. These results suggest that multi-scale geometric slices provide a useful transferable static signal for algorithm selection, although a small number of heavy-tail regimes remain and continue to dominate the mean. Our code is available at $\href{https://github.com/BradWangW/GeoPAS}{GitHub}$.

AI.tldr: GeoPAS uses multi-scale geometric slices and a permutation-invariant, validity-aware encoder to predict log-scale performance for solver selection, yielding improved selection over baselines on COCO/BBOB with a 12-solver portfolio; highlights transferable geometric signals but heavy-tail regimes still affect means.
AI.motivation: Automated algorithm selection in continuous black-box optimization relies on landscape descriptors that often fail to generalize across problem splits or benchmarks. There is a need for descriptors that are robust, transferable, and informative under limited probing budgets.
AI.method: Represent an instance by multiple coarse 2D slices sampled across location, orientation, and logarithmic scale. A shared validity-aware convolutional encoder embeds each slice; embeddings are conditioned on slice-scale and amplitude statistics and aggregated in a permutation-invariant way to produce features for risk-aware solver selection. The predictor optimizes log-scale performance with an explicit penalty on tail failures.
AI.result: On COCO/BBOB with 12 solvers across dimensions 2–10, GeoPAS outperforms the single best solver under leave-instance-out, grouped random, and leave-problem-out evaluations.
AI.conclusion: Multi-scale geometric slices provide a transferable static signal for algorithm selection, though a few heavy-tail regimes dominate the mean; code is available on GitHub for reproduction.

title: EquiformerV3: Scaling Efficient, Expressive, and General SE(3)-Equivariant Graph Attention Transformers
summary: As $SE(3)$-equivariant graph neural networks mature as a core tool for 3D atomistic modeling, improving their efficiency, expressivity, and physical consistency has become a central challenge for large-scale applications. In this work, we introduce EquiformerV3, the third generation of the $SE(3)$-equivariant graph attention Transformer, designed to advance all three dimensions: efficiency, expressivity, and generality. Building on EquiformerV2, we have the following three key advances. First, we optimize the software implementation, achieving $1.75\times$ speedup. Second, we introduce simple and effective modifications to EquiformerV2, including equivariant merged layer normalization, improved feedforward network hyper-parameters, and attention with smooth radius cutoff. Third, we propose SwiGLU-$S^2$ activations to incorporate many-body interactions for better theoretical expressivity and to preserve strict equivariance while reducing the complexity of sampling $S^2$ grids. Together, SwiGLU-$S^2$ activations and smooth-cutoff attention enable accurate modeling of smoothly varying potential energy surfaces (PES), generalizing EquiformerV3 to tasks requiring energy-conserving simulations and higher-order derivatives of PES. With these improvements, EquiformerV3 trained with the auxiliary task of denoising non-equilibrium structures (DeNS) achieves state-of-the-art results on OC20, OMat24, and Matbench Discovery.

AI.tldr: EquiformerV3 advances SE(3)-equivariant graph attention transformers for 3D atomistic modeling by boosting efficiency, expressivity, and generality: software speedups, normalization and attention refinements, and SwiGLU-S^2 activations for many-body interactions and energy-conserving PES, enabling higher-order derivatives; achieves state-of-the-art on OC20, OMat24, Matbench Discovery with DeNS auxiliary task.
AI.motivation: Improve efficiency, expressivity, and physical consistency of SE(3)-equivariant GNNs for large-scale 3D atomistic modeling, enabling accurate PES, energy-conserving simulations, and higher-order derivatives.
AI.method: Threefold advancement: (1) software optimization yielding 1.75x speedup; (2) modifications to EquiformerV2 including equivariant merged layer normalization, improved feedforward network hyperparameters, and attention with smooth radius cutoff; (3) SwiGLU-S^2 activations to capture many-body interactions while preserving strict equivariance and reducing sampling complexity of S^2 grids; integration with DeNS auxiliary task during training.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art results on OC20, OMat24, and Matbench Discovery, with improved PES modeling and capability to handle energy-conserving simulations and higher-order PES derivatives.
AI.conclusion: EquiformerV3 substantially enhances efficiency, expressivity, and generality of SE(3)-equivariant graph attention transformers for 3D atomistic tasks, enabling accurate, energy-conserving simulations and broader applicability while outperforming its predecessor.

title: MAG-3D: Multi-Agent Grounded Reasoning for 3D Understanding
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) have achieved strong performance in multimodal understanding and reasoning, yet grounded reasoning in 3D scenes remains underexplored. Effective 3D reasoning hinges on accurate grounding: to answer open-ended queries, a model must first identify query-relevant objects and regions in a complex scene, and then reason about their spatial and geometric relationships. Recent approaches have demonstrated strong potential for grounded 3D reasoning. However, they often rely on in-domain tuning or hand-crafted reasoning pipelines, which limit their flexibility and zero-shot generalization to novel environments. In this work, we present MAG-3D, a training-free multi-agent framework for grounded 3D reasoning with off-the-shelf VLMs. Instead of relying on task-specific training or fixed reasoning procedures, MAG-3D dynamically coordinates expert agents to address the key challenges of 3D reasoning. Specifically, we propose a planning agent that decomposes the task and orchestrates the overall reasoning process, a grounding agent that performs free-form 3D grounding and relevant frame retrieval from extensive 3D scene observations, and a coding agent that conducts flexible geometric reasoning and explicit verification through executable programs. This multi-agent collaborative design enables flexible training-free 3D grounded reasoning across diverse scenes and achieves state-of-the-art performance on challenging benchmarks.

AI.tldr: MAG-3D is a training-free multi-agent framework for grounded 3D reasoning using off-the-shelf VLMs, coordinating planning, grounding, and coding agents to achieve strong zero-shot generalization without task-specific training.
AI.motivation: Grounded 3D reasoning requires identifying query-relevant objects/regions and reasoning about spatial relations in complex scenes; existing methods often rely on domain-specific tuning or fixed pipelines, hindering generalization.
AI.method: A three-agent system: (1) planning agent decomposes tasks and coordinates reasoning, (2) grounding agent performs free-form 3D grounding and frame retrieval from large 3D scene observations, (3) coding agent conducts flexible geometric reasoning and explicit verification via executable programs; all training-free and leveraging off-the-shelf VLMs.
AI.result: Demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on challenging 3D grounded reasoning benchmarks and shows robust zero-shot generalization across diverse scenes and environments.
AI.conclusion: A multi-agent collaboration enables flexible, training-free 3D grounded reasoning with VLMs, achieving strong performance without task-specific training or fixed reasoning pipelines.

title: Score-Driven Rating System for Sports
summary: This paper introduces a score-driven rating system, a generalization of the classical Elo rating system that employs the score, i.e. the gradient of the log-likelihood, as the updating mechanism for player and team ratings. The proposed framework extends beyond simple win/loss game outcomes and accommodates a wide range of game results, such as point differences, win/draw/loss outcomes, or complete rankings. Theoretical properties of the score are derived, showing that it has zero expected value, sums to zero across all players, and decreases with increasing value of a player's rating, thereby ensuring internal consistency and fairness. Furthermore, the score-driven rating system exhibits a reversion property, meaning that ratings tend to follow the underlying unobserved true skills over time. The proposed framework provides a theoretical rationale for existing dynamic models of sports performance and offers a systematic approach for constructing new ones.

AI.tldr: A generalized, score-driven rating framework extends Elo by using the score (gradient of the log-likelihood) for updates, enabling richer outcomes, while maintaining fairness and reversion to true skills; it provides theoretical grounding for dynamic performance models.
AI.motivation: To generalize rating systems beyond binary outcomes, allowing diverse game results (score differences, draws, rankings) and to establish a principled, likelihood-based update mechanism with internal consistency and a clear connection to dynamic performance models.
AI.method: Define the update rule via the score, i.e., the gradient of the log-likelihood with respect to player/team ratings, yielding a general Elo-like framework. Derive properties (zero mean, zero-sum, monotone decrease with rating) and demonstrate reversion to unobserved true skills. Show how the framework subsumes existing dynamic models and offers a systematic way to build new ones.
AI.result: Proof of key properties: the score has zero expected value, sums to zero across players, and decreases with increasing rating; establishment of a reversion property toward true skills; a unified, likelihood-based basis for dynamic performance models and a method to construct new models.
AI.conclusion: The score-driven framework provides a principled, flexible approach to rating that generalizes Elo, explains existing dynamic models, and enables systematic construction of new rating systems for varied game outcomes.

title: ELT: Elastic Looped Transformers for Visual Generation
summary: We introduce Elastic Looped Transformers (ELT), a highly parameter-efficient class of visual generative models based on a recurrent transformer architecture. While conventional generative models rely on deep stacks of unique transformer layers, our approach employs iterative, weight-shared transformer blocks to drastically reduce parameter counts while maintaining high synthesis quality. To effectively train these models for image and video generation, we propose the idea of Intra-Loop Self Distillation (ILSD), where student configurations (intermediate loops) are distilled from the teacher configuration (maximum training loops) to ensure consistency across the model's depth in a single training step. Our framework yields a family of elastic models from a single training run, enabling Any-Time inference capability with dynamic trade-offs between computational cost and generation quality, with the same parameter count. ELT significantly shifts the efficiency frontier for visual synthesis. With $4\times$ reduction in parameter count under iso-inference-compute settings, ELT achieves a competitive FID of $2.0$ on class-conditional ImageNet $256 \times 256$ and FVD of $72.8$ on class-conditional UCF-101.

AI.tldr: ELT introduces parameter-efficient, weight-shared recurrent transformers for visual generation, enabling Any-Time inference via intra-loop self-distillation (ILSD) to distill deeper loops into shallower ones from a single training run, achieving ~4× parameter reduction with competitive metrics (FID 2.0 on ImageNet-256, FVD 72.8 on UCF-101).
AI.motivation: Reduce parameter count and compute in visual generative models while maintaining or improving generation quality; enable flexible depth-aware trade-offs (Any-Time inference) without retraining.
AI.method: Adopt iterative, weight-shared transformer blocks (recurrent transformer), train with intra-loop self-distillation (ILSD) where intermediate loops (students) are distilled from the full-depth teacher in a single training step; produce elastic models from one training run for dynamic inference budgets.
AI.result: Under iso-inference-compute settings, ELT achieves ~4× parameter reduction while maintaining competitive synthesis quality, delivering FID 2.0 on class-conditional ImageNet-256 and FVD 72.8 on class-conditional UCF-101.
AI.conclusion: ELT shifts the efficiency frontier for visual synthesis by enabling a family of elastic models with consistent quality across depths from a single training run, supporting Any-Time inference without extra parameter cost.

title: CORA: Conformal Risk-Controlled Agents for Safeguarded Mobile GUI Automation
summary: Graphical user interface (GUI) agents powered by vision language models (VLMs) are rapidly moving from passive assistance to autonomous operation. However, this unrestricted action space exposes users to severe and irreversible financial, privacy or social harm. Existing safeguards rely on prompt engineering, brittle heuristics and VLM-as-critic lack formal verification and user-tunable guarantees. We propose CORA (COnformal Risk-controlled GUI Agent), a post-policy, pre-action safeguarding framework that provides statistical guarantees on harmful executed actions. CORA reformulates safety as selective action execution: we train a Guardian model to estimate action-conditional risk for each proposed step. Rather than thresholding raw scores, we leverage Conformal Risk Control to calibrate an execute/abstain boundary that satisfies a user-specified risk budget and route rejected actions to a trainable Diagnostician model, which performs multimodal reasoning over rejected actions to recommend interventions (e.g., confirm, reflect, or abort) to minimize user burden. A Goal-Lock mechanism anchors assessment to a clarified, frozen user intent to resist visual injection attacks. To rigorously evaluate this paradigm, we introduce Phone-Harm, a new benchmark of mobile safety violations with step-level harm labels under real-world settings. Experiments on Phone-Harm and public benchmarks against diverse baselines validate that CORA improves the safety--helpfulness--interruption Pareto frontier, offering a practical, statistically grounded safety paradigm for autonomous GUI execution. Code and benchmark are available at cora-agent.github.io.

AI.tldr: CORA is a conformal risk-controlled post-policy safety framework for GUI agents that uses a Guardian to estimate action risk and a Diagnostician for rejected actions, achieving a better safety–usefulness–interruption trade-off on Phone-Harm and public benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Unrestricted action spaces in vision-language GUI agents can cause serious harm; existing safeguards rely on brittle heuristics and lack formal guarantees or user-tunable risk settings.
AI.method: Train a Guardian model to estimate action-conditional risk for proposed steps. Apply Conformal Risk Control to calibrate an execute/abstain boundary based on a user-specified risk budget. Route rejected actions to a trainable Diagnostician that reasons over rejected actions to propose interventions (confirm, reflect, abort). Use a Goal-Lock mechanism to align assessments with clarified user intent. Evaluate on Phone-Harm and public benchmarks; provide code and benchmark.
AI.result: CORA improves the safety–helpfulness–interruption Pareto frontier compared with diverse baselines on Phone-Harm and public benchmarks and offers statistical guarantees via conformal risk control; code and benchmark are publicly available at cora-agent.github.io.
AI.conclusion: CORA provides a practical, statistically grounded safety paradigm for autonomous GUI execution with post-policy risk management and user-tunable guarantees.

title: UniSemAlign: Text-Prototype Alignment with a Foundation Encoder for Semi-Supervised Histopathology Segmentation
comment: Accepted at CVPR 2026 Workshop. 11 pages, 5 figures, 4 tables
summary: Semi-supervised semantic segmentation in computational pathology remains challenging due to scarce pixel-level annotations and unreliable pseudo-label supervision. We propose UniSemAlign, a dual-modal semantic alignment framework that enhances visual segmentation by injecting explicit class-level structure into pixel-wise learning. Built upon a pathology-pretrained Transformer encoder, UniSemAlign introduces complementary prototype-level and text-level alignment branches in a shared embedding space, providing structured guidance that reduces class ambiguity and stabilizes pseudo-label refinement. The aligned representations are fused with visual predictions to generate more reliable supervision for unlabeled histopathology images. The framework is trained end-to-end with supervised segmentation, cross-view consistency, and cross-modal alignment objectives. Extensive experiments on the GlaS and CRAG datasets demonstrate that UniSemAlign substantially outperforms recent semi-supervised baselines under limited supervision, achieving Dice improvements of up to 2.6% on GlaS and 8.6% on CRAG with only 10% labeled data, and strong improvements at 20% supervision. Code is available at: https://github.com/thailevann/UniSemAlign

AI.tldr: UniSemAlign introduces dual-modal prototype and text-level alignment to guide pixel-wise learning in semi-supervised histopathology segmentation, improving reliability of pseudo-labels.
AI.motivation: Semi-supervised semantic segmentation in computational pathology suffers from scarce pixel-level annotations and unreliable pseudo-label supervision, causing class ambiguity and unstable learning.
AI.method: A pathology-pretrained Transformer encoder is augmented with two alignment branches—prototype-level and text-level—operating in a shared embedding space. The aligned representations are fused with visual predictions and optimized end-to-end with supervised segmentation, cross-view consistency, and cross-modal alignment objectives.
AI.result: On GlaS and CRAG, UniSemAlign yields Dice improvements up to 2.6% and 8.6% respectively with 10% labeled data, with notable gains at 20% supervision and outperforming recent semi-supervised baselines.
AI.conclusion: Dual-modal alignment injections provide structured guidance that stabilizes pseudo-label refinement and reduces class ambiguity in histopathology segmentation, yielding more reliable supervision and improved performance; code is released.

title: Truncated Rectified Flow Policy for Reinforcement Learning with One-Step Sampling
summary: Maximum entropy reinforcement learning (MaxEnt RL) has become a standard framework for sequential decision making, yet its standard Gaussian policy parameterization is inherently unimodal, limiting its ability to model complex multimodal action distributions. This limitation has motivated increasing interest in generative policies based on diffusion and flow matching as more expressive alternatives. However, incorporating such policies into MaxEnt RL is challenging for two main reasons: the likelihood and entropy of continuous-time generative policies are generally intractable, and multi-step sampling introduces both long-horizon backpropagation instability and substantial inference latency. To address these challenges, we propose Truncated Rectified Flow Policy (TRFP), a framework built on a hybrid deterministic-stochastic architecture. This design makes entropy-regularized optimization tractable while supporting stable training and effective one-step sampling through gradient truncation and flow straightening. Empirical results on a toy multigoal environment and 10 MuJoCo benchmarks show that TRFP captures multimodal behavior effectively, outperforms strong baselines on most benchmarks under standard sampling, and remains highly competitive under one-step sampling.

AI.tldr: TRFP introduces a hybrid deterministic–stochastic truncated-rectified flow policy to enable tractable entropy-regularized MaxEnt RL with expressive multimodal action distributions, addressing intractable likelihood/entropy and long-horizon backprop; it achieves stable one-step sampling and strong empirical performance.
AI.motivation: Standard MaxEnt RL with Gaussian policies is unimodal and cannot capture multimodal action distributions. Generative policies via diffusion/flow matching offer expressiveness but suffer from intractable likelihood/entropy and impractical multi-step sampling (backprop instability and latency).
AI.method: Proposes Truncated Rectified Flow Policy (TRFP), a hybrid deterministic-stochastic architecture with gradient truncation and flow straightening. This makes entropy-regularized optimization tractable and enables efficient one-step sampling while maintaining multimodal expressivity.
AI.result: In toy multigoal tasks and 10 MuJoCo benchmarks, TRFP captures multimodal behavior, outperforms strong baselines under standard sampling, and remains highly competitive under one-step sampling.
AI.conclusion: TRFP provides a practical framework to integrate expressive, multimodal generative policies into MaxEnt RL, achieving stable training, tractable entropy, and strong empirical performance across benchmarks, including settings that require efficient one-step sampling.

title: MixFlow: Mixed Source Distributions Improve Rectified Flows
summary: Diffusion models and their variations, such as rectified flows, generate diverse and high-quality images, but they are still hindered by slow iterative sampling caused by the highly curved generative paths they learn. An important cause of high curvature, as shown by previous work, is independence between the source distribution (standard Gaussian) and the data distribution. In this work, we tackle this limitation by two complementary contributions. First, we attempt to break away from the standard Gaussian assumption by introducing $κ\texttt{-FC}$, a general formulation that conditions the source distribution on an arbitrary signal $κ$ that aligns it better with the data distribution. Then, we present MixFlow, a simple but effective training strategy that reduces the generative path curvatures and considerably improves sampling efficiency. MixFlow trains a flow model on linear mixtures of a fixed unconditional distribution and a $κ\texttt{-FC}$-based distribution. This simple mixture improves the alignment between the source and data, provides better generation quality with less required sampling steps, and accelerates the training convergence considerably. On average, our training procedure improves the generation quality by 12\% in FID compared to standard rectified flow and 7\% compared to previous baselines under a fixed sampling budget. Code available at: $\href{https://github.com/NazirNayal8/MixFlow}{https://github.com/NazirNayal8/MixFlow}$

AI.tldr: Introduce κ-FC and MixFlow to reduce curvature and speed up sampling in diffusion models by conditioning the source distribution and training on mixtures of unconditional and κ-FC-based distributions; yields notable FID gains under a fixed sampling budget.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models with highly curved generative paths suffer from slow iterative sampling. A key cause is the independence between the source distribution (standard Gaussian) and the data distribution. Aligning the source with data can reduce path curvature and improve efficiency.
AI.method: κ-FC generalizes the source distribution by conditioning it on an arbitrary signal κ to better match the data. MixFlow training jointly trains a flow on linear mixtures of a fixed unconditional distribution and a κ-FC-based distribution, reducing curvature and accelerating convergence.
AI.result: Empirically, MixFlow improves generation quality by 12% in FID over standard rectified flow and 7% over prior baselines under a fixed sampling budget, and accelerates training convergence.
AI.conclusion: A simple yet effective approach to reduce generative path curvature and increase sampling efficiency. κ-FC plus MixFlow provides improved alignment and generation quality with modest training adjustments; code is available.

title: Automated Batch Distillation Process Simulation for a Large Hybrid Dataset for Deep Anomaly Detection
summary: Anomaly detection (AD) in chemical processes based on deep learning offers significant opportunities but requires large, diverse, and well-annotated training datasets that are rarely available from industrial operations. In a recent work, we introduced a large, fully annotated experimental dataset for batch distillation under normal and anomalous operating conditions. In the present study, we augment this dataset with a corresponding simulation dataset, creating a novel hybrid dataset. The simulation data is generated in an automated workflow with a novel Python-based process simulator that employs a tailored index-reduction strategy for the underlying differential-algebraic equations. Leveraging the rich metadata and structured anomaly annotations of the experimental database, experimental records are automatically translated into simulation scenarios. After calibration to a single reference experiment, the dynamics of the other experiments are well predicted. This enabled the fully automated, consistent generation of time-series data for a large number of experimental runs, covering both normal operation and a wide range of actuator- and control-related anomalies. The resulting hybrid dataset is released openly. From a process simulation perspective, this work demonstrates the automated, consistent simulation of large-scale experimental campaigns, using batch distillation as an example. From a data-driven AD perspective, the hybrid dataset provides a unique basis for simulation-to-experiment style transfer, the generation of pseudo-experimental data, and future research on deep AD methods in chemical process monitoring.

AI.tldr: A novel hybrid dataset for batch distillation combining fully annotated experimental data with automatically generated simulation data using a Python-based simulator and index-reduction for DAEs, enabling simulation-to-experiment transfer and aiding deep anomaly detection.
AI.motivation: Address scarcity of large, annotated industrial datasets for anomaly detection in chemical processes by creating a hybrid, openly released dataset bridging experiments and simulations.
AI.method: Developed a Python-based process simulator with index-reduction for DAEs; automatically translate experimental records into simulation scenarios using rich metadata; calibrate simulator to a reference experiment; automate generation of time-series data across many runs with normal and various actuator/control anomalies; release hybrid dataset openly.
AI.result: Automated, consistent simulation of large-scale experimental campaigns; predicted dynamics for other experiments after calibration; extensive time-series covering normal and anomalous conditions; dataset released openly.
AI.conclusion: Validates automated, consistent simulation of large experimental campaigns; enables simulation-to-experiment transfer, pseudo-experimental data generation, and future data-driven anomaly detection research in chemical process monitoring.

title: Vision Transformers for Preoperative CT-Based Prediction of Histopathologic Chemotherapy Response Score in High-Grade Serous Ovarian Carcinoma
summary: Purpose. High-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSOC) is characterized by pronounced biological and spatial heterogeneity and is frequently diagnosed at an advanced stage. Neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT) followed by delayed primary surgery is commonly employed in patients unsuitable for primary cytoreduction. The Chemotherapy Response Score (CRS) is a validated histopathological biomarker of response to NACT, but it is only available postoperatively. In this study, we investigate whether pre-treatment computed tomography (CT) imaging and clinical data can be used to predict CRS as an investigational decision-support adjunct to inform multidisciplinary team (MDT) discussions regarding expected treatment response. Methods. We proposed a 2.5D multimodal deep learning framework that processes lesion-dense omental slices using a pre-trained Vision Transformer encoder and integrates the resulting visual representations with clinical variables through an intermediate fusion module to predict CRS. Results. Our multimodal model, integrating imaging and clinical data, achieved a ROC-AUC of 0.95 alongside 95% accuracy and 80% precision on the internal test cohort (IEO, n=41 patients). On the external test set (OV04, n=70 patients), it achieved a ROC-AUC of 0.68, alongside 67% accuracy and 75% precision. Conclusion. These preliminary results demonstrate the feasibility of transformer-based deep learning for preoperative prediction of CRS in HGSOC using routine clinical data and CT imaging. As an investigational, pre-treatment decision-support tool, this approach may assist MDT discussions by providing early, non-invasive estimates of treatment response.

AI.tldr: 2.5D multimodal transformer model using pre-treatment CT and clinical data predicts CRS in HGSOC with high internal performance but weak external generalization; represents a promising but preliminary preoperative decision-support tool.
AI.motivation: CRS is a validated postoperative biomarker guiding neoadjuvant chemotherapy decisions in HGSOC. A pre-treatment predictor could inform MDT discussions and treatment planning without waiting for post-NACT pathology.
AI.method: A 2.5D multimodal DL framework processes lesion-dense omental CT slices with a pre-trained Vision Transformer encoder. Visual representations are fused with clinical variables via an intermediate fusion module to predict CRS. Internal test cohort (IEO, n=41) and external test set (OV04, n=70) were used, reporting ROC-AUC, accuracy, and precision.
AI.result: Internal ROC-AUC 0.95, accuracy 95%, precision 80%. External ROC-AUC 0.68, accuracy 67%, precision 75%.
AI.conclusion: Transformer-based DL can preoperatively estimate CRS from routine CT and clinical data, offering investigational decision-support for MDT discussions. However, external generalization is limited; larger, multi-center validation and methodological refinements are needed before clinical adoption.

title: Generalization and Scaling Laws for Mixture-of-Experts Transformers
summary: We develop a theory of generalization and scaling for Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) Transformers that cleanly separates \emph{active} per-input capacity from routing combinatorics. By conditioning on fixed routing patterns and union-bounding across them, we derive a sup-norm covering-number bound whose metric entropy scales with the active parameter budget and incurs a MoE-specific routing overhead. Combined with a standard ERM analysis for squared loss, this yields a generalization bound under a $d$-dimensional manifold data model and $C^β$ targets, showing that approximation and estimation trade off as in dense networks once active parameters are accounted for appropriately. We further prove a constructive approximation theorem for MoE architectures, showing that, under the approximation construction, error can decrease either by scaling active capacity or by increasing the number of experts, depending on the dominant bottleneck. From these results we derive neural scaling laws for model size, data size, and compute-optimal tradeoffs. Overall, our results provide a transparent statistical reference point for reasoning about MoE scaling, clarifying which behaviors are certified by worst-case theory and which must arise from data-dependent routing structure or optimization dynamics.

AI.tldr: A theory of generalization and scaling for Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) Transformers that decouples active input capacity from routing combinatorics, deriving bounds and scaling laws.
AI.motivation: To understand how MoE architectures generalize and scale by separating per-input capacity from routing overhead, and to provide a principled worst-case reference for MoE behavior.
AI.method: Condition on fixed routing patterns; apply union bound across patterns; derive a sup-norm covering-number bound whose metric entropy scales with the active parameter budget plus a routing overhead; pair with standard ERM analysis for squared loss; model data on a d-dimensional manifold with C^β targets; prove constructive MoE approximation theorem; derive neural scaling laws.
AI.result: A generalization bound under a d-dimensional manifold data model and C^β targets; approximation-estimation trade-offs similar to dense nets when active capacity is accounted; constructive approximation shows error can decrease by scaling active capacity or increasing the number of experts depending on bottleneck; neural scaling laws for size, data, compute; clarifies certified theory vs data-dependent routing/optimization.
AI.conclusion: Provides a transparent statistical reference for MoE scaling, clarifying what worst-case theory certifies and what must arise from data-dependent routing or optimization, and guiding principled scaling decisions.

title: Globally Optimal Pose from Orthographic Silhouettes
summary:
We solve the problem of determining the pose of known shapes in $\mathbb{R}^3$ from their unoccluded silhouettes. The pose is determined up to global optimality using a simple yet under-explored property of the area-of-silhouette: its continuity w.r.t trajectories in the rotation space. The proposed method utilises pre-computed silhouette-signatures, modelled as a response surface of the area-of-silhouettes. Querying this silhouette-signature response surface for pose estimation leads to a strong branching of the rotation search space, making resolution-guided candidate search feasible. Additionally, we utilise the aspect ratio of 2D ellipses fitted to projected silhouettes as an auxiliary global shape signature to accelerate the pose search. This combined strategy forms the first method to efficiently estimate globally optimal pose from just the silhouettes, without being guided by correspondences, for any shape, irrespective of its convexity and genus. We validate our method on synthetic and real examples, demonstrating significantly improved accuracy against comparable approaches.
  Code, data, and supplementary in: https://agnivsen.github.io/pose-from-silhouette/

AI.tldr: Globally optimal pose estimation from silhouettes in R^3 via pre-computed silhouette-signatures (area-of-silhouette) and ellipse aspect-ratio signatures, enabling a resolution-guided, correspondences-free search for arbitrary shapes; validated on synthetic/real data with improved accuracy; code available.
AI.motivation: Estimate the pose of known 3D shapes from unoccluded silhouettes with global optimality, without relying on correspondences, and regardless of shape convexity or genus. Existing methods struggle with local minima and lack a general global solution.
AI.method: Pre-compute a silhouette-signature response surface representing the area of the silhouette as a function of rotation. Use this surface to guide a branching search over rotation space, enabling a resolution-guided exploration toward the global optimum. Augment with the aspect ratio of 2D ellipses fitted to projected silhouettes as an auxiliary global shape signature to accelerate search. Combine these components to estimate the pose from silhouettes without correspondences for any shape.
AI.result: Demonstrates significantly improved accuracy compared with comparable approaches on both synthetic and real datasets. The approach enables efficient, globally optimal pose search from silhouettes.
AI.conclusion: This work presents the first method to efficiently obtain globally optimal pose estimates from silhouettes for any shape (convex or non-convex, any genus) without relying on correspondences, and provides code/data for replication.

title: On the Role of DAG topology in Energy-Aware Cloud Scheduling : A GNN-Based Deep Reinforcement Learning Approach
summary: Cloud providers must assign heterogeneous compute resources to workflow DAGs while balancing competing objectives such as completion time, cost, and energy consumption. In this work, we study a single-workflow, queue-free scheduling setting and consider a graph neural network (GNN)-based deep reinforcement learning scheduler designed to minimize workflow completion time and energy usage. We identify specific out-of-distribution (OOD) conditions under which GNN-based deep reinforcement learning schedulers fail and provide a principled explanation of why these failures occur. Through controlled OOD evaluations, we demonstrate that performance degradation stems from structural mismatches between training and deployment environments, which disrupt message passing and undermine policy generalization. Our analysis exposes fundamental limitations of current GNN-based schedulers and highlights the need for more robust representations to ensure reliable scheduling performance under distribution shifts.

AI.tldr: GNN-based DRL scheduler for cloud workflow scheduling; identifies OOD failure conditions due to structural mismatches that disrupt message passing and policy generalization; highlights limitations and need for robust representations.
AI.motivation: Address efficient, time- and energy-aware scheduling of DAG workflows on cloud resources; examine robustness of GNN-based DRL schedulers under distribution shifts.
AI.method: Controlled OOD evaluations in a single-workflow, queue-free setting using a GNN-based DRL scheduler; analyze failure modes and their relation to graph structure and message passing.
AI.result: Shows performance degradation under OOD due to structural mismatches; provides principled explanations for failures; reveals fundamental limitations of current GNN-based schedulers.
AI.conclusion: Calls for more robust representations and methods to ensure reliable scheduling under distribution shifts; emphasizes improving generalization and resilience to structure changes.

title: CT-1: Vision-Language-Camera Models Transfer Spatial Reasoning Knowledge to Camera-Controllable Video Generation
summary: Camera-controllable video generation aims to synthesize videos with flexible and physically plausible camera movements. However, existing methods either provide imprecise camera control from text prompts or rely on labor-intensive manual camera trajectory parameters, limiting their use in automated scenarios. To address these issues, we propose a novel Vision-Language-Camera model, termed CT-1 (Camera Transformer 1), a specialized model designed to transfer spatial reasoning knowledge to video generation by accurately estimating camera trajectories. Built upon vision-language modules and a Diffusion Transformer model, CT-1 employs a Wavelet-based Regularization Loss in the frequency domain to effectively learn complex camera trajectory distributions. These trajectories are integrated into a video diffusion model to enable spatially aware camera control that aligns with user intentions. To facilitate the training of CT-1, we design a dedicated data curation pipeline and construct CT-200K, a large-scale dataset containing over 47M frames. Experimental results demonstrate that our framework successfully bridges the gap between spatial reasoning and video synthesis, yielding faithful and high-quality camera-controllable videos and improving camera control accuracy by 25.7% over prior methods.

AI.tldr: CT-1 is a Vision-Language-Camera model that enables accurate, automated camera trajectory control for video generation by combining vision-language reasoning, diffusion transformers, and a Wavelet-based regularization loss; trained on CT-200K (47M frames), it improves camera-control accuracy by 25.7% over prior work.
AI.motivation: Existing camera-controllable video generation either relies on imprecise prompts or labor-intensive manual trajectory specification, hindering automated workflows. There is a need to fuse spatial reasoning with video synthesis to produce faithful, camera-aware videos.
AI.method: CT-1 integrates vision-language modules with a Diffusion Transformer to model camera trajectories. It introduces a Wavelet-based Regularization Loss in the frequency domain to capture complex trajectory distributions. A dedicated data pipeline curates CT-200K (≈47M frames) for training, and the learned trajectories are incorporated into a video diffusion model to enable spatially aware camera control.
AI.result: Experiments show that CT-1 yields faithful, high-quality camera-controllable videos. It achieves a 25.7% improvement in camera-control accuracy over prior methods.
AI.conclusion: CT-1 successfully bridges spatial reasoning and video synthesis, enabling accurate, automated camera trajectories in video generation and demonstrating the value of frequency-domain regularization and large-scale data in this task.

title: Statistical Properties of the King Wen Sequence: An Anti-Habituation Structure That Does Not Improve Neural Network Training
comment: 9 pages, 8 tables, negative results paper. Code and data: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.14679537
summary: The King Wen sequence of the I-Ching (c. 1000 BC) orders 64 hexagrams -- states of a six-dimensional binary space -- in a pattern that has puzzled scholars for three millennia. We present a rigorous statistical characterization of this ordering using Monte Carlo permutation analysis against 100,000 random baselines. We find that the sequence has four statistically significant properties: higher-than-random transition distance (98.2nd percentile), negative lag-1 autocorrelation (p=0.037), yang-balanced groups of four (p=0.002), and asymmetric within-pair vs. between-pair distances (99.2nd percentile). These properties superficially resemble principles from curriculum learning and curiosity-driven exploration, motivating the hypothesis that they might benefit neural network training. We test this hypothesis through three experiments: learning rate schedule modulation, curriculum ordering, and seed sensitivity analysis, conducted across two hardware platforms (NVIDIA RTX 2060 with PyTorch and Apple Silicon with MLX). The results are uniformly negative. King Wen LR modulation degrades performance at all tested amplitudes. As curriculum ordering, King Wen is the worst non-sequential ordering on one platform and within noise on the other. A 30-seed sweep confirms that only King Wen's degradation exceeds natural seed variance. We explain why: the sequence's high variance -- the very property that makes it statistically distinctive -- destabilizes gradient-based optimization. Anti-habituation in a fixed combinatorial sequence is not the same as effective training dynamics.

AI.tldr: The King Wen sequence of the I-Ching is statistically characterized against random baselines, revealing four significant ordering properties. When tested as curricula or learning-rate cues for neural networks, these properties do not improve training; instead, King Wen’s high-variance structure degrades optimization across tasks and platforms. The work cautions against assuming fixed symbolic orderings imply beneficial training dynamics.
AI.motivation: Assess whether ancient symbolic orderings encode generalizable learning principles that could transfer to modern neural network training, e.g., through curriculum design or learning-rate strategies.
AI.method: Monte Carlo permutation analysis against 100,000 random baselines to identify statistically significant structural properties of the King Wen sequence. Four properties identified: higher-than-random transition distance, negative lag-1 autocorrelation, yang-balanced groups of four, and asymmetry between within-pair vs between-pair distances. These properties were then evaluated as training aids via three experiments (learning-rate modulation, curriculum ordering, seed sensitivity analysis) on two hardware platforms (NVIDIA RTX 2060 with PyTorch; Apple Silicon with MLX).
AI.result: Four properties are statistically significant: transition distance at the 98.2nd percentile, lag-1 autocorrelation with p=0.037, yang-balanced groups of four with p=0.002, and asymmetry with the 99.2nd percentile. All three ML experiments yield negative results: LR modulation degrades performance across amplitudes; King Wen as a curriculum ordering is worst on one platform and effectively non-informative on the other; a 30-seed sweep shows King Wen’s degradation exceeds natural seed variance. The authors attribute this to the sequence’s high variance destabilizing gradient-based optimization; anti-habituation in a fixed combinatorial sequence does not translate to effective training dynamics.
AI.conclusion: Even striking statistical structure in a historical sequence does not guarantee beneficial transfer to training dynamics. The distinct high-variance properties of King Wen hinder gradient-based optimization, suggesting caution when importing fixed symbolic orders as curricula or training cues; further work should explore when such structures might aid learning, or derive curricula that preserve beneficial attributes without destabilizing optimization.

title: Long-SCOPE: Fully Sparse Long-Range Cooperative 3D Perception
summary: Cooperative 3D perception via Vehicle-to-Everything communication is a promising paradigm for enhancing autonomous driving, offering extended sensing horizons and occlusion resolution. However, the practical deployment of existing methods is hindered at long distances by two critical bottlenecks: the quadratic computational scaling of dense BEV representations and the fragility of feature association mechanisms under significant observation and alignment errors. To overcome these limitations, we introduce Long-SCOPE, a fully sparse framework designed for robust long-distance cooperative 3D perception. Our method features two novel components: a Geometry-guided Query Generation module to accurately detect small, distant objects, and a learnable Context-Aware Association module that robustly matches cooperative queries despite severe positional noise. Experiments on the V2X-Seq and Griffin datasets validate that Long-SCOPE achieves state-of-the-art performance, particularly in challenging 100-150 m long-range settings, while maintaining highly competitive computation and communication costs.

AI.tldr: Long-SCOPE introduces a fully sparse framework for long-range cooperative 3D perception, tackling dense BEV quadratic cost and fragile feature association with a geometry-guided query generation and a learnable context-aware association, achieving state-of-the-art performance at 100–150 m with efficient compute/communication.
AI.motivation: Extend cooperative perception sensing horizon and reduce computational/communication bottlenecks. Address two main issues: (i) quadratic scaling of dense Bird-Eye-View representations, and (ii) fragile feature association under large observation/alignment errors.
AI.method: A fully sparse framework with two novel components: (1) Geometry-guided Query Generation to accurately detect small, distant objects, and (2) Learnable Context-Aware Association to robustly match cooperative queries despite severe positional noise.
AI.result: Empirical results on V2X-Seq and Griffin datasets show state-of-the-art performance, particularly in 100–150 m long-range settings, while maintaining competitive computation and communication costs.
AI.conclusion: Long-SCOPE demonstrates robust, scalable long-distance cooperative 3D perception by combining sparse representations with geometry-guided querying and context-aware association, making practical deployment more feasible.

title: DiffHLS: Differential Learning for High-Level Synthesis QoR Prediction with GNNs and LLM Code Embeddings
summary: High-Level Synthesis (HLS) compiles C/C++ into RTL, but exploring pragma-driven optimization choices remains expensive because each design point requires time-consuming synthesis. We propose \textbf{\DiffHLS}, a differential learning framework for HLS Quality-of-Result (QoR) prediction that learns from kernel--design pairs: a kernel baseline and a pragma-inserted design variant. \DiffHLS~encodes kernel and design intermediate-representation graphs with dedicated graph neural network (GNN) branches, and augments the delta pathway with code embeddings from a pretrained code large language model (LLM). Instead of regressing absolute targets directly, we jointly predict the kernel baseline and the design-induced delta, and compose them to obtain the design prediction. On PolyBench, \DiffHLS~attains lower average MAPE than GNN baselines under four GNN backbones, and LLM code embeddings consistently improve over a GNN-only ablation. We further validate scalability on the ForgeHLS dataset.

AI.tldr: DiffHLS is a differential QoR predictor for HLS that learns from kernel baseline and pragma-inserted designs. It encodes kernel and design IR graphs with GNNs, augments the delta path with pretrained code-LLM embeddings, and predicts baseline QoR plus delta to derive design QoR, outperforming GNN baselines and scalable to ForgeHLS.
AI.motivation: Exploring pragma-driven HLS optimizations is expensive due to time-consuming synthesis for each design point; an accurate QoR predictor is needed to rapidly estimate outcomes and guide optimization.
AI.method: Encode kernel and design IRs with dedicated GNN branches; augment the delta pathway with pretrained code embeddings from a code LLM; predict the kernel baseline QoR and the design-induced delta jointly, then compose them to obtain the design QoR.
AI.result: On PolyBench, DiffHLS achieves lower average MAPE than four GNN baselines; LLM code embeddings improve performance over a GNN-only ablation; scalability validated on the ForgeHLS dataset.
AI.conclusion: DiffHLS provides an effective and scalable differential QoR prediction framework for HLS, leveraging both graph-based representations and code-language model embeddings to more accurately predict the impact of pragma-driven design changes.

title: SHIFT: Steering Hidden Intermediates in Flow Transformers
summary: Diffusion models have become leading approaches for high-fidelity image generation. Recent DiT-based diffusion models, in particular, achieve strong prompt adherence while producing high-quality samples. We propose SHIFT, a simple but effective and lightweight framework for concept removal in DiT diffusion models via targeted manipulation of intermediate activations at inference time, inspired by activation steering in large language models. SHIFT learns steering vectors that are dynamically applied to selected layers and timesteps to suppress unwanted visual concepts while preserving the prompt's remaining content and overall image quality. Beyond suppression, the same mechanism can shift generations into a desired \emph{style domain} or bias samples toward adding or changing target objects. We demonstrate that SHIFT provides effective and flexible control over DiT generation across diverse prompts and targets without time-consuming retraining.

AI.tldr: SHIFT enables inference-time activation steering for DiT diffusion models to remove concepts and steer style/objects without retraining.
AI.motivation: There is a need for controllable image generation with high fidelity and strict prompt adherence while removing unwanted concepts, without expensive retraining.
AI.method: Learned steering vectors are dynamically applied to chosen layers and timesteps during inference, modulating intermediate activations to suppress unwanted concepts while preserving the rest of the prompt; can also shift to a target style domain or bias toward adding/changing objects.
AI.result: Demonstrates effective and flexible control across diverse prompts and targets, preserving image quality without retraining and enabling style/object manipulation.
AI.conclusion: SHIFT provides a simple, lightweight inference-time framework for targeted concept removal and style/object manipulation in DiT diffusion models.

title: The causal relation between off-street parking and electric vehicle adoption in Scotland
summary: The transition to electric mobility hinges on maximising aggregate adoption while also facilitating equitable access. This study examines whether the 'charging divide' between households with and without off-street parking reflects a genuine infrastructure constraint or a by-product of socio-economic disparity. Moving beyond conventional predictive models, we apply a probabilistic causal framework to a nationally representative dataset of Scottish households, enabling estimation of policy interventions while explicitly neutralising the confounding effect of other causal factors. The results reveal a structural hierarchy in the EV adoption process. Private off-street parking functions as a conversion catalyst: enabling access to home-charging increases the probability of EV ownership from 3.3% to 5.6% (a 70% relative, 2.3 percentage point absolute increase). However, this effect primarily accelerates households already economically positioned to purchase an EV rather than recruiting new entrants. By contrast, household income operates as the fundamental affordability ceiling. A causal contrast between lower- and higher-income strata, shows a reduction in market non-participation by 23.1 percentage points, identifying financial capacity as the principal gatekeeper to entering the EV transition funnel. Crucially, the analysis demonstrates that standard observational models overstate the isolated effect of off-street parking infrastructure. The apparent effect emerges from selection bias: higher-income households are disproportionately likely to possess both private parking and the means to purchase EVs. These findings support a dual-track policy strategy: lowering the affordability ceiling for non-participants through financial instruments, while addressing EV home-charging access for the 'latent intent' cohort in high-density urban contexts.

AI.tldr: Probabilistic causal analysis on Scottish households shows the 'charging divide' is largely due to selection bias: income limits EV participation, while private off-street parking only modestly accelerates adoption for those already financially positioned; policy should target affordability and urban home-charging access for latent intent groups.
AI.motivation: To distinguish real infrastructure constraints from socio-economic confounding in EV adoption, addressing equity and efficiency of the transition.
AI.method: Probabilistic causal framework (counterfactual/policy-evaluation) applied to a nationally representative Scottish dataset to estimate intervention effects while neutralising other causal factors.
AI.result: EV ownership rises from 3.3% to 5.6% with home charging access (2.3pp absolute, 70% relative) but mainly for higher-income households; income is the fundamental affordability barrier, reducing market non-participation by 23.1pp between low/high income groups; observational models overstate the impact of off-street parking due to selection bias; advocates a dual-track policy.
AI.conclusion: Policy should combine financial instruments to expand affordability for non-participants and improved home-charging access for latent-intent groups in dense urban areas to accelerate equitable EV adoption.

title: TinyNeRV: Compact Neural Video Representations via Capacity Scaling, Distillation, and Low-Precision Inference
comment: Submitted to "Computers and Electrical Engineering", Elsevier
summary: Implicit neural video representations encode entire video sequences within the parameters of a neural network and enable constant time frame reconstruction. Recent work on Neural Representations for Videos (NeRV) has demonstrated competitive reconstruction performance while avoiding the sequential decoding process of conventional video codecs. However, most existing studies focus on moderate or high capacity models, leaving the behavior of extremely compact configurations required for constrained environments insufficiently explored. This paper presents a systematic study of tiny NeRV architectures designed for efficient deployment. Two lightweight configurations, NeRV-T and NeRV-T+, are introduced and evaluated across multiple video datasets in order to analyze how aggressive capacity reduction affects reconstruction quality, computational complexity, and decoding throughput. Beyond architectural scaling, the work investigates strategies for improving the performance of compact models without increasing inference cost. Knowledge distillation with frequency-aware focal supervision is explored to enhance reconstruction fidelity in low-capacity networks. In addition, the impact of lowprecision inference is examined through both post training quantization and quantization aware training to study the robustness of tiny models under reduced numerical precision. Experimental results demonstrate that carefully designed tiny NeRV variants can achieve favorable quality efficiency trade offs while substantially reducing parameter count, computational cost, and memory requirements. These findings provide insight into the practical limits of compact neural video representations and offer guidance for deploying NeRV style models in resource constrained and real-time environments. The official implementation is available at https: //github.com/HannanAkhtar/TinyNeRV-Implementation.

AI.tldr: Systematic study of extremely compact NeRV-based video representations (NeRV-T and NeRV-T+) highlighting the trade-offs between reconstruction quality, compute, memory, and throughput, augmented by frequency-aware distillation and quantization strategies; demonstrates favorable quality-efficiency results for constrained deployments; code released.
AI.motivation: Investigate how far neural video representations can be compressed before reconstruction quality and throughput become unacceptable, enabling deployment in resource-constrained and real-time environments.
AI.method: Design of two tiny NeRV variants (NeRV-T, NeRV-T+); evaluation on multiple video datasets; analysis of aggressive capacity reduction on quality, compute, and decoding throughput; knowledge distillation with frequency-aware focal supervision; post-training quantization and quantization-aware training; thorough empirical assessment of robustness under low-precision inference.
AI.result: Tiny NeRV variants achieve favorable quality-efficiency trade-offs with substantial reductions in parameters, flops, and memory; distillation and quantization strategies improve reconstruction fidelity and robustness in compact models; provides practical deployment guidance.
AI.conclusion: The study maps practical limits of compact neural video representations and offers actionable guidance for deploying NeRV-style models in resource-constrained and real-time environments; code is publicly available for replication and further exploration.

title: Distributed Online Convex Optimization with Compressed Communication: Optimal Regret and Applications
summary: Distributed online convex optimization (D-OCO) is a powerful paradigm for modeling distributed scenarios with streaming data. However, the communication cost between local learners and the central server is substantial in large-scale applications. To alleviate this bottleneck, we initiate the study of D-OCO with compressed communication. Firstly, to quantify the compression impact, we establish the $Ω(δ^{-1/2}\sqrt{T})$ and $Ω(δ^{-1}\log{T})$ lower bounds for convex and strongly convex loss functions, respectively, where $δ\in (0,1]$ is the compression ratio. Secondly, we propose an optimal algorithm, which enjoys regret bounds of $O(δ^{-1/2}\sqrt{T})$ and $O(δ^{-1} \log T)$ for convex and strongly convex loss functions, respectively. Our method incorporates the error feedback mechanism into the Follow-the-Regularized-Leader framework to address the coupling between the compression error and the projection error. Furthermore, we employ the online compression strategy to mitigate the accumulated error arising from the bidirectional compression. Our online method has great generality, and can be extended to the offline stochastic setting via online-to-batch conversion. We establish convergence rates of $O(δ^{-1/2}T^{-1/2})$ and $O(δ^{-1} T^{-1})$ for convex and strongly convex loss functions, respectively, providing the first guarantees for distributed non-smooth optimization with compressed communication and domain constraints.

AI.tldr: Introduces distributed online convex optimization with compressed communication, establishing lower bounds and proposing an optimal algorithm using error feedback within FTRL and online compression to achieve tight regret and convergence rates under both convex and strongly convex losses, including extensions to offline stochastic settings and domain constraints.
AI.motivation: Quantify how compression affects distributed online optimization performance and to mitigate communication bottlenecks in large-scale streaming data settings.
AI.method: Combine error-feedback in Follow-the-Regularized-Leader (FTRL) with online compression to decouple compression and projection errors, and apply online compression to counteract accumulated bidirectional compression errors. Analyze both convex and strongly convex losses to derive lower bounds and design an optimal algorithm with corresponding regret bounds; extend the approach to offline stochastic via online-to-batch conversion.
AI.result: Lower bounds: Ω(δ^{-1/2}√T) for convex and Ω(δ^{-1} log T) for strongly convex losses. Algorithm achieves regret bounds: O(δ^{-1/2}√T) (convex) and O(δ^{-1} log T) (strongly convex). Online method yields convergence rates: O(δ^{-1/2}T^{-1/2}) (convex) and O(δ^{-1}T^{-1}) (strongly convex). First guarantees for distributed non-smooth optimization with compressed communication and domain constraints.
AI.conclusion: Presents a general online-compression-based framework that attains nearly optimal regret and convergence bounds under compressed communication, with applicability to offline stochastic settings and enforcing domain constraints.

title: Hitem3D 2.0: Multi-View Guided Native 3D Texture Generation
summary: Although recent advances have improved the quality of 3D texture generation, existing methods still struggle with incomplete texture coverage, cross-view inconsistency, and misalignment between geometry and texture. To address these limitations, we propose Hitem3D 2.0, a multi-view guided native 3D texture generation framework that enhances texture quality through the integration of 2D multi-view generation priors and native 3D texture representations. Hitem3D 2.0 comprises two key components: a multi-view synthesis framework and a native 3D texture generation model. The multi-view generation is built upon a pre-trained image editing backbone and incorporates plug-and-play modules that explicitly promote geometric alignment, cross-view consistency, and illumination uniformity, thereby enabling the synthesis of high-fidelity multi-view images. Conditioned on the generated views and 3D geometry, the native 3D texture generation model projects multi-view textures onto 3D surfaces while plausibly completing textures in unseen regions. Through the integration of multi-view consistency constraints with native 3D texture modeling, Hitem3D 2.0 significantly improves texture completeness, cross-view coherence, and geometric alignment. Experimental results demonstrate that Hitem3D 2.0 outperforms existing methods in terms of texture detail, fidelity, consistency, coherence, and alignment.

AI.tldr: A multi-view guided native 3D texture generation framework (Hitem3D 2.0) that fuses 2D multi-view priors with native 3D texture representations to improve texture completeness, cross-view consistency, and alignment between texture and geometry via a two-stage system: multi-view synthesis with alignment/consistency/illumination modules and a native 3D texture model that projects and completes textures on 3D surfaces.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D texture generation struggles with incomplete texture coverage, cross-view inconsistency, and misalignment between geometry and texture. Leveraging 2D multi-view priors and native 3D texture representations can address coverage gaps, coherence across views, and accurate geometric alignment.
AI.method: Two-key framework: (1) a multi-view synthesis module built on a pre-trained image editing backbone with plug-and-play components promoting geometric alignment, cross-view consistency, and illumination uniformity to generate high-fidelity multi-view images; (2) a native 3D texture generation model that, conditioned on generated views and 3D geometry, projects textures onto surfaces and plausibly completes unseen regions; the system enforces multi-view consistency constraints within native 3D texture modeling.
AI.result: Experimentally, Hitem3D 2.0 outperforms existing methods in texture detail, fidelity, consistency, coherence, and alignment.
AI.conclusion: Integrating 2D multi-view generation priors with native 3D texture modeling yields substantial gains in texture completeness, cross-view coherence, and geometric-texture alignment, as demonstrated by improved quantitative and qualitative results.

title: Are Independently Estimated View Uncertainties Comparable? Unified Routing for Trusted Multi-View Classification
summary: Trusted multi-view classification typically relies on a view-wise evidential fusion process: each view independently produces class evidence and uncertainty, and the final prediction is obtained by aggregating these independent opinions. While this design is modular and uncertainty-aware, it implicitly assumes that evidence from different views is numerically comparable. In practice, however, this assumption is fragile. Different views often differ in feature space, noise level, and semantic granularity, while independently trained branches are optimized only for prediction correctness, without any constraint enforcing cross-view consistency in evidence strength. As a result, the uncertainty used for fusion can be dominated by branch-specific scale bias rather than true sample-level reliability. To address this issue, we propose Trusted Multi-view learning with Unified Routing (TMUR), which decouples view-specific evidence extraction from fusion arbitration. TMUR uses view-private experts and one collaborative expert, and employs a unified router that observes the global multi-view context to generate sample-level expert weights. Soft load-balancing and diversity regularization further encourage balanced expert utilization and more discriminative expert specialization. We also provide theoretical analysis showing why independent evidential supervision does not identify a common cross-view evidence scale, and why unified global routing is preferable to branch-local arbitration when reliability is sample-dependent.

AI.tldr: TMUR decouples per-view evidence from fusion in trusted multi-view classification using view-private experts and a global unified router, plus soft load-balancing and diversity regularization; it theoretically analyzes cross-view evidence scale issues and argues global routing is better for sample-dependent reliability.
AI.motivation: Different views differ in feature space, noise, and granularity; independent evidential supervision leads to scale bias and unreliable fusion. A unified routing mechanism aims to provide sample-level, cross-view evidence weighting.
AI.method: Introduce view-private experts for each view and a collaborative expert; a unified router observes the global multi-view context to generate sample-level weights for experts; employ soft load-balancing and diversity regularization to balance expert utilization and specialization.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis showing that independent evidential supervision cannot identify a common cross-view evidence scale and that unified global routing outperforms branch-local arbitration for sample-dependent reliability; methodology components designed to improve cross-view fusion.
AI.conclusion: TMUR offers a principled decoupling of evidence extraction from fusion, enabled by a global router and auxiliary regularization, which helps align cross-view evidence scales and improves reliability of multi-view predictions.

title: Neural Distribution Prior for LiDAR Out-of-Distribution Detection
summary: LiDAR-based perception is critical for autonomous driving due to its robustness to poor lighting and visibility conditions. Yet, current models operate under the closed-set assumption and often fail to recognize unexpected out-of-distribution (OOD) objects in the open world. Existing OOD scoring functions exhibit limited performance because they ignore the pronounced class imbalance inherent in LiDAR OOD detection and assume a uniform class distribution. To address this limitation, we propose the Neural Distribution Prior (NDP), a framework that models the distributional structure of network predictions and adaptively reweights OOD scores based on alignment with a learned distribution prior. NDP dynamically captures the logit distribution patterns of training data and corrects class-dependent confidence bias through an attention-based module. We further introduce a Perlin noise-based OOD synthesis strategy that generates diverse auxiliary OOD samples from input scans, enabling robust OOD training without external datasets. Extensive experiments on the SemanticKITTI and STU benchmarks demonstrate that NDP substantially improves OOD detection performance, achieving a point-level AP of 61.31\% on the STU test set, which is more than 10$\times$ higher than the previous best result. Our framework is compatible with various existing OOD scoring formulations, providing an effective solution for open-world LiDAR perception.

AI.tldr: NDP learns a neural distribution prior over logits to reweight OOD scores, augmented by Perlin-noise OOD synthesis; yields strong open-world LiDAR OOD detection, e.g., 61.31% point-level AP on STU (over 10x prior best).
AI.motivation: Open-world LiDAR perception suffers from class-imbalanced data and uniform-distribution assumptions in OOD scoring, leading to weak detection of unknown objects in autonomous driving scenarios.
AI.method: Neural Distribution Prior (NDP) models the logit distribution of training data and uses an attention-based module to adjust OOD scores toward the learned prior; introduces a Perlin noise-based OOD synthesis strategy to generate diverse auxiliary OOD samples from input scans, enabling robust OOD training without external datasets; framework is compatible with existing OOD scoring formulations.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on SemanticKITTI and STU show substantial OOD detection gains, achieving 61.31% point-level AP on STU test—more than 10x higher than the previous best—and demonstrating compatibility with various OOD scoring methods.
AI.conclusion: NDP provides an effective, adaptable solution for open-world LiDAR perception by explicitly modeling predictive-distribution structure and augmenting training with synthetic OOD data, improving robustness without requiring external data.

title: Meta-Learned Basis Adaptation for Parametric Linear PDEs
summary: We propose a hybrid physics-informed framework for solving families of parametric linear partial differential equations (PDEs) by combining a meta-learned predictor with a least-squares corrector. The predictor, termed \textbf{KAPI} (Kernel-Adaptive Physics-Informed meta-learner), is a shallow task-conditioned model that maps query coordinates and PDE parameters to solution values while internally generating an interpretable, task-adaptive Gaussian basis geometry. A lightweight meta-network maps PDE parameters to basis centers, widths, and activity patterns, thereby learning how the approximation space should adapt across the parametric family. This predictor-generated geometry is transferred to a second-stage corrector, which augments it with a background basis and computes the final solution through a one-shot physics-informed Extreme Learning Machine (PIELM)-style least-squares solve. We evaluate the method on four linear PDE families spanning diffusion, transport, mixed advection--diffusion, and variable-speed transport. Across these cases, the predictor captures meaningful physics through localized and transport-aligned basis placement, while the corrector further improves accuracy, often by one or more orders of magnitude. Comparisons with parametric PINNs, physics-informed DeepONet, and uniform-grid PIELM correctors highlight the value of predictor-guided basis adaptation as an interpretable and efficient strategy for parametric PDE solving.

AI.tldr: Hybrid KAPI meta-learner with a PIELM-style corrector for parametric linear PDEs yields interpretable, adaptive Gaussian-basis representations and large accuracy gains across diffusion/transport families.
AI.motivation: Address solving families of parametric linear PDEs efficiently by learning an adaptive approximation space. Traditional PINNs struggle with parametric dependency and interpretability; a meta-learned, basis-adaptive predictor plus a least-squares corrector can offer scalable accuracy and interpretability.
AI.method: Two-stage framework: (1) KAPI predictor maps coordinates and PDE parameters to solution values and induces a task-adaptive Gaussian basis geometry; a lightweight meta-network assigns basis centers, widths, and activity patterns across the parameter family. (2) A second-stage corrector augments this geometry with a background basis and solves a one-shot physics-informed Extreme Learning Machine (PIELM)-style least-squares problem to obtain the final solution. Evaluated on four linear PDE families (diffusion, transport, mixed advection–diffusion, variable-speed transport).
AI.result: The predictor learns localized, transport-aligned basis placement that captures governing physics; the corrector provides substantial accuracy improvements, often by 1+ orders of magnitude. Comparisons against parametric PINNs, physics-informed DeepONet, and uniform-grid PIELM correctors demonstrate the benefit of predictor-guided basis adaptation as an interpretable and efficient parametric PDE solver.
AI.conclusion: Predictor-guided basis adaptation offers an interpretable and efficient strategy for parametric linear PDE solving, with significant accuracy gains over established baselines. The approach emphasizes basis geometry learning as a core mechanism for capturing parametric physics.

title: FashionStylist: An Expert Knowledge-enhanced Multimodal Dataset for Fashion Understanding
summary: Fashion understanding requires both visual perception and expert-level reasoning about style, occasion, compatibility, and outfit rationale. However, existing fashion datasets remain fragmented and task-specific, often focusing on item attributes, outfit co-occurrence, or weak textual supervision, and thus provide limited support for holistic outfit understanding. In this paper, we introduce FashionStylist, an expert-annotated benchmark for holistic and expert-level fashion understanding. Constructed through a dedicated fashion-expert annotation pipeline, FashionStylist provides professionally grounded annotations at both the item and outfit levels. It supports three representative tasks: outfit-to-item grounding, outfit completion, and outfit evaluation.
