These tasks cover realistic item recovery from complex outfits with layering and accessories, compatibility-aware composition beyond co-occurrence matching, and expert-level assessment of style, season, occasion, and overall coherence. Experimental results show that FashionStylist serves not only as a unified benchmark for multiple fashion tasks, but also as an effective training resource for improving grounding, completion, and outfit-level semantic evaluation in MLLM-based fashion systems.

AI.tldr: A new expert-annotated benchmark, FashionStylist, enables holistic fashion understanding with item- and outfit-level annotations and three tasks: outfit-to-item grounding, outfit completion, and outfit evaluation; serves as a unified benchmark and training resource for MLLMs.
AI.motivation: Current fashion datasets are fragmented and task-specific, lacking holistic, expert-grounded reasoning that spans perception and style/season/occasion reasoning.
AI.method: Developed an expert-driven annotation pipeline to produce professionally grounded annotations at both item and outfit levels, enabling three tasks: outfit-to-item grounding, outfit completion, and outfit evaluation.
AI.result: FashionStylist functions as a unified benchmark across multiple tasks and also provides effective training data to improve grounding, completion, and outfit-level semantic evaluation in MLLM-based fashion systems.
AI.conclusion: FashionStylist advances holistic fashion understanding by providing a scalable, expert-grounded resource that supports evaluation and training of models on grounding, completion, and semantic assessment.

title: Stability Enhanced Gaussian Process Variational Autoencoders
summary: A novel stability-enhanced Gaussian process variational autoencoder (SEGP-VAE) is proposed for indirectly training a low-dimensional linear time invariant (LTI) system, using high-dimensional video data. The mean and covariance function of the novel SEGP prior are derived from the definition of an LTI system, enabling the SEGP to capture the indirectly observed latent process using a combined probabilistic and interpretable physical model. The search space of LTI parameters is restricted to the set of semi-contracting systems via a complete and unconstrained parametrisation. As a result, the SEGP-VAE can be trained using unconstrained optimisation algorithms. Furthermore, this parametrisation prevents numerical issues caused by the presence of a non-Hurwitz state matrix. A case study applies SEGP-VAE to a dataset containing videos of spiralling particles. This highlights the benefits of the approach and the application-specific design choices that enabled accurate latent state predictions.

AI.tldr: SEGP-VAE introduces a stability-enhanced Gaussian process variational autoencoder with a latent LTI prior derived from system dynamics, using semi-contracting parameterization to enable unconstrained optimization and avoid non-Hurwitz issues; validated on videos of spiralling particles with accurate latent state predictions.
AI.motivation: To learn interpretable, physically-consistent latent dynamics from high-dimensional video data while ensuring stability and tractable optimization.
AI.method: Develop a GP-VAE where the prior is based on an LTI system; restrict LTI parameters to a semi-contracting set via a complete unconstrained parametrization; train with unconstrained optimization; apply to video data of spiralling particles to recover latent states.
AI.result: The approach yields accurate latent state predictions on the spiralling-particle video dataset, illustrating the benefits of a physics-informed prior and unconstrained training.
AI.conclusion: SEGP-VAE provides a stable, interpretable latent-dynamics model for indirectly observed video data; the semi-contracting LTI parametrization enables unconstrained optimization and mitigates numerical issues from non-Hurwitz matrices, with applicability to similar dynamical system identification tasks.

title: Mosaic: Multimodal Jailbreak against Closed-Source VLMs via Multi-View Ensemble Optimization
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are powerful but remain vulnerable to multimodal jailbreak attacks. Existing attacks mainly rely on either explicit visual prompt attacks or gradient-based adversarial optimization. While the former is easier to detect, the latter produces subtle perturbations that are less perceptible, but is usually optimized and evaluated under homogeneous open-source surrogate-target settings, leaving its effectiveness on commercial closed-source VLMs under heterogeneous settings unclear. To examine this issue, we study different surrogate-target settings and observe a consistent gap between homogeneous and heterogeneous settings, a phenomenon we term surrogate dependency. Motivated by this finding, we propose Mosaic, a Multi-view ensemble optimization framework for multimodal jailbreak against closed-source VLMs, which alleviates surrogate dependency under heterogeneous surrogate-target settings by reducing over-reliance on any single surrogate model and visual view. Specifically, Mosaic incorporates three core components: a Text-Side Transformation module, which perturbs refusal-sensitive lexical patterns; a Multi-View Image Optimization module, which updates perturbations under diverse cropped views to avoid overfitting to a single visual view; and a Surrogate Ensemble Guidance module, which aggregates optimization signals from multiple surrogate VLMs to reduce surrogate-specific bias. Extensive experiments on safety benchmarks demonstrate that Mosaic achieves state-of-the-art Attack Success Rate and Average Toxicity against commercial closed-source VLMs.

AI.tldr: Mosaic introduces a multi-view ensemble optimization framework to perform multimodal jailbreaks on closed-source VLMs, addressing surrogate dependency and improving attack effectiveness.
AI.motivation: To understand and mitigate the gap in attack effectiveness between homogeneous (single surrogate) and heterogeneous (multi-surrogate) surrogate-target settings for Vision-Language Models, especially against commercial closed-source VLMs.
AI.method: Three components: (1) Text-Side Transformation to perturb refusal-sensitive lexical patterns; (2) Multi-View Image Optimization over diverse cropped views to avoid overfitting to a single visual view; (3) Surrogate Ensemble Guidance aggregating signals from multiple surrogate VLMs to reduce surrogate-specific bias.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on safety benchmarks show that Mosaic achieves state-of-the-art Attack Success Rate and Average Toxicity against commercial closed-source VLMs.
AI.conclusion: Mosaic reduces surrogate dependency under heterogeneous settings and demonstrates vulnerable behavior of closed-source VLMs to multi-view ensemble jailbreaks, underscoring need for robust defenses against multimodal prompts and adversarial perturbations.

title: Hierarchical Flow Decomposition for Turning Movement Prediction at Signalized Intersections
comment: Accepted to IEEE SoutheastCon 2026. 6 pages, 5 figures
summary: Accurate prediction of intersection turning movements is essential for adaptive signal control but remains difficult due to the high volatility of directional flows. This study proposes HFD-TM (Hierarchical Flow-Decomposition for Turning Movement Prediction), a hierarchical deep learning framework that predicts turning movements by first forecasting corridor through-movements and then expanding these predictions to individual turning streams. This design is motivated by empirical traffic structure, where corridor flows account for 65.1% of total volume, exhibit lower volatility than turning movements, and explain 35.5% of turning-movement variance. A physics-informed loss function enforces flow conservation to maintain structural consistency. Evaluated on six months of 15-minute interval LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging) data from a six-intersection corridor in Nashville, Tennessee, HFD-TM achieves a mean absolute error of 2.49 vehicles per interval, reducing MAE by 5.7% compared to a Transformer and by 27.0% compared to a GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit). Ablation results show that hierarchical decomposition provides the largest performance gain, while training time is 12.8 times lower than DCRNN (Diffusion Convolutional Recurrent Neural Network), demonstrating suitability for real-time traffic applications.

AI.tldr: A hierarchical deep-learning framework (HFD-TM) forecasts corridor through-movements first, then expands to turning streams, aided by a physics-informed loss for flow conservation; it improves turning-movement prediction accuracy and real-time feasibility on LiDAR data from a multi-intersection corridor.
AI.motivation: Turning movements are highly volatile and difficult to predict; corridor through-movements are more stable and explain a large fraction of turning-movement variance, suggesting a hierarchical structure could improve accuracy and efficiency for adaptive signal control.
AI.method: Propose Hierarchical Flow-Decomposition for Turning Movement Prediction (HFD-TM). First forecast corridor through-movements, then decompose into individual turning streams. Incorporate a physics-informed loss to enforce flow conservation. Evaluate on six months of 15-minute LiDAR data from a six-intersection Nashville corridor; compare against Transformer and GRU; perform ablations and compare training time to DCRNN.
AI.result: MAE of 2.49 vehicles per interval; 5.7% MAE improvement over Transformer; 27.0% improvement over GRU; ablation shows hierarchical decomposition yields largest gains; training time 12.8x lower than DCRNN.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical decomposition plus flow-conservation constraints yields improved turning-movement prediction with substantial efficiency gains, supporting real-time deployment for adaptive signal control; corridor flows account for a majority of traffic volume and variance, reinforcing the structural rationale.

title: Visually-Guided Policy Optimization for Multimodal Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has significantly advanced the reasoning ability of vision-language models (VLMs). However, the inherent text-dominated nature of VLMs often leads to insufficient visual faithfulness, characterized by sparse attention activation to visual tokens. More importantly, our empirical analysis reveals that temporal visual forgetting along reasoning steps exacerbates this deficiency. To bridge this gap, we propose Visually-Guided Policy Optimization (VGPO), a novel framework to reinforce visual focus during policy optimization. Specifically, VGPO initially introduces a Visual Attention Compensation mechanism that leverages visual similarity to localize and amplify visual cues, while progressively elevating visual expectations in later steps to counteract visual forgetting. Building on this mechanism, we implement a dual-grained advantage re-weighting strategy: the intra-trajectory level highlights tokens exhibiting relatively high visual activation, while the inter-trajectory level prioritizes trajectories demonstrating superior visual accumulation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that VGPO achieves better visual activation and superior performance in mathematical multimodal reasoning and visual-dependent tasks.

AI.tldr: VGPO enhances visual focus and mitigates visual forgetting in reinforcement-learning-based vision-language models via Visual Attention Compensation and dual-grained advantage re-weighting.
AI.motivation: To improve visual faithfulness and reasoning accuracy in text-dominated VLMs by addressing sparse visual token activation and temporal forgetting during reasoning.
AI.method: VGPO introduces a Visual Attention Compensation mechanism that uses visual similarity to localize and amplify informative visual cues, and gradually increases visual expectations across reasoning steps to counteract forgetting. It also employs a dual-grained advantage re-weighting: intra-trajectory re-weights tokens with high visual activation, while inter-trajectory re-weights trajectories with strong visual accumulation.
AI.result: Experiments show VGPO leads to stronger visual activation and superior performance on mathematical multimodal reasoning and other visual-dependent tasks compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: VGPO provides a robust policy-optimization framework that reinforces visual focus, mitigates visual forgetting, and improves multimodal reasoning in RL-based VLMs.

title: Beyond Segmentation: Structurally Informed Facade Parsing from Imperfect Images
summary: Standard object detectors typically treat architectural elements independently, often resulting in facade parsings that lack the structural coherence required for downstream procedural reconstruction. We address this limitation by augmenting the YOLOv8 training objective with a custom lightweight alignment loss. This regularization encourages grid-consistent arrangements of bounding boxes during training, effectively injecting geometric priors without altering the standard inference pipeline. Experiments on the CMP dataset demonstrate that our method successfully improves structural regularity, correcting alignment errors caused by perspective and occlusion while maintaining a controllable trade-off with standard detection accuracy.

AI.tldr: Lightweight alignment loss during YOLOv8 training improves structural regularity of detections without altering inference, trading off a controllable amount of accuracy for better geometric coherence.
AI.motivation: Standard detectors treat architectural elements independently, producing facades with limited structural coherence. There is a need to impose geometric priors to support downstream procedural reconstruction and facade parsing.
AI.method: Introduce a lightweight alignment loss during training that enforces grid-consistent bounding box arrangements. This regularization injects geometric priors without changing the inference pipeline.
AI.result: On the CMP dataset, the method enhances structural regularity and corrects alignment errors due to perspective and occlusion, while allowing a controllable trade-off with standard detection accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Training-time geometric regularization yields more coherent structural representations without modifying inference, offering a tunable balance between regularity and detection accuracy.

title: Drift-Aware Online Dynamic Learning for Nonstationary Multivariate Time Series: Application to Sintering Quality Prediction
summary: Accurate prediction of nonstationary multivariate time series remains a critical challenge in complex industrial systems such as iron ore sintering. In practice, pronounced concept drift compounded by significant label verification latency rapidly degrades the performance of offline-trained models. Existing methods based on static architectures or passive update strategies struggle to simultaneously extract multi-scale spatiotemporal features and overcome the stability-plasticity dilemma without immediate supervision. To address these limitations, a Drift-Aware Multi-Scale Dynamic Learning (DA-MSDL) framework is proposed to maintain robust multi-output predictive performance via online adaptive mechanisms on nonstationary data streams. The framework employs a multi-scale bi-branch convolutional network as its backbone to disentangle local fluctuations from long-term trends, thereby enhancing representational capacity for complex dynamic patterns. To circumvent the label latency bottleneck, DA-MSDL leverages Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) for unsupervised drift detection. By quantifying online statistical deviations in feature distributions, DA-MSDL proactively triggers model adaptation prior to inference. Furthermore, a drift-severity-guided hierarchical fine-tuning strategy is developed. Supported by prioritized experience replay from a dynamic memory queue, this approach achieves rapid distribution alignment while effectively mitigating catastrophic forgetting. Long-horizon experiments on real-world industrial sintering data and a public benchmark dataset demonstrate that DA-MSDL consistently outperforms representative baselines under severe concept drift. Exhibiting strong cross-domain generalization and predictive stability, the proposed framework provides an effective online dynamic learning paradigm for quality monitoring in nonstationary environments.

AI.tldr: DA-MSDL introduces a drift-aware online learning framework for nonstationary multivariate time series, using a multi-scale CNN backbone and MMD-based drift detection, with a drift-severity-guided fine-tuning strategy and dynamic memory to mitigate forgetting, achieving superior performance under severe concept drift in industrial sintering data and a public benchmark.
AI.motivation: Nonstationary data with concept drift and significant label latency degrade offline models; need online, adaptive learning that can extract multi-scale spatiotemporal features while balancing stability and plasticity.
AI.method: A multi-scale bi-branch convolutional backbone disentangles local fluctuations from long-term trends; MMD-based unsupervised drift detection triggers pre-inference adaptation; a drift-severity-guided hierarchical fine-tuning strategy with prioritized experience replay from a dynamic memory queue mitigates catastrophic forgetting; online adaptation aligns distributions for robust multi-output prediction.
AI.result: Long-horizon experiments on real-world sintering data and a public dataset show DA-MSDL consistently outperforms representative baselines under severe concept drift; demonstrates cross-domain generalization and predictive stability.
AI.conclusion: DA-MSDL provides an effective online dynamic learning paradigm for quality monitoring in nonstationary environments, with strong robustness to drift and label latency.

title: GeRM: A Generative Rendering Model From Physically Realistic to Photorealistic
summary: For decades, Physically-Based Rendering (PBR) is the fundation of synthesizing photorealisitic images, and therefore sometimes roughly referred as Photorealistic Rendering (PRR). While PBR is indeed a mathematical simulation of light transport that guarantees physical reality, photorealism has additional reliance on the realistic digital model of geometry and appearance of the real world, leaving a barely explored gap from PBR to PRR (P2P). Consequently, the path toward photorealism faces a critical dilemma: the explicit simulation of PRR encumbered by unreachable realistic digital models for real-world existence, while implicit generation models sacrifice controllability and geometric consistency. Based on this insight, this paper presents the problem, data, and approach of mitigating P2P gap, followed by the first multi-modal generative rendering model, dubbed GeRM, to unify PBR and PRR. GeRM integrates physical attributes like G-buffers with text prompts, and progressive incremental injection to generate controllable photorealistic images, allowing users to fluidly navigate the continuum between strict physical fidelity and perceptual photorealism. Technically, we model the transition between PBR and PRR images as a distribution transfer and aim to learn a distribution transfer vector field (DTV Field) to guide this process. To define the learning objective, we first leverage a multi-agent VLM framework to construct an expert-guided pairwise P2P transfer dataset, named P2P-50K, where each paired sample in the dataset corresponds to a transfer vector in the DTV Field. Subsequently, we propose a multi-condition ControlNet to learn the DTV Field, which synthesizes PBR images and progressively transitions them into PRR images, guided by G-buffers, text prompts, and cues for enhanced regions.

AI.tldr: GeRM proposes the first multi-modal generative rendering model to bridge physics-based rendering (PBR) and photorealistic rendering (PRR) using a distribution transfer vector field (DTV Field), learned via a multi-agent VLM-derived P2P dataset (P2P-50K) and a multi-condition ControlNet conditioned on G-buffers and text prompts to progressively transform PBR images into PRR while preserving controllability and geometric consistency.
AI.motivation: There is a fundamental gap between physically correct light transport (PBR) and perceptually realistic rendering (PRR) due to reliance on accurate digital geometry/appearance. Implicit generative models offer realism but sacrifice controllability and geometric fidelity. Bridging PBR and PRR aims to enable controllable, geometry-consistent photorealism without requiring perfect real-world models.
AI.method: Define a distribution transfer vector field (DTV Field) to guide the transition from PBR to PRR. Build P2P-50K, a dataset of PBR→PRR pairs with expert-guided transfer vectors using a multi-agent VLM framework. Train a multi-condition ControlNet to synthesize PBR images and progressively transform them toward PRR, guided by G-buffers, textual prompts, and cues for enhanced regions. The approach integrates G-buffers with prompts and progressive injection to allow controllable navigation along the PBR-PRR continuum.
AI.result: Conceptual framework with dataset and model architecture proposed; no empirical results are reported in the abstract. The contribution is methodological: a data-driven DTV Field learning approach and a controllable, multi-modal rendering pipeline.
AI.conclusion: GeRM unifies PBR and PRR by learning a distribution transfer mechanism and enabling controlled traversal along the PBR→PRR continuum, leveraging G-buffers and text guidance to achieve perceptually photorealistic yet geometrically coherent rendering.

title: Bringing Clustering to MLL: Weakly-Supervised Clustering for Partial Multi-Label Learning
summary: Label noise in multi-label learning (MLL) poses significant challenges for model training, particularly in partial multi-label learning (PML) where candidate labels contain both relevant and irrelevant labels. While clustering offers a natural approach to exploit data structure for noise identification, traditional clustering methods cannot be directly applied to multi-label scenarios due to a fundamental incompatibility: clustering produces membership values that sum to one per instance, whereas multi-label assignments require binary values that can sum to any number. We propose a novel weakly-supervised clustering approach for PML (WSC-PML) that bridges clustering and multi-label learning through membership matrix decomposition. Our key innovation decomposes the clustering membership matrix $\mathbf{A}$ into two components: $\mathbf{A} = \mathbfΠ \odot \mathbf{F}$, where $\mathbfΠ$ maintains clustering constraints while $\mathbf{F}$ preserves multi-label characteristics. This decomposition enables seamless integration of unsupervised clustering with multi-label supervision for effective label noise handling. WSC-PML employs a three-stage process: initial prototype learning from noisy labels, adaptive confidence-based weak supervision construction, and joint optimization via iterative clustering refinement. Extensive experiments on 24 datasets demonstrate that our approach outperforms six state-of-the-art methods across all evaluation metrics.

AI.tldr: Introduces WSC-PML, a weakly-supervised clustering framework for partial multi-label learning that decomposes the clustering membership matrix A into A = Π ⊙ F to reconcile clustering with multi-label assignments, enabling noise-robust learning; a three-stage pipeline yields strong empirical gains on 24 datasets against six baselines.
AI.motivation: Partial multi-label learning suffers from candidate-label noise; standard clustering cannot handle multi-label outputs due to differing value constraints; a unified approach leveraging structure via clustering while preserving multi-label signals is needed.
AI.method: Propose A = Π ⊙ F, with Π enforcing clustering constraints and F encoding multi-label information; three-stage process: (1) prototype learning from noisy labels, (2) adaptive confidence-based weak supervision construction, (3) joint optimization with iterative clustering refinement; integrates unsupervised clustering with multi-label supervision.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on 24 datasets shows WSC-PML outperforms six state-of-the-art methods across all metrics.
AI.conclusion: The decomposition-based weak supervision bridging clustering and MLL effectively handles label noise in PML and yields improved performance, demonstrating the viability of the approach.

title: Rays as Pixels: Learning A Joint Distribution of Videos and Camera Trajectories
comment: 9 pages, 6 figures, 4 tables. Project page: https://wbjang.github.io/raysaspixels/
summary: Recovering camera parameters from images and rendering scenes from novel viewpoints have long been treated as separate tasks in computer vision and graphics. This separation breaks down when image coverage is sparse or poses are ambiguous, since each task needs what the other produces. We propose Rays as Pixels, a Video Diffusion Model (VDM) that learns a joint distribution over videos and camera trajectories. We represent each camera as dense ray pixels (raxels) and denoise them jointly with video frames through Decoupled Self-Cross Attention mechanism. A single trained model handles three tasks: predicting camera trajectories from video, jointly generating video and camera trajectory from input images, and generating video from input images along a target camera trajectory. Because the model can both predict trajectories from a video and generate views conditioned on its own predictions, we evaluate it through a closed-loop self-consistency test, demonstrating that its forward and inverse predictions agree. Notably, trajectory prediction requires far fewer denoising steps than video generation, even a few denoising steps suffice for self-consistency. We report results on pose estimation and camera-controlled video generation.

AI.tldr: A unified diffusion model jointly learned over videos and camera trajectories, representing cameras as dense ray pixels (raxels) and denoising them with video frames via Decoupled Self-Cross Attention. A single model supports three tasks: (1) predicting trajectories from video, (2) joint video+trajectory generation from images, and (3) video generation from images along a target trajectory. It uses closed-loop self-consistency to validate forward/inverse predictions; trajectory inference needs few denoising steps, enabling efficiency. Demonstrated on pose estimation and camera-controlled video generation.
AI.motivation: Traditional separation of camera parameter recovery and novel-view rendering breaks down with sparse image coverage or ambiguous poses. A joint model that leverages both tasks can exploit mutual information to improve robustness, data efficiency, and consistency.
AI.method: Train a Video Diffusion Model that learns a joint distribution over videos and camera trajectories. Represent each camera as dense ray pixels (raxels) and jointly denoise them with video frames using a Decoupled Self-Cross Attention mechanism. A single model supports three tasks: (i) trajectory prediction from video, (ii) joint video+trajectory generation from input images, (iii) video generation from input images along a specified camera trajectory. Evaluate via a closed-loop self-consistency test where forward and inverse predictions agree.
AI.result: The approach achieves self-consistent forward/inverse predictions, with trajectory prediction requiring far fewer denoising steps than full video generation. Demonstrated capability on pose estimation and camera-controlled video generation.
AI.conclusion: A unified framework for joint camera-parameter learning and view synthesis via diffusion, enabling efficient trajectory prediction and consistent video generation by leveraging decoupled self-cross attention and a single model capable of multiple related tasks.

title: VAGNet: Vision-based Accident Anticipation with Global Features
summary: Traffic accidents are a leading cause of fatalities and injuries across the globe. Therefore, the ability to anticipate hazardous situations in advance is essential. Automated accident anticipation enables timely intervention through driver alerts and collision avoidance maneuvers, forming a key component of advanced driver assistance systems. In autonomous driving, such predictive capabilities support proactive safety behaviors, such as initiating defensive driving and human takeover when required. Using dashcam video as input offers a cost-effective solution, but it is challenging due to the complexity of real-world driving scenes. Accident anticipation systems need to operate in real-time. However, current methods involve extracting features from each detected object, which is computationally intensive. We propose VAGNet, a deep neural network that learns to predict accidents from dash-cam video using global features of traffic scenes without requiring explicit object-level features. The network consists of transformer and graph modules, and we use the vision foundation model VideoMAE-V2 for global feature extraction. Experiments on four benchmark datasets (DAD, DoTA, DADA, and Nexar) show that our method anticipates accidents with higher average precision and mean time-to-accident while being computationally more efficient compared to existing methods.

AI.tldr: VAGNet leverages global scene features and transformer/graph modules with VideoMAE-V2 to anticipate accidents from dashcam video, reducing computational load by avoiding per-object features while improving accuracy and MTTA.
AI.motivation: Real-time accident anticipation is vital for ADAS/autonomous driving. Object-centric feature extraction is computationally expensive and may miss contextual cues; a global-scene approach can be more efficient and robust.
AI.method: An end-to-end deep network, VAGNet, uses VideoMAE-V2 for global feature extraction, followed by transformer and graph modules to model temporal and relational scene information, avoiding explicit object-level features and predicting accident likelihood and MTTA from dashcam video.
AI.result: On four benchmarks (DAD, DoTA, DADA, Nexar), VAGNet attains higher average precision and mean time-to-accident, with lower computational cost compared to state-of-the-art object-based methods.
AI.conclusion: Global-scene, object-free representations with transformer/graph reasoning offer effective and efficient accident anticipation suitable for real-time ADAS/autonomy; supports safer proactive interventions and human takeover when needed.

title: Stochastic-Dimension Frozen Sampled Neural Network for High-Dimensional Gross-Pitaevskii Equations on Unbounded Domains
summary: In this paper, we propose a stochastic-dimension frozen sampled neural network (SD-FSNN) for solving a class of high-dimensional Gross-Pitaevskii equations (GPEs) on unbounded domains. SD-FSNN is unbiased across all dimensions, and its computational cost is independent of the dimension, avoiding the exponential growth in computational and memory costs associated with Hermite-basis discretizations. Additionally, we randomly sample the hidden weights and biases of the neural network, significantly outperforming iterative, gradient-based optimization methods in terms of training time and accuracy. Furthermore, we employ a space-time separation strategy, using adaptive ordinary differential equation (ODE) solvers to update the evolution coefficients and incorporate temporal causality. To preserve the structure of the GPEs, we integrate a Gaussian-weighted ansatz into the neural network to enforce exponential decay at infinity, embed a normalization projection layer for mass normalization, and add an energy conservation constraint to mitigate long-time numerical dissipation. Comparative experiments with existing methods demonstrate the superior performance of SD-FSNN across a range of spatial dimensions and interaction parameters. Compared to existing random-feature methods, SD-FSNN reduces the complexity from linear to dimension-independent. Additionally, SD-FSNN achieves better accuracy and faster training compared to general high-dimensional solvers, while focusing specifically on high-dimensional GPEs on unbounded domains.

AI.tldr: SD-FSNN achieves dimension-independent complexity for high-dimensional GPEs on unbounded domains by using stochastic-dimension frozen sampling, Gaussian-weighted ansatz, mass normalization, and energy constraints, outperforming random-feature and general high-dimensional solvers in accuracy and training time.
AI.motivation: Tackle the curse of dimensionality in solving high-dimensional Gross-Pitaevskii equations on unbounded domains and reduce computational cost relative to Hermite discretizations and gradient-based optimizers.
AI.method: Propose stochastic-dimension frozen sampled neural networks (SD-FSNN) with random hidden weights/biases, space-time separation via adaptive ODE solvers, Gaussian-weighted ansatz for exponential decay, a normalization projection layer, and an energy-conserving constraint; complexity is dimension-independent and training relies on random features rather than gradient-based optimization.
AI.result: Empirical results show SD-FSNN outperforms existing methods across various dimensions and interaction parameters, achieves dimension-independent complexity (unlike linear scaling in random-feature baselines), and offers higher accuracy with faster training compared to general high-dimensional solvers.
AI.conclusion: SD-FSNN provides an effective framework for solving high-dimensional GPEs on unbounded domains by combining dimension-independent random-feature networks with physics-informed components, yielding improved efficiency and accuracy and mitigating long-time numerical dissipation.

title: ECHO: Efficient Chest X-ray Report Generation with One-step Block Diffusion
summary: Chest X-ray report generation (CXR-RG) has the potential to substantially alleviate radiologists' workload. However, conventional autoregressive vision--language models (VLMs) suffer from high inference latency due to sequential token decoding. Diffusion-based models offer a promising alternative through parallel generation, but they still require multiple denoising iterations. Compressing multi-step denoising to a single step could further reduce latency, but often degrades textual coherence due to the mean-field bias introduced by token-factorized denoisers. To address this challenge, we propose \textbf{ECHO}, an efficient diffusion-based VLM (dVLM) for chest X-ray report generation. ECHO enables stable one-step-per-block inference via a novel Direct Conditional Distillation (DCD) framework, which mitigates the mean-field limitation by constructing unfactorized supervision from on-policy diffusion trajectories to encode joint token dependencies. In addition, we introduce a Response-Asymmetric Diffusion (RAD) training strategy that further improves training efficiency while maintaining model effectiveness. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ECHO surpasses state-of-the-art autoregressive methods, improving RaTE and SemScore by \textbf{64.33\%} and \textbf{60.58\%} respectively, while achieving an \textbf{$8\times$} inference speedup without compromising clinical accuracy.

AI.tldr: ECHO is a diffusion-based vision-language model for chest X-ray report generation that achieves one-step-per-block inference via Direct Conditional Distillation (DCD) and Response-Asymmetric Diffusion (RAD). It significantly accelerates inference (8x) while outperforming autoregressive baselines in RaTE (~64.33% gain) and SemScore (~60.58% gain) without sacrificing clinical accuracy.
AI.motivation: Reduce inference latency and improve coherence in CXR report generation. Address mean-field bias in token-factorized denoisers typical of one-step diffusion and autoregressive VLMs. Leverage parallel diffusion generation to achieve fast, coherent reports with minimal loss in clinical fidelity.
AI.method: Introduce ECHO with stable one-step-per-block inference using Direct Conditional Distillation, which creates unfactorized supervision from on-policy diffusion trajectories to capture joint token dependencies. Pair with a RAD training strategy to enhance efficiency during training.
AI.result: ECHO surpasses state-of-the-art autoregressive methods in standard metrics (RaTE and SemScore) by 64.33% and 60.58%, respectively, and delivers an 8× reduction in inference latency while maintaining clinical accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The proposed DCD-enabled diffusion-based VLM with RAD training yields faster, more coherent CXR reports and outperforms autoregressive baselines, suggesting diffusion-based VLMs with joint-token supervision are promising for medical report generation.

title: Structure-Aware Fine-Grained Gaussian Splatting for Expressive Avatar Reconstruction
comment: The code is on Github: https://github.com/Su245811YZ/SFGS
summary: Reconstructing photorealistic and topology-aware human avatars from monocular videos remains a significant challenge in the fields of computer vision and graphics. While existing 3D human avatar modeling approaches can effectively capture body motion, they often fail to accurately model fine details such as hand movements and facial expressions. To address this, we propose Structure-aware Fine-grained Gaussian Splatting (SFGS), a novel method for reconstructing expressive and coherent full-body 3D human avatars from a monocular video sequence. The SFGS use both spatial-only triplane and time-aware hexplane to capture dynamic features across consecutive frames. A structure-aware gaussian module is designed to capture pose-dependent details in a spatially coherent manner and improve pose and texture expression. To better model hand deformations, we also propose a residual refinement module based on fine-grained hand reconstruction. Our method requires only a single-stage training and outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both quantitative and qualitative evaluations, generating high-fidelity avatars with natural motion and fine details. The code is on Github: https://github.com/Su245811YZ/SFGS

AI.tldr: Single-stage Structure-aware Fine-grained Gaussian Splatting (SFGS) reconstructs expressive full-body 3D human avatars from monocular video using spatial triplane and time-aware hexplane, with a structure-aware Gaussian module for pose-dependent detail and a residual hand refinement, outperforming SOTA baselines.
AI.motivation: Current 3D avatar methods lose fine details (hands, facial expressions) and struggle with temporal coherence from monocular video; there is a need for a single-stage, photorealistic, topology-aware full-body avatar reconstruction.
AI.method: SFGS combines a spatial-only triplane and a time-aware hexplane to capture dynamic features across frames. It introduces a structure-aware Gaussian module to model pose-dependent, spatially coherent details, and a residual refinement module for accurate hand deformations. The framework enables single-stage training from monocular video.
AI.result: The approach outperforms state-of-the-art baselines both quantitatively and qualitatively, producing high-fidelity avatars with natural motion and fine details.
AI.conclusion: SFGS provides an effective, single-stage pipeline for photorealistic, topology-aware full-body avatar reconstruction from monocular video, with improved pose-dependent details and hand fidelity; code is available on GitHub.

title: Is More Data Worth the Cost? Dataset Scaling Laws in a Tiny Attention-Only Decoder
comment: Presented as a paper at 3rd DATA-FM workshop @ ICLR 2026, Brazil. Published at 13th IEEE Swiss Conference on Data Science and AI (SDS 2026)
summary: Training Transformer language models is expensive, as performance typically improves with increasing dataset size and computational budget. Although scaling laws describe this trend at large scale, their implications in controlled, smaller-scale settings remain less explored. In this work, we isolate dataset-size effects using a strongly reduced attention-only decoder architecture. By training on progressively larger power-of-two subsets, we observe smooth performance improvements accompanied by clear diminishing returns, consistent with scaling-law behavior. Using only about 30% of the training data is sufficient to reach approximately 90% of the full-data validation token-level accuracy. These results provide actionable insights into dataset scaling in a controlled, component-isolated setting and offer practical guidance for balancing dataset size and computational cost in compute- and data-restricted environments, such as small research labs and exploratory model development.

AI.tldr: In a controlled study of dataset scaling, the paper shows scaling-law-like behavior in a reduced transformer setup: increasing dataset size yields smooth gains with diminishing returns, and ~30% of data yields ~90% of full-data validation token accuracy, informing data/computation trade-offs for small labs.
AI.motivation: Quantify how dataset size affects performance in a tightly controlled, small-scale setting to understand diminishing returns and guide data–compute trade-offs.
AI.method: Train an attention-only decoder transformer on progressively larger, power-of-two subsets of data, then evaluate on a token-level validation metric to isolate the effect of dataset size from model architecture and compute.
AI.result: Performance improves smoothly with dataset size and exhibits clear diminishing returns; ~30% of training data achieves ~90% of full-data validation accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Dataset scaling in this controlled setup follows a scaling-law-like pattern and provides practical guidance for balancing data size and compute in data- and compute-constrained environments.

title: SafeAdapt: Provably Safe Policy Updates in Deep Reinforcement Learning
summary: Safety guarantees are a prerequisite to the deployment of reinforcement learning (RL) agents in safety-critical tasks. Often, deployment environments exhibit non-stationary dynamics or are subject to changing performance goals, requiring updates to the learned policy. This leads to a fundamental challenge: how to update an RL policy while preserving its safety properties on previously encountered tasks? The majority of current approaches either do not provide formal guarantees or verify policy safety only a posteriori. We propose a novel a priori approach to safe policy updates in continual RL by introducing the Rashomon set: a region in policy parameter space certified to meet safety constraints within the demonstration data distribution. We then show that one can provide formal, provable guarantees for arbitrary RL algorithms used to update a policy by projecting their updates onto the Rashomon set. Empirically, we validate this approach across grid-world navigation environments (Frozen Lake and Poisoned Apple) where we guarantee an a priori provably deterministic safety on the source task during downstream adaptation. In contrast, we observe that regularisation-based baselines experience catastrophic forgetting of safety constraints while our approach enables strong adaptation with provable guarantees that safety is preserved.

AI.tldr: Safety-preserving continual RL via Rashomon set with a priori guarantees; project updates onto a set of safe policies; validated on grid-world tasks; outperforms regularization baselines that forget safety.
AI.motivation: Safety guarantees are essential for RL in safety-critical domains; non-stationarity requires policy updates while preserving prior safety properties; existing methods either lack formal guarantees or verify safety post hoc.
AI.method: Define Rashomon set as the region in policy parameter space that satisfies safety constraints under the demonstration data distribution. Provide a projection mechanism to enforce updates onto this set, yielding formal guarantees for any RL algorithm used to update the policy.
AI.result: Empirical validation on grid-world environments (Frozen Lake, Poisoned Apple) showing a priori provable deterministic safety during downstream adaptation; regularization-based baselines forget safety constraints, while the proposed approach preserves safety with strong adaptation.
AI.conclusion: Introduces a general, a priori safety-preserving update framework for continual RL via the Rashomon set; provides provable guarantees for downstream policy updates and demonstrates practical effectiveness against non-stationarity; potential for broader application and extension.

title: From Frames to Events: Rethinking Evaluation in Human-Centric Video Anomaly Detection
summary: Pose-based Video Anomaly Detection (VAD) has gained significant attention for its privacy-preserving nature and robustness to environmental variations. However, traditional frame-level evaluations treat video as a collection of isolated frames, fundamentally misaligned with how anomalies manifest and are acted upon in the real world. In operational surveillance systems, what matters is not the flagging of individual frames, but the reliable detection, localization, and reporting of a coherent anomalous event, a contiguous temporal episode with an identifiable onset and duration. Frame-level metrics are blind to this distinction, and as a result, they systematically overestimate model performance for any deployment that requires actionable, event-level alerts. In this work, we propose a shift toward an event-centric perspective in VAD. We first audit widely used VAD benchmarks, including SHT[19], CHAD[6], NWPUC[4], and HuVAD[25], to characterize their event structure. We then introduce two strategies for temporal event localization: a score-refinement pipeline with hierarchical Gaussian smoothing and adaptive binarization, and an end-to-end Dual-Branch Model that directly generates event-level detections. Finally, we establish the first event-based evaluation standard for VAD by adapting Temporal Action Localization metrics, including tIoU-based event matching and multi-threshold F1 evaluation. Our results quantify a substantial performance gap: while all SoTA models achieve frame-level AUC-ROC exceeding 52% on the NWPUC[4], their event-level localization precision falls below 10% even at a minimal tIoU=0.2, with an average event-level F1 of only 0.11 across all thresholds. The code base for this work is available at https://github.com/TeCSAR-UNCC/EventCentric-VAD.

AI.tldr: Event-centric evaluation for pose-based VAD reveals a large gap between frame-level and event-level performance; introduces two temporal localization strategies and the first event-based VAD standard using tIoU and multi-threshold F1; code released.
AI.motivation: Frame-level VAD metrics misrepresent real-world actionable performance because anomalies occur as contiguous events; auditing benchmarks is needed to understand event structure and drive end-to-end event localization.
AI.method: Audit VAD benchmarks (SHT, CHAD, NWPUC, HuVAD) to characterize event structure; propose two localization approaches: (1) score-refinement with hierarchical Gaussian smoothing and adaptive binarization; (2) end-to-end Dual-Branch Model for direct event-level detections; adapt Temporal Action Localization metrics for VAD: tIoU-based event matching and multi-threshold F1.
AI.result: Demonstrates substantial gap: frame-level AUC-ROC > 0.52 on NWPUC, but event-level precision < 0.10 at tIoU=0.2, average event-level F1 = 0.11; benchmarks and metrics reveal misalignment and room for improvement; codebase available at provided URL.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes the first event-based evaluation standard for VAD, highlights the need to shift from frame- to event-level reporting, and offers two methodological approaches along with an open-source codebase.

title: Efficient Unlearning through Maximizing Relearning Convergence Delay
summary: Machine unlearning poses challenges in removing mislabeled, contaminated, or problematic data from a pretrained model. Current unlearning approaches and evaluation metrics are solely focused on model predictions, which limits insight into the model's true underlying data characteristics. To address this issue, we introduce a new metric called relearning convergence delay, which captures both changes in weight space and prediction space, providing a more comprehensive assessment of the model's understanding of the forgotten dataset. This metric can be used to assess the risk of forgotten data being recovered from the unlearned model. Based on this, we propose the Influence Eliminating Unlearning framework, which removes the influence of the forgetting set by degrading its performance and incorporates weight decay and injecting noise into the model's weights, while maintaining accuracy on the retaining set. Extensive experiments show that our method outperforms existing metrics and our proposed relearning convergence delay metric, approaching ideal unlearning performance. We provide theoretical guarantees, including exponential convergence and upper bounds, as well as empirical evidence of strong retention and resistance to relearning in both classification and generative unlearning tasks.

AI.tldr: Introduce relearning convergence delay as a metric and the Influence Eliminating Unlearning framework to erase data influence, using weight decay and noise injection to achieve robust unlearning with theoretical guarantees.
AI.motivation: Current unlearning metrics only track prediction changes and ignore underlying data characteristics; a metric that captures weight-space and prediction-space changes is needed to assess forgotten-data recovery risk and genuine unlearning.
AI.method: Define relearning convergence delay (RCD) as a measure of time/effort for a model to revert to its pre-forgetting state in both weights and predictions. Propose Influence Eliminating Unlearning (IEU), which degrades forgetting-set influence by weight decay and injecting noise into weights, while preserving accuracy on the retaining set.
AI.result: Empirical results show IEU outperforms existing metrics and the proposed RCD, approaching ideal unlearning. The approach yields exponential convergence with upper bounds and demonstrates strong retention and resistance to relearning in both classification and generative tasks.
AI.conclusion: IEU provides a theoretically grounded, practically effective unlearning framework with a new, comprehensive metric for evaluating true unlearning, including stability guarantees across tasks.

title: OASIS: Online Activation Subspace Learning for Memory-Efficient Training
summary: Training large language models (LLMs) is constrained by memory requirements, with activations accounting for a substantial fraction of the total footprint. Existing approaches reduce memory using low-rank weight parameterizations or low-rank gradient subspaces for optimizer states, while activation memory is addressed through architectural modifications or compression schemes based on periodically updated projections. We propose OASIS, an online activation subspace learning algorithm for memory-efficient training that tracks and continuously updates a low-dimensional activation subspace during training. Intermediate activations are projected onto this evolving subspace, reducing memory without modifying forward-pass computations. The evolving activation subspace induces low-rank gradient representations, enabling both gradients and optimizer states to be maintained directly in this subspace, while a projection-aware optimizer consistently transports optimizer states across subspace updates for stable training. Across various finetuning and pretraining tasks, OASIS achieves up to $2\times$ lower peak memory than full fine-tuning while matching its performance and outperforming prior low-rank methods.

AI.tldr: OASIS is an online activation subspace learning method for memory-efficient LLM training that maintains a evolving low-dimensional activation subspace to project activations, enabling low-rank gradients and optimizer states; it achieves up to 2x peak memory reduction during finetuning with performance matching full fine-tuning and outperforming prior low-rank methods.
AI.motivation: Training LLMs is memory-bound, with activations a major contributor to peak memory. Existing strategies often target weights or optimizer states; activation memory remains challenging. An online, evolving activation subspace promises memory savings without altering forward computations.
AI.method: Maintain and continuously update a low-dimensional activation subspace during training. Project intermediate activations onto this subspace during the forward pass. Exploit the induced low-rank gradient representations to store gradients and optimizer states within the subspace. Use a projection-aware optimizer that transports optimizer states across subspace updates for stable training.
AI.result: Empirical results across multiple finetuning and pretraining tasks show up to 2× reduction in peak memory compared to full fine-tuning, while achieving performance on par with full fine-tuning and surpassing previous low-rank methods.
AI.conclusion: OASIS offers a memory-efficient training paradigm that preserves forward computations while enabling stable training through projection-aware optimization and subspace-aware parameter and gradient representations; it generalizes to standard finetuning and pretraining tasks and outperforms prior low-rank approaches.

title: VISOR: Agentic Visual Retrieval-Augmented Generation via Iterative Search and Over-horizon Reasoning
summary: Visual Retrieval-Augmented Generation (VRAG) empowers Vision-Language Models to retrieve and reason over visually rich documents. To tackle complex queries requiring multi-step reasoning, agentic VRAG systems interleave reasoning with iterative retrieval.. However, existing agentic VRAG faces two critical bottlenecks. (1) Visual Evidence Sparsity: key evidence is scattered across pages yet processed in isolation, hindering cross-page reasoning; moreover, fine-grained intra-image evidence often requires precise visual actions, whose misuse degrades retrieval quality; (2) Search Drift in Long Horizons: the accumulation of visual tokens across retrieved pages dilutes context and causes cognitive overload, leading agents to deviate from their search objective. To address these challenges, we propose VISOR (Visual Retrieval-Augmented Generation via Iterative Search and Over-horizon Reasoning), a unified single-agent framework. VISOR features a structured Evidence Space for progressive cross-page reasoning, coupled with a Visual Action Evaluation and Correction mechanism to manage visual actions. Additionally, we introduce a Dynamic Trajectory with Sliding Window and Intent Injection to mitigate search drift. They anchor the evidence space while discarding earlier raw interactions, preventing context from being overwhelmed by visual tokens. We train VISOR using a Group Relative Policy Optimization-based Reinforcement Learning (GRPO-based RL) pipeline with state masking and credit assignment tailored for dynamic context reconstruction. Extensive experiments on ViDoSeek, SlideVQA, and MMLongBench demonstrate that VISOR achieves state-of-the-art performance with superior efficiency for long-horizon visual reasoning tasks.

AI.tldr: VISOR is a single-agent VRAG framework that tackles long-horizon visual reasoning bottlenecks—visual evidence sparsity across pages and search drift due to accumulating visual tokens—by introducing a structured Evidence Space, Visual Action Evaluation and Correction, and a Dynamic Trajectory with Sliding Window and Intent Injection. Trained with GRPO-based RL, VISOR achieves state-of-the-art results and better efficiency on ViDoSeek, SlideVQA, and MMLongBench.
AI.motivation: The abstract identifies two core bottlenecks in agentic VRAG systems: (1) Visual Evidence Sparsity, where key cross-page evidence is scattered and fine-grained intra-image cues require precise actions, risking poor retrieval when actions are misused; (2) Search Drift in Long Horizons, where accumulating visual tokens across retrieved pages degrades context and overwhelms the agent, causing deviation from the objective.
AI.method: VISOR introduces a structured Evidence Space for progressive cross-page reasoning, a Visual Action Evaluation and Correction mechanism to supervise and rectify visual actions, and a Dynamic Trajectory with Sliding Window and Intent Injection to curb drift by anchoring evidence and discarding early raw interactions. The framework is trained with GRPO-based reinforcement learning using state masking and credit assignment tailored to dynamic context reconstruction.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on ViDoSeek, SlideVQA, and MMLongBench show VISOR achieves state-of-the-art performance with superior efficiency for long-horizon visual reasoning tasks, outperforming existing agentic VRAG approaches.
AI.conclusion: VISOR provides a unified single-agent solution that effectively addresses visual evidence sparsity and search drift in long-horizon VRAG, enabling robust cross-page reasoning with improved efficiency across diverse visually-rich reasoning benchmarks.

title: LuMon: A Comprehensive Benchmark and Development Suite with Novel Datasets for Lunar Monocular Depth Estimation
comment: This paper will be published in CVPRW2026
summary: Monocular Depth Estimation (MDE) is crucial for autonomous lunar rover navigation using electro-optical cameras. However, deploying terrestrial MDE networks to the Moon brings a severe domain gap due to harsh shadows, textureless regolith, and zero atmospheric scattering. Existing evaluations rely on analogs that fail to replicate these conditions and lack actual metric ground truth. To address this, we present LuMon, a comprehensive benchmarking framework to evaluate MDE methods for lunar exploration. We introduce novel datasets featuring high-quality stereo ground truth depth from the real Chang'e-3 mission and the CHERI dark analog dataset. Utilizing this framework, we conduct a systematic zero-shot evaluation of state-of-the-art architectures across synthetic, analog, and real datasets. We rigorously assess performance against mission critical challenges like craters, rocks, extreme shading, and varying depth ranges. Furthermore, we establish a sim-to-real domain adaptation baseline by fine tuning a foundation model on synthetic data. While this adaptation yields drastic in-domain performance gains, it exhibits minimal generalization to authentic lunar imagery, highlighting a persistent cross-domain transfer gap. Our extensive analysis reveals the inherent limitations of current networks and sets a standard foundation to guide future advancements in extraterrestrial perception and domain adaptation.

AI.tldr: LuMon: a lunar monocular depth estimation benchmarking framework with real Chang'e-3 stereo ground truth and CHERI analog data; zero-shot evaluation across synthetic, analog, real; sim-to-real adaptation shows in-domain gains but poor cross-domain generalization; sets baselines and reveals transfer gaps.
AI.motivation: Address the domain gap between terrestrial MDE models and lunar environments, where harsh shadows, textureless regolith, and absent atmosphere distort depth cues; lack of real ground-truth datasets and appropriate benchmarks hinders progress.
AI.method: Develop LuMon framework and datasets with real stereo depth from Chang'e-3 and CHERI analog scenes; perform zero-shot evaluation of state-of-the-art MDE architectures across synthetic, analog, and real datasets; assess key lunar challenges (craters, rocks, extreme shading, varying depth ranges); establish a sim-to-real baseline by fine-tuning a foundation model on synthetic data.
AI.result: In-domain performance gains from sim-to-real fine-tuning are substantial, but generalization to authentic lunar imagery is limited, indicating a persistent cross-domain transfer gap; extensive analysis reveals current networks' limitations and provides a standard benchmark for extraterrestrial perception and domain adaptation.
AI.conclusion: LuMon offers a standardized benchmark to evaluate MDE in lunar exploration, highlights the cross-domain transfer gap, and guides future research in extraterrestrial perception and domain adaptation.

title: NOMAD: Generating Embeddings for Massive Distributed Graphs
summary:
Successful machine learning on graphs or networks requires embeddings that not only represent nodes and edges as low-dimensional vectors but also preserve the graph structure. Established methods for generating embeddings require flexible exploration of the entire graph through repeated use of random walks that capture graph structure with samples of nodes and edges. These methods create scalability challenges for massive graphs with millions-to-billions of edges because single-node solutions have inadequate memory and processing capabilities.
  We present NOMAD, a distributed-memory graph embedding framework using the Message Passing Interface (MPI) for distributed graphs. NOMAD implements proximity-based models proposed in the widely popular LINE (Large-scale Information Network Embedding) algorithm. We propose several practical trade-offs to improve the scalability and communication overheads confronted by irregular and distributed graph embedding methods, catering to massive-scale graphs arising in web and science domains. NOMAD demonstrates median speedups of 10/100x on CPU-based NERSC Perlmutter cluster relative to the popular reference implementations of multi-threaded LINE and node2vec, 35-76x over distributed PBG, and competitive embedding quality relative to LINE, node2vec, and GraphVite, while yielding 12-370x end-to-end speedups on real-world graphs.

AI.tldr: NOMAD delivers scalable, distributed-memory graph embeddings by implementing LINE in MPI, achieving large speedups with competitive embedding quality on massive graphs.
AI.motivation: Existing random-walk-based embeddings struggle with memory and compute on graphs with millions-to-billions of edges; need scalable, distributed solutions.
AI.method: NOMAD uses a distributed-memory MPI framework, implements LINE-based proximity models, and introduces practical trade-offs to reduce communication and handle irregular distributed graphs, enabling massive-scale graph embedding.
AI.result: Median speedups: 10–100x vs CPU-based LINE/node2vec on NERSC Perlmutter; 35–76x vs distributed PBG; 12–370x end-to-end on real graphs; embedding quality competitive with LINE, node2vec, GraphVite.
AI.conclusion: NOMAD provides scalable, efficient graph embeddings for very large graphs with competitive accuracy; framework addresses memory/communication bottlenecks; further work could broaden model types and streaming updates.

title: Envisioning the Future, One Step at a Time
comment: CVPR 2026. For code and models, see http://compvis.github.io/myriad
summary: Accurately anticipating how complex, diverse scenes will evolve requires models that represent uncertainty, simulate along extended interaction chains, and efficiently explore many plausible futures. Yet most existing approaches rely on dense video or latent-space prediction, expending substantial capacity on dense appearance rather than on the underlying sparse trajectories of points in the scene. This makes large-scale exploration of future hypotheses costly and limits performance when long-horizon, multi-modal motion is essential. We address this by formulating the prediction of open-set future scene dynamics as step-wise inference over sparse point trajectories. Our autoregressive diffusion model advances these trajectories through short, locally predictable transitions, explicitly modeling the growth of uncertainty over time. This dynamics-centric representation enables fast rollout of thousands of diverse futures from a single image, optionally guided by initial constraints on motion, while maintaining physical plausibility and long-range coherence. We further introduce OWM, a benchmark for open-set motion prediction based on diverse in-the-wild videos, to evaluate accuracy and variability of predicted trajectory distributions under real-world uncertainty. Our method matches or surpasses dense simulators in predictive accuracy while achieving orders-of-magnitude higher sampling speed, making open-set future prediction both scalable and practical. Project page: http://compvis.github.io/myriad.

AI.tldr: Open-set future scene prediction via sparse point trajectories using an autoregressive diffusion model; enables fast, diverse long-horizon forecasts; introduces OWM benchmark; achieves competitive accuracy with dense simulators at much higher sampling speed.
AI.motivation: Dense video or latent-space predictions overuse capacity on dense appearance, hindering long-horizon, multi-modal motion exploration; need a dynamics-centric, uncertainty-aware representation that is scalable.
AI.method: Formulates prediction as step-wise inference over sparse point trajectories using an autoregressive diffusion model. Transitions are short and locally predictable, with explicit modeling of uncertainty growth over time. Enables fast rollout of thousands of futures from a single image, optionally guided by initial motion constraints, while preserving physical plausibility and long-range coherence.
AI.result: The method matches or surpasses dense simulators in predictive accuracy while offering orders-of-magnitude faster sampling. Introduces OWM, a benchmark for open-set motion prediction on diverse in-the-wild videos to evaluate trajectory-distribution accuracy and variability.
AI.conclusion: A dynamics-centric, sparse-trajectory representation enables scalable open-set future prediction. The approach achieves practical predictive performance with much higher sampling efficiency, validated against a real-world benchmark (OWM).

title: Arbitration Failure, Not Perceptual Blindness: How Vision-Language Models Resolve Visual-Linguistic Conflicts
summary: When a Vision-Language Model (VLM) sees a blue banana and answers "yellow", is the problem of perception or arbitration? We explore the question in ten VLMs with various sizes and reveal an Encoding--Grounding Dissociation: models that fail to report what they see (and thus provide a wrong answer) still encode the visual evidence as strongly as models that provide the correct answer. Using Multimodal Arbitration Crossover (MAC) analysis with layer-by-layer Logit Lens probing, we track the competition between visual and prior signals across every layer of each model. We show that visual attributes can be linearly decodable from early layers (AUC > 0.86). The accuracy remains nearly identical for both successful and failed samples. However, the gap in the final-layer logit -- not the strength of encoding -- better predicts grounding outcomes with a correlation of . After having studied when VLMs base their answers on image clues rather than prior knowledge, we want to understand the causal relationships. We establish causality through full-sequence activation patching. The standard last-token interventions in LLM interpretability do not affect VLMs. In contrast, replacing the full token sequence at layers identified by MAC alters 60 to 84% of outputs. Partial-token decomposition shows that image tokens carry almost all of the causal impact, while text tokens have none. Scaling addresses the remaining architectural differences to achieve perfect retention. Moving from diagnosis to intervention, we show that training-free activation steering -- both linear and sparse autoencoder-guided -- in early layers can improve visual grounding by up to +3.8% with degrading performance in some setups. Overall, these findings lead to a clear conclusion: VLMs already see well, but the challenge is acting on what they see. Targeted interventions can help to bridge this gap.

AI.tldr: VLMs encode visual evidence strongly yet often do not ground to it, revealing an Encoding–Grounding Dissociation. Across ten models, early visual cues are decodable, but decisions rely on post-hoc arbitration. Using MAC analysis and layerwise Logit Lens, the final-layer logit gap best predicts grounding failures. Activation patching shows image tokens carry most casual impact, while text tokens do little. Training-free activation steering in early layers can improve grounding up to 3.8%, suggesting perception is intact but action on perception requires targeted interventions.
AI.motivation: To disentangle whether failures in VLMs stem from perceptual encoding or arbitration/decision processes, quantify how visual and prior information compete across layers, and explore interventions to align perception with action.
AI.method: Evaluate ten VLMs of varying sizes. Apply Multimodal Arbitration Crossover (MAC) analysis with layer-by-layer Logit Lens probing to track competition between visual and prior signals. Use full-sequence activation patching for causal analysis; perform partial-token decomposition; test training-free linear and sparse autoencoder-guided activation steering, then scale architectures to ensure robust effects.
AI.result: Visual attributes are decodable in early layers (AUC > 0.86). Accuracy is similar for correct and failed samples, indicating encoding strength does not differentiate outcomes. The final-layer logit gap strongly correlates with grounding outcomes. Full-sequence activation patching shows image tokens carry 60–84% of the causal impact; last-token interventions fail to affect VLMs, whereas image tokens drive most causality. Activation steering in early layers yields up to +3.8% grounding improvement in some setups; scaling helps retain effects.
AI.conclusion: VLMs already perceive well, but bridging perception to action requires targeted interventions. Training-free, early-layer activation steering can improve visual grounding, moving from diagnosis to practical intervention.

title: Offline Local Search for Online Stochastic Bandits
comment: Part of this work has been accepted at ACM SIGMETRICS 2026
summary: Combinatorial multi-armed bandits provide a fundamental online decision-making environment where a decision-maker interacts with an environment across $T$ time steps, each time selecting an action and learning the cost of that action. The goal is to minimize regret, defined as the loss compared to the optimal fixed action in hindsight under full-information. There has been substantial interest in leveraging what is known about offline algorithm design in this online setting. Offline greedy and linear optimization algorithms (both exact and approximate) have been shown to provide useful guarantees when deployed online. We investigate local search methods, a broad class of algorithms used widely in both theory and practice, which have thus far been under-explored in this context. We focus on problems where offline local search terminates in an approximately optimal solution and give a generic method for converting such an offline algorithm into an online stochastic combinatorial bandit algorithm with $O(\log^3 T)$ (approximate) regret. In contrast, existing offline-to-online frameworks yield regret (and approximate regret) which depend sub-linearly, but polynomially on $T$. We demonstrate the flexibility of our framework by applying it to three online stochastic combinatorial optimization problems: scheduling to minimize total completion time, finding a minimum cost base of a matroid and uncertain clustering.

AI.tldr: Generic offline-to-online reduction for local-search based offline algorithms in stochastic combinatorial bandits yields O(log^3 T) approximate regret; demonstrated on scheduling, matroid base, and uncertain clustering.
AI.motivation: Leverage offline local search guarantees to improve online regret; address limitations of previous offline-to-online frameworks that incur polynomial-in-T regret; exploit that local search terminates at approx-opt solutions.
AI.method: Develop a generic framework converting offline local search termination into online bandit policy; analyze regret under full-information; apply to three problems; derive O(log^3 T) approximate regret.
AI.result: The framework achieves O(log^3 T) approximate regret; shows online policies with local-search offline starting points; demonstrates applicability to scheduling, matroid base, uncertain clustering.
AI.conclusion: Local search methods can be effectively imported into online combinatorial bandits; the offline-to-online reduction is flexible and broadly applicable; potential for further problems.

title: VL-Calibration: Decoupled Confidence Calibration for Large Vision-Language Models Reasoning
summary: Large Vision Language Models (LVLMs) achieve strong multimodal reasoning but frequently exhibit hallucinations and incorrect responses with high certainty, which hinders their usage in high-stakes domains. Existing verbalized confidence calibration methods, largely developed for text-only LLMs, typically optimize a single holistic confidence score using binary answer-level correctness. This design is mismatched to LVLMs: an incorrect prediction may arise from perceptual failures or from reasoning errors given correct perception, and a single confidence conflates these sources while visual uncertainty is often dominated by language priors. To address these issues, we propose VL-Calibration, a reinforcement learning framework that explicitly decouples confidence into visual and reasoning confidence. To supervise visual confidence without ground-truth perception labels, we introduce an intrinsic visual certainty estimation that combines (i) visual grounding measured by KL-divergence under image perturbations and (ii) internal certainty measured by token entropy. We further propose token-level advantage reweighting to focus optimization on tokens based on visual certainty, suppressing ungrounded hallucinations while preserving valid perception. Experiments on thirteen benchmarks show that VL-Calibration effectively improves calibration while boosting visual reasoning accuracy, and it generalizes to out-of-distribution benchmarks across model scales and architectures.

AI.tldr: VL-Calibration introduces a reinforcement learning framework that decouples confidence into visual and reasoning components for LVLMs, using intrinsic visual certainty estimation (KL-divergence under perturbations plus token entropy) and token-level advantage reweighting to suppress hallucinations while preserving perception. It demonstrates improved calibration and visual reasoning accuracy across 13 benchmarks and generalizes across scales and architectures.
AI.motivation: LVLMs often give high-certainty but incorrect responses due to hallucinations that arise from perceptual failures or reasoning errors. Existing text-centric calibration optimizes a single holistic confidence score, which conflates the sources of error and underutilizes visual uncertainty; thus, a decoupled, multimodal confidence calibration is needed.
AI.method: Propose VL-Calibration: a reinforcement learning framework that separately optimizes visual confidence and reasoning confidence. Visual confidence is supervised via intrinsic estimation combining (i) visual grounding measured by KL-divergence under image perturbations and (ii) internal certainty measured by token entropy. A token-level advantage reweighting mechanism focuses optimization on tokens with higher visual certainty, reducing ungrounded hallucinations while preserving perceptual integrity.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on thirteen benchmarks shows improved calibration and boosted visual reasoning accuracy. The method generalizes to out-of-distribution benchmarks across different LVLM scales and architectures.
AI.conclusion: VL-Calibration provides an effective decoupled confidence calibration framework for LVLMs, yielding better calibrated outputs and improved visual reasoning while remaining robust across model sizes and architectures.

title: Robust 4D Visual Geometry Transformer with Uncertainty-Aware Priors
summary: Reconstructing dynamic 4D scenes is an important yet challenging task. While 3D foundation models like VGGT excel in static settings, they often struggle with dynamic sequences where motion causes significant geometric ambiguity. To address this, we present a framework designed to disentangle dynamic and static components by modeling uncertainty across different stages of the reconstruction process. Our approach introduces three synergistic mechanisms: (1) Entropy-Guided Subspace Projection, which leverages information-theoretic weighting to adaptively aggregate multi-head attention distributions, effectively isolating dynamic motion cues from semantic noise; (2) Local-Consistency Driven Geometry Purification, which enforces spatial continuity via radius-based neighborhood constraints to eliminate structural outliers; and (3) Uncertainty-Aware Cross-View Consistency, which formulates multi-view projection refinement as a heteroscedastic maximum likelihood estimation problem, utilizing depth confidence as a probabilistic weight. Experiments on dynamic benchmarks show that our approach outperforms current state-of-the-art methods, reducing Mean Accuracy error by 13.43\% and improving segmentation F-measure by 10.49\%. Our framework maintains the efficiency of feed-forward inference and requires no task-specific fine-tuning or per-scene optimization.

AI.tldr: A three-pronged framework for disentangling dynamic and static components in 4D scene reconstruction, improving accuracy and segmentation while preserving feed-forward efficiency.
AI.motivation: Dynamic sequences introduce significant geometric ambiguity that static 3D foundation models struggle to resolve; there is a need to separate motion cues from static structure under uncertainty.
AI.method: Entropy-Guided Subspace Projection to adaptively weight multi-head attention distributions for dynamic cue isolation; Local-Consistency Driven Geometry Purification using radius-based neighborhood constraints to enforce spatial continuity; Uncertainty-Aware Cross-View Consistency formulated as heteroscedastic maximum likelihood estimation with depth confidence as a probabilistic weight.
AI.result: On dynamic benchmarks, the method reduces Mean Accuracy error by 13.43% and improves segmentation F-measure by 10.49%, while maintaining feed-forward efficiency and requiring no task-specific fine-tuning or per-scene optimization.
AI.conclusion: The framework advances dynamic 4D reconstruction by effectively separating motion cues, purifying geometry, and leveraging uncertainty-aware cross-view refinement; demonstrated gains on dynamic benchmarks with practical efficiency.

title: AdaCubic: An Adaptive Cubic Regularization Optimizer for Deep Learning
summary: A novel regularization technique, AdaCubic, is proposed that adapts the weight of the cubic term. The heart of AdaCubic is an auxiliary optimization problem with cubic constraints that dynamically adjusts the weight of the cubic term in Newton's cubic regularized method. We use Hutchinson's method to approximate the Hessian matrix, thereby reducing computational cost. We demonstrate that AdaCubic inherits the cubically regularized Newton method's local convergence guarantees. Our experiments in Computer Vision, Natural Language Processing, and Signal Processing tasks demonstrate that AdaCubic outperforms or competes with several widely used optimizers.
