Unlike other adaptive algorithms that require hyperparameter fine-tuning, AdaCubic is evaluated with a fixed set of hyperparameters, rendering it a highly attractive optimizer in settings where fine-tuning is infeasible. This makes AdaCubic an attractive option for researchers and practitioners alike. To our knowledge, AdaCubic is the first optimizer to leverage cubic regularization in scalable deep learning applications.

AI.tldr: AdaCubic is a cubic-regularized Newton optimizer that adaptively weights the cubic term via an auxiliary optimization problem, using Hutchinson's Hessian approximation for scalability. It preserves local convergence guarantees, requires fixed hyperparameters, and shows competitive performance across CV, NLP, and signal processing tasks in scalable DL settings.
AI.motivation: Reduce sensitivity to hyperparameter tuning in cubic-regularized Newton methods and improve scalability for deep learning by adaptively controlling the cubic term weight while maintaining convergence guarantees.
AI.method: Introduce an auxiliary optimization problem with cubic constraints to dynamically adjust the cubic term weight; apply Hutchinson's method to approximate the Hessian for scalability; establish that AdaCubic inherits the local convergence guarantees of cubic-regularized Newton methods; validate on CV, NLP, and Signal Processing tasks with fixed hyperparameters.
AI.result: Empirical results show AdaCubic either outperforms or matches several widely used optimizers across the tested domains; the algorithm does not require hyperparameter tuning and is scalable due to HessianApproximation, making it attractive for research and practice.
AI.conclusion: AdaCubic is presented as the first optimizer to leverage cubic regularization in scalable deep learning, offering automatic cubic-term weighting, fixed hyperparameters, and solid convergence guarantees with competitive performance.

title: VisionFoundry: Teaching VLMs Visual Perception with Synthetic Images
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) still struggle with visual perception tasks such as spatial understanding and viewpoint recognition. One plausible contributing factor is that natural image datasets provide limited supervision for low-level visual skills. This motivates a practical question: can targeted synthetic supervision, generated from only a task keyword such as Depth Order, address these weaknesses? To investigate this question, we introduce VisionFoundry, a task-aware synthetic data generation pipeline that takes only the task name as input and uses large language models (LLMs) to generate questions, answers, and text-to-image (T2I) prompts, then synthesizes images with T2I models and verifies consistency with a proprietary VLM, requiring no reference images or human annotation. Using VisionFoundry, we construct VisionFoundry-10K, a synthetic visual question answering (VQA) dataset containing 10k image-question-answer triples spanning 10 tasks. Models trained on VisionFoundry-10K achieve substantial improvements on visual perception benchmarks: +7% on MMVP and +10% on CV-Bench-3D, while preserving broader capabilities and showing favorable scaling behavior as data size increases. Our results suggest that limited task-targeted supervision is an important contributor to this bottleneck and that synthetic supervision is a promising path toward more systematic training for VLMs.

AI.tldr: VisionFoundry proposes a synthetic, task-aware data generation pipeline driven by LLMs to create VQA data from task keywords, improving visual perception in VLMs by training on synthetic 10K dataset; shows gains on MMVP +7 and CV-Bench-3D +10.
AI.motivation: Address the bottleneck where natural image datasets provide limited supervision for low-level visual skills; investigate whether targeted synthetic supervision from task keywords can substantially improve VLM visual perception tasks.
AI.method: Use a task-aware synthetic data pipeline (VisionFoundry) that uses LLMs to generate questions, answers, and T2I prompts from only the task name; synthesize images with text-to-image models; verify consistency with a proprietary VLM without reference images or human annotation; create VisionFoundry-10K (10k VQA triples across 10 tasks); train models on this data.
AI.result: Pretraining/fine-tuning on VisionFoundry-10K yields substantial improvements on visual perception benchmarks: +7% on MMVP and +10% on CV-Bench-3D; shows favorable scaling with data size and preserves broader capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Limited, task-targeted synthetic supervision can mitigate bottlenecks in VLM visual perception skills; synthetic supervision is a promising direction for more systematic VLM training.

title: EpiAgent: An Agent-Centric System for Ancient Inscription Restoration
summary: Ancient inscriptions, as repositories of cultural memory, have suffered from centuries of environmental and human-induced degradation. Restoring their intertwined visual and textual integrity poses one of the most demanding challenges in digital heritage preservation. However, existing AI-based approaches often rely on rigid pipelines, struggling to generalize across such complex and heterogeneous real-world degradations. Inspired by the skill-coordinated workflow of human epigraphers, we propose EpiAgent, an agent-centric system that formulates inscription restoration as a hierarchical planning problem. Following an Observe-Conceive-Execute-Reevaluate paradigm, an LLM-based central planner orchestrates collaboration among multimodal analysis, historical experience, specialized restoration tools, and iterative self-refinement. This agent-centric coordination enables a flexible and adaptive restoration process beyond conventional single-pass methods. Across real-world degraded inscriptions, EpiAgent achieves superior restoration quality and stronger generalization compared to existing methods. Our work marks an important step toward expert-level agent-driven restoration of cultural heritage. The code is available at https://github.com/blackprotoss/EpiAgent.

AI.tldr: EpiAgent uses an agent-based, hierarchical planning framework to restore degraded inscriptions, combining LLM-driven planning with multimodal analysis, restoration tools, and iterative self-refinement for flexible, generalizable restoration beyond single-pass pipelines.
AI.motivation: Ancient inscriptions suffer complex, heterogeneous degradation that standard AI pipelines struggle to generalize or adapt to; emulate expert epigraphers by coordinating specialized analysis and restoration steps through an agent.
AI.method: Observe-Conceive-Execute-Reevaluate loop with an LLM-based central planner coordinating multimodal analysis, historical experience, restoration tools, and iterative self-refinement in a hierarchical planning architecture.
AI.result: Demonstrates superior restoration quality and generalization on real-world degraded inscriptions compared to existing methods (no numeric metrics provided in abstract); code released at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: Agent-centric, hierarchical planning enables flexible, expert-level restoration of cultural heritage inscriptions and generalizes across diverse degradations; marks a step toward scalable agent-driven preservation; code available.

title: Seeing is Believing: Robust Vision-Guided Cross-Modal Prompt Learning under Label Noise
summary: Prompt learning is a parameter-efficient approach for vision-language models, yet its robustness under label noise is less investigated. Visual content contains richer and more reliable semantic information, which remains more robust under label noise. However, the prompt itself is highly susceptible to label noise. Motivated by this intuition, we propose VisPrompt, a lightweight and robust vision-guided prompt learning framework for noisy-label settings. Specifically, we exploit a cross-modal attention mechanism to reversely inject visual semantics into prompt representations. This enables the prompt tokens to selectively aggregate visual information relevant to the current sample, thereby improving robustness by anchoring prompt learning to stable instance-level visual evidence and reducing the influence of noisy supervision. To address the instability caused by using the same way of injecting visual information for all samples, despite differences in the quality of their visual cues, we further introduce a lightweight conditional modulation mechanism to adaptively control the strength of visual information injection, which strikes a more robust balance between text-side semantic priors and image-side instance evidence. The proposed framework effectively suppresses the noise-induced disturbances, reduce instability in prompt updates, and alleviate memorization of mislabeled samples. VisPrompt significantly improves robustness while keeping the pretrained VLM backbone frozen and introducing only a small amount of additional trainable parameters. Extensive experiments under synthetic and real-world label noise demonstrate that VisPrompt generally outperforms existing baselines on seven benchmark datasets and achieves stronger robustness. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/gezbww/Vis_Prompt.

AI.tldr: VisPrompt: a lightweight, robust vision-guided prompt learning method for noisy-label settings in vision-language models. It uses cross-modal attention to inject visual semantics into prompt representations and a conditional modulation mechanism to adapt injection strength per sample, keeping the backbone frozen. It improves robustness against label noise across seven datasets with minimal extra parameters.
AI.motivation: Prompt-based tuning of vision-language models is efficient but highly sensitive to label noise. Visual content provides stable, instance-level semantic cues that can anchor prompts, suggesting a method that fuses visual information into prompts to enhance robustness.
AI.method: Introduce VisPrompt, which employs a cross-modal attention mechanism to inject visual semantics into prompt tokens, enabling selective aggregation of image information relevant to each sample. Add a lightweight conditional modulation module to adaptively control the strength of visual information injection per sample. Freeze the VLM backbone and keep the additional trainable parameters minimal.
AI.result: VisPrompt substantially improves robustness under synthetic and real-world label noise, outperforming existing baselines on seven benchmark datasets while maintaining a frozen backbone and few extra parameters. Experiments demonstrate reduced noise-induced disturbances, greater stability in prompt updates, and less memorization of mislabeled samples. Code is released.
AI.conclusion: VisPrompt effectively anchors prompt learning to stable instance-level visual evidence and adapts injection strength to sample quality, achieving robust prompt learning under noisy labels with minimal computational overhead and without fine-tuning the VLM backbone.

title: Region-Constrained Group Relative Policy Optimization for Flow-Based Image Editing
summary: Instruction-guided image editing requires balancing target modification with non-target preservation. Recently, flow-based models have emerged as a strong and increasingly adopted backbone for instruction-guided image editing, thanks to their high fidelity and efficient deterministic ODE sampling. Building on this foundation, GRPO-based reward-driven post-training has been explored to directly optimize editing-specific rewards, improving instruction following and editing consistency. However, existing methods often suffer from noisy credit assignment: global exploration also perturbs non-target regions, inflating within-group reward variance and yielding noisy GRPO advantages. To address this, we propose RC-GRPO-Editing, a region-constrained GRPO post-training framework for flow-based image editing under deterministic ODE sampling. It suppresses background-induced nuisance variance to enable cleaner localized credit assignment, improving editing region instruction adherence while preserving non-target content. Concretely, we localize exploration via region-decoupled initial noise perturbations to reduce background-induced reward variance and stabilize GRPO advantages, and introduce an attention concentration reward that aligns cross-attention with the intended editing region throughout the rollout, reducing unintended changes in non-target regions. Experiments on CompBench show consistent improvements in editing region instruction adherence and non-target preservation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RC-GRPO-Editing, a region-constrained GRPO post-training method for flow-based image editing under deterministic ODE sampling. It localizes exploration to the editing region and adds an attention concentration reward to reduce non-target changes, yielding cleaner credit assignment and better adherence to editing instructions while preserving non-target content. Evaluated on CompBench, it shows improved editing region adherence and non-target preservation.
AI.motivation: Instruction-guided image editing must modify the target region while preserving non-target areas. Global exploration in GRPO-based post-training introduces background variance that degrades credit assignment and editing fidelity. A region-aware approach is needed to stabilize learning and improve region-specific adherence.
AI.method: RC-GRPO-Editing uses region-decoupled initial noise perturbations to localize exploration, reducing background-induced reward variance. It introduces an attention concentration reward that aligns cross-attention with the intended editing region during rollout, minimizing unintended non-target changes.
AI.result: Experimental results on CompBench show consistent improvements in editing region instruction adherence and non-target preservation compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: Region-constrained exploration and attention-focused rewards stabilize GRPO post-training for flow-based editing, enabling cleaner credit assignment and better target-region fidelity without sacrificing non-target content.

title: EGLOCE: Training-Free Energy-Guided Latent Optimization for Concept Erasure
summary: As text-to-image diffusion models grow increasingly prevalent, the ability to remove specific concepts-mostly explicit content and many copyrighted characters or styles-has become essential for safety and compliance. Existing unlearning approaches often require costly re-training, modify parameters at the cost of degradation of unrelated concept fidelity, or depend on indirect inference-time adjustment that compromise the effectiveness of concept erasure. Inspired by the success of energy-guided sampling for preservation of the condition of diffusion models, we introduce Energy-Guided Latent Optimization for Concept Erasure (EGLOCE), a training-free approach that removes unwanted concepts by re-directing noisy latent during inference. Our method employs a dual-objective framework: a repulsion energy that steers generation away from target concepts via gradient descent in latent space, and a retention energy that preserves semantic alignment to the original prompt. Combined with previous approaches that either require erroneous modified model weights or provide weak inference-time guidance, EGLOCE operates entirely at inference and enhances erasure performance, enabling plug-and-play integration. Extensive experiments demonstrate that EGLOCE improves concept removal while maintaining image quality and prompt alignment across baselines, even with adversarial attacks. To the best of our knowledge, our work is the first to establish a new paradigm for safe and controllable image generation through dual energy-based guidance during sampling.

AI.tldr: Training-free energy-guided latent optimization (EGLOCE) for concept erasure in text-to-image diffusion models, enabling inference-time removal of unwanted concepts via dual-energy guidance (repulsion from target concepts and retention of prompt semantics) with improved erasure, image quality, and prompt alignment; plug-and-play and robust to adversarial perturbations.
AI.motivation: To address safety/compliance concerns in diffusion models by removing specific concepts (e.g., explicit content, copyrighted styles) without costly re-training or degrading unrelated concepts, and without relying on inference-time post-processing that undermines erasure effectiveness.
AI.method: Introduces Energy-Guided Latent Optimization for Concept Erasure (EGLOCE), a training-free approach that operates during sampling. It uses a dual-objective energy framework: a repulsion energy that gradient-descent-reorients the noisy latent away from target concepts, and a retention energy that preserves alignment to the original prompt. This energy-based guidance is applied in latent space during inference, and can be integrated with existing models without modifying weights.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation shows that EGLOCE enhances concept erasure while maintaining image quality and prompt alignment across baselines, including resilience to adversarial attacks.
AI.conclusion: EGLOCE establishes a new inference-time, dual-energy-guided sampling paradigm for safe and controllable image generation, offering a training-free, plug-and-play solution that improves erasure performance without sacrificing fidelity.

title: Integrated electro-optic attention nonlinearities for transformers
summary: Transformers have emerged as the dominant neural-network architecture, achieving state-of-the-art performance in language processing and computer vision. At the core of these models lies the attention mechanism, which requires a nonlinear, non-negative mapping using the Softmax function. However, although Softmax operations account for less than 1% of the total operation count, they can disproportionately bottleneck overall inference latency. Here, we use thin-film lithium niobate (TFLN) Mach-Zehnder modulators (MZMs) as analog nonlinear computational elements to drastically reduce the latency of nonlinear computations. We implement electro-optic alternatives to digital Softmax and Sigmoid, and evaluate their performance in Vision Transformers and Large Language Models. Our system maintains highly competitive accuracy, even under aggressive 4-bit input-output quantization of the analog units. We further characterize system noise at encoding speeds up to 10 GBaud and assess model robustness under various noise conditions. Our findings suggest that TFLN modulators can serve as nonlinear function units within hybrid co-packaged hardware, enabling high-speed and energy-efficient nonlinear computation.

AI.tldr: Thin-film lithium niobate modulators enable analog nonlinear computation to replace Softmax/Sigmoid in transformers, reducing latency with minimal accuracy loss and robust performance under low-bit quantization.
AI.motivation: Softmax-like nonlinearities are a small portion of compute but can bottleneck latency in transformers; there is a need for faster, energy-efficient nonlinear units, particularly for co-packaged photonic-hybrid accelerators.
AI.method: Implement electro-optic equivalents of Softmax and Sigmoid using thin-film LiNbO3 Mach-Zehnder modulators as analog nonlinear units; evaluate in Vision Transformers (ViT) and Large Language Models (LLMs); test under aggressive 4-bit input/output quantization; characterize system noise up to 10 GBaud; assess robustness.
AI.result: Analog nonlinear units with 4-bit quantization maintain competitive accuracy; nonlinear computation latency is reduced; system noise is characterized and manageable; viability demonstrated for hybrid co-packaged hardware.
AI.conclusion: Thin-film LiNbO3 modulators can serve as fast, energy-efficient nonlinear function units enabling high-speed transformers within hybrid photonic-digital hardware.

title: SynFlow: Scaling Up LiDAR Scene Flow Estimation with Synthetic Data
summary: Reliable 3D dynamic perception requires models that can anticipate motion beyond predefined categories, yet progress is hindered by the scarcity of dense, high-quality motion annotations. While self-supervision on unlabeled real data offers a path forward, empirical evidence suggests that scaling unlabeled data fails to close the performance gap due to noisy proxy signals. In this paper, we propose a shift in paradigm: learning robust real-world motion priors entirely from scalable simulation. We introduce SynFlow, a data generation pipeline that generates large-scale synthetic dataset specifically designed for LiDAR scene flow. Unlike prior works that prioritize sensor-specific realism, SynFlow employs a motion-oriented strategy to synthesize diverse kinematic patterns across 4,000 sequences ($\sim$940k frames), termed SynFlow-4k. This represents a 34x scale-up in annotated volume over existing real-world benchmarks. Our experiments demonstrate that SynFlow-4k provides a highly domain-invariant motion prior. In a zero-shot regime, models trained exclusively on our synthetic data generalize across multiple real-world benchmarks, rivaling in-domain supervised baselines on nuScenes and outperforming state-of-the-art methods on TruckScenes by 31.8%. Furthermore, SynFlow-4k serves as a label-efficient foundation: fine-tuning with only 5% of real-world labels surpasses models trained from scratch on the full available budget. We open-source the pipeline and dataset to facilitate research in generalizable 3D motion estimation. More detail can be found at https://kin-zhang.github.io/SynFlow.

AI.tldr: SynFlow is a synthetic data pipeline for LiDAR scene flow, generating SynFlow-4k (≈940k frames) to learn robust real-world motion priors; zero-shot real-world generalization; label-efficient fine-tuning with 5% real labels; open-source.
AI.motivation: Dense motion annotations are scarce; self-supervision on unlabeled data is hindered by noisy proxy signals; scalable simulation offers a path to robust, diverse motion priors that transfer to real data.
AI.method: Motion-oriented synthetic data generation across 4k sequences (≈940k frames) focused on diverse kinematic patterns rather than sensor realism; 34x annotated-volume scale-up; dataset SynFlow-4k for LiDAR scene flow; zero-shot evaluation on real benchmarks; open-source release.
AI.result: Models trained solely on synthetic data generalize to real-world benchmarks in a zero-shot regime; rival in-domain supervised baselines on nuScenes; outperform SOTA on TruckScenes by 31.8%; fine-tuning with 5% of real labels surpasses models trained from scratch on full budget.
AI.conclusion: SynFlow-4k provides a highly domain-invariant motion prior and a label-efficient foundation for 3D motion estimation; open-sourced to catalyze research in generalizable LiDAR scene flow.

title: Toward World Models for Epidemiology
summary: World models have emerged as a unifying paradigm for learning latent dynamics, simulating counterfactual futures, and supporting planning under uncertainty. In this paper, we argue that computational epidemiology is a natural and underdeveloped setting for world models. This is because epidemic decision-making requires reasoning about latent disease burden, imperfect and policy-dependent surveillance signals, and intervention effects are mediated by adaptive human behavior. We introduce a conceptual framework for epidemiological world models, formulating epidemics as controlled, partially observed dynamical systems in which (i) the true epidemic state is latent, (ii) observations are noisy and endogenous to policy, and (iii) interventions act as sequential actions whose effects propagate through behavioral and social feedback. We present three case studies that illustrate why explicit world modeling is necessary for policy-relevant reasoning: strategic misreporting in behavioral surveillance, systematic delays in time-lagged signals such as hospitalizations and deaths, and counterfactual intervention analysis where identical histories diverge under alternative action sequences.

AI.tldr: Proposes epidemiological world models: treat epidemics as controlled, partially observed dynamical systems and demonstrates via three case studies why explicit world modeling is needed for policy decisions.
AI.motivation: Epidemics involve latent disease burden, noisy and policy-dependent surveillance, and intervention effects mediated by adaptive human behavior; existing models struggle with counterfactual reasoning and uncertainty.
AI.method: Develop a conceptual framework that formalizes epidemics as controlled, partially observed dynamical systems: latent true state; observations endogenously linked to policy; interventions as sequential actions with behavioral/social feedback; illustrate with three case studies.
AI.result: Demonstrates that explicit world modeling is necessary for policy-relevant reasoning, via case studies on strategic misreporting in surveillance, delays in lagged signals, and counterfactual intervention analysis where histories diverge under different action sequences.
AI.conclusion: World models provide a natural, underdeveloped framework for computational epidemiology, enabling policy-relevant reasoning under uncertainty and guiding surveillance design and intervention analysis.

title: Do Vision Language Models Need to Process Image Tokens?
summary: Vision Language Models (VLMs) have achieved remarkable success by integrating visual encoders with large language models (LLMs). While VLMs process dense image tokens across deep transformer stacks (incurring substantial computational overhead), it remains fundamentally unclear whether sustained image-token processing is necessary for their performance or visual representations meaningfully evolve from early to later layers. In this work, we systematically investigate the functional role of image tokens in VLMs and show that visual representations rapidly converge to a bounded-complexity regime, \ie their entropy stabilizes, intrinsic dimensionality compresses, and trajectory curvature approaches a near-constant profile. In contrast, textual representations continue to undergo substantial restructuring across depth. Once stabilized, visual representations become largely interchangeable between layers, indicating limited additional transformation in deeper stages. Further, depth-wise visual truncation reveals that the necessity of visual processing is task-dependent, where single-token predictions remain comparatively robust to truncated visual depth, but multi-token generation require sustained access to visual representations. Under deterministic decoding, reducing visual depth perturbs intermediate reasoning trajectories more strongly than final outputs, suggesting that image tokens influence the structure of reasoning more than the ultimate conclusions. Collectively, these findings \textbf{question the assumption} that deeper visual processing is uniformly essential in VLMs, challenging the current paradigm of multimodal LLM architectures.

AI.tldr: Visual tokens in VLMs saturate quickly to a low-complexity, stable representation; deep visual processing is not universally necessary, while textual representations continue to evolve; the impact of visual tokens is more on reasoning structure than final outputs.
AI.motivation: To determine whether sustained image-token processing is essential for VLM performance and to characterize how visual representations and their role evolve across network depth.
AI.method: Systematic layer-wise analysis of visual vs textual representations across depth; measure entropy, intrinsic dimensionality, and curvature; perform depth-wise truncation of visual tokens; compare single-token versus multi-token tasks under deterministic decoding; assess robustness of predictions to visual truncation.
AI.result: Visual representations converge to a bounded-complexity regime with stabilized entropy, compressed intrinsic dimensionality, and near-constant trajectory curvature; after stabilization, visual representations are largely interchangeable across layers; textual representations continue to reconfigure with depth; single-token predictions tolerate reduced visual depth, but multi-token generation requires sustained visual information; under deterministic decoding, image tokens influence intermediate reasoning more than final conclusions.
AI.conclusion: These findings challenge the assumption that deeper visual processing is always necessary in VLMs and suggest a potential shift in multimodal LLM design toward more efficient, depth-distributed visual processing.

title: ANTIC: Adaptive Neural Temporal In-situ Compressor
summary: The persistent storage requirements for high-resolution, spatiotemporally evolving fields governed by large-scale and high-dimensional partial differential equations (PDEs) have reached the petabyte-to-exabyte scale. Transient simulations modeling Navier-Stokes equations, magnetohydrodynamics, plasma physics, or binary black hole mergers generate data volumes that are prohibitive for modern high-performance computing (HPC) infrastructures. To address this bottleneck, we introduce ANTIC (Adaptive Neural Temporal in situ Compressor), an end-to-end in situ compression pipeline. ANTIC consists of an adaptive temporal selector tailored to high-dimensional physics that identifies and filters informative snapshots at simulation time, combined with a spatial neural compression module based on continual fine-tuning that learns residual updates between adjacent snapshots using neural fields. By operating in a single streaming pass, ANTIC enables a combined compression of temporal and spatial components and effectively alleviates the need for explicit on-disk storage of entire time-evolved trajectories. Experimental results demonstrate how storage reductions of several orders of magnitude relate to physics accuracy.

AI.tldr: An in situ, end-to-end adaptive temporal and spatial compression pipeline for high-dimensional PDE simulations (ANTIC), achieving substantial storage reductions in a single streaming pass.
AI.motivation: Address extreme data volumes from transient simulations of complex physics (e.g., Navier–Stokes, MHD, plasma, binary black holes) by reducing storage needs while preserving essential physics.
AI.method: AN-TIC combines an adaptive temporal selector that filters informative snapshots during simulation with a spatial neural compression module based on continual fine-tuning to learn residual updates between adjacent snapshots via neural fields; operates in a single streaming pass to compress both temporal and spatial components in situ.
AI.result: Demonstrates storage reductions of several orders of magnitude while maintaining physics accuracy, enabling effective in situ compression without storing full time-evolved trajectories.
AI.conclusion: ANTIC provides a practical end-to-end in situ compression pipeline that mitigates the need for explicit on-disk storage of entire simulations by integrating temporal selection and neural spatial compression in a streaming workflow.

title: SCoRe: Clean Image Generation from Diffusion Models Trained on Noisy Images
summary: Diffusion models trained on noisy datasets often reproduce high-frequency training artifacts, significantly degrading generation quality. To address this, we propose SCoRe (Spectral Cutoff Regeneration), a training-free, generation-time spectral regeneration method for clean image generation from diffusion models trained on noisy images. Leveraging the spectral bias of diffusion models, which infer high-frequency details from low-frequency cues, SCoRe suppresses corrupted high-frequency components of a generated image via a frequency cutoff and regenerates them via SDEdit. Crucially, we derive a theoretical mapping between the cutoff frequency and the SDEdit initialization timestep based on Radially Averaged Power Spectral Density (RAPSD), which prevents excessive noise injection during regeneration. Experiments on synthetic (CIFAR-10) and real-world (SIDD) noisy datasets demonstrate that SCoRe substantially outperforms post-processing and noise-robust baselines, restoring samples closer to clean image distributions without any retraining or fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: Training-free spectral regeneration (SCoRe) for diffusion models trained on noisy data: apply a spectral cutoff to suppress corrupted high-frequency components and regenerate them via SDEdit; establish a theoretical mapping between cutoff frequency and SDEdit timestep using Radially Averaged Power Spectral Density (RAPSD); empirical results on CIFAR-10 and SIDD show improvements without retraining.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models trained on noisy datasets tend to reproduce high-frequency artifacts, degrading generation quality. A training-free, generation-time method is needed to clean images without retraining or fine-tuning.
AI.method: Apply a frequency cutoff to suppress corrupted high-frequency components in generated images, then regenerate these components via SDEdit. Derive a mapping between the cutoff frequency and the SDEdit initialization timestep using Radially Averaged Power Spectral Density (RAPSD) to avoid injecting excessive noise during regeneration.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on synthetic CIFAR-10 and real-world SIDD datasets shows that SCoRe substantially outperforms post-processing and noise-robust baselines, producing samples closer to the distribution of clean images without retraining or fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: SCoRe offers a training-free, generation-time solution that leverages the spectral bias of diffusion models to clean outputs from noisy training data, achieving notable quality gains without model updates.

title: AsymLoc: Towards Asymmetric Feature Matching for Efficient Visual Localization
summary:
Precise and real-time visual localization is critical for applications like AR/VR and robotics, especially on resource-constrained edge devices such as smart glasses, where battery life and heat dissipation can be a primary concerns. While many efficient models exist, further reducing compute without sacrificing accuracy is essential for practical deployment. To address this, we propose asymmetric visual localization: a large Teacher model processes pre-mapped database images offline, while a lightweight Student model processes the query image online. This creates a challenge in matching features from two different models without resorting to heavy, learned matchers.
  We introduce AsymLoc, a novel distillation framework that aligns a Student to its Teacher through a combination of a geometry-driven matching objective and a joint detector-descriptor distillation objective, enabling fast, parameter-less nearest-neighbor matching. Extensive experiments on HPatches, ScanNet, IMC2022, and Aachen show that AsymLoc achieves up to 95% of the teacher's localization accuracy using an order of magnitude smaller models, significantly outperforming existing baselines and establishing a new state-of-the-art efficiency-accuracy trade-off.

AI.tldr: AsymLoc enables near-teacher performance for visual localization with a lightweight Student model guided by a large offline Teacher, achieving strong accuracy with an order-of-magnitude efficiency gain for edge devices.
AI.motivation: Reduce compute, energy, and heat for real-time visual localization on resource-constrained edge devices (e.g., smart glasses) while preserving high accuracy.
AI.method: AsymLoc uses asymmetric distillation: a large Teacher processes pre-mapped database images offline and a lightweight Student processes the query online. It introduces a geometry-driven matching objective and a joint detector-descriptor distillation objective to realize parameter-free nearest-neighbor matching between Student and Teacher features.
AI.result: On HPatches, ScanNet, IMC2022, and Aachen, AsymLoc attains up to 95% of the Teacher’s localization accuracy with an order-of-magnitude smaller models, outperforming baselines and achieving a new efficiency-accuracy frontier.
AI.conclusion: AsymLoc provides an effective asymmetric distillation framework for visual localization, delivering high accuracy with significantly reduced model size and computational cost, suitable for edge devices.

title: Realizing Immersive Volumetric Video: A Multimodal Framework for 6-DoF VR Engagement
comment: Journal extension of CVPR 2025. See also arXiv:2503.14359 . Project page and code: https://github.com/Metaverse-AI-Lab-THU/ImViD
summary: Fully immersive experiences that tightly integrate 6-DoF visual and auditory interaction are essential for virtual and augmented reality. While such experiences can be achieved through computer-generated content, constructing them directly from real-world captured videos remains largely unexplored. We introduce Immersive Volumetric Videos, a new volumetric media format designed to provide large 6-DoF interaction spaces, audiovisual feedback, and high-resolution, high-frame-rate dynamic content. To support IVV construction, we present ImViD, a multi-view, multi-modal dataset built upon a space-oriented capture philosophy. Our custom capture rig enables synchronized multi-view video-audio acquisition during motion, facilitating efficient capture of complex indoor and outdoor scenes with rich foreground--background interactions and challenging dynamics. The dataset provides 5K-resolution videos at 60 FPS with durations of 1-5 minutes, offering richer spatial, temporal, and multimodal coverage than existing benchmarks. Leveraging this dataset, we develop a dynamic light field reconstruction framework built upon a Gaussian-based spatio-temporal representation, incorporating flow-guided sparse initialization, joint camera temporal calibration, and multi-term spatio-temporal supervision for robust and accurate modeling of complex motion. We further propose, to our knowledge, the first method for sound field reconstruction from such multi-view audiovisual data. Together, these components form a unified pipeline for immersive volumetric video production. Extensive benchmarks and immersive VR experiments demonstrate that our pipeline generates high-quality, temporally stable audiovisual volumetric content with large 6-DoF interaction spaces. This work provides both a foundational definition and a practical construction methodology for immersive volumetric videos.

AI.tldr: Introduces Immersive Volumetric Videos (IVV) and ImViD dataset for high-fidelity 6-DoF audiovisual immersion from real-world capture; develops a Gaussian-based spatio-temporal dynamic light-field representation with flow-guided initialization, temporal calibration, and multi-term supervision; presents first method for sound-field reconstruction from multi-view audiovisual data; delivers a complete production pipeline and extensive benchmarks validating high-quality, temporally stable AV volumetric content.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between real-world capture and immersive 6-DoF audiovisual experiences; enable large interaction spaces with synchronized multi-view video and audio; provide datasets and methods to support realistic motion and audio phenomena.
AI.method: Create ImViD dataset with space-oriented capture; build Immersive Volumetric Video (IVV) framework; propose Gaussian-based spatio-temporal representation; flow-guided sparse initialization; joint camera temporal calibration; multi-term spatio-temporal supervision; introduce sound-field reconstruction from multi-view audiovisual data; assemble a unified production pipeline.
AI.result: High-quality, temporally stable audiovisual volumetric content with large 6-DoF interaction spaces; robust modeling of complex motion; 5K-resolution video at 60 FPS; 1–5 minute sequences; extensive benchmarks and immersive VR experiments validate the pipeline.
AI.conclusion: Provides a foundational definition and practical construction methodology for immersive volumetric videos; establishes a scalable pipeline from capture to playback enabling immersive 6-DoF experiences from real-world scenes; outlines avenues for future work in capture fidelity, reconstruction accuracy, and audiovisual consistency.

title: Online3R: Online Learning for Consistent Sequential Reconstruction Based on Geometry Foundation Model
summary: We present Online3R, a new sequential reconstruction framework that is capable of adapting to new scenes through online learning, effectively resolving inconsistency issues. Specifically, we introduce a set of learnable lightweight visual prompts into a pretrained, frozen geometry foundation model to capture the knowledge of new environments while preserving the fundamental capability of the foundation model for geometry prediction. To solve the problems of missing groundtruth and the requirement of high efficiency when updating these visual prompts at test time, we introduce a local-global self-supervised learning strategy by enforcing the local and global consistency constraints on predictions. The local consistency constraints are conducted on intermediate and previously local fused results, enabling the model to be trained with high-quality pseudo groundtruth signals; the global consistency constraints are operated on sparse keyframes spanning long distances rather than per frame, allowing the model to learn from a consistent prediction over a long trajectory in an efficient way. Our experiments demonstrate that Online3R outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods on various benchmarks. Project page: https://shunkaizhou.github.io/online3r-1.0/

AI.tldr: Online3R provides online adaptation for sequential 3D reconstruction by attaching lightweight visual prompts to a frozen geometry foundation model, using local-global self-supervised consistency to train prompts with pseudo groundtruth and sparse long-range keyframes.
AI.motivation: Enable efficient, data-efficient adaptation of geometry models to new scenes without ground-truth annotations or expensive test-time updates, preserving the base model's geometric capabilities.
AI.method: Introduce learnable lightweight visual prompts to a frozen geometry foundation model. Use local self-supervised consistency on intermediate/local fused results to generate pseudo-groundtruth and global self-supervised consistency on sparse keyframes across long trajectories for efficiency. Train prompts online at test-time to adapt to new environments while keeping the base model fixed.
AI.result: Empirically surpasses prior state-of-the-art methods on multiple benchmarks, demonstrating competitive or superior reconstruction quality and efficiency, with a project page for code and results.
AI.conclusion: Online3R enables efficient online adaptation of geometry foundation models for sequential reconstruction using lightweight prompts and local-global self-supervision, improving performance without ground-truth data and with scalable test-time updates.

title: RIRF: Reasoning Image Restoration Framework
summary: Universal image restoration (UIR) aims to recover clean images from diverse and unknown degradations using a unified model. Existing UIR methods primarily focus on pixel reconstruction and often lack explicit diagnostic reasoning over degradation composition, severity, and scene semantics prior to restoration. We propose Reason and Restore (R\&R), a novel framework that integrates structured Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning into the image restoration pipeline. R\&R introduces an explicit reasoner, implemented by fine-tuning Qwen3-VL, to diagnose degradation types, quantify degradation severity, infer key degradation-related factors, and describe relevant scene and object semantics. The resulting structured reasoning provides interpretable and fine-grained diagnostic priors for the restorer. To further improve restoration quality, the quantified degradation severity produced by the reasoner is leveraged as reinforcement learning (RL) signals to guide and strengthen the restorer. Unlike existing multimodal LLM-based agentic systems that decouple reasoning from low-level vision tasks, R\&R tightly couples semantic diagnostic reasoning with pixel-level restoration in a unified framework. Extensive experiments across diverse UIR benchmarks demonstrate that R\&R achieves state-of-the-art performance while offering unique interpretability into the restoration process.

AI.tldr: Reason and Restore (R&R) integrates explicit chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning into universal image restoration by using a fine-tuned reasoner (Qwen3-VL) to diagnose degradation types and severity, infer degradation-related factors, and describe scene semantics. The quantified severity guides the restorer via reinforcement learning, enabling a tightly coupled framework that achieves state-of-the-art results and provides interpretable restoration priors.
AI.motivation: UIR methods focus on pixel reconstruction but lack explicit diagnostic reasoning about degradation composition, severity, and scene semantics. There is a need for interpretable priors that can guide restoration and improve generalization across diverse, unknown degradations.
AI.method: Propose Reason and Restore (R&R). Introduce an explicit reasoner (fine-tuned Qwen3-VL) to diagnose degradation types, quantify degradation severity, infer key degradation-related factors, and describe relevant scene/object semantics. The reasoning outputs provide structured priors to a restorer. The severity estimates serve as reinforcement learning signals to train and guide the restorer. The framework tightly couples semantic diagnostic reasoning with pixel-level restoration in a unified system.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on diverse UIR benchmarks show state-of-the-art performance and improved interpretability. The explicit reasoning yields fine-grained priors that help the restorer achieve higher quality restorations across diverse degradations.
AI.conclusion: Structured CoT reasoning can effectively guide image restoration, offering interpretable priors and improved performance. Using quantified degradation severity as RL feedback strengthens the restorer, and the unified framework better handles unknown, diverse degradations.

title: EgoTL: Egocentric Think-Aloud Chains for Long-Horizon Tasks
summary: Large foundation models have made significant advances in embodied intelligence, enabling synthesis and reasoning over egocentric input for household tasks. However, VLM-based auto-labeling is often noisy because the primary data sources lack accurate human action labels, chain-of-thought (CoT), and spatial annotations; these errors are amplified during long-horizon spatial instruction following. These issues stem from insufficient coverage of minute-long, daily household planning tasks and from inaccurate spatial grounding. As a result, VLM reasoning chains and world-model synthesis can hallucinate objects, skip steps, or fail to respect real-world physical attributes. To address these gaps, we introduce EgoTL. EgoTL builds a think-aloud capture pipeline for egocentric data. It uses a say-before-act protocol to record step-by-step goals and spoken reasoning with word-level timestamps, then calibrates physical properties with metric-scale spatial estimators, a memory-bank walkthrough for scene context, and clip-level tags for navigation instructions and detailed manipulation actions. With EgoTL, we are able to benchmark VLMs and World Models on six task dimensions from three layers and long-horizon generation over minute-long sequences across over 100 daily household tasks. We find that foundation models still fall short as egocentric assistants or open-world simulators. Finally, we finetune foundation models with human CoT aligned with metric labels on the training split of EgoTL, which improves long-horizon planning and reasoning, step-wise reasoning, instruction following, and spatial grounding.

AI.tldr: EgoTL introduces a comprehensive think-aloud data capture pipeline for egocentric tasks to improve VLM and world-model grounding, evaluation, and long-horizon reasoning; it benchmarks across six task dimensions, and finetunes models with CoT aligned to metric labels to boost planning and grounding, though current models still underperform as egocentric assistants/open-world simulators.
AI.motivation: Current VLM-based auto-labeling for egocentric tasks is noisy due to missing human action labels, CoT, and spatial annotations, compounded by insufficient coverage of minute-long daily tasks and inaccurate spatial grounding, leading to hallucinations in reasoning and world-model synthesis.
AI.method: Propose EgoTL: a think-aloud capture pipeline with a say-before-act protocol that records step-by-step goals and spoken reasoning with word-level timestamps; calibrates physical properties using metric-scale spatial estimators; uses a memory-bank walkthrough for scene context and clip-level tags for navigation and manipulation; benchmarks VLMs and world models on six task dimensions across three layers for minute-long sequences over 100 tasks; finetunes foundation models with human CoT aligned to metric labels on EgoTL's training split.
AI.result: Findings indicate foundation models still underperform as egocentric assistants or open-world simulators; finetuning with CoT aligned to metric labels improves long-horizon planning, step-wise reasoning, instruction following, and spatial grounding.
AI.conclusion: EgoTL provides a valuable dataset and benchmarking framework to assess and enhance egocentric AI; while CoT-aligned finetuning yields gains, substantial gaps in long-horizon reasoning and grounding remain, underscoring the need for stronger multimodal grounding and memory/context mechanisms.

title: Tango: Taming Visual Signals for Efficient Video Large Language Models
summary: Token pruning has emerged as a mainstream approach for developing efficient Video Large Language Models (Video LLMs). This work revisits and advances the two predominant token-pruning paradigms: attention-based selection and similarity-based clustering. Our study reveals two critical limitations in existing methods: (1) conventional top-k selection strategies fail to fully account for the attention distribution, which is often spatially multi-modal and long-tailed in magnitude; and (2) direct similarity-based clustering frequently generates fragmented clusters, resulting in distorted representations after pooling. To address these bottlenecks, we propose Tango, a novel framework designed to optimize the utilization of visual signals. Tango integrates a diversity-driven strategy to enhance attention-based token selection, and introduces Spatio-temporal Rotary Position Embedding (ST-RoPE) to preserve geometric structure via locality priors. Comprehensive experiments across various Video LLMs and video understanding benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness and generalizability of our approach. Notably, when retaining only 10% of the video tokens, Tango preserves 98.9% of the original performance on LLaVA-OV while delivering a 1.88$\times$ inference speedup.

AI.tldr: Tango is a token-pruning framework for Video LLMs that (i) diversifies attention-based token selection to cover multi-modal attention distributions and (ii) uses Spatio-temporal Rotary Position Embedding to preserve geometry, achieving high retention with low token usage and faster inference.
AI.motivation: Existing token-pruning methods suffer from (a) top-k selection failing to capture multi-modal, long-tailed attention distributions, and (b) similarity-based clustering producing fragmented clusters that distort pooled representations. There is a need to improve efficiency without sacrificing accuracy in Video LLMs.
AI.method: Tango combines a diversity-driven strategy to enhance attention-based token selection and introduces Spatio-temporal Rotary Position Embedding (ST-RoPE) to enforce locality priors and preserve geometric structure in video tokens.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across various Video LLMs and video-understanding benchmarks show Tango’s effectiveness and generalizability. Notably, with only 10% of video tokens kept, Tango preserves 98.9% of original performance on LLaVA-OV and achieves a 1.88× inference speedup.
AI.conclusion: Tango effectively addresses the bottlenecks of existing token-pruning methods, improving efficiency while maintaining performance; ST-RoPE helps preserve geometric structure in video representations and the approach generalizes across models and benchmarks.
